# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### Question 1: Define an EIA Case Study. List any four project categories where EIA case studies are commonly conducted. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]`

**Model Answer:**
An **EIA Case Study** is a critical post-hoc (or live) analysis of a specific development project to assess the effectiveness of the Environmental Impact Assessment process and Environmental Management Plan (EMP).

**Four Project Categories:**
1. **River Valley / Hydroelectric Projects** (e.g., Silent Valley).
2. **Thermal Power Plants** (e.g., Singrauli).
3. **Mining and Quarrying** (e.g., Kudremukh).
4. **Industrial / Petrochemical Plants** (e.g., Bhopal, Vizag Refinery).

> [!TIP]
> **Word Count Discipline:** KTU expects **40–60 words** for a 3-mark Part A answer. Avoid exceeding the limit.

---

### Question 2: State any three reasons why the Silent Valley project was rejected. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Expected Pattern]`

**Model Answer:**
The Silent Valley project was rejected due to the following reasons:
1. **Ecological Value:** It would have sub-merged **830 hectares** of the last remaining tropical evergreen forest in peninsular India — habitat of the endangered **Lion-tailed Macaque**.
2. **Biodiversity Loss:** Threatened >1000 endemic species including giant grizzled squirrel and rare orchids.
3. **Tribal Displacement:** Would have displaced **~500 tribal families** (Muduga, Irula, Kurumba) dependent on forest produce, raising serious rehabilitation concerns.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A: Comprehensive Case Study Analysis (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**(a)** Discuss the **environmental impacts** predicted and observed in the **Singrauli Super Thermal Power Plant** case study. Use a comparative matrix. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Critically evaluate the **mitigation measures** implemented and the **role of public participation / legal interventions** in Singrauli. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Introduction:** Singrauli, located in the Sonbhadra-Singrauli belt on the UP–MP border, is one of India's largest **thermal power clusters** with combined capacity exceeding **7,000 MW**. Proponents include NTPC, UPSEB, Reliance, and Hindalco.

**Predicted Impacts (from EIA Reports):**
- **Air Emissions:** $\text{SO}_2$, $\text{NO}_x$, particulate matter (PM), and mercury emissions from coal combustion.
- **Fly Ash Generation:** $\sim 3$–$5$ million tonnes per year per 1,000 MW unit.
- **Water Drawl:** Heavy abstraction from Rihand Reservoir; thermal discharge raising river temperature.
- **Forest Loss:** Removal of sal and mixed deciduous forest cover.
- **Displacement:** Local Adivasi (Gond, Kol) communities displaced for ash ponds and townships.

**Actual Observed Impacts:**
- Singrauli is among India's most **polluted industrial zones**; PM 2.5 and PM 10 levels often exceed CPCB limits.
- **Groundwater contamination** due to fly ash leachate; heavy metals reported in soil.
- **Ash dyke failures** causing downstream flooding and crop loss.
- **Public health studies** document increased incidence of **respiratory diseases (COPD, asthma)** in surrounding villages within a 5-km radius.
- **Crop yield reduction** in agricultural lands adjacent to ash ponds.

**Comparative Matrix (pred vs. actual):**

| Parameter | EIA Predicted | Actually Observed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| PM emissions | Within CPCB limits | Often exceed limits |
| Ash utilization | 100% utilization | <60% utilized |
| R and R | Adequate | Inadequate |
| Forest cover | Compensatory afforestation | Net loss in quality |

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Mitigation Measures — Implementation Gap:**
- **Electrostatic Precipitators (ESPs):** Installed but efficiency degraded; many units <99% efficiency.
- **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD):** **Missing or non-operational** in most units despite NGT (2014) order.
- **Fly Ash Utilization:** Far below 100% target; huge ash pond expansion continues.
- **Green Belt:** 100 m greenbelt partially developed; survival rate <60%.

**Public Participation:**
- **Public hearings** were held but **manipulated** — affected communities often not given adequate notice.
- **Local panchayats** filed multiple complaints with the **UP Pollution Control Board** without redressal.

**Legal Interventions:**
- **NGT 2014 Judgment:** Mandated FGD installation in coal-based plants; compliance still incomplete.
- **CPCB Show-Cause Notices** issued in 2017, 2019; few convictions.
- **Villagers' PIL (2019)** in NGT for health compensation — pending.

**Conclusion:** Singrauli is a textbook case of **EIA Prediction–Reality Gap**. The EMP was comprehensive on paper but **implementation was weak**, demonstrating the need for **independent third-party monitoring** and **post-commissioning audits**.

---

### Question B: Alternative Case Study (Internal Choice) (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme Pattern]`

**(a)** Discuss the **Sardar Sarovar Dam** case study in the light of **predicted vs. actual environmental and social impacts**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Examine the **role of the Narmada Bachao Andolan (NBA)** and **Supreme Court judgments** in shaping the EIA framework for large dams. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Project Profile:** Sardar Sarovar Dam on the Narmada River, Gujarat, with capacity **1,450 MW** and irrigation across **1.8 million hectares**.

**Predicted Impacts:**
- Submergence of **~13,000 hectares** of forest.
- Displacement of **~40,000 families** (majority Adivasi).
- Increased seismic risk in the Koyna region.
- Downstream salinity intrusion in the Gulf of Khambhat.

**Actual Impacts:**
- **Displacement:** Over **85,000 people** displaced (numbers exceed original estimate due to incremental project expansion).
- **Rehabilitation:** Widely criticized; many families not rehabilitated per Gujarat R&R Policy.
- **Forest Submergence:** Substantial; downstream ecology affected.
- **Power Generation:** **PLF below 60%** (vs. projected 80%).
- **Irrigation:** Achieved coverage is <50% of the target area.
- **Environmental Flow:** Negligible historically; **NGT (2017)** mandated minimum 30% e-flow.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Role of Narmada Bachao Andolan (NBA):**
- Founded by **Medha Patkar, Baba Amte, and others** in 1985.
- World-renowned **people's movement** for displacement rights.
- Brought global attention via World Bank withdrawal (1993).
- Forced policy review of R&R policies across India.

**Supreme Court Verdicts:**
- **SC (2000):** Allowed construction, but emphasized *"environment cannot be sacrificed at the altar of development."*
- Mandated **comprehensive R&R** and **e-flow** provisions.
- Established the principle of **"sustainable development"** as a balancing test.

**Legacy in EIA Framework:**
- The Narmada case became a **template** for participatory EIA.
- Influenced the **Forest Rights Act, 2006** and **EPA amendments**.
- The **Morse & Berger Report (1992)** — commissioned by World Bank — became a global case study in dam economics.

**Conclusion:** Sardar Sarovar is a **mixed-outcome** case — engineering achievement, but a **cautionary tale** for unaccounted social and ecological costs. It reinforced the need for **continuous, evolving EMPs** rather than one-time clearances.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Do NOT narrate project history** (when built, who inaugurated it) — Examiners want **environmental analysis only**.
> 2. **Do NOT skip the "Actual vs. Predicted" comparison** — This is the **core of any case study answer** (3-4 marks dedicated).
> 3. **Do NOT conclude with a one-liner** — Always end with **Lessons Learned / Recommendations**.
> 4. **Do NOT use absolute value `|x|` in tables** — Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX to avoid markdown parsing errors.
> 5. **Do NOT ignore legal outcomes** — NGT/SC judgments are **high-yield** and frequently asked.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorize These 25 Points:**

1. An EIA Case Study is a **post-hoc critical analysis** of a real project's EIA process.
2. The **6-Pillar Framework**: Background → Baseline → Prediction → Mitigation → Predicted vs. Actual → Public/Legal Outcomes.
3. **Silent Valley (1984)** — **first rejected project in India**; led to the **EPA 1986**.
4. **Sardar Sarovar (Narmada)** — **45,000+ displaced**; **SC 2000**; **e-flow 30% by NGT 2017**.
5. **Singrauli Thermal** — India's most polluted industrial zone; **FGD missing**; NGT 2014 order.
6. **Kudremukh** — Mining **closed by SC 2006**; part of **Western Ghats UNESCO site**.
7. **Bhopal (1984)** — **3,800+ deaths**; led to **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991**.
8. **EIA Notification 2006 + 2020 Amendment** — current legal framework.
9. **Category A projects** cleared by **MoEFCC (EAC)**; **Category B** by **SEIAA**.
10. **Leopold Matrix** is the most common impact prediction tool.
11. **RIAM** (Rapid Impact Assessment Matrix) is widely used in **post-hoc** analysis.
12. **NEB** (Net Environmental Benefit) = Benefits − Costs − Losses.
13. **Public Hearing** is mandatory under Section 3(v) of EIA Notification 2006.
14. **NBA (Narmada Bachao Andolan)** is a landmark people's movement.
15. **Medha Patkar + Baba Amte** = iconic environmental activists.
16. **Morse & Berger Report (1992)** challenged Narmada cost-benefit.
17. **Western Ghats Ecology Expert Panel (Gadgil Committee, 2011)** and **Kasturirangan Committee (2013)** are key references.
18. **Ash ponds** are the largest pollution source in thermal projects.
19. **Compensatory afforestation** is often inadequate — net forest loss is common.
20. **Plant Load Factor (PLF)** of thermal plants is often below projections in case studies.
21. **Largest cause of EIA failure** = implementation gap, not prediction gap.
22. **Coastal projects** require **CRZ clearance** in addition to EIA.
23. **Forest clearance** under **Forest Conservation Act, 1980** is mandatory for projects involving forest land.
24. **Tribal displacement** is governed by **PESA 1996 + Forest Rights Act 2006**.
25. The **Bhopal disaster** is the only major case study that **predates the EIA framework** in India — used to demonstrate *why* EIA was needed.

> **End of Notes — All the best for your KTU Examination!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Pollution (KTU 2024 Definition):** Water pollution is the contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, oceans, aquifers, and groundwater) by anthropogenic or natural substances to such an extent that it measurably alters the physical, chemical, biological, or radiological properties of water, making it unfit for its intended beneficial use (drinking, irrigation, recreation, aquatic life support).

Water quality degradation is quantified through **physical parameters** (turbidity, color, temperature, TDS), **chemical parameters** (pH, hardness, dissolved oxygen, BOD, COD, nutrients, heavy metals), and **biological parameters** (coliform count, algal biomass).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to identify pollution sources, compute key indicator values (BOD, DO, COD), interpret the **Streeter-Phelps DO sag curve**, and apply **IS 10500:2012** / **CPCB water quality criteria** in EIA studies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a clean river as the **human bloodstream** — it carries life-sustaining oxygen (DO) to every cell (aquatic organism). Now imagine pouring sewage, factory waste, and fertilizer runoff into this bloodstream. Just as a person becomes ill, the river becomes "sick" — dissolved oxygen drops, fish die, bacteria proliferate, and the system collapses. **BOD is essentially the river's "fever"** — it measures how much oxygen the pollution is "consuming" from the water, just as a fever consumes energy from the body. **DO Sag Curve** is the river's "recovery timeline" — showing how the river struggles downstream before slowly healing.

## 1.3 Classification of Water Pollutants

| Pollutant Category | Examples | Primary Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Pathogens** | *E. coli*, *Vibrio cholerae*, viruses | Waterborne diseases |
| **Organic Wastes** | Sewage, food processing, paper mill effluent | Oxygen depletion (BOD load) |
| **Nutrients** | Nitrates, Phosphates (from fertilizers) | Eutrophication / algal bloom |
| **Heavy Metals** | Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr(VI) | Bioaccumulation & toxicity |
| **Thermal Pollutants** | Hot water from power plants | Reduces DO solubility |
| **Suspended Solids** | Silt, clay, industrial particulates | Turbidity, light blockage |
| **Radioactive** | Uranium, Cs-137, Sr-90 | Long-term carcinogenic risk |
| **Pesticides / POPs** | DDT, Endosulfan, PCBs | Biomagnification in food chain |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric:** Water quality is reported in **mg/L (ppm)** for most chemicals, **% saturation** for Dissolved Oxygen, and **MPN/100 mL** (Most Probable Number) for coliform bacteria.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources of Water Pollution

### A. Point Sources
Discrete, identifiable discharge points — easy to monitor and regulate.
- Municipal sewage outfalls
- Industrial effluent pipes (textile, tannery, pharmaceutical)
- Power plant cooling water discharges
- Mining drainage

### B. Non-Point (Diffuse) Sources
Spread across a catchment; difficult to trace.
- Agricultural runoff (fertilizers, pesticides)
- Urban stormwater
- Atmospheric deposition
- Livestock waste
- Construction site erosion

## 2.2 Key Water Quality Parameters — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)
**Why it matters:** All aerobic aquatic life depends on DO. Cold water can hold more DO than warm water.

**How it's governed:**
- **Re-aeration** from atmosphere: $K_2$ rate
- **De-oxygenation** by BOD exertion: $K_1$ rate
- **Photosynthesis** adds DO in daylight
- **Respiration & BOD decay** consumes DO

### 2.2.2 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)
The amount of dissolved oxygen consumed by microorganisms to biologically oxidize organic matter over **5 days at 20°C (BOD₅)** — the universal pollution index.

**First-Stage BOD Equation (KTU High-Yield):**
The exerted BOD at any time $t$ follows first-order kinetics:

$$L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-K_1 t}\right)$$

where $L_0$ = ultimate carbonaceous BOD (mg/L), $K_1$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically **0.1 to 0.35 per day** at 20°C.

### 2.2.3 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)
Measure of total oxidizable matter (both biodegradable and non-biodegradable) using strong dichromate oxidation. **Always COD > BOD** because COD oxidizes everything BOD does plus non-biodegradable fractions.

### 2.2.4 Eutrophication
**Definition (KTU):** Nutrient-enriched accelerated aging of a water body leading to excessive algal growth, dissolved oxygen depletion, fish kills, and biodiversity loss.

**Eutrophication Index (Carlson's Trophic State Index):**
$$TSI = 10 \left(6 - \frac{\ln 2}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln SD\right) \text{ where } SD = \text{ Secchi depth (m)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-K_1 t}\right)$ | First-stage BOD at time $t$ | $L$ in mg/L, $t$ in days |
| 2 | $K_{1,T} = K_{1,20} \cdot \theta^{(T-20)}$, $\theta = 1.056$ | Temperature correction for $K_1$ | $K$ in day⁻¹ |
| 3 | $D_t = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2 - K_1} \left(e^{-K_1 t} - e^{-K_2 t}\right) + D_0 \, e^{-K_2 t}$ | **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag** | $D$ in mg/L |
| 4 | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{K_2 - K_1} \ln \left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$ | Time to critical (minimum) DO | days |
| 5 | $D_c = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2} \, e^{-K_1 t_c}$ | Critical oxygen deficit | mg/L |
| 6 | $f = \dfrac{BOD_5}{BOD_L} = 1 - e^{-K_1 \cdot 5}$ | Ratio of 5-day to ultimate BOD | dimensionless |
| 7 | $DO_{sat} = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$ | DO saturation (approx., 0–40°C) | mg/L |
| 8 | $DO_{\% \, sat} = \dfrac{DO_{measured}}{DO_{sat}} \times 100$ | Percent saturation | % |
| 9 | $TSI = 60 - 14.41 \ln SD$ | Carlson's TSI (Secchi depth basis) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_f = C_i \cdot e^{-k t}$ | First-order decay of pollutant | $C$ in mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Temperature correction constant** $\theta = 1.056$ is the universally accepted KTU/JNTU value for BOD reaction rate.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of Water Pollution Concepts |
|---|---|
| **Sewage Treatment Plant Design** | Compute influent BOD load → sizing of aeration tank, clarifier |
| **River Self-Purification Studies** | Streeter-Phelps model for assimilative capacity |
| **EIA for Industrial Discharges** | Predict downstream DO impact |
| **Irrigation Suitability** | Use SAR, RSC, EC criteria |
| **Drinking Water Supply** | Compliance with IS 10500:2012 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Equation

### Step 1 — Mass Balance Setup
Consider a uniform reach of river downstream of a point discharge. Two competing processes govern dissolved oxygen $D$ (oxygen deficit $= DO_{sat} - DO_{actual}$):

- **De-oxygenation rate** (BOD exertion): proportional to remaining oxidizable matter
- **Re-aeration rate** (atmospheric replenishment): proportional to oxygen deficit

### Step 2 — Governing Differential Equation
The rate of change of oxygen deficit $D$ with travel time $t$ (or distance $x$):

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = K_1 L_t - K_2 D$$

where $K_1$ = BOD rate constant, $K_2$ = re-aeration rate constant, $L_t$ = BOD remaining at time $t$.

### Step 3 — Substituting BOD Decay
From first-stage BOD kinetics: $L_t = L_0 \, e^{-K_1 t}$

Substitute into the differential equation:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = K_1 L_0 \, e^{-K_1 t} - K_2 D$$

### Step 4 — Standard First-Order Linear ODE
Rearrange to standard form:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} + K_2 D = K_1 L_0 \, e^{-K_1 t}$$

### Step 5 — Integrating Factor Solution
The integrating factor is $e^{K_2 t}$. Multiply both sides:

$$e^{K_2 t} \frac{dD}{dt} + K_2 e^{K_2 t} D = K_1 L_0 \, e^{(K_2 - K_1) t}$$

The left side is the derivative of $D \, e^{K_2 t}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(D \, e^{K_2 t}\right) = K_1 L_0 \, e^{(K_2 - K_1) t}$$

### Step 6 — Integration
Integrate from $0$ to $t$:

$$D \, e^{K_2 t} - D_0 = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2 - K_1} \left[e^{(K_2 - K_1) t} - 1\right]$$

### Step 7 — Solve for D
Divide by $e^{K_2 t}$ and rearrange:

$$\boxed{D_t = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2 - K_1} \left(e^{-K_1 t} - e^{-K_2 t}\right) + D_0 \, e^{-K_2 t}}$$

This is the **Streeter-Phelps DO Sag Equation** (1925).

### Step 8 — Critical Point Derivation
The critical deficit occurs where $\dfrac{dD}{dt} = 0$. Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} = -K_1 \cdot \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2 - K_1} e^{-K_1 t} + K_2 D_t - K_2 D_0 e^{-K_2 t} = 0$$

After algebraic simplification (Streeter's classical result):

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{K_2 - K_1} \ln \left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

Substitute $t_c$ back into the DO sag equation to obtain $D_c$:

$$D_c = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2} \, e^{-K_1 t_c}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A municipal outfall discharges into a river with the following data:
- $K_1 = 0.23 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $K_2 = 0.40 \, \text{day}^{-1}$
- $L_0 = 50 \, \text{mg/L}$ (ultimate BOD)
- $D_0 = 2.0 \, \text{mg/L}$ (initial oxygen deficit)
- River flow velocity $u = 30 \, \text{km/day}$

**Find:** (a) Critical time $t_c$ and (b) Critical deficit $D_c$ (c) Distance to critical point.

### Part (a): Critical Time
$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{K_2 - K_1} \ln \left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.40 - 0.23} \ln \left(\dfrac{0.40}{0.23}\right) = \dfrac{1}{0.17} \times \ln(1.7391)$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.17} \times 0.5539 = 3.26 \, \text{days}$$

### Part (b): Critical Deficit
$$D_c = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2} \, e^{-K_1 t_c}$$

$$D_c = \dfrac{0.23 \times 50}{0.40} \, e^{-0.23 \times 3.26}$$

$$D_c = 28.75 \times e^{-0.750} = 28.75 \times 0.4724 = 13.58 \, \text{mg/L}$$

### Part (c): Distance to Critical Point
$$x_c = u \cdot t_c = 30 \, \text{km/day} \times 3.26 \, \text{days} = 97.8 \, \text{km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** At ~98 km downstream, the river reaches its lowest DO. If $DO_{sat} \approx 8.5$ mg/L at ambient T, then minimum DO = $8.5 - 13.58 < 0$, meaning the river becomes **anaerobic** — fish kill zone.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quality Standards Reference

| Parameter | IS 10500:2012 (Drinking) | CPCB River (Outdoor Bathing) | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 6.5 – 8.5 | 6.0 – 9.0 | — |
| DO | ≥ 5.0 (recommended) | ≥ 5.0 | mg/L |
| BOD₅ | ≤ 2.0 (acceptable) | ≤ 3.0 | mg/L |
| Total Coliforms | 0 (MPN) | 500 (MPN/100 mL) | MPN |
| Fluoride | ≤ 1.0 | — | mg/L |
| Nitrate | ≤ 45 | — | mg/L |
| Lead | ≤ 0.01 | ≤ 0.1 | mg/L |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sources & Pathways of Water Pollution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Point Source: Sewage Outfall] --> riverA[River Receiving Body]
    srcB[Point Source: Industrial Effluent] --> riverA
    srcC[Point Source: Power Plant Cooling] --> riverA
    srcD[Non-Point: Agricultural Runoff] --> riverA
    srcE[Non-Point: Urban Stormwater] --> riverA
    srcF[Non-Point: Atmospheric Deposition] --> riverA
    srcG[Non-Point: Livestock Waste] --> riverA

    riverA --> phys[Physical Changes: Turbidity, Temperature]
    riverA --> chem[Chemical Changes: pH, BOD, COD, DO drop]
    riverA --> bio[Biological Changes: Algal Bloom, Eutrophy]
    riverA --> heavy[Heavy Metal Accumulation in Sediments]

    phys --> impact[Aquatic Ecosystem Damage]
    chem --> impact
    bio --> impact
    heavy --> impact
    impact --> human[Human Health Risk via Food Chain]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DO Sag Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph upstream[A Upstream of Outfall]
        ua[Clean River DO close to Saturation] --> discharge
    end

    discharge[Discharge Point: BOD Added, DO Drops] --> zone1

    subgraph zone1[B Zone of Degradation]
        z1a[Rapid DO Decline - High BOD Exertion] --> z1b[Visible Pollution, Turbidity High]
    end

    z1b --> zone2

    subgraph zone2[C Zone of Active Decomposition]
        z2a[Minimum DO - Sag Point] --> z2b[Anaerobic conditions possible, H2S smell]
    end

    z2b --> zone3

    subgraph zone3[D Zone of Recovery]
        z3a[Reaeration dominates, DO rising] --> z3b[Aerobic conditions restored]
    end

    z3b --> zone4

    subgraph zone4[E Zone of Cleaner Water]
        z4a[DO nearly back to saturation] --> z4b[Normal aquatic life resumes]
    end
```

## 4.3 Sewage Treatment Process Flow (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inf[Influent: Raw Sewage] --> ps[Preliminary: Screening, Grit Removal]
    ps --> prim[Primary Clarifier: Settle Suspended Solids]
    prim --> aer[Aeration Tank: Biological Oxidation of BOD]
    aer --> sec[Secondary Clarifier: Sludge Recycle]
    sec --> dis[Disinfection: Chlorination or UV]
    dis --> eff[Treated Effluent: BOD less than 20 mg/L]

    prim --> sludge1[Primary Sludge]
    sec --> sludge2[Secondary Sludge WAS]
    sludge1 --> dig[Anaerobic Digester: Biogas Production]
    sludge2 --> dig
    dig --> bios[Biosolids for Land Application]
```

## 4.4 Eutrophication Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fert[Excess N and P from Fertilizer] --> runoff[Runoff to Lake]
    runoff --> algae[Rapid Algal Growth Bloom]
    algae --> light[Light Blockage, Submerged Plants Die]
    algae --> die[Algae Die, Decomposition begins]
    die --> o2[Bacteria Consume DO]
    o2 --> fish[Hypoxic Fish Kill]
    o2 --> ana[Anaerobic Bottom Sediments]
    ana --> rec[Long-term Ecosystem Damage]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than BOD for the same wastewater sample?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)** is the amount of dissolved oxygen utilized by aerobic microorganisms to biologically stabilize biodegradable organic matter in 5 days at 20°C. (1 mark)
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)** is the amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter using strong oxidants like potassium dichromate. (1 mark)
- **Why COD > BOD:** COD measures **total** oxidizable matter, including substances that are toxic to microbes (e.g., hydrocarbons, lignin, phenols) and cannot be biologically oxidized, whereas BOD only captures the **biodegradable fraction** acted upon by living organisms. (1 mark)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by Eutrophication? List two major consequences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Eutrophication** is the nutrient-enrichment (N, P) of a water body leading to excessive growth of algae and aquatic plants, followed by oxygen depletion upon their decay. (1.5 marks)
- **Consequences:** (i) Fish kills due to hypoxia/anoxia, (ii) Loss of biodiversity and dominance of nuisance species, (iii) Production of cyanotoxins (microcystins) harmful to drinking water, (iv) Taste and odor problems. (1.5 marks — any two)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Streeter-Phelps derivations, students often **forget the integrating factor step** and lose 3–4 marks. Always show $\frac{d}{dt}[D e^{K_2 t}]$.
> - In numericals, **units mismatch** (mixing km/day and m/s) is a common 1-mark deduction.
> - Sketching the **DO sag curve with proper labels** (zones A–E, critical point, $D_c$, $t_c$) is mandatory for full marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the various **physical, chemical, and biological** parameters used to characterize water quality with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A river with initial BOD of 2.0 mg/L and DO of 8.0 mg/L receives a municipal discharge such that the mixed BOD ($L_0$) is 25 mg/L, initial deficit $D_0 = 3.0$ mg/L, $K_1 = 0.25$ day⁻¹, $K_2 = 0.45$ day⁻¹. Determine:
(i) The critical time $t_c$ at which the DO is minimum,
(ii) The critical oxygen deficit $D_c$ and the corresponding minimum DO if saturation DO is 9.0 mg/L,
(iii) The distance to critical point if river velocity is 25 km/day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Water Quality Parameters — Classification:**

1. **Physical Parameters (2 marks)**
   - Temperature — affects DO saturation
   - Turbidity — measured in NTU
   - Total Suspended Solids (TSS)
   - Color, Odor, Conductivity
   - Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)

2. **Chemical Parameters (2.5 marks)**
   - pH (6.5–8.5 desirable)
   - Alkalinity, Hardness
   - Dissolved Oxygen, BOD, COD
   - Nitrogen species — ammonia, nitrate, nitrite
   - Phosphates, chlorides, sulfates
   - Heavy metals — Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr
   - Pesticides, oils & grease

3. **Biological Parameters (1.5 marks)**
   - Total coliforms (MPN/100 mL)
   - Fecal coliforms / *E. coli*
   - Algal count and diversity
   - BOD and biota-based indices

4. **Bacteriological Indicator Significance (1 mark):** Coliforms indicate fecal contamination and potential pathogen presence — the universally accepted pollution indicator organism.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $K_1 = 0.25 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $K_2 = 0.45 \, \text{day}^{-1}$, $L_0 = 25 \, \text{mg/L}$, $D_0 = 3.0 \, \text{mg/L}$, $DO_{sat} = 9.0 \, \text{mg/L}$, $u = 25 \, \text{km/day}$

**(i) Critical Time $t_c$:**

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{K_2 - K_1} \ln \left(\dfrac{K_2}{K_1}\right)$$

$$t_c = \dfrac{1}{0.45 - 0.25} \ln \left(\dfrac{0.45}{0.25}\right) = \dfrac{1}{0.20} \ln(1.8)$$

$$t_c = 5.0 \times 0.5878 = 2.94 \, \text{days}$$

**(ii) Critical Deficit $D_c$ and Minimum DO:**

$$D_c = \dfrac{K_1 L_0}{K_2} \, e^{-K_1 t_c}$$

$$D_c = \dfrac{0.25 \times 25}{0.45} \, e^{-0.25 \times 2.94}$$

$$D_c = 13.889 \times e^{-0.735} = 13.889 \times 0.4795 = 6.66 \, \text{mg/L}$$

Minimum DO = $DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.0 - 6.66 = 2.34 \, \text{mg/L}$

Since 2.34 mg/L < 5 mg/L (CPCB standard for bathing), the river is **unfit for aquatic life support** at the critical point.

**(iii) Distance to Critical Point:**

$$x_c = u \cdot t_c = 25 \times 2.94 = 73.5 \, \text{km}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the **Streeter-Phelps model** for dissolved oxygen in a polluted river. Derive the expression for critical oxygen deficit. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **sources and effects of heavy metal pollution** in water bodies. Explain Bioaccumulation and Biomagnification with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) Streeter-Phelps Model (7 marks):**

1. **Concept (1.5 marks):** Developed by H.W. Streeter and E.B. Phelps (1925). Predicts DO variation downstream of a point discharge assuming steady-state, uniform flow, and first-order BOD decay.
2. **Assumptions (1.5 marks):** Single uniform reach, no lateral inflow, constant $K_1$ and $K_2$, complete mixing at outfall, no photosynthesis/respiration considered.
3. **Mass Balance & Derivation (3 marks):** Show the differential equation $\frac{dD}{dt} = K_1 L_0 e^{-K_1 t} - K_2 D$ and the integrated form (refer to Section 3.1 above).
4. **Critical Point (1 mark):** State $t_c$ and $D_c$ expressions with physical meaning.

**(b) Heavy Metal Pollution (7 marks):**

1. **Sources (2 marks):** Mining, smelting, electroplating, tanneries (Cr), battery industry (Pb, Cd), pesticides (As, Hg), coal combustion, e-waste.
2. **Effects (2 marks):** Toxic to aquatic life, kidney damage (Cd), neurological disorders (Pb, Hg), carcinogenic (As, Cr VI), teratogenic effects.
3. **Bioaccumulation (1.5 marks):** Progressive buildup of a substance in an organism's body over time, because uptake exceeds elimination. Example: Hg in muscle tissue of fish.
4. **Biomagnification (1.5 marks):** Increase in concentration of a persistent pollutant as it moves up the food chain across trophic levels. Classic example: **DDT in food chain** — water (0.003 ppm) → zooplankton (0.04 ppm) → small fish (0.5 ppm) → large fish (2.0 ppm) → fish-eating birds (25 ppm).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **BOD₅ = $L_0 (1 - e^{-K_1 \cdot 5})$**; typically $\approx 0.68 \cdot L_0$ for $K_1 = 0.23$ day⁻¹
- ✅ **Always** state Streeter-Phelps **assumptions** (steady, uniform, single reach, no PSF)
- ✅ **Temperature correction:** $K_{1,T} = K_{1,20} \cdot (1.056)^{T-20}$
- ✅ **DO Sag curve has 5 zones:** Clean water → Degradation → Active decomposition → Recovery → Clean water
- ✅ **Critical deficit > Saturation DO** ⇒ Anaerobic conditions ⇒ Fish kill
- ✅ **COD > BOD** for same sample (chemical vs biological oxidation)
- ✅ **Eutrophication triggers:** N > 0.3 mg/L or P > 0.02 mg/L in lakes
- ✅ **BIS Drinking Water Standard:** IS 10500:2012; **CPCB River:** DO ≥ 5 mg/L, BOD ≤ 3 mg/L
- ✅ **Biomagnification requires:** Persistent + Lipophilic + Bioaccumulative chemicals (e.g., DDT, Hg, PCBs)
- ✅ **Numerical Units:** $K$ in day⁻¹, $L$ in mg/L, $D$ in mg/L, $t$ in days, $u$ in km/day
- ✅ **Remember to sketch the DO sag curve** in any 14-mark Streeter-Phelps question — visualizing the river's zones fetches easy 2 marks
- ✅ **Merits of Streeter-Phelps model:** Simple, intuitive, basis for assimilative capacity. **Demerits:** Ignores PSF, settling, benthic demand, multiple sources — improved by O'Connor-Dobbins and Thomas models
- ✅ **Thermal pollution** reduces DO because gas solubility decreases with rising temperature (Henry's Law)
- ✅ **MPN** = Most Probable Number per 100 mL — the standard microbiological reporting unit for coliforms

> **End of Module 2 — Water Pollution Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point and Non-point Sources of Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Point and Non-point Sources of Pollution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Point Sources of Pollution — Formal KTU Definition

A **Point Source of Pollution** is a single, discrete, localized, and identifiable point of discharge from which pollutants are released into a receiving environmental medium (air, water, or land). The emission originates from a confined, well-defined outlet such as a pipe, ditch, tunnel, well, container, or concentrated animal feeding operation (CAFO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Memorize this verbatim):**
> *"A point source is a stationary, single, identifiable conveyance — usually a pipe, ditch, or stack — from which pollutants are or may be discharged into the environment."*
> — U.S. Clean Water Act (CWA) §502(14), as adopted in KTU EIA syllabus framework.

The pollutant load from a point source is **measurable at the outlet** using effluent monitoring instruments, flow meters, and grab/ composite sampling. This is the reason regulatory bodies (KSPCB, CPCB, MoEFCC) can issue **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** permits specifically to point sources.

## 1.2 Non-Point Sources of Pollution — Formal KTU Definition

A **Non-Point Source (NPS) of Pollution** (also called *Diffuse Source* or *Area Source*) is a pollution source that does not discharge through a single, confined conveyance. Instead, pollutants are carried off the land surface by **hydrological and meteorological transport mechanisms** — primarily stormwater runoff, atmospheric deposition, soil erosion, snowmelt, and seepage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Characteristics of Non-Point Sources:**
> - No fixed outlet or single point of discharge
> - Spatially distributed over large catchment / watershed areas
> - Intermittent — driven by rainfall, snowmelt, or wind events
> - Quantification requires **areal loading rates** (kg/ha/yr) rather than concentration × flow
> - Identified as the **leading cause of water quality impairment** globally by US-EPA

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Point Source Analogy — "The Single Tap"**
Imagine a kitchen tap dripping oil. You can see *exactly* where the oil comes from, you can place a bucket under it, measure the volume per minute, and turn it off by closing the tap. That tap = **point source**. A factory chimney, a sewage treatment plant outfall, a refinery discharge pipe — all are "taps" of pollution.

**Non-Point Source Analogy — "The Rain Washing the Car"**
Now imagine the same oily car parked in a driveway. When it rains, oil is washed off *the entire surface* of the car, parking lot, and surrounding ground. There is no single "tap" — the pollution is *everywhere*. The rainwater carries the oil into the storm drain. That diffuse runoff = **non-point source**. Agricultural fields, urban streets, construction sites, and logged forests behave the same way.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Viva:**
> - **Point = Pipe** (you can put a pipe symbol on a map 📍)
> - **Non-Point = Pavement** (oil smeared on a road, no pipe anywhere 🛣️)

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Use in Board Answers)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|
| Effluent flow rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ or MLD |
| Pollutant concentration | $C$ | $\text{mg/L}$ |
| Pollutant mass loading | $L$ | $\text{kg/day}$ |
| Areal loading rate (NPS) | $A_L$ | $\text{kg/ha/yr}$ |
| Population equivalent | $PE$ | persons |
| Stormwater runoff coefficient | $C_r$ | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification Logic of Pollution Sources

Pollution sources are classified along **three orthogonal axes** in the KTU 2024 EIA framework:

1. **Spatial Axis** → Point vs. Non-Point
2. **Medium Axis** → Air, Water, Land (soil & noise)
3. **Origin Axis** → Anthropogenic (man-made) vs. Natural (geogenic, biogenic)

For Module 2, we focus on the **Spatial Axis** because it directly drives the EIA scoping, baseline monitoring design, and mitigation strategy.

## 2.2 Detailed Characteristics of Point Sources

- **Conduit-bound:** Discharge occurs through a single engineered outlet (pipe, stack, ditch, outfall).
- **Continuous or batch-discharge:** Operational schedule is deterministic — the unit is either ON or OFF.
- **Quantifiable directly:** Load = $Q \times C$ measured at the outlet.
- **Regulated under:** Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974; Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.
- **Examples in Kerala context:**
  * Eloor Industrial Belt effluent outlets into Periyar River
  * Hindustan Newsprint Ltd. outfall at Velloor
  * Cochin Port Trust ballast water discharge
  * Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETPs) in various industrial estates

## 2.3 Detailed Characteristics of Non-Point Sources

- **No fixed outlet:** Distributed across a catchment, watershed, or airshed.
- **Event-driven:** Strongly correlated with rainfall intensity, antecedent dry period, and land use.
- **Quantification via modeling:** Rational method, SCS-CN method, Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE), export coefficients.
- **Regulated under:** No single-point consent mechanism; managed through **Integrated Watershed Management Programmes** and **Pollution Control Board advisories**.
- **Examples in Kerala context:**
  * Runoff from paddy fields carrying fertilizers (Nilam, NPK) into Vembanad Lake
  * Coconut husk retting zones in backwaters
  * Road surface runoff from National Highways carrying oil, heavy metals
  * Construction site erosion in hill districts (Wayanad, Idukki)

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in 14-mark derivation and 3-mark short-answer questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = Q \times C$ | Point source mass loading | $\text{kg/day}$ | Direct effluent calculation |
| 2 | $Q = A \times V$ | Volumetric flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Pipe / channel discharge |
| 3 | $L = C_r \times I \times A$ | Rational method (NPS runoff) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Stormwater peak discharge |
| 4 | $A = S_c \times \text{CN}$ | SCS Curve Number abstraction | mm | Runoff depth estimation |
| 5 | $A_L = EC \times \text{Concentration}$ | Export coefficient method | $\text{kg/ha/yr}$ | NPS load from land use |
| 6 | $PE = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_{\text{load}} \times 10^6}{\text{per-capita BOD}}$ | Population equivalent | persons | STP / discharge capacity |
| 7 | $R_\text{BOD} = \text{BOD}_{\text{in}} - \text{BOD}_{\text{out}}$ | BOD removal | $\text{mg/L}$ | Treatment efficiency |

> **Note on units:** $Q$ = discharge in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$; $C$ in $\text{mg/L} = \text{g/m}^3$; multiply by $10^{-3}$ to get kg/day conversion as $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EIA Scoping:** Determines whether to install a continuous effluent monitoring station (point) or design a watershed sampling grid (non-point).
- **Discharge Consent Fees (KSPCB):** Calculated from point source load; NPS pay no direct fees — managed through policy.
- **Treatment Plant Design (CETP, ETP, STP):** Sized using point source load; receiving water assimilative capacity is the counter-check.
- **Best Management Practices (BMPs) for NPS:** Riparian buffers, contour bunding, constructed wetlands, green infrastructure, no-till farming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Point Source Pollutant Mass Loading

The mass of pollutant discharged per unit time from a point source is the product of volumetric flow rate and pollutant concentration.

**Starting Statement:** The mass loading $L$ of a pollutant from a point source equals the volume discharged per unit time multiplied by the pollutant concentration in that volume.

$$
L = Q \times C
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute consistent units.**
Given $Q$ in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ and $C$ in $\text{mg/L}$, we must convert because $1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$.

$$
1 \text{ mg/L} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mg}}{10^{-3} \text{ m}^3} = 10^{3} \text{ mg/m}^3 = 1 \text{ g/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the load equation.**

$$
L = Q \times C \times 10^{-6} \quad [\text{kg/day}]
$$

where $Q$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ and $C$ is in $\text{mg/L}$ (the $10^{-6}$ factor converts g to kg).

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style).**

A distillery discharges effluent at $Q = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ with $\text{BOD} = 2000 \text{ mg/L}$. Find the daily BOD mass loading.

$$
L_{\text{BOD}} = Q \times C \times 10^{-6}
$$

Substitute values:

$$
L_{\text{BOD}} = 500 \times 2000 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
L_{\text{BOD}} = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \text{ kg/day}
$$

> **Wait — re-evaluate the factor.** $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3$, so $C = 2000 \text{ mg/L} = 2000 \text{ g/m}^3 = 2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$
L = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \times 2 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 1000 \text{ kg/day} = 1.0 \text{ tonne/day}
$$

> This is a typical BOD load of a molasses-based distillery. (Memory hook: $1 \text{ mg/L} \equiv 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$ — keep this straight!)

## 3.2 Derivation: Non-Point Source Areal Loading Using Export Coefficient Method

**Starting Concept:** Unlike point sources, NPS load is estimated per unit area of land use and summed across the catchment.

$$
L_{\text{NPS}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} EC_i \times A_i \times P_i
$$

where $EC_i$ = export coefficient of pollutant for land use $i$ (kg/ha/yr); $A_i$ = area of land use $i$ (ha); $P_i$ = precipitation/ runoff correction factor (dimensionless); $n$ = number of land-use classes.

**Step 1 — Identify land uses.** For a Kerala watershed: paddy, coconut, rubber, urban, water body, fallow.

**Step 2 — Assign export coefficients** (typical literature values):

| Land Use | EC for Total N (kg/ha/yr) | EC for Total P (kg/ha/yr) |
|---|---|---|
| Paddy | 8.0 | 1.2 |
| Coconut | 5.0 | 0.6 |
| Rubber | 4.0 | 0.4 |
| Urban | 12.0 | 2.5 |
| Fallow | 2.0 | 0.3 |

**Step 3 — Multiply and sum across the watershed.**

For a 1000 ha watershed: Paddy 400 ha, Coconut 300 ha, Urban 100 ha, Fallow 200 ha, $P = 1.0$ (annual average).

$$
L_N = (8.0 \times 400) + (5.0 \times 300) + (12.0 \times 100) + (2.0 \times 200)
$$

$$
L_N = 3200 + 1500 + 1200 + 400 = 6300 \text{ kg/yr}
$$

For Phosphorus:

$$
L_P = (1.2 \times 400) + (0.6 \times 300) + (2.5 \times 100) + (0.3 \times 200)
$$

$$
L_P = 480 + 180 + 250 + 60 = 970 \text{ kg/yr}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Population Equivalent (PE) — Critical for EIA Wastewater Sizing

**Concept:** PE converts industrial wastewater load into the equivalent number of persons that would produce the same BOD load using domestic sewage.

Standard per-capita BOD generation = **$54 \text{ g/person/day}$** (CPCB norms) or **$60 \text{ g/person/day}$** (older IS norms). Use **$54 \text{ g/person/day}$** unless specified.

$$
PE = \dfrac{\text{Industrial BOD load (g/day)}}{54 \text{ g/person/day}}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute industrial BOD load.** $L_{\text{BOD}} = Q \times C \times 10^3$ (to get g/day when $Q$ is $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ and $C$ is $\text{mg/L}$).

**Step 2 — Divide by 54.**

**Worked Example:** Dairy plant discharges $Q = 200 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ with $\text{BOD} = 1200 \text{ mg/L}$.

$$
\text{BOD Load} = 200 \times 1200 \times 10^3 \text{ mg/day} = 2.4 \times 10^8 \text{ mg/day} = 240 \text{ kg/day} = 2.4 \times 10^5 \text{ g/day}
$$

$$
PE = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^5}{54} \approx 4444 \text{ persons}
$$

This dairy plant discharges BOD load equivalent to a town of ~4444 people.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (KTU Two-Mark Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison table is the highest-yield content for KTU Module 2 — appears almost every exam cycle.**

| Parameter | Point Source | Non-Point Source |
|---|---|---|
| Outlet | Single, identifiable | Diffuse, spread out |
| Spatial scale | Localized (m²) | Areal (ha to km²) |
| Temporal pattern | Continuous / batch | Storm / event-driven |
| Measurement | Direct (Q, C at outlet) | Indirect (modeling, sampling) |
| Regulatory tool | CTE / CTO under Water Act | Watershed management plans |
| Mitigation cost | End-of-pipe treatment (high OPEX) | BMPs (low OPEX, high land need) |
| Load calculation | $L = Q \times C$ | $L = \sum EC_i \times A_i$ |
| Sampling frequency | Continuous / composite grab | Storm event sampling |
| Examples | STP outfall, factory stack | Agricultural runoff, urban stormwater |
| Kerala example | Eloor ETP, FACT discharge | Vembanad paddy runoff |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flow — Pollution Source Taxonomy

This block diagram maps the complete taxonomy of pollution sources as required in KTU Module 2, showing the spatial and medium-based bifurcation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[SOURCES OF POLLUTION] --> B1[Spatial Classification]
    A0 --> B2[Medium Classification]
    A0 --> B3[Origin Classification]
    B1 --> C1A[Point Source]
    B1 --> C1B[Non Point Source]
    B2 --> C2A[Air Emission]
    B2 --> C2B[Water Discharge]
    B2 --> C2C[Land / Soil]
    B2 --> C2D[Noise]
    B3 --> C3A[Anthropogenic]
    B3 --> C3B[Natural / Geogenic]
    C1A --> D1A[Industrial Outfall Pipe]
    C1A --> D1B[Domestic Sewage Outlet]
    C1A --> D1C[Power Plant Stack]
    C1A --> D1D[Hospital Waste Pipe]
    C1A --> D1E[Marine Vessel Discharge]
    C1B --> D2A[Agricultural Runoff]
    C1B --> D2B[Urban Stormwater]
    C1B --> D2C[Construction Erosion]
    C1B --> D2D[Logging Forest Roads]
    C1B --> D2E[Atmospheric Deposition]
    C1A --> E1A[Measured as L equals Q times C]
    C1B --> E2A[Estimated as sum of EC times A]
    E1A --> F1[Regulated via Consent CTE CTO]
    E2A --> F2[Managed via Watershed BMPs]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NPS Pollutant Transport Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1A[Land Surface Activities] --> S2A[Pollutant Accumulation Phase]
    S2A --> S3A[Precipitation Event]
    S3A --> S4A[Runoff Generation]
    S4A --> S5A[Overland Flow Transport]
    S5A --> S6A[Conveyance via Drains and Streams]
    S6A --> S7A[Receiving Water Body]
    S7A --> S8A[Water Quality Degradation]
    S2A --> S9A[Soil Erosion Component]
    S9A --> S5A
    S3A --> S10A[Atmospheric Washout]
    S10A --> S5A
    S8A --> S11A[EIA Mitigation BMPs]
    S11A --> S12A[Vegetative Buffer]
    S11A --> S12B[Constructed Wetland]
    S11A --> S12C[Retention Pond]
    S11A --> S12D[Contour Bunding]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Point Source Regulatory Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Industry Unit Proposal] --> P2[Apply for CTE Consent to Establish]
    P2 --> P3[KSPCB Technical Review]
    P3 --> P4A{Approved}
    P3 --> P4B{Rejected with EIA Deficiency}
    P4A --> P5[Construction of ETP / STP]
    P5 --> P6[Apply for CTO Consent to Operate]
    P6 --> P7[Continuous Effluent Monitoring]
    P7 --> P8A{Within Standards}
    P7 --> P8B{Violation Detected}
    P8A --> P9[Operational License Continues]
    P8B --> P10[Legal Notice Closure and Penalty]
    P10 --> P11[Compliance Report Resubmission]
    P11 --> P7
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Differentiate between point and non-point sources of pollution. Give two examples of each relevant to Kerala.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks = 1 mark definition × 2 + 1 mark examples):**

| Aspect | Point Source | Non-Point Source |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Discrete, identifiable, single point of discharge through a pipe, ditch, or stack (1 mark) | Diffuse, dispersed pollution with no single outlet, transported by runoff, wind, or seepage (1 mark) |
| **Kerala Example 1** | Eloor industrial estate effluent outfall into Periyar River | Paddy field fertilizer runoff into Vembanad Lake |
| **Kerala Example 2** | FACT Udyogamandal ammonia plant stack emission | Urban stormwater from Kochi city roads carrying oil & heavy metals |

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is meant by "Population Equivalent" in EIA? A dairy plant discharges 250 m³/day of wastewater with BOD = 1500 mg/L. Calculate the Population Equivalent (assume per-capita BOD = 54 g/day).**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** *Population Equivalent (PE) is the number of persons that would produce the same BOD load per day as the industrial discharge, assuming each person generates 54 g of BOD per day (CPCB norm).*

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{BOD load} = Q \times C \times 10^{-3} = 250 \times 1500 \times 10^{-3} = 375 \text{ kg/day}
$$

Converting to grams: $375 \times 10^3 = 3.75 \times 10^5 \text{ g/day}$.

$$
PE = \dfrac{3.75 \times 10^5}{54} \approx 6944 \text{ persons}
$$

> **The dairy plant's wastewater BOD is equivalent to that of a residential colony of ~6944 people.**

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern: Module Internal Choice means students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Each is 14 marks with sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Option 1

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the characteristics of point sources of pollution. With neat sketches, describe how a typical industrial effluent treatment plant (ETP) outlet is monitored for regulatory compliance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pharmaceutical industry discharges $Q = 800 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ of effluent with $\text{BOD} = 800 \text{ mg/L}$ and $\text{COD} = 2200 \text{ mg/L}$. Calculate: (i) Daily BOD and COD mass loading in kg/day. (ii) Population Equivalent. (iii) BOD/COD ratio and comment on biodegradability.

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Characteristics of point sources (4 marks):**
  * Single, confined, identifiable discharge point (pipe, stack, ditch)
  * Continuous or batch — deterministic operational pattern
  * Quantifiable through direct measurement ($Q$ and $C$ at outlet)
  * Regulated under Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981
  * Examples: Industrial ETP outlets, domestic STP outfalls, refinery stacks

- **ETP Outlet Monitoring (3 marks):**
  * Online Continuous Effluent Monitoring System (OCEMS) installed at the final discharge point
  * Parameters: pH, TSS, COD, BOD, flow rate, temperature
  * Real-time data telemetered to CPCB / KSPCB servers
  * Composite sampling done manually weekly for verification
  * Stack monitoring uses isokinetic sampling for PM, SO₂, NOₓ

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Mass loading:**

$$
L_{\text{BOD}} = Q \times C \times 10^{-3} = 800 \times 800 \times 10^{-3} = 640 \text{ kg/day}
$$

$$
L_{\text{COD}} = 800 \times 2200 \times 10^{-3} = 1760 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**(ii) Population Equivalent:**

$$
PE = \dfrac{640 \times 10^3}{54} \approx 11{,}852 \text{ persons}
$$

**(iii) BOD/COD ratio:**

$$
\text{BOD/COD} = \dfrac{800}{2200} \approx 0.36
$$

> **Comment:** A BOD/COD ratio of 0.36 is **moderately biodegradable** (borderline range 0.3–0.5). Biological treatment (activated sludge, MBBR) is feasible but pre-treatment for recalcitrant COD (chemical oxidation, adsorption) is recommended.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Option 2

**(a) [7 Marks]** Define non-point sources of pollution. Describe the export coefficient method for estimating non-point source nutrient loading in a watershed. List four best management practices (BMPs) for NPS control.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 600-hectare mixed-land-use watershed has the following data. Estimate the annual Total Nitrogen loading using the export coefficient method (assume precipitation factor $P = 1.0$).

| Land Use | Area (ha) | EC for N (kg/ha/yr) |
|---|---|---|
| Paddy | 200 | 8.0 |
| Coconut | 150 | 5.0 |
| Urban | 50 | 12.0 |
| Forest | 200 | 1.5 |

**Model Answer (a) — 7 Marks:**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A non-point source of pollution is a diffuse, spatially distributed source that does not discharge through a single, confined conveyance. Pollutants are transported by stormwater runoff, soil erosion, atmospheric deposition, and seepage.

- **Export Coefficient Method (3.5 marks):**
  * Each land-use class $i$ is assigned an export coefficient $EC_i$ (kg/ha/yr)
  * Total load $L = \sum EC_i \times A_i \times P_i$
  * $P_i$ adjusts for precipitation variability
  * Simple, requires only land-use map and literature ECs
  * Limitation: static, doesn't capture storm-event dynamics

- **Four BMPs for NPS Control (2 marks):**
  1. **Vegetative buffer strips** along water bodies to trap sediment & nutrients
  2. **Constructed wetlands** for nutrient polishing
  3. **Contour bunding / terracing** in sloping agricultural land
  4. **Retention / detention ponds** for urban stormwater management

**Model Answer (b) — 7 Marks:**

$$
L_N = (8.0 \times 200) + (5.0 \times 150) + (12.0 \times 50) + (1.5 \times 200)
$$

Stepwise:

$$
L_N = 1600 + 750 + 600 + 300
$$

$$
L_N = 3250 \text{ kg/yr}
$$

> The 600 ha watershed delivers ~3.25 tonnes of total nitrogen per year to the receiving water body. Per hectare average areal loading = $3250 / 600 = 5.42 \text{ kg/ha/yr}$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Valuation (Internal Faculty + Board Examiner Consensus):**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion error in $L = Q \times C$** — The biggest killer. $C$ in mg/L MUST be multiplied by $10^{-6}$ (or $C \times 10^{-3}$ when both $Q$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ and result in kg/day). Students frequently write $L = Q \times C$ and put kg/day directly — losing 2–3 marks silently.
>
> 2. **Using wrong per-capita BOD** — Always state **$54 \text{ g/person/day}$ (CPCB)** unless the question explicitly gives a different value. Using "60" or "80" without justification is a mark deduction.
>
> 3. **Confusing PE with sewered population** — PE is a *load equivalent*, not the number of people actually connected to the plant.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the precipitation factor $P$** in export coefficient method — even if the question gives $P=1$, mention it in your equation.
>
> 5. **Examples not from Kerala context** — When KTU asks "give examples," Kerala-specific examples (Eloor, Vembanad, Periyar) score higher than generic global ones.
>
> 6. **No diagram in Part B 7-marker** — Always include a neat labelled block diagram / flow chart of ETP or NPS pathway. Missing diagram = 1–2 marks lost.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this aloud twice before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **Point source** = single pipe / stack / ditch, measurable $L = Q \times C$, regulated by CTE/CTO
- [ ] **Non-point source** = diffuse, event-driven, estimated via $L = \sum EC_i \times A_i$, regulated by watershed programs
- [ ] **Unit conversion mantra:** $1 \text{ mg/L} = 1 \text{ g/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \text{ kg/m}^3$
- [ ] **PE formula:** $PE = \dfrac{L_{\text{BOD, g/day}}}{54}$ (CPCB per-capita norm)
- [ ] **BOD/COD ratio interpretation:** $> 0.5$ biodegradable; $0.3$–$0.5$ moderately; $< 0.3$ recalcitrant
- [ ] **Kerala iconic examples to remember:** Eloor (point), Vembanad paddy runoff (NPS), Periyar river, FACT stack
- [ ] **BMPs for NPS:** Buffer strips, constructed wetlands, retention ponds, contour bunding
- [ ] **Monitoring tools:** OCEMS for point sources; storm-event grab sampling + modeling for NPS
- [ ] **Acts to recall:** Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974; Air Act 1981; EIA Notification 2006
- [ ] **Memorize one derivation** (PE calculation) and **one comparative table** — these are 14-mark savers
- [ ] **Always draw a block diagram** in 7-mark sub-questions — it pads marks even if your text is incomplete

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Point and Non-point Sources of Pollution**

topics.title: Major Pollutants of Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Major Pollutants of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Pollutant (KTU Definition):**
> A water pollutant is any physical, chemical, biological, or radiological substance introduced into a water body (surface water or groundwater) in such a concentration that it measurably degrades the natural quality of water, rendering it unsuitable for its designated beneficial use — domestic, industrial, agricultural, or ecological.

> [!NOTE]
> **Designated Beneficial Use (DBU):** The KTU/IS 2296 classification categorizes water bodies by intended use (Class A: Drinking, Class B: Bathing, Class C: Propagation of wildlife, Class D: Irrigation, Class E: Industrial cooling). The pollutant impact is evaluated relative to the relevant DBU.

## 1.2 Classification of Water Pollutants

According to the CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) framework adopted in the EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020), water pollutants are classified into **9 primary categories**:

1. **Pathogens** – Disease-causing microorganisms
2. **Oxygen-Demanding Wastes** – Biodegradable organics
3. **Nutrients** – Nitrogen and Phosphorus compounds
4. **Suspended Solids / Sediments**
5. **Heavy Metals** – Toxic inorganic trace elements
6. **Synthetic Organic Chemicals** – Pesticides, PCBs, PAHs
7. **Thermal Discharges**
8. **Radioactive Substances**
9. **Oil and Grease**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Aquarium Principle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Understanding:** Imagine a sealed glass aquarium containing fish, plants, and balanced microbes. Now consider what happens when you perform the following actions:
> - **Drop leftover food** (organic waste) → fish suffocate (oxygen depletion → BOD rise)
> - **Overfeed with fish food containing N/P** → green algae bloom → oxygen crash (eutrophication)
> - **Spray insect repellent nearby** → mercury-leaching compounds enter water → fish die (toxicity)
> - **Pour hot tea** → warm water holds less oxygen → thermal stress
> 
> Every pollutant category mimics one of these "aquarium stressors." A water body has a finite **assimilative capacity** — the threshold beyond which the natural self-purification mechanism collapses. This capacity is quantified using parameters like BOD, COD, and DO.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics (Mandatory Knowledge)

The following constants and standards are routinely cited in KTU exam answers and EIA reports:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Key Reference |
|-----------|--------|---------------|----------------|
| Dissolved Oxygen | DO | **mg/L** | Saturation = **9.1 mg/L at 20 °C, 1 atm** |
| Biochemical Oxygen Demand | BOD | **mg/L** | BOD₅ standard = **≤ 3 mg/L (drinking)** |
| Chemical Oxygen Demand | COD | **mg/L** | Industrial effluent limit = **≤ 250 mg/L** (CPCB) |
| Total Suspended Solids | TSS | **mg/L** | Inland surface water = **≤ 50 mg/L** |
| pH | – | – | Acceptable range = **6.5 to 8.5** |
| Most Probable Number | MPN | **per 100 mL** | Drinking water = **0 MPN/100 mL coliforms** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumed Temperature Reference:** Unless stated otherwise, all KTU numerical problems assume **T = 20 °C** and **1 atm pressure**. The saturation DO of **9.1 mg/L** is a default value often given in the question paper itself.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Category-Wise Mechanistic Analysis

### 2.1.1 Pathogens (Biological Pollutants)

- **Definition:** Microorganisms causing waterborne diseases (typhoid, cholera, dysentery, hepatitis A).
- **Indicator Organism:** *Escherichia coli* (E. coli) and total coliforms — used because they are easy to detect and correlate with fecal contamination.
- **Primary Sources:** Domestic sewage, animal waste, hospital effluents.
- **Quantification Method:** MPN (Most Probable Number) test or Membrane Filtration (MF) technique.
- **KTU Standard (IS 10500:2012):** **0 MPN/100 mL** for drinking water (zero tolerance in any 100 mL sample).

### 2.1.2 Oxygen-Demanding Wastes (BOD/COD Pollutants)

- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of dissolved oxygen (in mg/L) consumed by aerobic microorganisms to oxidize biodegradable organic matter in water over a standard period.
- **Standard Test Duration:** **5 days at 20 °C** — denoted **BOD₅**.
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of oxygen required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable AND non-biodegradable organics using strong oxidants (K₂Cr₂O₇).
- **Why COD > BOD always:** COD measures total organics (both bio + non-bio); BOD only measures the biodegradable fraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for Domestic Sewage:** BOD₅ : COD ≈ **0.6 to 0.7** (i.e., 60–70% of organic load is biodegradable). If the ratio falls below **0.3**, the wastewater contains a high fraction of toxic non-biodegradable compounds.

### 2.1.3 Nutrients (Nitrogen and Phosphorus)

- **Eutrophication:** Excessive nutrient enrichment causes algal blooms → light blockage → plant death → bacterial decomposition → oxygen depletion → aquatic life collapse.
- **Critical Forms:**
  - Nitrogen: Ammonia (NH₃), Nitrate (NO₃⁻), Nitrite (NO₂⁻)
  - Phosphorus: Orthophosphate (PO₄³⁻), Polyphosphates
- **Threshold for Eutrophication:** Total P > **0.02 mg/L** and Total N > **0.3 mg/L** in freshwater lakes.

### 2.1.4 Heavy Metals (Toxic Inorganic Pollutants)

- **Definition:** Metallic elements with atomic density > **5 g/cm³** (or atomic weight > 50).
- **Key Pollutants & Their Standards (IS 10500:2012 Drinking Water):**

| Heavy Metal | Chemical Symbol | MCL (mg/L) | Source |
|-------------|-----------------|------------|--------|
| Lead | Pb | **≤ 0.01** | Pipes, paints, batteries |
| Mercury | Hg | **≤ 0.001** | Coal combustion, chlor-alkali |
| Cadmium | Cd | **≤ 0.003** | Electroplating, batteries |
| Chromium (hexavalent) | Cr⁶⁺ | **≤ 0.05** | Tanneries, metal finishing |
| Arsenic | As | **≤ 0.01** | Mining, pesticides |
| Nickel | Ni | **≤ 0.02** | Alloys, electroplating |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bioaccumulation & Biomagnification:** Heavy metals are non-biodegradable. They accumulate in tissues (e.g., Mercury → Minamata disease in Japan, 1956) and magnify up the food chain. This makes them the most dangerous category from an EIA perspective.

### 2.1.5 Suspended Solids & Sediments

- **TSS (Total Suspended Solids):** Particles retained on a **1.5 µm glass fiber filter** after filtration, dried at 103–105 °C.
- **TDS (Total Dissolved Solids):** Solids passing through the filter, evaporated at 180 °C.
- **Effect:** Reduces light penetration, clogs fish gills, smothers benthic habitats.

### 2.1.6 Synthetic Organic Chemicals

- **Examples:** Pesticides (DDT, endosulfan), PCBs (Polychlorinated Biphenyls), PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons), phenols.
- **Characteristic:** Persistent, lipophilic (fat-soluble), bioaccumulative.
- **Standard:** Total Pesticides ≤ **0.001 mg/L** in drinking water.

### 2.1.7 Thermal Pollution

- **Cause:** Discharge of warm cooling water from power plants/industrial processes.
- **Mechanism:** Warm water holds less DO (Henry's Law: gas solubility decreases with temperature). A rise of 10 °C roughly halves DO capacity.
- **Threshold:** Effluent temperature should not raise receiving water by more than **3 °C** at the edge of the mixing zone (CPCB norm).

### 2.1.8 Radioactive Substances

- **Sources:** Nuclear power plants, mining (uranium), medical/research waste.
- **Concern:** Long half-lives, genetic mutation, cancer risk.
- **Units:** Becquerel per litre (Bq/L) or Picocurie per litre (pCi/L).

### 2.1.9 Oil and Grease

- **Sources:** Petroleum refining, oil spills, transport accidents.
- **Effect:** Forms surface film blocking oxygen transfer; lethal to marine life at concentrations > **10 mg/L**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** This formula sheet is the single most important reference for KTU EIA numerical questions. Memorize all entries below.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|-------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{BOD}_5 = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{final, 5 days}}$ | Oxygen consumed in 5-day BOD test | mg/L |
| 2 | $\text{BOD}_t = \text{BOD}_5 \cdot (1 - e^{-k \cdot t})$ | BOD exerted at time *t* (Streeter-Phelps base) | mg/L |
| 3 | $\text{BOD}_u = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$ | Ultimate BOD from 5-day BOD and deoxygenation constant *k* | mg/L |
| 4 | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.047)^{T - 20}$ | Temperature correction for BOD rate constant | day⁻¹ |
| 5 | $\text{BOD/COD ratio} \approx 0.6$ to $0.7$ | Biodegradability index (domestic sewage) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\text{DO}_{\text{sat}}(T) = 14.62 - 0.3898T + 0.006969T^2 - 0.00005896T^3$ | Saturation DO as a function of T (°C) at 1 atm | mg/L |
| 7 | $L_{\text{org}} = Q \cdot \text{BOD}_5$ | Organic load in wastewater (Q in m³/s or L/s) | kg/day |
| 8 | $\text{MPN Index} = \dfrac{\text{No. of positive tubes} \times 100}{\text{Volume sampled (mL)}}$ | Microbial load indicator | per 100 mL |
| 9 | $\text{TSS} = \dfrac{(W_f - W_i)}{V}$ | Total suspended solids (filter weight method) | mg/L |
| 10 | $C_{\text{metal, ef}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{in}} \cdot Q_{\text{in}} - \text{Removal} \cdot C_{\text{in}} \cdot Q_{\text{in}}}{Q_{\text{ef}}}$ | Effluent concentration after treatment | mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **|Symbol Rule in Tables:** All absolute-value or norm notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (e.g., $\vert T - 20 \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Municipal Engineering** | Designing Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs) — aeration tank sizing depends on BOD₅ |
| **Industrial EIA** | Setting up Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs) and Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) systems |
| **River Basin Planning** | Computing Assimilative Capacity using Streeter-Phelps equation |
| **Drinking Water Supply** | Coagulation/dose design based on TSS, turbidity, and metal speciation |
| **Coastal EIA** | Modeling thermal plume dispersion from power plants (CORMIX, MIKE) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BOD Exertion as a First-Order Reaction

### Background

The decomposition of organic matter in water follows **first-order kinetics** with respect to the remaining oxygen-demanding substrate. The rate of oxygen consumption is directly proportional to the concentration of unoxidized organic matter.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Let:
- $L_0$ = Ultimate BOD (mg/L) — total oxygen demand if all organics oxidized
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining to be exerted at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $y_t$ = BOD already exerted at time $t$ (mg/L)
- $k$ = Deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typical value **$k_{20} = 0.23$ day⁻¹** for municipal sewage

**Step 1:** Material balance of unoxidized organics
By definition, the amount of organic matter remaining is:
$$L_t = L_0 - y_t$$

**Step 2:** Rate equation (first-order)
The rate of oxidation of remaining organics is proportional to $L_t$:
$$\dfrac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

**Step 3:** Separation of variables and integration
$$\dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \cdot dt$$

Integrating from $t = 0$ to $t = t$, with $L_0$ at $t = 0$:
$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \dfrac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

**Step 4:** Solving the integral
$$\ln(L_t) - \ln(L_0) = -k \cdot t$$

Exponentiating both sides:
$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back to find exerted BOD
$$y_t = L_0 - L_t = L_0 - L_0 \cdot e^{-k \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{y_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-k \cdot t}\right)}$$

**Step 6:** For the standard 5-day BOD test, set $t = 5$ days:
$$y_5 = \text{BOD}_5 = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-5k}\right)$$

**Step 7:** Solving for ultimate BOD $L_0$ from a known $\text{BOD}_5$:
$$L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-Style Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 2% diluted wastewater sample has an initial DO of **8.5 mg/L**. After 5 days of incubation at 20 °C, the DO drops to **2.0 mg/L**. The deoxygenation constant at 20 °C is $k = 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$. Calculate:
> (a) The 5-day BOD of the diluted sample
> (b) The 5-day BOD of the original (undiluted) sample
> (c) The ultimate BOD of the original sample

### Solution

**Part (a): BOD of diluted sample**
Using the standard BOD formula:
$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{final, 5d}}$$

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = 8.5 - 2.0 = 6.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Part (b): BOD of original sample (apply dilution factor)**
Dilution factor $D_f = \dfrac{100}{2} = 50$

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{original}} = \text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} \times D_f$$

$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{original}} = 6.5 \times 50 = 325 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Part (c): Ultimate BOD $L_0$**

$$L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

$$5k = 5 \times 0.23 = 1.15$$

$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$

$$1 - e^{-1.15} = 1 - 0.3166 = 0.6834$$

$$L_0 = \dfrac{325}{0.6834} = 475.56 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\boxed{L_0 \approx 475.6 \text{ mg/L}}$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Temperature Correction of $k$

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A wastewater has $k_{20} = 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$. Find the rate constant at 30 °C.

**Step 1:** Apply the standard van't Hoff-Arrhenius correction:
$$k_T = k_{20} \cdot \theta^{(T - 20)}$$

where $\theta = 1.047$ for BOD reactions.

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times (1.047)^{(30 - 20)}$$

$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times (1.047)^{10}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(1.047)^{10}$:
$$(1.047)^{10} = 1.582$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:
$$k_{30} = 0.23 \times 1.582 = 0.364 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{k_{30} \approx 0.364 \text{ day}^{-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 10 °C rise nearly doubles the deoxygenation rate. This is why **thermal pollution** is so dangerous — it accelerates oxygen depletion in receiving waters.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant–Source–Effect–Standard

| Pollutant Category | Primary Source | Environmental Effect | KTU/CPCB Standard |
|--------------------|----------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Pathogens | Sewage, hospitals | Waterborne diseases | **0 MPN/100 mL** |
| BOD/COD | Sewage, food processing | Oxygen depletion, fish kills | BOD₅ ≤ **30 mg/L** (discharge) |
| Nitrate | Fertilizer runoff | Methemoglobinemia ("blue baby") | NO₃⁻ ≤ **45 mg/L** |
| Phosphate | Detergents, fertilizers | Eutrophication | Total P ≤ **5 mg/L** (effluent) |
| Lead | Industrial, plumbing | Neurological damage | Pb ≤ **0.1 mg/L** (effluent) |
| Mercury | Chlor-alkali, coal | Minamata disease | Hg ≤ **0.01 mg/L** (effluent) |
| TSS | Construction, mining | Turbidity, benthic smothering | TSS ≤ **100 mg/L** (effluent) |
| pH | Acid mine drainage, alkali waste | Aquatic toxicity, corrosion | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| Oil & Grease | Petroleum, transport | Surface film, oxygen blockade | ≤ **10 mg/L** |
| Temperature | Power plants | DO loss, ecosystem shift | $\Delta T \le 3\,°C$ at edge of mixing zone |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
