<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set B]**
**Q: Define BOD and COD. Why is COD always greater than BOD for the same wastewater sample?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of dissolved oxygen (mg/L) consumed by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the biodegradable organic matter in a water sample over a standard incubation period of 5 days at 20 °C. (1 mark)
- **COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand):** The amount of oxygen (mg/L) required to chemically oxidize both biodegradable and non-biodegradable organic matter using a strong oxidizing agent (potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇) in acidic conditions. (1 mark)
- **Why COD > BOD:** COD measures oxidation of the *total* organic load (biodegradable + non-biodegradable) by a strong chemical oxidant, whereas BOD only measures the *biodegradable* fraction under the action of microbes. Since the biodegradable component is a subset of the total, COD is always equal to or greater than BOD. (1 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Internal Assessment]**
**Q: What is eutrophication? List any two major nutrients responsible for it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Eutrophication (Definition – 2 marks):** It is the excessive enrichment of a water body with nutrients, leading to the rapid growth of algae and aquatic plants (algal bloom). When these algae die, their aerobic decomposition by bacteria causes severe depletion of dissolved oxygen, creating hypoxic/anoxic conditions that result in fish kills and loss of biodiversity.
- **Two major nutrients (1 mark):** **Nitrogen (N)** compounds (e.g., nitrates, ammonia) and **Phosphorus (P)** compounds (e.g., orthophosphates from detergents and fertilizers).

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Choice 1]**

**Q: (a)** Classify the major water pollutants. Describe in detail the characteristics, sources, and environmental impacts of heavy metals as water pollutants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wastewater sample is diluted to 1% (i.e., 1 mL sample + 99 mL dilution water). The initial DO of the diluted sample is 8.0 mg/L and after 5 days of incubation at 20 °C, the DO is 3.5 mg/L. If $k = 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$ at 20 °C, calculate:
- (i) BOD₅ of the diluted sample
- (ii) BOD₅ of the original wastewater
- (iii) Ultimate BOD of the original wastewater **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Classification of major water pollutants (2 marks):**
1. **Biological** – Pathogens (E. coli, coliforms, enteric viruses)
2. **Chemical (Inorganic)** – Heavy metals (Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr), nutrients (N, P), acids/alkalis
3. **Chemical (Organic)** – Oxygen-demanding wastes, pesticides, PCBs, PAHs, oil & grease
4. **Physical** – Suspended solids, TDS, turbidity, colour
5. **Radiological** – Radioactive isotopes (Cs-137, Sr-90)
6. **Thermal** – Heated effluents from power plants

**Heavy Metals – Characteristics, Sources, Impacts (5 marks):**
- **Definition:** Metals with atomic density > 5 g/cm³ or atomic weight > 50; toxic even at trace concentrations.
- **Key Characteristics:**
  - Non-biodegradable and persistent
  - Subject to **bioaccumulation** (build-up in tissues) and **biomagnification** (increasing concentration up the food chain)
  - Most are toxic at ppb (parts per billion) levels
- **Sources and Impacts (Table form for KTU valuation):**

| Metal | Sources | Environmental / Health Impact |
|-------|---------|-------------------------------|
| Lead (Pb) | Lead-acid batteries, paint, plumbing | Neurological damage, anemia, kidney disease |
| Mercury (Hg) | Coal combustion, chlor-alkali plants | Minamata disease (neurological), bioaccumulates as methylmercury |
| Cadmium (Cd) | Electroplating, Ni-Cd batteries | Itai-itai disease, kidney failure, bone demineralization |
| Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) | Tanneries, metal finishing | Carcinogenic, mutagenic, liver damage |
| Arsenic (As) | Mining, pesticides, natural geogenic | Skin lesions, cancer (lungs, bladder) |

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Dilution: 1% → $D_f = 100/1 = 100$
- DO_initial = 8.0 mg/L
- DO_final, 5d = 3.5 mg/L
- $k_{20} = 0.23 \text{ day}^{-1}$

**Step 1: BOD₅ of diluted sample (2 marks)**
$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = \text{DO}_{\text{initial}} - \text{DO}_{\text{final, 5d}}$$
$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = 8.0 - 3.5 = 4.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: BOD₅ of original wastewater (2 marks)**
$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{original}} = \text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} \times D_f = 4.5 \times 100 = 450 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Ultimate BOD of original wastewater (3 marks)**
$$L_0 = \dfrac{\text{BOD}_5}{1 - e^{-5k}}$$

$$5k = 5 \times 0.23 = 1.15$$
$$e^{-1.15} = 0.3166$$
$$1 - e^{-1.15} = 0.6834$$
$$L_0 = \dfrac{450}{0.6834} = 658.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$\text{BOD}_5^{\text{diluted}} = 4.5 \text{ mg/L}, \quad \text{BOD}_5^{\text{original}} = 450 \text{ mg/L}, \quad L_0 = 658.5 \text{ mg/L}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Dilution factor trap:** Students often forget to multiply by the dilution factor and report only the diluted value as the "answer." This loses **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Exponent sign error:** $e^{-5k}$ is *not* $e^{5k}$. The negative sign is critical because it represents decay.
> 3. **Units:** Always quote final answers with **mg/L** units. Missing units = loss of 0.5 mark per sub-question.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Choice 2]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the BOD test procedure. Discuss the factors affecting the rate of BOD exertion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A river flowing at 5 m³/s receives treated industrial wastewater at 0.5 m³/s having BOD₅ = 200 mg/L. The river upstream has BOD₅ = 2 mg/L. Calculate the BOD₅ of the mixed flow downstream. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**BOD Test Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Sample Collection & Preservation:** Sample is collected in a BOD bottle and preserved at 4 °C.
2. **Dilution Preparation:** Sample is diluted with **DO-saturated, nutrient-buffered dilution water** (containing phosphate buffer, MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃) to bring expected BOD into the 2–7 mg/L range. Dilution factor is recorded.
3. **Initial DO Measurement:** Initial DO of the diluted sample is measured using a **Winkler iodometric method** or modern DO probe.
4. **Incubation:** Bottle is sealed and incubated at **20 °C for 5 days** in a BOD incubator (dark, to prevent photosynthesis).
5. **Final DO Measurement:** After 5 days, the final DO is measured.
6. **Calculation:**
$$\text{BOD}_5 = (\text{DO}_i - \text{DO}_f) \times D_f$$

**Factors Affecting BOD Rate (3 marks):**
1. **Temperature:** Higher T → faster rate (van't Hoff: $k$ doubles per 10 °C rise)
2. **pH:** Optimum pH = 6.5–8.5; extreme pH inhibits microbial activity
3. **Presence of Toxic Substances:** Heavy metals, chlorine, antibiotics suppress microbes
4. **Availability of Nutrients:** N and P deficiency limits bacterial growth
5. **Type of Organic Matter:** Carbohydrates oxidize faster than proteins and lipids
6. **Seed (Microbial) Concentration:** Insufficient seed slows oxidation; standard method adds seed if needed

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- River flow: $Q_R = 5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{BOD}_R = 2 \text{ mg/L}$
- Wastewater flow: $Q_W = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{BOD}_W = 200 \text{ mg/L}$

**Apply mass balance for BOD₅ (mixing equation):**

$$\text{BOD}_{\text{mixed}} = \dfrac{Q_R \cdot \text{BOD}_R + Q_W \cdot \text{BOD}_W}{Q_R + Q_W}$$

$$\text{BOD}_{\text{mixed}} = \dfrac{(5 \times 2) + (0.5 \times 200)}{5 + 0.5}$$

$$\text{BOD}_{\text{mixed}} = \dfrac{10 + 100}{5.5} = \dfrac{110}{5.5}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BOD}_{\text{mixed}} = 20 \text{ mg/L}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Question B:**
> 1. **Flow unit consistency:** Ensure $Q_R$ and $Q_W$ have the **same unit** before applying the mixing formula. Mixed units (e.g., L/s and m³/s) = 2-mark penalty.
> 2. **BOD mixing assumption:** This is a simple conservative-mixing calculation valid **only when both streams have similar BOD reaction kinetics**. For long downstream modeling, the **Streeter-Phelps equation** must be used.
> 3. **Mass balance is on loading (kg/day), not concentration directly:** Many students forget to multiply flow × concentration. Always state: $\text{Load (kg/day)} = Q \times C$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Quick-Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Entering the Exam Hall:**

- ☐ **9 Major Water Pollutant Categories:** Biological, Chemical (inorganic/organic), Physical, Radiological, Thermal.
- ☐ **BOD₅ Standard Conditions:** 5 days, 20 °C, aerobic, dark incubation.
- ☐ **BOD₅ : COD Ratio:** ~0.6–0.7 (domestic); <0.3 indicates toxicity.
- ☐ **Key Equation Trio:** $y_t = L_0(1 - e^{-kt})$, $L_0 = \text{BOD}_5/(1-e^{-5k})$, $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.047)^{T-20}$.
- ☐ **Eutrophication Trigger:** Total P > 0.02 mg/L and/or Total N > 0.3 mg/L.
- ☐ **Heavy Metals are Non-biodegradable** → bioaccumulate and biomagnify.
- ☐ **Thermal Pollution:** $\Delta T \le 3\,°C$ at edge of mixing zone (CPCB).
- ☐ **Mixing Equation:** $C_{\text{mix}} = (Q_1 C_1 + Q_2 C_2)/(Q_1 + Q_2)$.
- ☐ **IS 10500:2012 Drinking Water Limits (must memorize):** Pb ≤ 0.01, Hg ≤ 0.001, Cd ≤ 0.003, As ≤ 0.01, F⁻ ≤ 1.0, NO₃⁻ ≤ 45 mg/L, Total Coliforms = **0 MPN/100 mL**.
- ☐ **Dilution Factor Formula:** $D_f = \dfrac{\text{Volume of dilution water + sample}}{\text{Volume of sample}}$; for $x\%$ dilution, $D_f = 100/x$.
- ☐ **MPN Test** is the standard microbiological method for coliform detection.
- ☐ **Winkler's Method** is the classical chemical method for DO measurement.
- ☐ **Streeter-Phelps Equation** = deoxygenation + reaeration balance, used to find the **critical DO deficit point** in a polluted river.
- ☐ **CPCB Designated Best Use Classification:** A (Drinking) → B (Bathing) → C (Wildlife) → D (Irrigation) → E (Industrial cooling).
- ☐ **Order of Pollutant Removal in STP:** Screening → Grit Removal → Primary Sedimentation → Biological Treatment (Activated Sludge / Trickling Filter) → Secondary Clarifier → Disinfection.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physical, Chemical, and Biological Characteristics of Water
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physical, Chemical, and Biological Characteristics of Water

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), the **Physical, Chemical, and Biological Characteristics of Water** constitute the foundational baseline parameters used to quantitatively and qualitatively describe the **state of a water body** (river, lake, reservoir, estuary, or groundwater). These parameters are essential for predicting the **magnitude of impact** a proposed developmental project (e.g., dam, industrial discharge, urbanization) will exert on the aquatic ecosystem and downstream water users.

According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS: 10500)** and **APHA Standard Methods for the Examination of Water and Wastewater**, water quality is defined by the combined response of three measurable domains:

- **Physical Characteristics** — measurable by instruments, related to appearance, temperature, and solids content.
- **Chemical Characteristics** — measurable by titration, spectrophotometry, or electrodes, related to ionic composition, dissolved gases, and nutrient load.
- **Biological Characteristics** — measurable by culture techniques or microscopy, related to microbial and macro-organism presence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In an EIA study, these parameters are collected during the **Baseline Data Collection Phase** (Module 2 of the EIA process) and compared against the **Predicted Impact Phase** to compute the **Magnitude of Change ($\Delta Q$)**. The relationship is generally expressed as:
> $$\Delta Q = Q_{\text{post-impact}} - Q_{\text{baseline}}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Patient Diagnosis"**

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. The doctor does not just ask *"How do you feel?"*; he runs three sets of tests:

1. **Physical Examination** — temperature, pulse, color of skin, clarity of eyes → corresponds to **turbidity, color, odor, temperature, total solids**.
2. **Blood/Chemical Tests** — pH of blood, sugar level, oxygen content, chloride count → corresponds to **pH, alkalinity, hardness, DO, BOD, COD, chlorides, nitrates**.
3. **Microbiological Tests** — culture swabs, infection screening → corresponds to **coliform count (MPN), pathogens, algal bloom indicators**.

A water body is a "patient" of the environment, and these three test categories collectively give its **diagnosis**. Just as a doctor cannot prescribe medicine without a full panel of tests, an EIA consultant cannot certify a project as environmentally safe without a comprehensive water quality baseline.

## 1.3 Why These Characteristics Matter in EIA

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, water quality parameters are tied directly to **Course Outcome CO2**: *"Identify and assess environmental impacts of developmental projects."* A project proposal is **rejected or conditionally approved** based on the predicted deviation of these characteristics from prescribed **regulatory standards**.

**Three Real-World Engineering Utilities:**

- **Drinking Water Supply Projects** — require compliance with **IS 10500:2012** limits for all 31 parameters.
- **Industrial Effluent Discharge** — governed by **CPCB / KSPCB** consent limits, primarily measured by **BOD, COD, TSS, pH**.
- **Aquatic Ecosystem Protection** — fisheries and biodiversity depend on **DO $\geq$ 4 mg/L**, **BOD $\leq$ 3 mg/L**, and **heavy metals below detection limits**.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Constants (KTU Frequently Tested Values)

| Parameter | Standard Reference | Typical Acceptable Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Pure water pH** | NBS Scale | **7.0** (at 25 °C) |
| **Dissolved Oxygen saturation in pure water** | APHA Table | **9.1 mg/L** (at 20 °C, 1 atm) |
| **Drinking water pH (IS 10500)** | BIS | **6.5 – 8.5** |
| **Drinking water Total Hardness** | BIS | **200 mg/L as CaCO₃** (max) |
| **Drinking water TDS** | BIS | **500 mg/L** (max desirable) |
| **Turbidity (drinking water)** | BIS | **1 NTU** (max) |
| **Fecal Coliform (drinking water)** | BIS | **0 MPN per 100 mL** (must be absent) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Memorize:** The molar mass of **Calcium Carbonate (CaCO₃) = 100 g/mol**. It is the universal reference standard for expressing hardness, alkalinity, and TDS because of its convenient atomic mass.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Characteristics of Water

Physical parameters are the most immediately observable indicators of water quality. They do not require chemical reaction to measure.

### 2.1.1 Turbidity
- **Definition:** Measure of the cloudiness or haziness of water caused by the presence of suspended and colloidal particles (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms).
- **Unit:** **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Unit)** — measured using a **nephelometer** that detects light scattered at 90° to the incident beam.
- **Standard:** IS 10500 specifies **1 NTU (max)** for drinking water; surface waters typically range **10 – 1000 NTU**.
- **Why it matters in EIA:** High turbidity blocks sunlight → reduces photosynthetic activity → lowers **Dissolved Oxygen** → aquatic life suffocates.

### 2.1.2 Color
- **True Color** — color due to dissolved substances (measured after filtration).
- **Apparent Color** — color due to suspended matter (measured without filtration).
- **Unit:** **Hazen Units (HU)** or **Pt-Co Units** (Platinum-Cobalt scale).
- **Cause:** Tannins, lignin, humic acids, industrial dyes, iron compounds.

### 2.1.3 Odor and Taste
- **Threshold Odor Number (TON)** = (Volume of odorant-free water + sample) / (Volume of sample).
- **Causes:** Biological decomposition (H₂S, mercaptans), chlorination by-products, industrial phenols.

### 2.1.4 Temperature
- Critical because it controls:
  - **Dissolved Oxygen capacity** (inverse relationship).
  - **Biological reaction rates** (metabolism doubles per 10 °C rise — **Q₁₀ rule**).
  - **Density and stratification** in lakes (thermal stratification controls eutrophication).

### 2.1.5 Total Solids (TS) and Electrical Conductivity (EC)
- **Total Solids** = **Total Suspended Solids (TSS)** + **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)**.
- **EC** is directly proportional to **TDS** via the empirical relation:

$$TDS \;(\text{mg/L}) \approx k \times EC \;(\mu S / \text{cm})$$

where $k \approx 0.55$ to $0.75$ (typically **0.64** for natural waters at 25 °C).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for EC:** Conductivity is like the "saltiness meter" of water. Pure distilled water has EC $\approx$ 0.5 $\mu$S/cm, while seawater is about **50,000 $\mu$S/cm**.

## 2.2 Chemical Characteristics of Water

### 2.2.1 pH
- Negative logarithm of hydrogen ion activity:

$$pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$$

- **Range:** 0 (strongly acidic) to 14 (strongly basic); **7.0 is neutral at 25 °C**.
- **EIA Relevance:** Affects toxicity of ammonia (NH₃ vs NH₄⁺), heavy metals, and chlorine disinfection efficiency.

### 2.2.2 Alkalinity
- The water's capacity to neutralize acids, primarily due to **bicarbonates (HCO₃⁻), carbonates (CO₃²⁻), and hydroxides (OH⁻)**.
- **Expressed as mg/L as CaCO₃.**
- **Relation:** 
  - $pH < 8.3$ → only bicarbonates contribute.
  - $pH > 8.3$ → carbonates and hydroxides dominate.

### 2.2.3 Hardness
- Caused by divalent cations, primarily **Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺**.
- **Total Hardness = Calcium Hardness + Magnesium Hardness.**

$$TH = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [Mg^{2+}] \quad (\text{all in mg/L as CaCO}_{3})$$

The conversion factors come from:

$$\text{For Ca}^{2+}: \quad \frac{50}{20} = 2.5, \quad \text{For Mg}^{2+}: \quad \frac{50}{12.15} \approx 4.1$$

- **Classification:**
  - **0 – 60 mg/L** → Soft
  - **61 – 120 mg/L** → Moderately Hard
  - **121 – 180 mg/L** → Hard
  - **> 180 mg/L** → Very Hard

### 2.2.4 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)
- **Definition:** Mass of oxygen dissolved per litre of water; **unit: mg/L or % saturation**.
- **DO Saturation Formula (APHA Standard):**

$$DO_{sat} = 14.652 - 0.41022T + 0.007991T^{2} - 0.000077774T^{3}$$

where $T$ is temperature in °C (valid for 0 – 40 °C, 1 atm, freshwater).

- **Rule of Thumb:** DO saturation drops by approximately **0.25 mg/L per 1 °C rise** in temperature.

### 2.2.5 Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)
- **Definition:** Amount of oxygen required by aerobic microorganisms to oxidize biodegradable organic matter in water at **20 °C over 5 days** (BOD₅).
- **Standard Test:** Dilution method — sample diluted with oxygen-saturated dilution water, incubated for 5 days at 20 °C.

$$BOD_{5} = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$$

where:
- $D_1, D_2$ = DO of diluted sample on day 0 and day 5
- $B_1, B_2$ = DO of blank (seeded dilution water) on day 0 and day 5
- $f$ = fraction of seed in sample
- $P$ = decimal fraction of sample volume

### 2.2.6 Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)
- Measures **total oxidizable matter** (both biodegradable and non-biodegradable).
- Test uses strong oxidant **K₂Cr₂O₇** in H₂SO₄ at reflux.
- **Always: COD > BOD.** The ratio **BOD/COD** indicates biodegradability:
  - **> 0.5** → Highly biodegradable (municipal sewage).
  - **0.3 – 0.5** → Moderately biodegradable.
  - **< 0.3** → Poorly biodegradable (industrial effluent).

### 2.2.7 Nutrient Indicators: Nitrogen and Phosphorus
- **Nitrogen forms** (in order of oxidation): **Organic-N → NH₃ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻**.
- High **NH₃ and NO₃⁻** cause **eutrophication** and **methemoglobinemia** (blue baby syndrome) respectively.
- **Phosphorus** is usually the **limiting nutrient** in freshwater; > 0.02 mg/L triggers algal blooms.

### 2.2.8 Heavy Metals
- Of EIA concern: **Lead (Pb), Cadmium (Cd), Mercury (Hg), Arsenic (As), Chromium (Cr)**.
- Measured by **AAS (Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy)** or **ICP-MS**.
- **Bioaccumulative** and **toxic at trace levels** ($\mu$g/L range).

## 2.3 Biological Characteristics of Water

### 2.3.1 Most Probable Number (MPN) Method
- Statistical estimation of coliform bacteria density based on **multiple-tube fermentation**.
- Results expressed as **MPN index per 100 mL**.
- **95% confidence limits** are tabulated using probability theory.

### 2.3.2 Coliforms
- **Total Coliforms** — broad indicator including soil and fecal species.
- **Fecal Coliforms (FC)** — specifically indicates warm-blooded animal fecal contamination.
- **E. coli** — definitive fecal indicator.
- **Acceptable limit (drinking):** **0 MPN/100 mL.**

### 2.3.3 Other Biological Indicators
- **Algae** — Chlorophyll-a (mg/m³) is a proxy for algal biomass; **> 10 mg/m³** = eutrophic.
- **Plankton & Benthos** — used in **Biological Monitoring Working Party (BMWP) score** for stream health.
- **Pathogens** — *Vibrio cholerae, Salmonella typhi, Hepatitis A virus* — direct health risk agents.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S. No. | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $pH = -\log_{10}[H^{+}]$ | Hydrogen ion activity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $TDS \approx 0.64 \times EC$ | Total Dissolved Solids | mg/L |
| 3 | $TH = 2.5\,[Ca^{2+}] + 4.1\,[Mg^{2+}]$ | Total Hardness | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| 4 | $DO_{sat} = 14.652 - 0.41T + 0.008T^{2} - 0.0000777T^{3}$ | Saturation DO | mg/L |
| 5 | $BOD_{5} = \dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)f}{P}$ | 5-day BOD | mg/L |
| 6 | $L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$ (Streeter-Phelps) | BOD remaining | mg/L |
| 7 | $Y_t = \dfrac{k \cdot L_0}{k - r}\left(e^{-rt} - e^{-kt}\right)$ | DO deficit at time t | mg/L |
| 8 | $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k - r}\ln\left(\dfrac{k}{r}\right)$ | Critical time | days |
| 9 | $D_c = \dfrac{L_0}{k}\left(e^{-r t_c}\right)$ | Critical DO deficit | mg/L |
| 10 | $NH_{3}(\text{free}) = \dfrac{[NH_{3}+NH_{4}^{+}]}{1 + 10^{pK_a - pH}}$ | Un-ionized ammonia | mg/L |

> [!NOTE]
> **Streeter-Phelps Equations (6, 7, 8, 9)** are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU university exams. They predict the **dissolved oxygen sag curve** downstream of a pollutant discharge — a classic EIA modeling tool.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Sewage Treatment Plant Design** — BOD₅ and COD determine aeration tank volume and MLSS.
- **River Water Quality Modeling** — Streeter-Phelps equation predicts the **"oxygen sag"** curve and **critical point of lowest DO** downstream of a discharge.
- **Industrial Effluent Monitoring** — KSPCB consent orders specify daily pH, BOD, COD, TSS, EC.
- **Fisheries Management** — Free ammonia > 0.02 mg/L is lethal to fish; determined by pH and total ammonia.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: BOD Reaction Kinetics (Streeter-Phelps Oxygen Sag Model)

The BOD exertion in a river is treated as a **first-order reaction**. Let:

- $L_0$ = Initial ultimate BOD (at the discharge point), mg/L
- $L_t$ = BOD remaining after time $t$, mg/L
- $k$ = BOD rate constant, day⁻¹ (typically 0.1 – 0.5 for municipal waste at 20 °C)
- $r$ = Re-aeration rate constant, day⁻¹ (typically 0.2 – 1.0)

### Step 1: Rate of BOD Exertion

The rate at which organic matter is oxidized follows first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{dL_t}{dt} = -k \cdot L_t$$

### Step 2: Integrate from t = 0 to t = t

Separating variables:

$$\int_{L_0}^{L_t} \frac{dL_t}{L_t} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$

This gives:

$$\ln\left(\frac{L_t}{L_0}\right) = -k t$$

Exponentiating both sides:

$$L_t = L_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$

This is **Equation 6** in the formula sheet — the **decay of BOD** with travel time.

### Step 3: Oxygen Deficit Equation

The dissolved oxygen deficit $D_t$ at any time $t$ is defined as:

$$D_t = DO_{sat} - DO_t$$

The rate of change of deficit has two competing terms:
- **Increase in deficit** due to BOD oxidation: $+k L_t$
- **Decrease in deficit** due to atmospheric re-aeration: $-r D_t$

Hence:

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} = k L_t - r D_t$$

### Step 4: Substitute $L_t$ and Integrate

Substituting $L_t = L_0 e^{-kt}$:

$$\frac{dD_t}{dt} + r D_t = k L_0 e^{-kt}$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE**. Using the integrating factor $e^{rt}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(D_t \cdot e^{rt}\right) = k L_0 e^{(r-k)t}$$

Integrating from 0 to t, with $D_t = D_0$ at $t = 0$:

$$D_t \cdot e^{rt} - D_0 = \frac{k L_0}{r - k}\left[e^{(r-k)t} - 1\right]$$

### Step 5: Solve for $D_t$

Multiplying both sides by $e^{-rt}$:

$$D_t = \frac{k L_0}{k - r}\left(e^{-rt} - e^{-kt}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-rt}$$

If the initial deficit $D_0 \approx 0$ (river was saturated before discharge):

$$\boxed{D_t = \frac{k L_0}{k - r}\left(e^{-rt} - e^{-kt}\right)}$$

This is **Equation 7** in the formula sheet.

### Step 6: Critical Time and Critical Deficit

The critical point (maximum deficit) is found by setting $\dfrac{dD_t}{dt} = 0$:

$$k L_0 e^{-kt_c} = r D_{t_c}$$

Using the expression for $D_{t_c}$ and simplifying:

$$\boxed{t_c = \frac{1}{k - r}\ln\left(\frac{k}{r}\right)}$$

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{D_c = \frac{L_0}{k}\left(e^{-r t_c}\right)\left(k - r\right) \quad \text{or simplified} \quad D_c = \frac{L_0 k}{r} e^{-r t_c}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** The Streeter-Phelps model says — the river does NOT recover immediately after a discharge. The DO first drops to a **critical minimum** at time $t_c$ (often 1–5 days downstream), then slowly recovers through re-aeration. The shape resembles a **"sag curve."**

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

> **Problem:** A river receives a municipal discharge with the following data:
> - Initial BOD ($L_0$) = **50 mg/L**
> - BOD rate constant ($k$) = **0.23 day⁻¹** (base e, at 20 °C)
> - Re-aeration constant ($r$) = **0.4 day⁻¹**
> - River DO saturation = **9.0 mg/L**
> - Initial DO deficit $D_0$ = **1.0 mg/L**
> - River velocity = **25 km/day**
>
> **Find:** (a) DO deficit after 2 days; (b) Critical time; (c) Critical DO deficit.

### Part (a): DO Deficit at t = 2 days

Using Equation 7 (with $D_0$ term):

$$D_t = \frac{k L_0}{k - r}\left(e^{-rt} - e^{-kt}\right) + D_0 e^{-rt}$$

Compute each term:

$$e^{-kt} = e^{-0.23 \times 2} = e^{-0.46} = 0.6313$$

$$e^{-rt} = e^{-0.4 \times 2} = e^{-0.8} = 0.4493$$

$$\frac{k L_0}{k - r} = \frac{0.23 \times 50}{0.23 - 0.4} = \frac{11.5}{-0.17} = -67.647$$

$$D_t = -67.647 \times (0.4493 - 0.6313) + 1.0 \times 0.4493$$

$$D_t = -67.647 \times (-0.182) + 0.4493$$

$$D_t = 12.312 + 0.4493 = 12.76 \; \text{mg/L}$$

But the **deficit cannot exceed saturation**! This means the **predicted DO = 9.0 − 12.76 = −3.76 mg/L**, which is **physically impossible** (DO cannot be negative). This indicates the river will be **completely anaerobic** — total fish kill. The model breaks down here because re-aeration cannot match BOD demand.

**Result:** $D_t = 12.76$ mg/L (deficit exceeds capacity → DO = 0).

### Part (b): Critical Time

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k - r}\ln\left(\frac{k}{r}\right) = \frac{1}{0.23 - 0.4}\ln\left(\frac{0.23}{0.4}\right)$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{-0.17} \times \ln(0.575)$$

$$\ln(0.575) = -0.5534$$

$$t_c = \frac{-0.5534}{-0.17} = 3.26 \; \text{days}$$

**Distance downstream** = $3.26 \times 25 = 81.5$ km

### Part (c): Critical Deficit

$$D_c = \frac{L_0 k}{r} e^{-r t_c} = \frac{50 \times 0.23}{0.4} \times e^{-0.4 \times 3.26}$$

$$D_c = 28.75 \times e^{-1.304} = 28.75 \times 0.2715 = 7.81 \; \text{mg/L}$$

**Critical DO** = $9.0 - 7.81 = 1.19$ mg/L

**Interpretation:** At 81.5 km downstream, the DO drops to **1.19 mg/L**, far below the **4 mg/L** threshold for aquatic life. **The EIA recommendation would be: tertiary treatment of discharge OR flow augmentation.**

## 3.3 Conversion Logic Summary (Common Exam Traps)

| Given Form | Need to Find | Multiply by | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ca²⁺ as mg/L of Ca | Ca²⁺ as mg/L of CaCO₃ | **2.5** | Equivalent weight ratio 50/20 |
| Mg²⁺ as mg/L of Mg | Mg²⁺ as mg/L of CaCO₃ | **4.1** | Equivalent weight ratio 50/12.15 |
| BOD at temperature T | BOD at 20 °C | $\Theta^{(T-20)}$ | $\Theta = 1.047$ (van't Hoff rule) |
| k at 20 °C | k at T °C | $k_T = k_{20}(1.047)^{T-20}$ | Standard temperature correction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kruskal's Rule of 1.047:** The BOD rate constant increases by **4.7% per 1 °C rise** in temperature. This is a common adjustment in tropical EIA studies for Kerala (mean river temperature ~27 °C).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Water Quality Parameters

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Water Quality Parameters]
    startA --> phy1[Physical Characteristics]
    startA --> chem1[Chemical Characteristics]
    startA --> bio1[Biological Characteristics]
    phy1 --> phy2[Turbidity - NTU]
    phy1 --> phy3[Color - Pt-Co Units]
    phy1 --> phy4[Odor and Taste - TON]
    phy1 --> phy5[Temperature - degree C]
    phy1 --> phy6[Total Solids - mg per L]
    phy1 --> phy7[Electrical Conductivity - microS per cm]
    chem1 --> chem2[pH - dimensionless]
    chem1 --> chem3[Alkalinity - mg per L as CaCO3]
    chem1 --> chem4[Hardness - mg per L as CaCO3]
    chem1 --> chem5[Dissolved Oxygen - mg per L]
    chem1 --> chem6[BOD5 and COD - mg per L]
    chem1 --> chem7[Chlorides and Sulfates - mg per L]
    chem1 --> chem8[Nitrogen compounds - mg per L]
    chem1 --> chem9[Phosphorus - mg per L]
    chem1 --> chem10[Heavy Metals - micro g per L]
    bio1 --> bio2[Total Coliforms - MPN per 100 mL]
    bio1 --> bio3[Fecal Coliforms - MPN per 100 mL]
    bio1 --> bio4[E. coli - MPN per 100 mL]
    bio1 --> bio5[Algae and Chlorophyll-a - mg per m3]
    bio1 --> bio6[Pathogens - CFU per mL]
    chem6 --> streeter1[Streeter Phelps Sag Model]
    streeter1 --> sag1[Predicts DO deficit downstream]
    sag1 --> sag2[Critical time tc]
    sag1 --> sag3[Critical deficit Dc]
    sag3 --> decision{DO at critical point greater than 4 mg per L?}
    decision --> yes1[EIA PASS - No significant impact]
    decision --> no1[EIA FAIL - Recommend treatment]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Streeter-Phelps Oxygen Sag Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[River Water Above Discharge Point] --> blockB[DO = DO sat]
    blockB --> blockC[Municipal or Industrial Effluent Discharge]
    blockC --> blockD[Mixing Zone - Initial Deficit D0]
    blockD --> blockE[BOD Exertion - k L t]
    blockE --> blockF[Re-aeration from Atmosphere - r D t]
    blockF --> blockG{Dt increasing or decreasing?}
    blockG --> blockH[Critical Point at t = tc]
    blockG --> blockI[Recovery Zone]
    blockH --> blockJ[Minimum DO = DO sat minus Dc]
    blockI --> blockK[DO Returns to Saturation]
    blockJ --> blockL{EIA Impact Assessment}
    blockK --> blockL
    blockL --> blockM[Acceptable if DO greater than 4 mg per L]
    blockL --> blockN[Unacceptable if DO less than 4 mg per L]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — EIA Baseline Monitoring Protocol

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[EIA Project Notification] --> s2[Identify Water Bodies in Study Area]
    s2 --> s3[Establish Sampling Stations - Upstream, Mixing, Downstream]
    s3 --> s4[Quarterly Sampling - 4 Seasons]
    s4 --> s5[In-situ Measurements - pH, DO, EC, Temp, Turbidity]
    s5 --> s6[Laboratory Analysis - BOD, COD, Nutrients, Metals, Coliforms]
    s6 --> s7[Compare with BIS 10500 and CPCB Standards]
    s7 --> s8{Deviations within limits?}
    s8 --> s9[Document as Baseline Data]
    s8 --> s10[Flag as Pre-existing Pollution]
    s9 --> s11[Input to Impact Prediction Model]
    s10 --> s11
    s11 --> s12[Streeter Phelps or QUAL2K Simulation]
    s12 --> s13[Generate EIA Report Water Chapter]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked "Draw the oxygen sag curve" in a 7-mark question, draw a horizontal axis as **Distance/Time downstream**, vertical axis as **DO (mg/L)**. The curve starts at $DO_{sat}$, dips to $D_c$ at $t_c$, then rises asymptotically back. Always mark the **4 mg/L threshold line** — this is the fish survival limit and is the most common examiner checkpoint.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between BOD and COD. Why is the BOD/COD ratio significant in EIA studies?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand) | COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Oxygen required by **aerobic microorganisms** to biodegrade organic matter in 5 days at 20 °C. | Oxygen equivalent of **organic matter oxidized chemically** by strong oxidant K₂Cr₂O₇. |
| **Oxidant** | Microbial (biological) | Chemical (dichromate) |
| **Time** | 5 days (standard) | 2–3 hours (reflux) |
| **Measures** | **Biodegradable** organics only | **Total** (bio + non-bio) organics |
| **Magnitude** | Always **less than** COD | Always **greater than** BOD |

**Significance of BOD/COD ratio (1 mark):**
- If **BOD/COD > 0.5** → effluent is **highly biodegradable** → biological treatment is feasible.
- If **BOD/COD < 0.3** → effluent contains **toxic/non-biodegradable** substances → biological treatment alone is insufficient; advanced treatment (RO, adsorption) is required.
- In EIA, this ratio decides the **type of Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** proposed in the EMP.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: What are the significance and standard limits of the following in drinking water as per IS 10500: (a) pH, (b) Total Hardness, (c) Fecal Coliforms?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

**(a) pH (range 6.5 – 8.5):**
- Indicates the **acid-base balance** of water.
- Low pH (< 6.5) → corrosive, leaches metals (Pb, Cu) from pipes.
- High pH (> 8.5) → scale formation, bitter taste, reduces disinfection efficiency of chlorine.

**(b) Total Hardness (max 200 mg/L as CaCO₃):**
- Caused by Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ salts.
- High hardness → scaling in boilers, soap wastage, skin irritation, kidney stones.

**(c) Fecal Coliforms (must be 0 MPN/100 mL):**
- Indicates **fecal contamination** from warm-blooded animals/humans.
- Presence → risk of pathogens causing **cholera, typhoid, dysentery, hepatitis A**.
- Treated water must show **zero** fecal coliforms in 100 mL sample.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**Q: (a) Explain the various physical and chemical characteristics of water used as parameters in EIA studies. (7 marks)**
**(b) A river with flow velocity 0.3 m/s receives an effluent with BOD₅ = 200 mg/L. If the deoxygenation constant k = 0.23/day and re-aeration constant r = 0.4/day at 20 °C, determine the critical time and critical DO deficit. Assume DO saturation = 9.0 mg/L and initial deficit = 0. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Physical and Chemical Characteristics (7 marks)

The water quality parameters assessed in EIA are classified into three domains:

**Physical Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

1. **Turbidity (NTU):** Cloudiness due to suspended/colloidal matter; high turbidity reduces photosynthesis and DO; measured by nephelometer; standard $\leq$ 1 NTU (drinking).

2. **Color (Pt-Co Units):** True color (dissolved) vs Apparent color (suspended); caused by humic acids, tannins, industrial dyes.

3. **Odor & Taste (TON):** Indicates biological activity or industrial contamination; Threshold Odor Number (TON) is the dilution factor at which odor becomes undetectable.

4. **Temperature (°C):** Inversely affects DO saturation; controls biological reaction rates; causes thermal stratification in lakes.

5. **Total Solids (mg/L):** Sum of **TSS** (filterable residue) + **TDS** (evaporated filtrate); TSS $\rightarrow$ sludge, TDS $\rightarrow$ salinity.

6. **Electrical Conductivity ($\mu$S/cm):** Indicator of ionic content; TDS $\approx 0.64 \times EC$ for natural waters.

**Chemical Characteristics (3.5 marks):**

1. **pH:** $pH = -\log[H^{+}]$; standard 6.5 – 8.5; affects toxicity of ammonia, metals, chlorine.

2. **Alkalinity (mg/L as CaCO₃):** Acid-neutralizing capacity from HCO₃⁻, CO₃²⁻, OH⁻; pH determines species.

3. **Total Hardness (mg/L as CaCO₃):** $TH = 2.5[Ca^{2+}] + 4.1[Mg^{2+}]$; classification into soft/hard/very hard.

4. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** Essential for aquatic life; saturation dependent on temperature, pressure, salinity.

5. **BOD₅ (mg/L):** Oxygen demand for biodegradation in 5 days at 20 °C; primary indicator of organic pollution.

6. **COD (mg/L):** Total chemical oxygen demand via K₂Cr₂O₇; BOD/COD ratio indicates treatability.

7. **Nutrients:** Nitrogen species (NH₃, NO₂⁻, NO₃⁻) and Phosphates cause eutrophication.

8. **Heavy Metals:** Pb, Cd, Hg, As, Cr — toxic at trace levels; bioaccumulative.

#### Solution to Part (b): Critical Time and Deficit Calculation (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $L_0 = 200$ mg/L (initial ultimate BOD; for first-cut approximation we use $L_0 \approx$ BOD₅ for stabilized systems; for kinetic calculation we treat $L_0$ as ultimate BOD)
- $k = 0.23$ day⁻¹
- $r = 0.4$ day⁻¹
- $DO_{sat} = 9.0$ mg/L
- $D_0 = 0$

**Step 1: Critical Time ($t_c$)**

$$t_c = \frac{1}{k - r}\ln\left(\frac{k}{r}\right)$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{0.23 - 0.4}\ln\left(\frac{0.23}{0.4}\right)$$

$$t_c = \frac{1}{-0.17}\ln(0.575) = \frac{-0.5534}{-0.17} = 3.255 \; \text{days}$$

**Step 2: Critical DO Deficit ($D_c$)**

$$D_c = \frac{L_0}{k} \cdot k \cdot e^{-r t_c} \quad \Rightarrow \quad D_c = \frac{L_0 k}{r}e^{-r t_c}$$

$$D_c = \frac{200 \times 0.23}{0.4} \times e^{-0.4 \times 3.255}$$

$$D_c = 115 \times e^{-1.302} = 115 \times 0.2717$$

$$\boxed{D_c = 31.25 \; \text{mg/L (deficit)}}$$

**Step 3: Critical DO**

$$DO_{critical} = DO_{sat} - D_c = 9.0 - 31.25 = -22.25 \; \text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation**

Since the critical DO deficit far exceeds the saturation value, the river water will become **completely anaerobic** at the critical point. The model indicates that with $L_0 = 200$ mg/L, **the discharge is environmentally catastrophic**. The EIA recommendation would be to either:

- Reduce the discharge BOD to **< 20 mg/L** through **secondary biological treatment**, or
- Provide **flow augmentation** by releasing reservoir water during low-flow periods.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert** BOD₅ to ultimate BOD $L_0$. For $k = 0.23$/day, $L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-5k}) = 200 / 0.685 = 292$ mg/L. The above solution uses $L_0 = 200$ for simplicity; in a 14-mark answer, **always show this conversion explicitly** for full marks.
> 2. **Sign error in $k - r$:** $k$ (BOD rate) is usually **less** than $r$ (re-aeration rate), making $k - r$ **negative**. Do not panic — the negative sign cancels with the log.
> 3. **Forgetting units of $k$ and $r$:** both must be in **day⁻¹** consistently.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) Discuss in detail the biological characteristics of water. Explain the MPN method for coliform estimation. (7 marks)**
**(b) A wastewater sample has the following analysis: $Ca^{2+} = 80$ mg/L, $Mg^{2+} = 24$ mg/L, $HCO_3^- = 150$ mg/L, $CO_3^{2-} = 30$ mg/L, pH = 8.2, DO = 5 mg/L, BOD₅ = 180 mg/L, COD = 420 mg/L. Calculate (i) Total Hardness, (ii) Total Alkalinity, (iii) BOD/COD ratio, and (iv) state the treatability class. (7 marks)**

#### Solution to Part (a): Biological Characteristics & MPN (7 marks)

**Biological Characteristics (4 marks):**

Biological characteristics of water assess the presence, type, and density of microorganisms, which are critical for:
- Public health (pathogen detection)
- Aquatic ecosystem balance (algae, plankton)
- Indicator organism approach (coliforms)

**Key biological parameters:**

1. **Total Coliforms** — broad group of Gram-negative, rod-shaped bacteria; includes fecal + soil species; useful as **primary indicator** of disinfection efficiency.

2. **Fecal Coliforms (FC)** — thermotolerant species (grow at 44.5 °C); includes *E. coli*; definitive indicator of recent fecal contamination from warm-blooded animals.

3. **Fecal Streptococci** — used as a **secondary indicator**; FC/FS ratio helps identify contamination source (human vs animal).

4. **E. coli** — the most reliable fecal indicator; specific enzymatic methods (e.g., MUG test) confirm presence.

5. **Pathogens** — *Vibrio cholerae* (cholera), *Salmonella typhi* (typhoid), Hepatitis A virus, Rotavirus; direct health risk.

6. **Algae & Chlorophyll-a** — Chlorophyll-a is the **quantitative proxy** for algal biomass; concentration > 10 mg/m³ indicates **eutrophic** conditions; causes taste/odor problems and filter clogging.

7. **Plankton, Benthos & Macrophytes** — used in BMWP and Saprobic Index for **bio-monitoring of stream health**.

**MPN Method (3 marks):**

**Most Probable Number (MPN)** is a **statistical, multi-tube fermentation technique** that estimates the coliform population density based on **probability theory**.

**Procedure:**
1. **Inoculation:** Five tubes of lactose broth (or 3 tubes in 3 dilutions: 10 mL, 1 mL, 0.1 mL) are inoculated with measured sample volumes.
2. **Presumptive Test:** Incubate at **37 °C for 48 hours**. Tubes showing **gas production and acid formation** are **positive**.
3. **Confirmed Test:** Positive tubes are sub-cultured to **brilliant green lactose bile broth (BGLB)** and incubated at 37 °C for 48 hours.
4. **Completed Test (optional):** Streak positive tubes on **EMB agar**; identify *E. coli* colonies with metallic sheen.

**Statistical Estimation:**
- Count the number of positive tubes in each dilution.
- Refer to **Standard MPN Tables** (APHA / IS 1622) for the most probable number.
- Result expressed as **MPN index per 100 mL** with **95% confidence limits**.

**Example:** If 10 mL tubes (5/5 positive), 1 mL tubes (3/5 positive), 0.1 mL tubes (1/5 positive), then MPN = **160 per 100 mL** with 95% CL of (47 – 467).

#### Solution to Part (b): Numerical Calculations (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- $Ca^{2+} = 80$ mg/L
- $Mg^{2+} = 24$ mg/L
- $HCO_3^- = 150$ mg/L
- $CO_3^{2-} = 30$ mg/L
- pH = 8.2
- DO = 5 mg/L
- BOD₅ = 180 mg/L
- COD = 420 mg/L

**(i) Total Hardness (TH):**

$$TH = 2.5 \times [Ca^{2+}] + 4.1 \times [Mg^{2+}]$$

$$TH = 2.5 \times 80 + 4.1 \times 24 = 200 + 98.4 = 298.4 \; \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

Classification: **> 180 mg/L → Very Hard water**

**(ii) Total Alkalinity (TA):**

$$TA = \frac{[HCO_3^-]}{1.22} + \frac{[CO_3^{2-}]}{1.20} \quad (\text{converted to CaCO}_3)$$

- For HCO₃⁻: Equivalent weight = 61; CaCO₃ equivalent weight = 50. Factor = 61/50 = 1.22
- For CO₃²⁻: Equivalent weight = 30; CaCO₃ equivalent weight = 50. Factor = 30/50 = 0.60 → so divide by 0.60 (i.e., multiply by 1.667)

Using the standard formula:

$$TA = \frac{150}{1.22} + \frac{30}{0.60} = 122.95 + 50.0 = 172.95 \; \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

Alternative: Using direct equivalents:

$$TA = \left(\frac{150}{61} \times 50\right) + \left(\frac{30}{30} \times 50\right) = 122.95 + 50.0 = 172.95 \; \text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**(iii) BOD/COD Ratio:**

$$\frac{BOD}{COD} = \frac{180}{420} = 0.4286 \approx 0.43$$

**(iv) Treatability Class:**

- BOD/COD = 0.43 falls in the range **0.3 – 0.5 → Moderately biodegradable**.
- The wastewater is **amenable to conventional biological treatment** (e.g., activated sludge process), but it is **not as easily treatable as domestic sewage** (BOD/COD > 0.5).
- **Recommended treatment train:** Primary clarification → Activated Sludge (or Trickling Filter) → Secondary clarification → Chlorination.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callouts:**
> 1. **Hardness Conversion Trap:** Students often forget to convert **Mg²⁺ concentration "as Mg"** to **"as CaCO₃ equivalent"** and lose 2 marks. Always apply the **2.5** and **4.1** factors.
> 2. **Alkalinity Speciation:** At **pH 8.2 (< 8.3)**, the species are predominantly **HCO₃⁻** with trace CO₃²⁻. Always use the **OH⁻ + CO₃²⁻ alkalinity at phenolphthalein endpoint (P)** and **HCO₃⁻ alkalinity at methyl orange endpoint (M)** for full clarity.
> 3. **BOD₅ vs Ultimate BOD:** In Streeter-Phelps, $L_0$ is the **ultimate BOD** ($L_0 = BOD_5 / (1 - e^{-k \times 5})$), NOT the BOD₅ directly. The numerical problem in Question A used $L_0 = 200$ for simplicity, but **the complete derivation should include this conversion** for full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU 2024**

- [ ] Water quality parameters are **3-domain**: **Physical, Chemical, Biological**.
- [ ] **Turbidity** is measured in **NTU** (nephelometric).
- [ ] **TDS $\approx 0.64 \times EC$** — empirical conversion.
- [ ] **Pure water pH = 7.0 at 25 °C**; drinking water range **6.5 – 8.5**.
- [ ] **Total Hardness = 2.5[Ca²⁺] + 4.1[Mg²⁺]** in mg/L as CaCO₃.
- [ ] **DO saturation** drops ~0.25 mg/L per 1 °C rise.
- [ ] **BOD₅ = 5-day test at 20 °C**; **COD** uses **K₂Cr₂O₇**.
- [ ] **Always COD > BOD**; BOD/COD ratio indicates biodegradability.
- [ ] **Streeter-Phelps** equations predict the **oxygen sag curve** downstream of discharge.
- [ ] **Critical time** $t_c = \dfrac{1}{k-r}\ln(k/r)$.
- [ ] **BOD rate constant** increases by **4.7% per °C** (van't Hoff-Arrhenius rule, $\Theta = 1.047$).
- [ ] **MPN** = statistical coliform count per **100 mL**; threshold drinking = **0**.
- [ ] **Chlorophyll-a > 10 mg/m³** = eutrophic water body.
- [ ] **Free ammonia (NH₃)** is toxic to fish; calculated from total ammonia and pH.
- [ ] **Heavy metals (Pb, Cd, Hg, As)** are toxic at **$\mu$g/L** level; bioaccumulative.
- [ ] **Fecal Coliforms** → indicate recent fecal contamination; treatment goal = **0 MPN/100 mL**.
- [ ] **Thermal stratification** in lakes controls eutrophication; temperature influences DO.
- [ ] **4 mg/L DO** is the **fish survival threshold** — most cited value in KTU problems.
- [ ] In Kerala EIA context, mean river temperature ~ **27 °C** — apply $\Theta$ correction.
- [ ] **pH < 8.3** → only bicarbonate alkalinity; **pH > 8.3** → carbonate + hydroxide.
- [ ] **EMB agar** confirms *E. coli*; **BGLB broth** confirms fecal coliforms.
- [ ] The **Streeter-Phelps sag curve** ALWAYS: starts at $DO_{sat}$, dips, then recovers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark numerical question in KTU Module 2, the most common test is the **Streeter-Phelps critical time and deficit calculation**. Memorize the four equations, practice one worked example, and always state **assumptions** (steady-state, uniform mixing, 1-D flow, temperature constant) before solving. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the assumption statement.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water-borne Diseases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water-borne Diseases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **water-borne disease** is defined as any illness caused by the consumption of, or contact with, water that has been contaminated by pathogenic microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, protozoa, or helminths) or by chemical toxins of biological origin (cyanotoxins, biotoxins). In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, water-borne diseases are categorized as **adverse bio-physical and socio-economic impacts** that arise from proposed developmental activities (dams, industrial effluents, urbanization, deforestation) which alter the natural hydrological regime and degrade the quality of surface and groundwater resources.

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, a disease is classified as water-borne when the etiological agent is transmitted through the **faecal–oral route** via contaminated drinking water, recreational water bodies, or water used for food preparation and personal hygiene.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For EIA evaluations, water-borne diseases are treated as **predictable health impacts** that must be quantified during the **Screening**, **Scoping**, and **Impact Prediction** stages of an EIA study. Failure to assess them leads to non-compliance with the **EIA Notification, 2006 (as amended in 2020)** under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Invisible Enemy in the Glass"

Imagine a perfectly clear, sparkling glass of water. To your eyes, it is pristine. Yet, hidden within those few millilitres are **thousands of microscopic pathogens** — bacteria smaller than the width of a human hair, viruses one-hundredth the size of bacteria, and protozoan cysts encased in protective armour. When you drink, you unknowingly invite these invaders into your body. This is the silent danger of water-borne diseases — the water **looks safe, smells safe, and tastes safe, but is microbiologically lethal**.

In EIA terms, this is analogous to a **project appearing environmentally benign on the surface** (clear EIA report, green clearance stamp) but harbouring **latent health hazards** downstream that surface only years later as epidemic outbreaks in receptor communities.

### 1.3 Classification of Water-Related Diseases (WHO Framework)

The WHO classifies water-related diseases into **four broad categories** based on their transmission pathway:

| S.No. | Category | Transmission Route | Classic Example |
|------|----------|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Water-borne** | Pathogens transmitted through ingestion of contaminated water | Cholera, Typhoid, Hepatitis A |
| 2 | **Water-washed** | Diseases from poor personal hygiene due to water scarcity | Trachoma, Scabies, Shigellosis |
| 3 | **Water-based** | Pathogens whose lifecycle involves aquatic intermediate hosts | Schistosomiasis, Dracunculiasis |
| 4 | **Water-related** | Diseases transmitted by insects breeding in water | Malaria, Dengue, Yellow Fever |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU module focuses primarily on **Category 1 (Water-borne)** but expects students to differentiate it from Categories 2–4 in the examination. This is a **frequently asked 3-mark question**.

### 1.4 Why This Matters in EIA — The Health Impact Dimension

In any major project (irrigation dam, SEZ, thermal power plant, highway), the **Terms of Reference (ToR)** issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** explicitly mandates:

- Baseline epidemiological survey of the **study area (10 km radius)**.
- Prediction of **incremental disease burden** using **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Years)** metrics.
- Formulation of a **Public Health Management Plan (PHMP)** with budgetary provision.
- Post-project **Environmental Monitoring (EM)** of coliform counts in potable water sources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A water-borne disease assessment is a **mandatory deliverable** in the **Environmental Impact Statement (EIS)** under the **CRZ Notification 2019** and **EIA Notification 2006**. The acceptable threshold is **0 MPN/100 mL** of total coliforms in drinking water as per **BIS IS 10500:2012**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Analytical Framework

### 2.1 The Faecal–Oral Transmission Pathway — Step-by-Step Logic

The transmission of water-borne diseases follows a well-defined **five-link epidemiological chain**, often called the **"Faecal–Oral Transmission Cycle"**:

**Link 1 — Pathogen Reservoir:**
The cycle begins in the **intestinal tract of an infected host** (human or animal). Pathogens such as *Vibrio cholerae*, *Salmonella typhi*, and rotavirus are shed in faeces at concentrations as high as **10⁶ to 10¹⁰ organisms per gram of stool**.

**Link 2 — Environmental Exit and Survival:**
Faecal matter enters water bodies through **domestic sewage discharge, open defecation, agricultural runoff (containing livestock waste), and inadequate septic tank effluents**. The pathogens survive in water for durations ranging from **2 days (Vibrio cholerae)** to **>60 days (Hepatitis A virus)** depending on temperature, pH, and sunlight exposure.

**Link 3 — Water Contamination:**
Contamination occurs when **untreated or partially treated wastewater** enters potable water sources. In India, **CPCB data (2023)** indicates that over **70% of surface water sources** in urban catchments exceed the permissible coliform limit of **500 MPN/100 mL**.

**Link 4 — Ingestion by New Host:**
A susceptible individual consumes the contaminated water through drinking, food preparation, or recreational contact. The **infectious dose** varies widely:
- *Vibrio cholerae*: **10⁶ – 10⁸ organisms** (high dose)
- *Shigella*: **10 – 200 organisms** (extremely low dose — high infectivity)
- *Cryptosporidium*: **1 – 10 oocysts** (very low dose)

**Link 5 — Disease Manifestation:**
After an incubation period (hours to weeks), the host develops clinical symptoms — diarrhoea, vomiting, fever, hepatic dysfunction — completing the cycle by shedding pathogens back into the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind EIA Relevance:**
> Any EIA project that breaks **Link 2 or Link 3** of this chain (e.g., by providing proper sanitation infrastructure or wastewater treatment plants) can be evaluated as delivering a **positive net health impact**, even if it generates some negative impacts in other domains. This is the essence of the **"Net Positive Health Impact" criterion** introduced in the **2020 EIA Draft Amendment**.

### 2.2 Classification of Water-Borne Pathogens — The Four Kingdom Model

Water-borne pathogens are classified into four microbiological kingdoms, each with distinct characteristics relevant to EIA water-quality testing protocols:

#### 2.2.1 Bacterial Pathogens
- **Morphology:** Unicellular prokaryotes, 0.5–5 µm in size.
- **Indicator Organism:** *Escherichia coli* (thermotolerant coliform).
- **Diseases Caused:** Cholera, Typhoid, Bacillary Dysentery, Leptospirosis.
- **Key EIA Parameter:** **Total Coliform Count (TCC)** and **Faecal Coliform Count (FCC)** measured in **MPN/100 mL (Most Probable Number per 100 millilitres)**.

#### 2.2.2 Viral Pathogens
- **Morphology:** Acellular entities, 20–300 nm, require host cells for replication.
- **Indicator Challenge:** Viruses are **not detected by standard coliform tests** — special assays like **RT-PCR** and **cell culture** are required.
- **Diseases Caused:** Hepatitis A & E, Poliomyelitis, Rotaviral gastroenteritis, Adenoviral infections.
- **Key EIA Parameter:** **Plaque-Forming Units (PFU/L)** or **Genome Copies (GC/L)** by molecular methods.

#### 2.2.3 Protozoan Parasites
- **Morphology:** Eukaryotic unicellular organisms, 5–50 µm; many form **environmentally resistant cysts/oocysts**.
- **Chlorine Resistance:** High — *Cryptosporidium* and *Giardia* cysts are **chlorine-tolerant** (CT value requirement is exceptionally high).
- **Diseases Caused:** Cryptosporidiosis, Giardiasis, Amoebiasis (*Entamoeba histolytica*).
- **Key EIA Parameter:** **Oocyst/Litre count** by immunofluorescence or flow cytometry.

#### 2.2.4 Helminthic Worms
- **Morphology:** Multicellular parasitic worms (roundworms, flatworms), 1 mm to several metres.
- **Diseases Caused:** Ascariasis (*Ascaris lumbricoides*), Dracunculiasis, Schistosomiasis.
- **Key EIA Parameter:** **Eggs per Litre (EPG/L)** detected by **modified Bailenger method**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Threshold Standards

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Symbol / Unit | Application in EIA |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **MPN Index** | Thomas equation approximation: $MPN = \dfrac{\# \text{positive tubes} \times 100}{\sqrt{\text{Volume sampled}}}$ | MPN / 100 mL | Faecal coliform enumeration |
| **Mortality Rate** | $MR = \dfrac{\text{Deaths from water-borne disease}}{\text{Total population at risk}} \times 1000$ | per 1000 population | Baseline health survey |
| **Attack Rate** | $AR = \dfrac{\text{New cases during outbreak}}{\text{Population at risk}} \times 100$ | Percentage | Epidemic investigation |
| **DALY** | $DALY = YLL + YLD$ | Years | Total disease burden assessment |
| **YLL (Years of Life Lost)** | $YLL = \sum N_{x} \times L_{x}$ | Years | Premature mortality |
| **YLD (Years Lived with Disability)** | $YLD = I \times DW \times L$ | Years | Morbidity assessment |
| **Drinking Water Standard (BIS IS 10500:2012)** | Total Coliforms: **0 MPN/100 mL**; E. coli: **0 MPN/100 mL** | MPN / 100 mL | Compliance benchmark |
| **Recreational Water (CPCB)** | Faecal Coliforms: **\leq 100 MPN/100 mL** ( bathing) | MPN / 100 mL | Project area water body |
| **Log Removal Value (LRV)** | $LRV = \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{N_{in}}{N_{out}}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Treatment plant efficiency |
| **CT Value (Disinfection)** | $CT = C \times t$ (C = mg/L, t = minutes) | mg·min/L | Chlorine contact time adequacy |

> [!NOTE]
> The **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year)** is the **gold-standard metric** used by WHO and now adopted in KTU 2024 syllabus for quantifying water-borne disease burden in EIA Health Impact Assessment (HIA) reports. Always show the **decomposition into YLL + YLD** in derivations.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Practice

In actual EIA consultancy practice, the above formulas are operationalized in the following deliverables:

1. **Baseline Health Profile Report** — Uses MR and AR to establish the **pre-project disease burden**.
2. **Impact Prediction Chapter** — Applies **dose-response models** to estimate the **incremental DALY loss** under different project scenarios (with/without mitigation).
3. **Mitigation Cost-Benefit Analysis** — Compares the **monetized value of DALYs averted** (WHO uses **1 DALY ≈ ₹4,30,000**, 2023 Indian reference value) against the **capital cost of water treatment infrastructure**.
4. **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** — Specifies the **CT value, LRV, and residual chlorine (0.2–0.5 mg/L)** targets for the proposed treatment plant.

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Disease Atlas

### 3.1 Comprehensive Disease Classification Matrix

The following analytical matrix is the **most exam-relevant single resource** for the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). It consolidates the etiologic agent, clinical features, and mitigation strategy for all major water-borne diseases:

| S.No. | Disease | Causative Agent | Agent Type | Incubation Period | Key Symptoms | Diagnostic Marker | Primary Mitigation in EIA Context |
|------|---------|----------------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|-------------------|-----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Cholera** | *Vibrio cholerae* (O1, O139) | Bacterium | 2 hrs – 5 days | Profuse watery "rice-water" diarrhoea, severe dehydration | Stool culture on TCBS agar, dark-field microscopy | Sewage interception; water treatment with residual Cl₂ \geq 0.5 mg/L |
| 2 | **Typhoid Fever** | *Salmonella typhi* | Bacterium | 6 – 30 days | Step-ladder fever, abdominal pain, rose spots, hepatosplenomegaly | Widal test, blood culture | Safe drinking water supply; chlorination; vaccination drives |
| 3 | **Bacillary Dysentery** | *Shigella dysenteriae* | Bacterium | 1 – 7 days | Bloody mucoid diarrhoea, tenesmus, fever | Stool microscopy, culture | Sanitation infrastructure; hand-wash stations |
| 4 | **Hepatitis A & E** | HAV / HEV (Picornaviridae / Hepeviridae) | Virus | 15 – 50 days (HAV); 15 – 60 days (HEV) | Jaundice, anorexia, dark urine, hepatomegaly | IgM anti-HAV/HEV, LFT elevation | Boiling water (HAV destroyed at >85°C); HEV vaccine for high-risk zones |
| 5 | **Poliomyelitis** | Poliovirus (Enterovirus) | Virus | 7 – 14 days | Asymmetric flaccid paralysis, fever | Stool RT-PCR, AFP surveillance | Chlorination; OPV/IPV immunization |
| 6 | **Rotaviral Gastroenteritis** | Rotavirus (Reoviridae) | Virus | 1 – 3 days | Severe watery diarrhoea in children <5 yrs, vomiting, fever | ELISA, Latex agglutination | Safe water + oral rehydration salts (ORS); Rotavirus vaccine |
| 7 | **Amoebic Dysentery** | *Entamoeba histolytica* | Protozoan | 2 – 4 weeks | Bloody diarrhoea, liver abscess (anchovy paste pus) | Trophozoite/cyst in stool, serology | Filtration (1 µm absolute); periodic anti-amoebic prophylaxis |
| 8 | **Giardiasis** | *Giardia lamblia* | Protozoan | 7 – 14 days | Greasy foul-smelling diarrhoea, malabsorption, weight loss | Stool antigen ELISA, cyst/trophozoite | UV disinfection (effective against cysts); boiling |
| 9 | **Cryptosporidiosis** | *Cryptosporidium parvum* | Protozoan | 2 – 10 days | Watery diarrhoea (esp. immunocompromised), weight loss | Modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain, IFA | **NOT killed by chlorine**; requires **ultrafiltration (UF) or ozonation** |
| 10 | **Ascariasis** | *Ascaris lumbricoides* | Helminth | 4 – 16 weeks | Abdominal distension, nutritional deficiency, Löffler's syndrome | Stool microscopy (fertilized eggs) | Proper sanitation; prevention of open defecation |
| 11 | **Dracunculiasis** | *Dracunculus medinensis* | Helminth | 10 – 14 months | Painful skin ulcer with worm emergence | Clinical (visible worm) | **Almost eradicated** (India certified guinea-worm free in 2000) |
| 12 | **Leptospirosis** | *Leptospira interrogans* | Bacterium (spirochaete) | 2 – 30 days | Fever, myalgia, conjunctival suffusion, jaundice (Weil's disease) | MAT serology, dark-field microscopy | Rodent control; post-flood doxycycline chemoprophylaxis |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example — DALY Calculation for a Hypothetical EIA Project

**Problem Statement:**
A proposed river-valley project is expected to cause an incremental burden of cholera in a downstream village of **population N = 10,000**. Estimate parameters:
- Expected additional cases per year: **200**
- Case fatality rate: **1%** (2 deaths per year)
- Average age at death: **45 years**
- Life expectancy: **75 years**
- Average duration of disability per case: **7 days**
- Disability weight for acute cholera: **DW = 0.281**

**Step 1 — Calculate Years of Life Lost (YLL):**

$$
YLL = N_{\text{deaths}} \times L_{\text{remaining}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{remaining}} = 75 - 45 = 30 \text{ years}
$$

$$
YLL = 2 \times 30 = 60 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Years Lived with Disability (YLD):**

$$
YLD = I \times DW \times L
$$

$$
L = \dfrac{7}{365} = 0.01918 \text{ years}
$$

$$
YLD = 200 \times 0.281 \times 0.01918
$$

$$
YLD = 200 \times 0.281 \times 0.01918 = 1.078 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total DALYs Lost:**

$$
DALY = YLL + YLD = 60 + 1.078 = 61.08 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 — Monetize the Loss (Optional, for EMP cost-benefit):**

$$
\text{Monetized loss} = 61.08 \times \text{₹4,30,000 per DALY}
$$

$$
\text{Monetized loss} = \text{₹2,62,64,400 per year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Note:** In KTU 2024 valuation, you **must** show the DALY decomposition. Marks are awarded specifically for the **YLL = Deaths × Residual life expectancy** formula and **YLD = Incidence × Disability Weight × Duration** formula. Skipping the unit conversion of days-to-years (dividing by 365) is a common mistake that costs **1 full mark**.

### 3.3 Vulnerability Mapping Matrix for EIA Receptor Communities

| Vulnerability Factor | High-Risk Profile | Low-Risk Profile |
|----------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| **Age** | Children <5 yrs and elderly >65 yrs | Adults 15–45 yrs |
| **Socio-economic** | BPL families; slum dwellers with no piped water | Middle-class with municipal water supply |
| **Geographic** | Floodplains, downstream of sewage outfalls, coastal zones | Elevated terrain with borewell supply |
| **Nutritional** | Malnourished, anaemic | Well-nourished, immunocompetent |
| **Occupational** | Agricultural labourers in irrigated fields | Indoor office workers |
| **Sanitation Access** | Open defecation prevalent; shared community toilets | Household toilets with septic tanks |

> [!NOTE]
> In the EIA **Public Health Management Plan (PHMP)**, the vulnerable groups above are prioritized for **additional monitoring, free water testing, and subsidized treatment**. This is a **Frequently Asked 7-mark question** in KTU 2024.
