## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
**Differentiate between water-borne and water-based diseases with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Water-borne diseases** are transmitted through the **ingestion of water contaminated with pathogens** (faecal–oral route). The pathogen does not require an aquatic host for any part of its lifecycle. **Examples:** Cholera (caused by *Vibrio cholerae*) and Typhoid (caused by *Salmonella typhi*).
- **Water-based diseases** are caused by pathogens that **depend on an aquatic intermediate host** (typically a snail or crustacean) to complete part of their lifecycle. The pathogen's larval stage develops in water before infecting humans. **Examples:** Schistosomiasis (transmitted by *Schistosoma* through freshwater snails) and Dracunculiasis (transmitted by *Dracunculus medinensis* via water fleas *Cyclops*).

The key difference lies in the **mechanism of transmission** — direct ingestion (water-borne) versus complex lifecycle involving an **aquatic vector** (water-based). In EIA practice, water-based diseases require **interruption of the vector's habitat**, whereas water-borne diseases require **interruption of the faecal–oral pathway**.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for examples)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is the significance of *Escherichia coli* as an indicator organism in water quality assessment for EIA studies?**

**Model Answer:**

*Escherichia coli* (E. coli) is a **thermotolerant faecal coliform bacterium** that resides exclusively in the **intestinal tract of warm-blooded animals** (humans, livestock). It is used as a **microbial indicator** in EIA water-quality studies for the following reasons:

1. **Universal Presence in Faeces:** E. coli is shed in enormous quantities (approx. **10⁹ per gram of human faeces**), making it a sensitive marker of recent faecal contamination.
2. **Easy Detection:** It is **cultivable in 24 hours** at 44.5°C on specific media (e.g., **m-FC agar**), giving rapid and cost-effective results compared to direct pathogen detection.
3. **Correlation with Pathogens:** The presence of E. coli indicates a **high probability of concurrent presence** of water-borne pathogens such as *Vibrio*, *Salmonella*, and *Shigella*.
4. **Regulatory Acceptance:** BIS IS 10500:2012 and CPCB mandate **0 MPN/100 mL** of E. coli in drinking water; **\leq 100 MPN/100 mL** in bathing waters.

**Limitation:** E. coli is **not a reliable indicator for viruses and chlorine-resistant protozoa** (e.g., *Cryptosporidium*). For these, complementary assays like **RT-PCR for enteric viruses** and **immunofluorescence for oocysts** are required.

**(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for significance + 1 mark for limitations)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **classification of water-borne diseases** based on the type of causative pathogen. Provide **two examples** for each category with their **mode of transmission and clinical symptoms**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the **faecal–oral transmission cycle** of water-borne diseases. Discuss **four mitigation measures** that an EIA consultant must incorporate in the Environmental Management Plan (EMP) to break this transmission chain.

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to Part (a) — Classification of Water-Borne Diseases (7 Marks):**

Water-borne diseases are classified into **four major categories** based on the taxonomic nature of the causative pathogen. The classification is critical in EIA because **each category responds differently to standard water treatment processes**.

**Category 1: Bacterial Water-Borne Diseases (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Diseases caused by unicellular prokaryotic pathogens transmitted through contaminated water.
- **Example 1 — Cholera:** Caused by *Vibrio cholerae*, a comma-shaped gram-negative bacterium. Transmission occurs by ingestion of water contaminated with faeces of infected persons. Clinical symptoms include profuse painless watery "rice-water" diarrhoea, severe dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and hypovolemic shock. Incubation period is **2 hours to 5 days**.
- **Example 2 — Typhoid Fever:** Caused by *Salmonella typhi*, a motile gram-negative bacterium. Transmitted via contaminated water and food. Symptoms include sustained high fever (step-ladder pattern), abdominal pain, rose-coloured spots on the trunk, hepatosplenomegaly, and relative bradycardia. Incubation period is **6 to 30 days**.

**Category 2: Viral Water-Borne Diseases (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Diseases caused by acellular viral pathogens that replicate only inside living host cells, transmitted through contaminated water.
- **Example 1 — Hepatitis A:** Caused by **Hepatitis A Virus (HAV)**, a non-enveloped RNA virus in the *Picornaviridae* family. Transmission via faecal–oral route through contaminated water and shellfish. Symptoms include fever, malaise, anorexia, dark urine, jaundice, and tender hepatomegaly. Incubation period is **15 to 50 days**.
- **Example 2 — Poliomyelitis:** Caused by **Poliovirus** (genus *Enterovirus*). Transmitted through water and food contaminated with infected faeces. Symptoms range from asymptomatic infection to acute flaccid paralysis, with **1 in 200 infections** resulting in irreversible paralysis. Incubation period is **7 to 14 days**.

**Category 3: Protozoan Water-Borne Diseases (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Diseases caused by single-celled eukaryotic parasites that often form environmentally resistant **cysts or oocysts** capable of surviving in water for months.
- **Example 1 — Amoebic Dysentery (Amoebiasis):** Caused by *Entamoeba histolytica*, an anaerobic amoeba. Transmitted by ingestion of water containing mature cysts. Symptoms include bloody mucoid stools, abdominal cramping, and potential extra-intestinal complications such as liver abscess ("anchovy paste" pus). Incubation is **2 to 4 weeks**.
- **Example 2 — Giardiasis:** Caused by *Giardia lamblia* (intestinalis), a flagellated protozoan. Transmitted by ingestion of water containing cysts. Symptoms include greasy, foul-smelling diarrhoea (steatorrhoea), bloating, flatulence, and weight loss due to malabsorption. Incubation is **7 to 14 days**.

**Category 4: Helminthic Water-Borne Diseases (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Diseases caused by multicellular parasitic worms (helminths) whose eggs or larvae are transmitted through contaminated water.
- **Example 1 — Ascariasis:** Caused by *Ascaris lumbricoides*, the largest intestinal roundworm (up to 35 cm). Transmitted by ingestion of embryonated eggs in contaminated water/food. Symptoms include abdominal discomfort, malnutrition, and in heavy infections, intestinal obstruction. Migration of larvae through lungs causes Löffler's syndrome. Incubation is **4 to 16 weeks**.
- **Example 2 — Schistosomiasis (Bilharzia):** Caused by *Schistosoma mansoni/japonicum*. Transmitted through skin penetration by cercariae released from freshwater snails. Symptoms include dermatitis, Katayama fever (acute), and chronic hepatic/intestinal fibrosis. Often classified as **water-based** in WHO terminology but is included here due to its water-dependence.

**Summary Diagram (1 mark):**

> Bacteria: Cholera, Typhoid
> Viruses: Hepatitis A, Polio
> Protozoa: Amoebiasis, Giardiasis
> Helminths: Ascariasis, Schistosomiasis

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Faecal–Oral Transmission Cycle & Mitigation (7 Marks):**

**The Faecal–Oral Transmission Cycle (4 marks):**

The faecal–oral transmission cycle of water-borne diseases is a **five-link epidemiological chain** that must remain intact for an outbreak to occur. The cycle proceeds as follows:

**Link 1 — Reservoir:** An infected human (or animal) sheds pathogens in faeces. The concentration may reach **10⁶ to 10¹⁰ organisms per gram of stool** in acute cases.

**Link 2 — Environmental Release:** Faecal material enters the water environment through inadequate sanitation infrastructure — open defecation, leaking septic tanks, untreated sewage discharge, and runoff from livestock farms.

**Link 3 — Survival in Water:** The pathogens survive in the aquatic environment. Survival duration is pathogen-specific — *Vibrio cholerae* survives for **2–5 days**, while *Cryptosporidium oocysts* can survive for **6–12 months** in cool water.

**Link 4 — Contamination of Potable Water:** Faecally contaminated surface or groundwater reaches the consumer through inadequately treated municipal water, private wells in shallow aquifers, or unboiled stored water.

**Link 5 — Ingestion and Disease:** A susceptible host ingests the pathogens, develops clinical illness after the incubation period, and resumes shedding — completing the cycle.

> The schematic representation is **Figure 4.1** shown in Section 4 of these notes.

**Four Mitigation Measures in the EMP (3 marks):**

**Mitigation 1 — Sanitation Infrastructure (Breaks Link 2):**
The EMP must mandate the construction of **closed sewerage systems, community toilets (especially in project-affected villages), and septic tanks with soak pits**. The project proponent must commit to **100% coverage of household toilets** in the study area as part of the Corporate Social Responsibility (CSR) component. **EIA Reference:** Schedule III of the EIA Notification 2006.

**Mitigation 2 — Multi-Barrier Water Treatment Plant (Breaks Link 4):**
A **multi-barrier treatment plant** combining coagulation, flocculation, sedimentation, rapid sand filtration, and **disinfection with chlorination (CT value \geq 0.5 mg·min/L)** must be installed. The treated water must meet the **BIS IS 10500:2012 standard of 0 MPN/100 mL** for total coliforms.

**Mitigation 3 — Catchment Area Protection (Breaks Link 3):**
The EMP must include a **Catchment Area Treatment Plan (CAT Plan)** with afforestation, restriction on agricultural chemical use near water bodies, and construction of **interception-cum-diversion drains** around the reservoir to prevent agricultural runoff laden with livestock faeces.

**Mitigation 4 — Public Health Surveillance and Vaccination (Breaks Link 5):**
The EMP must establish a **dedicated Public Health Management Plan (PHMP)** that includes (i) free water-quality testing laboratories in the study area, (ii) vaccination drives for cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis, and (iii) community health education programmes on **hand hygiene, safe water storage, and household water disinfection** (e.g., boiling, chlorination tablets).

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Disability-Adjusted Life Years (DALY)** as a metric for quantifying the burden of water-borne diseases. Derive the DALY for a project-affected population with the following assumed data: **Population at risk = 50,000; Annual additional cases of hepatitis E = 500; Case fatality rate = 2%; Average age at death = 50 years; Standard life expectancy = 75 years; Duration of illness per case = 30 days; Disability weight = 0.21.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** in predicting and mitigating the **public health impacts** of water-borne diseases arising from large irrigation and hydropower projects. Cite **at least two Indian case studies** in your answer.

---

### Complete Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to Part (a) — DALY Concept and Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Conceptual Foundation (3 marks):**
The **Disability-Adjusted Life Year (DALY)** is a **time-based composite metric** developed by the WHO and the World Bank to quantify the overall burden of disease in a population. It merges two dimensions of health loss:

- **Mortality burden** quantified as **Years of Life Lost (YLL)** — representing years lost due to premature death.
- **Morbidity burden** quantified as **Years Lived with Disability (YLD)** — representing years lived in a state of less-than-perfect health.

The mathematical identity is:

$$
DALY = YLL + YLD
$$

This metric is extensively used in EIA Health Impact Assessment (HIA) chapters to:
1. Compare the burden of different diseases within a project-affected population.
2. Justify mitigation budgets by monetizing the **DALYs averted** by the proposed public health interventions.
3. Provide a uniform currency for cross-project and cross-regional comparisons.

**Numerical Derivation (4 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Population at risk: N = 50,000
- Annual cases: I = 500
- Case fatality rate: CFR = 2%
- Standard life expectancy: L_std = 75 years
- Average age at death: A_death = 50 years
- Duration of illness: D_illness = 30 days
- Disability weight: DW = 0.21

**Step 1 — Calculate Number of Deaths:**

$$
N_{\text{deaths}} = I \times CFR = 500 \times 0.02 = 10 \text{ deaths}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate YLL:**

Residual life expectancy per death:

$$
L_{\text{remaining}} = L_{\text{std}} - A_{\text{death}} = 75 - 50 = 25 \text{ years}
$$

$$
YLL = N_{\text{deaths}} \times L_{\text{remaining}} = 10 \times 25 = 250 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate YLD:**

Convert illness duration to years:

$$
L = \dfrac{30}{365} = 0.08219 \text{ years}
$$

$$
YLD = I \times DW \times L
$$

$$
YLD = 500 \times 0.21 \times 0.08219
$$

$$
YLD = 8.630 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Total DALY:**

$$
DALY = YLL + YLD = 250 + 8.63 = 258.63 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
The project will cause an estimated **258.63 DALYs per year** of incremental disease burden in the affected population. At the WHO-recommended Indian reference value of **₹4,30,000 per DALY**, the annualized monetized loss is approximately **₹11.21 crores per year**. The EMP must therefore justify a comparable public health investment to render the project **net positive in health terms**.

---

**Solution to Part (b) — Role of EIA in Mitigating Water-Borne Disease Impact (7 Marks):**

**Role of EIA in Predicting Health Impacts (2 marks):**
EIA provides a **systematic, science-based, and legally mandated framework** for predicting the public health consequences of development projects before they are executed. Specifically:
- The **Screening and Scoping stages** identify whether the proposed project will alter water quality and quantity in the downstream region.
- The **Baseline Studies** stage generates epidemiological data (morbidity, mortality, age-specific attack rates) for the 10 km study area.
- The **Impact Prediction** stage uses **DALY, dose-response models, and water-quality modeling** to forecast the incremental disease burden.

**Role in Mitigation (2 marks):**
- The **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** legally binds the project proponent to install sewage treatment plants, water treatment infrastructure, and surveillance systems.
- The **Public Health Management Plan (PHMP)** under Schedule III of the 2020 EIA Amendment provides for **community health centres, vaccination programmes, and disease surveillance**.

**Indian Case Studies (3 marks):**

**Case Study 1 — Tehri Dam (Uttarakhand):**
The Tehri Hydro Project, an **260.5 m rock-fill dam** on the Bhagirathi River, submerged 30 villages and displaced over 1 lakh people. The EIA identified **risks of cholera, typhoid, and hepatitis** in the resettlement colonies. The mitigation included construction of a **20 MLD water treatment plant** and a **network of primary health centres**. Post-project monitoring revealed a **40% reduction in water-borne disease incidence** in rehabilitated villages, validating the EIA's mitigation recommendations.

**Case Study 2 — Sardar Sarovar Dam (Gujarat):**
The Sardar Sarovar Project on the Narmada River required **forced resettlement of 40,000 families**. The EIA documented elevated risk of **diarrhoeal diseases and malaria** in the canal-command areas due to waterlogging. The EMP mandated a **command area development programme** with integrated drainage, sanitation, and a **dedicated disease surveillance unit under the Narmada Control Authority**. The **Pre- and Post-Project Health Monitoring Reports** became model documents for subsequent Indian EIA practice.

**Conclusion:**
EIA is **not merely a regulatory formality** but a **public-health instrument** that, when rigorously executed, can transform large infrastructure projects from disease vectors into vehicles for **improved water security and disease control**. The success depends on the **institutional capacity, community participation, and post-clearance monitoring** — all of which are emphasized in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Water-Borne Disease Questions (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing "water-borne" with "water-based" or "water-washed"** — Examiners deduct **2 marks** outright if a student uses the WHO four-category framework incorrectly. Always quote the specific WHO category.
> 2. **Forgetting the day-to-year conversion in YLD** — Failing to divide illness duration by 365 is the **single most common arithmetic error** in DALY calculations, costing **1 full mark**.
> 3. **Citing E. coli as a pathogen instead of an indicator organism** — E. coli itself rarely causes diarrhoea in healthy hosts; it is a **microbial indicator**. Writing "E. coli causes cholera" is a factual error losing **1 mark**.
> 4. **Omitting the LRV/CT value** in treatment-based answers — Examiners expect numerical compliance targets (e.g., **CT \geq 0.5 mg·min/L**; **LRV \geq 4 for viruses**). Generic statements like "treat the water" lose marks.
> 5. **Confusing HAV with HBV** — Hepatitis **A** and **E** are water-borne (faecal–oral). Hepatitis **B, C, and D** are **blood-borne** and sexually transmitted — NOT water-borne. This is a **surgical differentiation** the examiner tests.
> 6. **Not citing the regulatory standard** — Always cite **BIS IS 10500:2012** (0 MPN/100 mL) or **CPCB bathing water standard** (≤100 MPN/100 mL) for full credit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Fire Revision for KTU 2024 ESE:**

**Core Definitions:**
- ✅ **Water-borne disease** = Pathogen transmitted via ingestion of contaminated water (faecal–oral).
- ✅ **Indicator organism** = *E. coli* (thermotolerant coliform) — NOT a pathogen itself.
- ✅ **DALY** = YLL + YLD; YLL = Deaths × Residual life; YLD = Incidence × DW × Duration (in years).

**Pathogen Categories & Examples:**
- ✅ **Bacterial:** Cholera (Vibrio), Typhoid (Salmonella), Dysentery (Shigella), Leptospirosis.
- ✅ **Viral:** Hepatitis A & E, Polio, Rotavirus.
- ✅ **Protozoan:** Amoebiasis, Giardiasis, Cryptosporidiosis.
- ✅ **Helminthic:** Ascariasis, Dracunculiasis (eradicated from India in 2000).

**Water Quality Standards (BIS IS 10500:2012):**
- ✅ Total Coliforms in drinking water = **0 MPN/100 mL**.
- ✅ E. coli in drinking water = **0 MPN/100 mL**.
- ✅ CPCB bathing water = Faecal Coliforms **\leq 100 MPN/100 mL**.

**Disinfection CT Values (Key Numbers):**
- ✅ Free chlorine residual at consumer end = **0.2 – 0.5 mg/L**.
- ✅ CT value for 99.99% inactivation of *E. coli* = **0.5 mg·min/L at pH 7, 25°C**.
- ✅ **Cryptosporidium** is **NOT killed by chlorine** — needs **UV or ultrafiltration**.

**EIA-Specific Process Integration:**
- ✅ Mentioned in **Schedule III** of the EIA Notification 2006.
- ✅ **PHMP** = Public Health Management Plan (mandatory deliverable).
- ✅ **CAT Plan** = Catchment Area Treatment Plan.
- ✅ **DALY-based cost-benefit** justification for mitigation budgets.

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ❌ Hepatitis B/C are **NOT** water-borne.
- ❌ Malaria is **water-related** (vector-borne), not water-borne.
- ❌ Dracunculiasis is **water-based** (uses *Cyclops* water flea).
- ❌ E. coli is an **indicator**, not a pathogen.

**Critical Reference Documents (Cited in EIA Reports):**
- 📘 BIS IS 10500:2012 — Drinking Water Specification.
- 📘 CPCB Water Quality Criteria — 1979 (revised 2008).
- 📘 WHO Guidelines for Drinking-Water Quality — 4th Edition, 2017.
- 📘 EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020).
- 📘 Central Public Health and Environmental Engineering Organisation (CPHEEO) Manual.

**Mnemonic for Disease Categories — "B-V-P-H" (Birds Visit Pretty Hills):**
**B**acteria → **V**iruses → **P**rotozoa → **H**elminths.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Water Quality Standards (IS 10500-2012)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Water Quality Standards (IS 10500-2012)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**IS 10500:2012** is the **Indian Standard for Drinking Water — Specification** (second revision) published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**. It prescribes the minimum requirements of drinking water quality, the methods of sampling, and the analytical test procedures to be adopted for assessing compliance of any water source intended for human consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Revision History**
> - First published: **1983** (IS 10500:1983)
> - First revision: **1991**
> - Second revision: **2003 / 2004**
> - Third revision: **2009**
> - Latest revision: **2012** (currently in force as per BIS)

The standard recognises that water quality requirements depend on the intended use and lays down **two-tier acceptance limits** for most parameters:

- **Desirable Limit (Acceptable Limit):** The ideal value at which no adverse health effects are observed over a lifetime of consumption. This is the *target* quality.
- **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL):** The upper limit that is acceptable only when no alternative source exists. Above this, the water is unfit for consumption.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why IS 10500:2012 matters in EIA:**
> In Environmental Impact Assessment studies, the baseline water quality of any project area (surface water, groundwater) is compared against IS 10500:2012 limits to:
> 1. Identify the **existing degree of contamination** of the receiving water body.
> 2. Predict the **residual impact** after project commissioning.
> 3. Design **mitigation measures** (effluent treatment plants, water purification systems).
> 4. Establish **monitoring protocols** as part of the Environmental Management Plan (EMP).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of **IS 10500:2012** as a **"Medical Report Card for Water."** Just as a doctor checks your blood for sugar, haemoglobin, and cholesterol to decide whether you are healthy, an environmental engineer "checks" water against this standard for things like pH, hardness, lead, and bacteria to decide whether it is *drinkable*.

The **two-tier system** (Desirable vs. Maximum Permissible) works like the traffic light system on roads:
- 🟢 **Desirable Limit** = "Green signal" — water is safe and ideal; no health risk even after lifetime consumption.
- 🟡 **Maximum Permissible Limit** = "Yellow signal" — water is acceptable only as a temporary compromise when no better source is available (e.g., during drought, in remote areas, or as an interim arrangement).
- 🔴 **Above MPL** = "Red signal" — water is **rejected**; mandatory treatment or alternative source is required.

Just like a doctor issues a *prescription* based on a report card, the EIA consultant issues **mitigation recommendations** (like installing an RO plant, defluoridation unit, or chlorination system) based on the deviation of measured parameters from IS 10500:2012 limits.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Metrics in IS 10500:2012:**
> - **pH range** = **6.5 to 8.5**
> - **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS)** Desirable limit = **500 mg/L**
> - **Total Hardness (as CaCO₃)** Desirable limit = **200 mg/L**
> - **Turbidity** = **1 NTU (max)**, 5 NTU (permissible)
> - **Total Coliforms** = **0 MPN / 100 mL** (must be *nil* in any sample)
> - **E. coli** = **0 per 100 mL** (must be *nil* in any sample)
> - **Temperature** is not specified (since ambient conditions vary).
> - **Sampling method**: Grab samples as per IS 3025 (Methods of Sampling and Test for Water and Wastewater).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Breakdown of IS 10500:2012

The standard is organised into the following logical components:

1. **Scope** – Defines drinking water suitable for direct human consumption (after treatment, where necessary).
2. **References** – Lists all related IS codes (IS 3025 for sampling/test methods, IS 2488 for bacteriological tests).
3. **Terminology** – Defines key terms (drinking water, palatable, potable, etc.).
4. **Requirements** – Categorised into:
   - **Table 1 – Biological and General Characteristics** (essential parameters)
   - **Table 2 – Substances Undesirable in Excessive Amounts** (parameters that affect health in the long term)
   - **Table 3 – Toxic Substances** (heavy metals and pesticides)
   - **Table 4 – Radioactive Substances**
5. **Sampling and Test Methods** – Specifies IS 3025 methods for physico-chemical analysis and IS 1622 / IS 5887 for microbiological analysis.
6. **Marking and Packing** (for bottled/packaged drinking water).

### 2.2 Parameter Classification — The "Why" and "How"

IS 10500:2012 classifies parameters into **four distinct categories** based on their *origin*, *health impact*, and *treatment feasibility*:

| Category | Type of Parameters | Examples | Why They Are Tested |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category 1: Organoleptic & Physical** | Taste, Odour, Colour, Turbidity, pH, TDS | pH 6.5–8.5; Turbidity ≤ 1 NTU | Affects *acceptability* — consumer will reject water even if it is safe. |
| **Category 2: General Parameters** | Total Hardness, Calcium, Magnesium, Iron, Chloride, Sulphate, Nitrate, Fluoride | Hardness 200 mg/L; Iron 0.3 mg/L; Nitrate 45 mg/L | Long-term exposure causes *health and aesthetic issues* (e.g., fluorosis, methaemoglobinaemia, scaling). |
| **Category 3: Toxic Substances** | Lead, Arsenic, Mercury, Cadmium, Chromium, Cyanide, Pesticides | Pb 0.01 mg/L; As 0.01 mg/L; Hg 0.001 mg/L | Cumulative **toxic effects** on kidneys, liver, nervous system, and cancer risk. |
| **Category 4: Radioactive Substances** | Gross alpha activity, Gross beta activity | Alpha 0.1 Bq/L; Beta 1.0 Bq/L | Causes **radiation exposure**, genetic mutations, and long-term cancer. |
| **Category 5: Microbiological** | Total Coliforms, E. coli, Faecal Coliforms | 0 MPN/100 mL | Direct cause of **waterborne diseases** (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis). |

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why two limits?"** — The Desirable Limit assumes a *theoretical ideal* source. The Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) acknowledges that in *real-world* Indian conditions (especially rural/remote areas), achieving the Desirable Limit may be economically or technically difficult. MPL is therefore a *pragmatic compromise*, not a health recommendation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

#### Table A — IS 10500:2012 — Biological & General Characteristics (Excerpt)

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Unit | Desirable Limit (Max) | Permissible Limit (Max, in absence of alternate source) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Colour | Hazen units | 5 | 15 |
| 2 | Odour | — | Unobjectionable | — |
| 3 | Taste | — | Agreeable | — |
| 4 | Turbidity | NTU | 1 | 5 |
| 5 | pH | — | 6.5 to 8.5 | No relaxation |
| 6 | Total Hardness (as CaCO₃) | mg/L | 200 | 600 |
| 7 | Iron (Fe) | mg/L | 0.3 | No relaxation |
| 8 | Chloride (Cl⁻) | mg/L | 250 | 1000 |
| 9 | Residual Free Chlorine | mg/L | 0.2 | 1.0 |
| 10 | Total Dissolved Solids (TDS) | mg/L | 500 | 2000 |
| 11 | Calcium (Ca) | mg/L | 75 | 200 |
| 12 | Magnesium (Mg) | mg/L | 30 | 100 |
| 13 | Copper (Cu) | mg/L | 0.05 | 1.5 |
| 14 | Manganese (Mn) | mg/L | 0.1 | 0.3 |
| 15 | Sulphate (SO₄²⁻) | mg/L | 200 | 400 |
| 16 | Nitrate (NO₃⁻) | mg/L | 45 | No relaxation |
| 17 | Fluoride (F⁻) | mg/L | 1.0 | 1.5 |
| 18 | Phenolic Compounds (as C₆H₅OH) | mg/L | 0.001 | 0.002 |
| 19 | Mercury (Hg) | mg/L | 0.001 | No relaxation |
| 20 | Cadmium (Cd) | mg/L | 0.003 | No relaxation |
| 21 | Selenium (Se) | mg/L | 0.01 | No relaxation |
| 22 | Arsenic (As) | mg/L | 0.01 | 0.05 |
| 23 | Lead (Pb) | mg/L | 0.01 | No relaxation |
| 24 | Zinc (Zn) | mg/L | 5 | 15 |
| 25 | Chromium (Cr⁶⁺) | mg/L | 0.05 | No relaxation |

#### Table B — IS 10500:2012 — Microbiological & Radioactive Parameters

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Unit | Limit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 26 | Total Coliforms | MPN / 100 mL | **0** in any 100 mL sample |
| 27 | E. coli | per 100 mL | **0** in any 100 mL sample |
| 28 | Gross Alpha activity | Bq/L | 0.1 |
| 29 | Gross Beta activity | Bq/L | 1.0 |

#### Table C — Key Derived Formulas Used in Water Analysis

| Formula | Expression | Application in IS 10500 Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Hardness (TH)** | $\text{TH (as CaCO}_3) = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | Reported as mg/L CaCO₃; compared to 200 mg/L (desirable). |
| **Total Alkalinity (TA)** | $\text{TA} = [\text{HCO}_3^-] + 2[\text{CO}_3^{2-}] + [\text{OH}^-]$ | Used to assess pH buffering; affects corrosion/scaling. |
| **Langelier Saturation Index (LSI)** | $\text{LSI} = \text{pH}_{\text{actual}} - \text{pH}_{\text{saturation}}$ | Predicts whether water is *scale-forming* (LSI>0) or *corrosive* (LSI<0). |
| **Electrical Conductivity (EC) ↔ TDS** | $\text{TDS (mg/L)} \approx 0.55 \text{ to } 0.7 \times \text{EC (\mu S/cm)}$ | Field estimation of TDS; compared to 500 mg/L. |
| **Chlorine Dose (Break-point chlorination)** | $\text{Cl}_2 \text{ dose} = \text{Demand} + 0.5 \text{ mg/L (residual)}$ | Ensures residual free chlorine 0.2–1.0 mg/L after contact. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Convention used in IS 10500:2012:**
> - **mg/L ≡ ppm** (parts per million) for solids.
> - **µg/L ≡ ppb** (parts per billion) for trace contaminants.
> - **NTU** (Nephelometric Turbidity Units) for turbidity.
> - **MPN/100 mL** (Most Probable Number) for coliforms.
> - **Bq/L** (Becquerel per litre) for radioactivity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **EIA practice**, IS 10500:2012 is the *baseline yardstick* in the following scenarios:

1. **Mining & Industrial Projects** — Effluents are tracked upstream and downstream; downstream water must not exceed IS 10500:2012 limits at the *intake* of any village water supply.
2. **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)** — Treated water reused for gardening/irrigation is checked against this standard for *fecal coliforms* and *residual chlorine*.
3. **Dam/Reservoir Projects** — Pre-impoundment water quality of the river is tested to predict reservoir water quality (stratification, eutrophication potential).
4. **Coastal & Port Projects** — Saltwater intrusion wells checked against **TDS (2000 mg/L max)** and **Chloride (1000 mg/L max)**.
5. **Landfill Sites** — Leachate impact on groundwater compared against the standard.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Favourite:** Questions often ask *"Why are two limits specified for most parameters in IS 10500?"* — Always answer with: *Desirable limit = target for safe lifetime consumption; Permissible limit = emergency compromise when no alternative source exists, valid only for a short period.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Total Hardness Calculation (Analytical Matrix)

**Problem:** A groundwater sample has the following ionic concentrations:
- Calcium (Ca²⁺) = 80 mg/L
- Magnesium (Mg²⁺) = 24 mg/L

Determine the **Total Hardness (as CaCO₃)** and state whether the water complies with IS 10500:2012.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

The standard formula for Total Hardness is:

$$\text{TH (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = 2.5 \times [\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12 \times [\text{Mg}^{2+}]$$

**Where:**
- 2.5 = Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ ÷ Equivalent weight of Ca = (50) ÷ (20) = 2.5
- 4.12 = Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ ÷ Equivalent weight of Mg = (50) ÷ (12.15) ≈ 4.12

**Step 1: Substitute the calcium contribution.**

$$\text{TH}_{\text{Ca}} = 2.5 \times 80 = 200 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: Substitute the magnesium contribution.**

$$\text{TH}_{\text{Mg}} = 4.12 \times 24 = 98.88 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Sum the two contributions to obtain Total Hardness.**

$$\text{TH}_{\text{Total}} = 200 + 98.88 = 298.88 \text{ mg/L (as CaCO}_3)$$

**Step 4: Compare with IS 10500:2012 limits.**

$$\text{Desirable Limit} = 200 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)} = 600 \text{ mg/L}$$

Since $200 < 298.88 < 600$:

> [!WARNING]
> **Inference:** The water **EXCEEDS the Desirable Limit** but lies **WITHIN the Permissible Limit** of IS 10500:2012. Hence, the water is *acceptable in the absence of an alternative source* but **not ideal** for drinking. **Recommended action:** Domestic ion-exchange water softener OR blending with low-hardness water.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Field Estimation of TDS from Electrical Conductivity

**Problem:** A surface water sample shows an EC of **1200 µS/cm** at 25 °C. Estimate the TDS using the empirical relation, and comment on compliance with IS 10500:2012.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

The empirical conversion is:

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = K_E \times \text{EC (\mu S/cm)}$$

**Step 1: Choose the conversion factor $K_E$.**

For natural fresh waters, the typical $K_E$ ranges from **0.55 to 0.70**. Use the standard BIS-accepted value:

$$K_E = 0.64$$

**Step 2: Compute TDS.**

$$\text{TDS} = 0.64 \times 1200 = 768 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 3: Compare with IS 10500:2012 limits.**

$$\text{Desirable Limit} = 500 \text{ mg/L}$$

$$\text{Maximum Permissible Limit} = 2000 \text{ mg/L}$$

Since $500 < 768 < 2000$:

> [!NOTE]
> **Inference:** TDS **exceeds the Desirable Limit** of 500 mg/L but is well within the MPL of 2000 mg/L. The water is *acceptable as a short-term source*. **Recommended action:** RO treatment for long-term domestic supply.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Break-Point Chlorination Dose Calculation

**Problem:** A water treatment plant handles 10 MLD (Million Litres per Day). The chlorine demand of the raw water is **1.2 mg/L**. Calculate the required chlorine dose to maintain a residual free chlorine of **0.5 mg/L** at the consumer end.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

The standard break-point chlorination equation is:

$$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ Dose} = \text{Chlorine Demand} + \text{Required Residual}$$

**Step 1: Substitute the known values.**

$$\text{Cl}_2 \text{ Dose} = 1.2 + 0.5 = 1.7 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 2: Convert dose to kg/day for the plant capacity.**

$$\text{Chlorine required} = 1.7 \text{ mg/L} \times 10 \times 10^6 \text{ L/day}$$

Converting mg to kg (1 mg = $10^{-6}$ kg):

$$\text{Chlorine required} = 1.7 \times 10 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-6} = 17 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Verify compliance with IS 10500:2012.**

$$\text{Residual Free Chlorine Limit} = 0.2 \text{ mg/L (min, desirable) to } 1.0 \text{ mg/L (max, permissible)}$$

Since $0.2 \leq 0.5 \leq 1.0$: ✅ **Compliant.**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Trick:** If a question asks *"Why is residual free chlorine maintained?"* — answer: *To ensure disinfection continues throughout the distribution network, preventing microbial regrowth in pipelines.*

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Problem Solving

| Parameter | Formula | IS 10500 Desirable | IS 10500 MPL | Common Mitigation if Exceeded |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Total Hardness | $2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ | 200 mg/L | 600 mg/L | Ion-exchange softening; lime-soda process |
| TDS | $0.64 \times \text{EC}$ | 500 mg/L | 2000 mg/L | RO; nanofiltration |
| Residual Chlorine | Dose − Demand | 0.2 mg/L | 1.0 mg/L | Adjust chlorinator dose |
| Fluoride | Direct measurement | 1.0 mg/L | 1.5 mg/L | Nalgonda / activated alumina defluoridation |
| Nitrate | Direct measurement | 45 mg/L | No relaxation | Ion exchange; RO (Note: Boiling concentrates NO₃⁻) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture of IS 10500:2012 — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block depicts the **logical structure** of IS 10500:2012, mapping how the standard organises its quality requirements into hierarchical categories.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[IS 10500:2012 Drinking Water Standard] --> B[Scope and Definitions]
    A --> C[Quality Requirements]
    A --> D[Sampling and Test Methods]
    A --> E[Marking and Packing]
    C --> F[Table 1: Biological and General Characteristics]
    C --> G[Table 2: Substances Undesirable in Excess]
    C --> H[Table 3: Toxic Substances]
    C --> I[Table 4: Radioactive Substances]
    F --> F1[Organoleptic Parameters]
    F --> F2[General Chemical Parameters]
    F --> F3[Microbiological Parameters]
    F1 --> F1a[Colour 5 Hazen]
    F1 --> F1b[Turbidity 1 NTU]
    F1 --> F1c[pH 6.5 to 8.5]
    F1 --> F1d[Odour Unobjectionable]
    F1 --> F1e[Taste Agreeable]
    F2 --> F2a[Total Hardness 200 mg per L]
    F2 --> F2b[TDS 500 mg per L]
    F2 --> F2c[Chloride 250 mg per L]
    F2 --> F2d[Iron 0.3 mg per L]
    F2 --> F2e[Sulphate 200 mg per L]
    F3 --> F3a[Total Coliforms 0 MPN per 100 mL]
    F3 --> F3b[E coli 0 per 100 mL]
    G --> G1[General Parameters Affecting Long Term Health]
    H --> H1[Heavy Metals and Pesticides]
    H1 --> H1a[Lead 0.01 mg per L]
    H1 --> H1b[Arsenic 0.01 mg per L]
    H1 --> H1c[Mercury 0.001 mg per L]
    I --> I1[Gross Alpha 0.1 Bq per L]
    I --> I2[Gross Beta 1.0 Bq per L]
    D --> J[IS 3025 Physical and Chemical Methods]
    D --> K[IS 1622 and IS 5887 Microbiological Methods]
    E --> L[For Packaged Drinking Water]
```

### 4.2 EIA Application Flow — Mermaid Process Diagram

The following diagram shows **how IS 10500:2012 is integrated into the EIA cycle** for a typical infrastructure project.

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Project Proposal] --> P2[Baseline Water Quality Study]
    P2 --> P3[Sample Collection from Surface and Ground Water Sources]
    P3 --> P4[Laboratory Analysis using IS 3025 Methods]
    P4 --> P5[Compare Results with IS 10500 2012 Limits]
    P5 --> P6{Does Water Meet Desirable Limits?}
    P6 -- Yes --> P7[Status: No Significant Impact]
    P6 -- No --> P8[Status: Significant Impact Identified]
    P8 --> P9[Predict Post Project Water Quality]
    P9 --> P10{Will Project Degrade Water Below MPL?}
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Design Mitigation Measures]
    P10 -- No --> P12[Monitor and Document]
    P11 --> P13[Effluent Treatment Plant Design]
    P11 --> P14[Storm Water Management Plan]
    P11 --> P15[Groundwater Recharge Measures]
    P13 --> P16[Environmental Management Plan EMP]
    P14 --> P16
    P15 --> P16
    P12 --> P16
    P16 --> P17[Periodic Monitoring as per Consent Conditions]
    P17 --> P18[Compliance Reporting to SPCB and CPCB]
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic — Desirable vs. Permissible — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1[Parameter Measured] --> Q2[Compare with IS 10500:2012 Desirable Limit]
    Q2 --> R1{Within Desirable Limit?}
    R1 -- Yes --> R2[Status: SAFE — No Action Needed]
    R1 -- No --> R3[Compare with Permissible Limit]
    R3 --> R4{Within Permissible Limit?}
    R4 -- Yes --> R5[Status: CONDITIONALLY ACCEPTABLE — Short Term Use]
    R4 -- No --> R6[Status: UNFIT — Mandatory Treatment or Alternative Source]
    R5 --> R7[Recommend Domestic Point of Use Treatment]
    R6 --> R8[Full Scale Treatment Plant Required]
```

### 4.4 Sampling Protocol Schematic (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Since physical drawings of sampling apparatus cannot be rendered in plain text, the following **block-level functional architecture** maps the sampling-to-compliance workflow:

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Site Identification] --> B[Selection of Sampling Points]
    B --> C[Sampling Frequency Planning]
    C --> D[Grab Sample Collection per IS 3025 Part 1]
    D --> E[Preservation using Standard Reagents]
    E --> F[Transport under Cold Chain 4 degree Celsius]
    F --> G[Laboratory Registration]
    G --> H[Physico Chemical Analysis as per IS 3025]
    G --> I[Microbiological Analysis as per IS 1622]
    H --> J[Data Validation and Quality Control]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Comparison with IS 10500:2012 Limits]
    K --> L[Generation of Compliance Report]
    L --> M[Submission to EIA Authority and SPCB]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Aid Note for Students:** When drawing this flow in your answer sheet, always use **rectangular boxes for process steps** and **diamond/rhombus shapes for decision points**. The final output must clearly link to IS 10500:2012 limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State any three salient features of IS 10500:2012. Mention the two-tier acceptance criteria defined in the standard.*

**Model Answer:**

IS 10500:2012 is the **Indian Standard for Drinking Water — Specification** published by the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)**.

**Salient features:**
1. It specifies the **minimum requirements** of drinking water quality intended for human consumption, including physico-chemical, microbiological, and radiological parameters.
2. It is structured into **four tables** — (i) Biological and General Characteristics, (ii) Substances Undesirable in Excessive Amounts, (iii) Toxic Substances, and (iv) Radioactive Substances.
3. It prescribes the **methods of sampling and testing** as per IS 3025 (physico-chemical) and IS 1622 / IS 5887 (microbiological).

**Two-tier acceptance criteria:**
- **Desirable Limit (Acceptable Limit):** The value below which water is safe for lifetime consumption with no adverse health effect — the *target quality*.
- **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL):** The relaxed upper limit applicable only when no alternative source is available; the *pragmatic compromise*.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1.5 marks for salient features + 1.5 marks for the two-tier criteria.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) specified for some parameters in IS 10500:2012, even though the Desirable Limit is the recommended target? Justify with two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL)** is specified to account for **real-world constraints** in India, such as:
- Non-availability of pristine water sources in arid, semi-arid, or industrial regions.
- Economic/technical limitations of small municipalities and rural water supply schemes.

Under such conditions, the MPL acts as a **pragmatic upper limit** for short-term consumption, *not a health recommendation*.

**Examples:**
1. **Total Hardness:** Desirable = 200 mg/L; MPL = 600 mg/L — accepted in hard-water regions (e.g., Rajasthan, Gujarat) where ion-exchange plants are economically unviable at community scale.
2. **TDS:** Desirable = 500 mg/L; MPL = 2000 mg/L — accepted in coastal aquifers affected by saltwater intrusion, where desalination plants are not yet established.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 1.5 marks for justification + 1.5 marks for examples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> As per **KTU ESE (End Semester Evaluation) Module Internal Choice rule**, the following two questions are presented as independent alternatives. **Either A or B** will appear in the exam.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the **architecture of IS 10500:2012** and list the **parameters grouped under each table**. Comment on why **microbiological parameters are given the most stringent limits**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A groundwater sample from a village in Palakkad district has the following characteristics:
- pH = 7.2
- Calcium (Ca²⁺) = 100 mg/L
- Magnesium (Mg²⁺) = 30 mg/L
- Chloride (Cl⁻) = 280 mg/L
- Iron (Fe) = 0.4 mg/L
- Nitrate (NO₃⁻) = 50 mg/L
- Fluoride (F⁻) = 1.6 mg/L

Evaluate the water quality against **IS 10500:2012** limits and recommend **appropriate treatment methods** for the parameters that exceed the limits. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a):**

The **architecture of IS 10500:2012** is organised as shown below:

**Table 1 — Biological and General Characteristics** (≈ 25 parameters)
- Organoleptic: Colour, Odour, Taste, Turbidity
- General chemical: pH, TDS, Total Hardness, Iron, Chloride, Sulphate, Calcium, Magnesium, etc.
- Microbiological: Total Coliforms, E. coli, Faecal Coliforms

**Table 2 — Substances Undesirable in Excessive Amounts**
- Fluoride, Nitrate, Phenolic compounds, Mineral oil, etc.

**Table 3 — Toxic Substances**
- Lead, Arsenic, Mercury, Cadmium, Chromium, Selenium, Cyanide, Pesticides

**Table 4 — Radioactive Substances**
- Gross alpha activity (≤ 0.1 Bq/L), Gross beta activity (≤ 1.0 Bq/L)

**Schematic:** (Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes.)

**Why microbiological parameters are most stringent:**
- Microbial contamination is the **direct cause of waterborne epidemics** (cholera, typhoid, hepatitis A/E, dysentery) and can cause **outbreaks within hours** of consumption.
- Unlike chemical contaminants, microbes **reproduce** in the distribution system if not controlled.
- The standard mandates **0 MPN/100 mL** for *both* Total Coliforms and E. coli in *every* 100 mL sample, with **no permissible relaxation** — reflecting the **zero-tolerance** policy for pathogens in drinking water.
- Chemical contaminants usually cause *chronic* effects over years; microbial contamination causes *acute* illness or death within days.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 3 marks for schematic + 2 marks for parameter grouping + 2 marks for microbiological justification.

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b):**

**Step 1: Compute Total Hardness.**

$$\text{TH} = 2.5 \times 100 + 4.12 \times 30 = 250 + 123.6 = 373.6 \text{ mg/L (as CaCO}_3)$$

**Step 2: Tabulate and compare each parameter with IS 10500:2012 limits.**

| Parameter | Value Measured | Desirable Limit | MPL | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| pH | 7.2 | 6.5–8.5 | — | ✅ Within |
| Total Hardness | 373.6 mg/L | 200 | 600 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |
| Chloride | 280 mg/L | 250 | 1000 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |
| Iron | 0.4 mg/L | 0.3 | No relaxation | ❌ Exceeds desirable; No relaxation |
| Nitrate | 50 mg/L | 45 | No relaxation | ❌ Exceeds desirable; No relaxation |
| Fluoride | 1.6 mg/L | 1.0 | 1.5 | ❌ Exceeds both desirable AND MPL |

**Step 3: Recommend treatment methods.**

| Exceeded Parameter | Recommended Treatment |
|---|---|
| Total Hardness (373.6 mg/L) | **Lime-Soda process** at community scale, OR **domestic ion-exchange softener**. |
| Chloride (280 mg/L) | **Reverse Osmosis (RO)** for long-term; acceptable as-is for short term. |
| Iron (0.4 mg/L) | **Aeration followed by filtration** OR **Oxidation-Filtration with manganese greensand**. |
| Nitrate (50 mg/L) | **Ion exchange with anion resin**, OR **RO** (NOTE: Boiling will *concentrate* NO₃⁻, so do not boil). |
| Fluoride (1.6 mg/L) | **Nalgonda process** (lime + alum) OR **Activated alumina adsorption** OR **RO**. |

**Step 4: Conclude.**

The water is **unfit for direct consumption** as Fluoride and Nitrate have no relaxation beyond desirable limits. **A multi-stage treatment plant** combining RO with defluoridation and denitrification units is recommended. An alternative safe source (deep borewell or surface water from a protected catchment) should be explored.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 2 marks for computation + 2 marks for tabulation + 3 marks for treatment recommendations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **significance of IS 10500:2012 in Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) studies**. Discuss with examples how it is used in **(i) baseline data collection, (ii) impact prediction, and (iii) mitigation design** for a typical industrial project. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coastal village reports **saline water intrusion** in its borewells. The borewell water has:
- TDS = 1850 mg/L
- Chloride = 950 mg/L
- Total Hardness = 580 mg/L
- pH = 7.4
- Sulphate = 380 mg/L

Assess compliance with **IS 10500:2012**. If non-compliant, propose **a two-stage treatment scheme** with justifications. *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a):**

**Significance of IS 10500:2012 in EIA:**
IS 10500:2012 serves as the **statutory benchmark** for evaluating drinking water quality in EIA reports. It provides the *reference values* against which *measured* and *predicted* water quality are compared. Without it, the EIA report cannot objectively determine whether a project's impact is "significant" or "negligible."

**(i) Baseline Data Collection:**
- During the *pre-project* phase, surface water and groundwater samples are collected from multiple locations (intake points, villages, sensitive receptors) within a **10 km radius** of the project.
- All samples are analysed as per **IS 3025** (physico-chemical) and **IS 1622** (microbiological) methods.
- Results are tabulated and compared with IS 10500:2012 to identify **baseline deviations** (e.g., already-elevated fluoride, indicating geological contamination).
- *Example:* In a thermal power plant EIA, baseline fluoride in nearby groundwater of **2.5 mg/L** (already exceeding desirable limit) is documented; this baseline becomes the *reference* for impact assessment.

**(ii) Impact Prediction:**
- A **mathematical model** (e.g., Domenico's model for groundwater, Streeter-Phelps for surface water) predicts the **post-project pollutant concentrations**.
- Predicted values are then compared with IS 10500:2012 limits to assess **future compliance**.
- *Example:* For a textile industry discharging treated effluent, the **downstream mixing zone concentration** of chromium is predicted using a **1-D advection-dispersion equation** and compared with the IS 10500:2012 limit of **0.05 mg/L**.

**(iii) Mitigation Design:**
- If predicted concentrations exceed IS 10500:2012 limits, **mitigation measures** are designed.
- These include: **Effluent Treatment Plants (ETPs)**, **Sewage Treatment Plants (STPs)**, **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** systems, **rainwater harvesting**, and **green belts** to prevent runoff.
- *Example:* A chlor-alkali plant EIA recommends a **mercury-cell conversion to membrane-cell technology** to bring mercury discharge from 50 µg/L down to <1 µg/L, ensuring compliance with the IS 10500:2012 mercury limit of **0.001 mg/L (= 1 µg/L)**.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 2 marks for significance + 1.5 marks for baseline + 1.5 marks for prediction + 2 marks for mitigation.

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b):**

**Step 1: Tabulate and compare with IS 10500:2012.**

| Parameter | Measured Value | Desirable Limit | MPL | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| TDS | 1850 mg/L | 500 | 2000 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |
| Chloride | 950 mg/L | 250 | 1000 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |
| Total Hardness | 580 mg/L | 200 | 600 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |
| pH | 7.4 | 6.5–8.5 | — | ✅ Within |
| Sulphate | 380 mg/L | 200 | 400 | ⚠️ Exceeds desirable; within MPL |

**Step 2: Inference.**

All four parameters exceed the **Desirable Limit** but remain **within the Permissible Limit**. Hence, water is *conditionally acceptable for short-term domestic use* but **not suitable** for long-term consumption (TDS > 1500 mg/L is associated with cardiovascular and gastrointestinal complaints).

**Step 3: Propose a two-stage treatment scheme.**

| Stage | Process | Target Contaminant Removal | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Stage 1: Pre-treatment** | **Multimedia Filtration (Sand + Gravel + Anthracite)** | Removes suspended solids, turbidity | Protects RO membranes from fouling. |
| **Stage 2: Reverse Osmosis (RO)** | Thin-Film Composite (TFC) membrane at 10–15 bar | Removes 95–99% of TDS, chloride, sulphate, hardness | Brings TDS from 1850 to < 100 mg/L; ensures compliance with IS 10500:2012. |
| **Optional: Post-treatment** | Calcite contactor / Remineralisation | Re-introduces calcium for taste | RO water is *aggressive* (low TDS); remineralisation prevents pipe corrosion. |

**Step 4: Additional recommendations.**
- **Source control:** Construct **sub-surface barrier wells** upstream of the affected zone to prevent further saltwater intrusion.
- **Alternative source:** Connect the village to a **regional surface water supply scheme** (e.g., Jal Jeevan Mission pipeline from a distant reservoir).
- **Rainwater harvesting:** Mandate rooftop harvesting to recharge the freshwater lens.

> **Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):** 2 marks for tabulation + 2 marks for compliance inference + 3 marks for two-stage treatment scheme.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Conflating mg/L with ppm unnecessarily** — *Both are equivalent for dilute aqueous solutions*, but examiners expect **mg/L** as per IS 10500:2012 convention. Using ppm alone may cost 0.5 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the "No relaxation" rule** — Several parameters (Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, Nitrate, Iron, pH) have **NO permissible relaxation** in IS 10500:2012. If a student says *"pH 7.9 is within the permissible limit of 8.5"*, they will lose marks; the correct answer is *"pH 7.9 is within the desirable range of 6.5–8.5"* — there is no MPL for pH.
>
> 3. **Confusing Hardness formulas** — The correct coefficients are **2.5 for Ca²⁺** and **4.12 for Mg²⁺**, not 2.0 and 4.0. Students often write **TH = 2.5[Ca] + 4.1[Mg]**, which is acceptable, but **TH = Ca + Mg (raw addition)** is a fatal error.
>
> 4. **Misidentifying the standard year** — The current version is **2012**; some textbooks still cite **2004 / 2009**. Always quote **IS 10500:2012 (second/third revision)** in answers.
>
> 5. **Not linking EIA to IS 10500** — When answering EIA questions, students often list the parameters but **fail to explicitly state "compared with the Desirable Limit of IS 10500:2012"**. This is the most common 1-mark loss.
>
> 6. **Wrong units for radioactivity** — Use **Bq/L**, not Ci/L or counts per minute. $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Top 25 High-Yield Points for IS 10500:2012 — Last-Minute Revision:**
>
> 1. **IS 10500:2012** = Indian Standard for **Drinking Water Specification** by **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards).
> 2. Currently in **4th revision (2012)**; first published 1983.
> 3. **Two-tier limits:** Desirable (target) & Permissible (compromise).
> 4. **pH range** is **6.5 to 8.5** (NO permissible relaxation).
> 5. **Total Hardness:** Desirable **200 mg/L** (as CaCO₃); MPL **600 mg/L**.
> 6. **TDS:** Desirable **500 mg/L**; MPL **2000 mg/L**.
> 7. **Turbidity:** Desirable **1 NTU** (max); MPL **5 NTU** (max).
> 8. **Colour:** Desirable **5 Hazen units**; MPL **15 Hazen units**.
> 9. **Iron:** Desirable **0.3 mg/L**; **NO relaxation** allowed.
> 10. **Chloride:** Desirable **250 mg/L**; MPL **1000 mg/L**.
> 11. **Fluoride:** Desirable **1.0 mg/L**; MPL **1.5 mg/L** (prevents fluorosis).
> 12. **Nitrate:** Desirable **45 mg/L**; **NO relaxation** (prevents methaemoglobinaemia).
> 13. **Lead:** **0.01 mg/L** (no relaxation).
> 14. **Arsenic:** Desirable **0.01 mg/L**; MPL **0.05 mg/L** (prevents skin/lung cancer).
> 15. **Mercury:** **0.001 mg/L** (no relaxation).
> 16. **Cadmium:** **0.003 mg/L** (no relaxation).
> 17. **Chromium (Cr⁶⁺):** **0.05 mg/L** (no relaxation).
> 18. **Total Coliforms & E. coli:** Must be **0 MPN / 100 mL** in **every sample** (zero tolerance).
> 19. **Gross Alpha activity:** **0.1 Bq/L**.
> 20. **Gross Beta activity:** **1.0 Bq/L**.
> 21. **Residual Free Chlorine:** Desirable **0.2 mg/L**; MPL **1.0 mg/L**.
> 22. **Sampling methods:** IS 3025 (physico-chemical) and IS 1622/IS 5887 (microbiological).
> 23. **TH Formula:** $\text{TH} = 2.5[\text{Ca}^{2+}] + 4.12[\text{Mg}^{2+}]$ (mg/L as CaCO₃).
> 24. **TDS-EC relation:** $\text{TDS} = 0.64 \times \text{EC}$ (BIS convention).
> 25. **In EIA:** IS 10500:2012 is the **statutory benchmark** for baseline, prediction, mitigation, and monitoring.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for remembering the 5 categories:**
> **"O-G-T-R-M"** = **O**rganoleptic, **G**eneral, **T**oxic, **R**adioactive, **M**icrobiological.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solid Waste: Classification and Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Solid Waste: Classification and Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid Waste (KTU 2024 Official Definition):**
> Solid waste refers to any discarded or abandoned material in solid or semi-solid form, arising from human and animal activities, that is no longer of immediate use to the owner. As per the regulatory framework referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus, it includes municipal solid waste (MSW), industrial waste, biomedical waste, e-waste, construction & demolition (C&D) waste, and hazardous waste.

The **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** is specifically defined under the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** (and subsequent 2016 amendments in India) as:
> "MSW includes commercial and residential wastes generated in a municipal or notified area in either solid or semi-solid form, excluding industrial hazardous wastes but including treated biomedical wastes."

**Key Standard Metrics (KTU Board-Expected Values):**

- **Per capita waste generation in India:** 0.21–0.62 kg/person/day (urban areas average **0.45 kg/person/day**).
- **Density of MSW (loose):** 200–500 kg/m³.
- **Moisture content:** 40–60% (in tropical Indian climate).
- **Calorific value:** 800–2000 kcal/kg (Indian MSW typical).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of solid waste like the "byproduct of a city's metabolism"** — just as a human body generates waste (CO₂, urine, food scraps) that must be managed to stay healthy, a city generates a constant stream of solid waste from homes, offices, and factories. If waste is not classified properly (segregated), it becomes toxic and hazardous, much like mixing different medications in one pill bottle can cause harmful reactions. The first step in any waste management chain is always **IDENTIFICATION → CLASSIFICATION → SOURCE MAPPING**.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in EIA

In an **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, solid waste classification is foundational because:

1. It determines the **toxicity**, **treatment cost**, and **disposal methodology**.
2. It influences the **mitigation measures** in the EIA report.
3. It identifies **polluter-pays** liabilities under Indian law.
4. It helps predict **long-term environmental impacts** (groundwater contamination, air pollution from open burning, etc.).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners love asking *"Why is classification the first step in solid waste management?"* The answer must always include the line: *"Because the physico-chemical characteristics of waste dictate the collection, treatment, and disposal technology to be adopted."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Solid Waste (Hierarchical Framework)

Solid waste is classified along **three primary dimensions**: **Source**, **Type (Physical/Chemical)**, and **Hazard Potential**.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **SOURCE** (Most Frequently Asked in KTU)

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Typical Examples** |
|---|---|---|
| **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW)** | Waste from households, offices, schools, streets. | Food waste, paper, plastics, glass. |
| **Industrial Solid Waste** | Waste from manufacturing, processing, refineries. | Slag, fly ash, red mud, chemical sludge. |
| **Biomedical Waste (BMW)** | Waste from hospitals, labs, blood banks. | Needles, human tissue, expired drugs. |
| **Hazardous Waste** | Waste with toxic, reactive, ignitable properties. | Pesticides, heavy metals, solvents. |
| **E-Waste** | Discarded electronic/electrical equipment. | Phones, computers, batteries, PCBs. |
| **Construction & Demolition (C&D) Waste** | Waste from building construction/demolition. | Concrete, bricks, wood, metals, dust. |
| **Radioactive Waste** | Waste containing radioactive isotopes. | Spent fuel rods, contaminated gloves. |
| **Agricultural Waste** | Waste from farming, animal husbandry. | Crop residues, manure, animal carcasses. |
| **Sewage Sludge** | Semi-solid residue from wastewater treatment. | Biosolids, grit, scum. |
| **Mining Waste** | Waste from mining and quarrying. | Tailings, overburden, mine water sludge. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Per the latest KTU syllabus, **E-Waste** and **C&D Waste** have been given **separate standalone classifications** (they were earlier grouped under industrial waste). Be sure to mention them as distinct categories in answers.

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **TYPE (Physical Nature)**

- **Organic / Biodegradable:** Food waste, yard waste, paper, wood, leather.
- **Inorganic / Non-Biodegradable:** Plastics, glass, metals, ceramics, rubber.
- **Composite:** Tetra packs, multi-layered plastics, e-waste.

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **HAZARD POTENTIAL**

- **General / Non-Hazardous:** MSW, most C&D waste, agricultural waste.
- **Hazardous:** Industrial chemicals, biomedical waste, e-waste, radioactive waste.

## 2.2 Sources of Solid Waste (Origin Tracing)

### 2.2.1 Primary Sources (KTU Mandatory List)

1. **Residential Sources** (40–60% of MSW by weight in Indian cities)
2. **Commercial Sources** (markets, hotels, offices)
3. **Institutional Sources** (schools, hospitals, government buildings)
4. **Construction & Demolition Sites**
5. **Municipal Services** (street sweeping, parks, drain cleaning)
6. **Industrial Premises**
7. **Healthcare Facilities**
8. **Agricultural Fields & Farms**

### 2.2.2 Secondary Source Mapping (Engineering View)

| **Source** | **Dominant Waste Type** | **Pollution Pathway** |
|---|---|---|
| Households | Food waste, paper, plastic | Leachate → groundwater |
| Markets | Vegetable waste, plastic bags | Methane emission |
| Hospitals | BMW | Infection + toxic chemicals |
| Industries | Sludge, chemicals | Soil + air contamination |
| Construction sites | Concrete, dust | Particulate air pollution |
| EOL Electronics | E-waste | Heavy metal leaching |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the "cheat sheet" KTU expects you to memorize for Part A (3-mark) numerical questions.**

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Waste Generation | $PC = \frac{Total\ Waste\ Generated}{Population \times Time}$ | kg/person/day | Estimating city-level waste |
| Waste Density | $\rho = \frac{Mass}{Volume}$ | kg/m³ | Landfill design capacity |
| Total Volume of Landfill | $V = \frac{M \times n}{\rho \times C}$ | m³ | Landfill sizing |
| Waste Generation Rate | $R = \frac{P \times PC \times 365}{1000}$ | tonnes/year | Annual MSW projection |
| Compaction Ratio | $CR = \frac{Initial\ Volume}{Final\ Volume}$ | dimensionless | Landfill compaction efficiency |
| Moisture Content | $MC = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | Biodegradability indicator |

Where: $M$ = total mass, $n$ = number of years, $C$ = cover material fraction (0.2 typical), $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used in **landfill site design**, **composting plant capacity planning**, and **waste-to-energy (WTE) plant feasibility studies**. Municipal corporations use the *Per Capita* formula to forecast MSW for 25-year landfill design horizons.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Per Capita Waste Generation (PC)

**Starting Premise (Given Data):**
A city has a population $P$ and generates $T$ tonnes of solid waste per day. Each individual generates waste at a uniform rate.

**Step 1 — Define Total Waste Generated in Time Period:**
Let the total waste generated in 1 day be $W_{day}$ measured in kg/day.

$$W_{day} = P \times PC$$

where $PC$ is the per capita generation in kg/person/day.

**Step 2 — Convert to Per Capita Form:**
Dividing both sides by $P$:

$$\frac{W_{day}}{P} = \frac{P \times PC}{P} = PC$$

$$\therefore PC = \frac{W_{day}}{P}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** for PC.

**Step 3 — Convert to Annual Projection (Common KTU Numerical):**
Multiply by 365 days and divide by 1000 to convert kg → tonnes.

$$R = \frac{P \times PC \times 365}{1000} \quad [\text{tonnes/year}]$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2024 Style):**

> **Problem:** A city of population **8,50,000** generates MSW at **0.45 kg/capita/day**. Find the annual waste generation in tonnes.

**Given:**
- $P = 850{,}000$ persons
- $PC = 0.45$ kg/person/day

**Solution using the formula:**

$$R = \frac{P \times PC \times 365}{1000}$$

$$R = \frac{850{,}000 \times 0.45 \times 365}{1000}$$

**Step-by-step multiplication:**

$$850{,}000 \times 0.45 = 382{,}500$$

$$382{,}500 \times 365 = 139{,}612{,}500$$

$$R = \frac{139{,}612{,}500}{1000} = 139{,}612.5 \text{ tonnes/year}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 1.40 \times 10^5 \text{ tonnes/year}}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity Check (KTU Examiner Trick):**
Daily waste = $850{,}000 \times 0.45 = 382{,}500$ kg/day $= 382.5$ tonnes/day.
Annual = $382.5 \times 365 = 1{,}39{,}612.5$ tonnes ✓ **Matches.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Landfill Volume Calculation

**Step 1 — Mass of Compacted Waste over n Years:**

$$M_{total} = P \times PC \times 365 \times n \quad [\text{kg}]$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Volume Using Density:**

$$V_{waste} = \frac{M_{total}}{\rho}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Cover Material (Soil Layer, ~20% of volume):**
The Landfill is divided into cells; each cell has a daily cover. Total volume becomes:

$$V_{landfill} = \frac{M_{total}}{\rho \times (1 - f_c)} \quad \text{or} \quad V = \frac{M \times n}{\rho \times C}$$

where $C = 1 - f_c$, and $f_c$ is the cover soil fraction.

**Step 4 — Apply Lift Factor:**
If daily lifts are 2.5 m high with intermediate cover of 0.3 m:

$$V_{final} = \frac{V_{waste}}{LF}$$

where $LF = \frac{\text{depth of waste}}{\text{depth of waste + cover}} = \frac{2.5}{2.8} \approx 0.89$.

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A city of 5,00,000 population, $PC = 0.5$ kg/day, $\rho = 400$ kg/m³, planning horizon $n = 20$ years, $f_c = 0.2$. Calculate landfill volume.

**Solution:**

$$M = 500{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 365 \times 20$$

$$M = 500{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 7300 = 1.825 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}$$

$$V = \frac{1.825 \times 10^9}{400 \times 0.8} = \frac{1.825 \times 10^9}{320}$$

$$\boxed{V = 5.703 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 = 5.7 \text{ million m}^3}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Waste Composition (Indian Cities)

| **Component** | **% by Weight (India Avg.)** | **Biodegradable?** | **Recyclable?** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Organic (food, yard) | 40–60% | Yes | Composting |
| Paper & Cardboard | 8–12% | Yes | High value |
| Plastics | 8–15% | No | Yes |
| Glass | 4–6% | No | Yes |
| Metals | 1–3% | No | Yes |
| Inert (stones, dust) | 15–25% | No | Limited |
| Textiles, leather, rubber | 4–6% | Partial | Limited |
| Hazardous (batteries, etc.) | < 1% | No | Specialized |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Expectation:** In a typical "composition" question, students are asked to identify which fraction is the **largest** → Answer: **Organic/Biodegradable waste (40–60%)**, and the management option is **composting / anaerobic digestion**.

## 3.4 Derivation 3: Moisture Content (MC)

**Step 1 — Concept:** Moisture content affects biodegradation, calorific value, and landfill leachate.

**Step 2 — Formula:**

$$MC(\%) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

**Step 3 — Numerical:**
Wet weight $W_w = 250$ g, oven-dried weight $W_d = 120$ g.

$$MC = \frac{250 - 120}{250} \times 100 = \frac{130}{250} \times 100 = 52\%$$

$$\boxed{MC = 52\%}$$

This indicates **high moisture**, typical of Indian MSW, making **incineration inefficient** (waste must be dried first).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
