<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**"Classify solid waste based on source with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer (Expected 3-Mark Structure):**

Solid waste is classified into the following source-based categories:

1. **Municipal Solid Waste (MSW):** Generated from households, offices, streets. Example: food waste, paper.
2. **Industrial Solid Waste:** From manufacturing and processing units. Example: fly ash, slag, sludge.
3. **Biomedical Waste (BMW):** From hospitals and laboratories. Example: human tissue, sharps.
4. **E-Waste:** From discarded electronic equipment. Example: mobile phones, PCBs.
5. **Construction & Demolition Waste:** From building sites. Example: concrete debris, dust.
6. **Hazardous Waste:** Toxic, reactive, ignitable. Example: pesticides, solvents.
7. **Agricultural Waste:** From farming activities. Example: crop residue, manure.
8. **Radioactive & Mining Waste:** From nuclear and mining operations.

> [!TIP]
> **Board Trick:** Always end your answer with the **significance line** — "This classification is the first step of EIA as it determines treatment, disposal, and regulatory compliance."

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**"Differentiate between biodegradable and non-biodegradable solid waste. Give two examples each."**

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Biodegradable** | **Non-Biodegradable** |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Decomposed by microbes in short time. | Does not decompose easily. |
| Time | Weeks to months. | Decades to centuries. |
| Examples | Food waste, paper, yard waste. | Plastics, glass, metals. |
| Treatment | Composting, anaerobic digestion. | Recycling, landfill, incineration. |
| Environmental risk | Methane from anaerobic decomposition. | Leachate, microplastics, soil contamination. |

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Part B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is provided — students answer **EITHER (a) OR (b)** of a sub-question. Below are TWO complete alternative 14-mark questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**"Explain in detail the various sources of municipal solid waste. How does source segregation impact the efficiency of downstream waste management?"**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of MSW (4 marks):**
1. **Residential sources** (40–60% by weight): Food waste, paper, plastics from kitchens and households.
2. **Commercial sources:** Markets, hotels, restaurants, offices, retail stores.
3. **Institutional sources:** Schools, colleges, government buildings, religious places.
4. **Municipal services:** Street sweeping, park maintenance, drain cleaning, construction debris from public works.
5. **Healthcare & small industries** within municipal limits.
6. **Construction & demolition** within notified area.

**Impact of Source Segregation (3 marks):**
- **Reduces contamination** of wet and dry streams → higher recycling value.
- **Lowers landfill volume by 40–60%** through effective diversion.
- **Reduces leachate toxicity** → simpler landfill design.
- **Enables composting** of wet waste at household/community level.
- **Improves economics** of waste-to-energy plants (RDF quality increases).
- **Complies with SWM Rules 2016** which mandate segregation at source.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**"A city has a population of 6,00,000 and per capita waste generation of 0.5 kg/day. If the waste density at landfill is 450 kg/m³ and the planning horizon is 15 years, calculate the landfill volume required. Assume cover material fraction = 0.2."**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step KTU Pattern):**

**Given:**
- $P = 6{,}00{,}000$
- $PC = 0.5$ kg/person/day
- $\rho = 450$ kg/m³
- $n = 15$ years
- Cover fraction $f_c = 0.2$ → $C = 1 - 0.2 = 0.8$

**Step 1: Total mass generated in 15 years:**

$$M = P \times PC \times 365 \times n$$

$$M = 6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 365 \times 15$$

**Step-by-step multiplication:**
$6{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 = 3{,}00{,}000$
$3{,}00{,}000 \times 365 = 1{,}09{,}500{,}000$ kg/year
$1{,}09{,}500{,}000 \times 15 = 1{,}64{,}25{,}00{,}000$ kg

$$\boxed{M = 1.6425 \times 10^9 \text{ kg}}$$

**Step 2: Apply landfill volume formula:**

$$V = \frac{M \times n}{\rho \times C}$$

$$V = \frac{1.6425 \times 10^9}{450 \times 0.8}$$

$$V = \frac{1.6425 \times 10^9}{360}$$

$$\boxed{V = 4.5625 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^3 \approx 4.56 \text{ million m}^3}$$

**Step 3: Sanity check (1 mark for verification):**
Daily waste = 6,00,000 × 0.5 = 3,00,000 kg/day = 300 tonnes/day
Annual = 300 × 365 = 1,09,500 tonnes/year
15-year total = 16,42,500 tonnes = 1.6425 × 10⁹ kg ✓

**Conclusion (Mandatory 1 mark):** The city requires approximately **4.56 million cubic meters** of engineered landfill capacity for a 15-year horizon.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks
**"Describe the classification of solid waste based on hazard potential. List the regulatory rules in India governing hazardous and biomedical waste."**

**Model Solution:**

**Hazard-Based Classification (4 marks):**
1. **General / Non-Hazardous Waste:** MSW, C&D waste, agricultural waste — handled via composting, recycling, sanitary landfilling.
2. **Hazardous Waste:** Toxic, reactive, ignitable, corrosive — handled via secure landfilling, stabilization, incineration. Examples: pesticides, solvents, heavy metal sludge.
3. **Infectious / Biomedical Waste:** Pathological, sharps, pharmaceuticals — handled via autoclaving, incineration as per BMW Rules 2016.
4. **Radioactive Waste:** Low, intermediate, high-level — handled by Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB).

**Regulatory Framework in India (3 marks):**
- **Biomedical Waste Management Rules, 2016** (amended 2018) — govern segregation, treatment, disposal of BMW.
- **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules, 2016** — govern industrial hazardous waste.
- **Municipal Solid Waste Management Rules, 2016** — govern MSW handling.
- **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2016 (amended 2022)** — govern e-waste.
- **Construction & Demolition Waste Management Rules, 2016** — govern C&D waste.
- **Plastic Waste Management Rules, 2016 (amended 2022)** — govern single-use plastics.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks
**"A waste sample weighs 320 g in wet condition and 145 g after oven drying. Calculate its moisture content and comment on its suitability for incineration."**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_w = 320$ g
- $W_d = 145$ g

**Step 1: Apply moisture content formula:**

$$MC(\%) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$$

$$MC = \frac{320 - 145}{320} \times 100$$

$$MC = \frac{175}{320} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{MC = 54.69\%}$$

**Step 2: Comment on Incineration Suitability (3 marks):**
- Moisture content **> 50%** is **unsuitable** for direct incineration.
- The latent heat of vaporization of water (~540 kcal/kg) consumes significant energy, making combustion inefficient.
- **Recommendation:** Pre-drying using **mechanical dewatering** or **thermal drying (rotary drum)** is required before incineration.
- Alternatively, this waste is **ideal for composting or anaerobic digestion** due to high organic moisture content.
- **Calorific value of such waste typically < 1000 kcal/kg**, which is below the **1500 kcal/kg threshold** for self-sustaining incineration.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write kg/person/day or tonnes/year — not just numbers. **[-1 mark]**
> 2. **Mixing up formula for PC and total mass:** PC is **per person per day** — students often forget to multiply by 365. **[-2 marks]**
> 3. **Not converting kg → tonnes:** The division by 1000 is mandatory in annual calculations. **[-1 mark]**
> 4. **Ignoring cover material fraction:** In landfill volume questions, students forget to multiply by $(1-f_c)$ or use $C$. **[-2 marks]**
> 5. **Not writing a conclusion line:** Examiners expect a final interpretive sentence in every numerical. **[-1 mark]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ **Solid waste** = any solid/semi-solid discarded material from human/animal activity.
- ✅ **10 source-based categories** to memorize: MSW, Industrial, Biomedical, E-Waste, C&D, Agricultural, Radioactive, Mining, Sewage Sludge, Hazardous.
- ✅ **Three classification dimensions:** Source, Physical Nature (Organic/Inorganic/Composite), Hazard Potential (Hazardous/Non-Hazardous).
- ✅ **Indian average per capita MSW:** 0.45 kg/person/day (urban); waste density 200–500 kg/m³.
- ✅ **Moisture content of Indian MSW:** 40–60% → high organic → low calorific value (800–2000 kcal/kg) → unsuitable for direct incineration.
- ✅ **Organic fraction = 40–60%** of Indian MSW → managed via composting / anaerobic digestion.
- ✅ **Master formulas:**
  - $PC = \frac{W_{day}}{P}$
  - $R = \frac{P \times PC \times 365}{1000}$ (tonnes/year)
  - $V = \frac{M \times n}{\rho \times C}$ (landfill)
  - $MC(\%) = \frac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$
- ✅ **Indian SWM Rules 2016** mandate **source segregation into wet, dry, and hazardous** streams.
- ✅ **ISWM Hierarchy:** Prevention → Reduction → Reuse → Recycle → Energy Recovery → Disposal.
- ✅ **Source–Pathway–Receptor** framework is the EIA model used to assess waste impact.
- ✅ **BMW Rules 2016, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2016/2022** — must be quoted with year.
- ✅ **In Part A (3-mark) answers:** Always end with a one-line **significance statement** to fetch the third mark.
- ✅ **In Part B numericals:** Always show **formula → substitution → calculation → result → conclusion**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Power Move:** Whenever a question mentions "waste," **always draw or describe the Source–Pathway–Receptor block diagram** — it instantly elevates your answer from "average" to "high-scoring" in the examiner's eyes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of Solid Waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Solid Waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Solid Waste Characteristics* refer to the physical, chemical, and biological properties of solid waste materials that determine their behaviour during collection, handling, storage, transportation, treatment, and ultimate disposal. These characteristics form the **fundamental data input** for designing an Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) of any solid waste management project or facility.

In the context of EIA, the characterization of solid waste is the systematic process of **quantifying** the qualitative and quantitative attributes of waste streams at a given location, time, and socio-economic context. Without accurate waste characterization, the predicted environmental impacts of a proposed waste facility (landfill, incinerator, composting plant) **cannot be reliably modelled**.

### Key Parameters of Characterization

The characterization of solid waste is conventionally divided into three major branches:

1. **Physical Characteristics** – density, moisture content, particle size, porosity, permeability, colour, odour.
2. **Chemical Characteristics** – proximate analysis (moisture, volatile matter, fixed carbon, ash), ultimate analysis (C, H, O, N, S), calorific value, pH, heavy metals.
3. **Biological Characteristics** – biodegradability, microbial activity, oxygen demand.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – "The Patient Diagnosis Analogy":**
> Imagine a doctor treating a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor must run blood tests, take X-rays, and measure vital signs. **You cannot treat what you have not measured.**
>
> Similarly, a solid waste facility is the "patient" in an EIA study. Before designing a landfill or an incinerator, the EIA consultant must first "diagnose" the waste by determining its physical, chemical, and biological properties. The **"diagnosis report"** is the *waste characterization*.
>
> For example, knowing the **moisture content** tells you whether the waste can burn easily (low moisture = high calorific value), and knowing the **biodegradable fraction** tells you whether landfill or composting is appropriate. Skipping this step is like a doctor prescribing medicine blindly.

### Why is Characterization Critical in EIA?

- It determines the **type and extent of environmental impacts** (leachate generation, methane emissions, air emissions).
- It governs the **suitability of treatment/disposal technology**.
- It forms the **baseline data** for the EIA Report and the EMP (Environmental Management Plan).
- Indian standards such as the **Municipal Solid Waste (Management and Handling) Rules, 2000** and the **SWM Rules, 2016** mandate waste characterization as a prerequisite for project clearance from the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) and the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).

### Standard Metrics Used (Bold Highlights)

The following metrics are **universally reported** in any KTU-aligned EIA study:
- **Bulk Density (kg/m³)** – mass per unit volume as collected.
- **Moisture Content (%)** – mass of water per unit mass of wet waste.
- **Calorific Value (kcal/kg or kJ/kg)** – energy released upon complete combustion.
- **C:N Ratio (dimensionless)** – carbon to nitrogen ratio, important for composting.
- **BOD/COD (mg/L)** – Biological/Oxygen Demand in leachate.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### A. Physical Characteristics of Solid Waste

Physical characteristics describe **how the waste looks, feels, and flows**. They govern the engineering design of collection vehicles, transfer stations, and compaction equipment.

#### 1. Density

Density is the mass of waste per unit volume. It exists in two forms:

- **Bulk Density ($\rho_b$)** – The mass of waste **as collected** (with voids) per unit volume.
- **Solid/Particle Density ($\rho_s$)** – The mass of waste **without voids** per unit volume.

Typical values:
- Residential waste: **100 – 200 kg/m³** (loose), **300 – 500 kg/m³** (compacted).
- Commercial waste: **150 – 300 kg/m³**.
- Construction & Demolition (C&D) waste: **1000 – 1500 kg/m³**.

#### 2. Moisture Content (MC)

The amount of water present in the waste, expressed as a percentage of the wet weight or dry weight.

- **Wet basis MC** is most commonly used in EIA studies.
- **High moisture content** reduces calorific value and increases leachate generation in landfills.

#### 3. Particle Size and Distribution

- Affects the **handling and processing** of waste.
- Determined by sieve analysis for granular waste.
- **Smaller particle size → higher surface area → faster decomposition → higher methane yield in landfills.**

#### 4. Porosity (n) and Permeability (k)

- **Porosity (n):** Fraction of void space in compacted waste. Typical values: **0.4 – 0.67**.
- **Permeability (k):** Ability of waste to transmit fluids. Important for **leachate migration** analysis in landfill EIA. Typical values: **$10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ cm/s**.

#### 5. Colour and Odour

- Subjective but important for **public health impact assessment** in EIA.
- Foul odour indicates **active anaerobic decomposition** and volatile fatty acid (VFA) release.

### B. Chemical Characteristics

Chemical characteristics determine **reactivity, combustibility, and pollution potential**.

#### 1. Proximate Analysis

Determines the major combustible and non-combustible fractions of waste.

| Component | Symbol | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Moisture | M | Reduces calorific value |
| Volatile Matter | VM | Burns easily; produces smoke/odour |
| Fixed Carbon | FC | Burns slowly; contributes to char |
| Ash | A | Inert residue; contributes to landfill mass |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proximate analysis is the "four-component fingerprint" of solid waste.** It is performed first because it is simple, fast, and inexpensive, and is **mandated in every EIA report for thermal treatment plants (incinerators).**

#### 2. Ultimate Analysis

Determines the elemental composition: **Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), and Chlorine (Cl)**.

- Important for predicting **flue gas composition** in incinerators.
- **Sulphur → SO₂ emissions**, **Nitrogen → NOₓ emissions**, **Chlorine → HCl and dioxin formation**.

#### 3. Calorific Value (CV)

The energy released upon complete combustion of unit mass of waste. Also called **Heating Value** or **Heat of Combustion**.

- **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** – Heat released assuming all water vapour condenses.
- **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** – Heat released assuming water leaves as vapour.
- For incinerator design, **NCV is preferred** because water exits as vapour.

#### 4. pH and Heavy Metals

- **pH** of waste affects leachate chemistry. Acidic leachate (pH < 6) is highly corrosive.
- **Heavy metals** (Pb, Cd, Hg, Cr, Ni) determine the **leachate toxicity** and need for leachate treatment in the EIA.

### C. Biological Characteristics

#### 1. Biodegradability

- Fraction of waste that can be decomposed by microorganisms.
- **Readily biodegradable:** food waste, yard waste, paper.
- **Slowly biodegradable:** textile, leather, wood.
- **Non-biodegradable:** plastics, glass, metals.

#### 2. C:N Ratio

- Optimum C:N for composting: **25:1 to 35:1**.
- Low C:N (< 20:1) → nitrogen loss as ammonia → odour.
- High C:N (> 40:1) → slow composting.

#### 3. BOD/COD

- Indicators of the **organic pollution potential** of leachate.
- BOD₅/COD ratio indicates **biodegradability** of the leachate.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarises **all key formulas** for the topic "Characteristics of Solid Waste" expected in KTU 2024 scheme examinations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Moisture Content (Wet basis) | $MC = \dfrac{(W_w - W_d)}{W_w} \times 100$ | % | $W_w$ = wet weight, $W_d$ = dry weight |
| 2 | Moisture Content (Dry basis) | $MC_{dry} = \dfrac{(W_w - W_d)}{W_d} \times 100$ | % | Used in drying calculations |
| 3 | Bulk Density | $\rho_b = \dfrac{m_{waste}}{V_{container}}$ | kg/m³ | m = mass, V = volume |
| 4 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{waste}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_{water} = 1000$ kg/m³ |
| 5 | Porosity | $n = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 0.4 – 0.67 |
| 6 | Void Ratio | $e = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{solids}}$ | dimensionless | $e = n/(1-n)$ |
| 7 | Volatile Solids (VS) | $VS = \dfrac{(W_{dry} - W_{ash})}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Indicator of organic content |
| 8 | Calorific Value (Dulong's) | $CV = 8080C + 34500(H - O/8) + 2240S$ | kcal/kg | Ultimate analysis based |
| 9 | Calorific Value (SI) | $CV = 33.8C + 144.2(H - O/8) + 9.3S$ | MJ/kg | SI form of Dulong's |
| 10 | Density-Compaction Relation | $\rho_{compacted} = \rho_{loose} \times CR$ | kg/m³ | $CR$ = Compaction Ratio (1.5 – 3) |
| 11 | Waste Generation Rate | $G = \dfrac{M_{total}}{P \times d}$ | kg/capita/day | $P$ = population, $d$ = days |
| 12 | C:N Ratio | $C\!:\!N = \dfrac{\%C}{\%N}$ | dimensionless | Optimum 25–35 for composting |
| 13 | Biodegradability Index | $BDI = \dfrac{C_{bio}}{C_{total}}$ | dimensionless | 0 to 1 |
| 14 | Ash Content | $A = \dfrac{W_{ash}}{W_{dry}} \times 100$ | % | Non-combustible residue |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Incinerator EIA** → CV, MC, ultimate analysis (for emission estimation).
- **Landfill EIA** → porosity, permeability, MC, BOD/COD (for leachate modelling).
- **Composting Plant EIA** → C:N ratio, VS, biodegradability (for process design).
- **Transfer Station Design** → bulk density, particle size (for storage and vehicle sizing).
- **Environmental Monitoring** → pH, heavy metals (for downstream water/soil quality).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Moisture Content Determination

**Given:** A solid waste sample weighs 2.50 kg in its wet condition. After oven-drying at 105°C for 24 hours, the dry weight is 1.15 kg.

**Step 1:** Identify the variables.
- $W_w$ = wet weight of sample = **2.50 kg**
- $W_d$ = oven-dry weight of sample = **1.15 kg**
- $M_w$ = mass of water evaporated = $W_w - W_d$

**Step 2:** Compute the mass of water evaporated.

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_w &= W_w - W_d \\
    &= 2.50 - 1.15 \\
    &= 1.35 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The difference between the wet weight and the dry weight represents the mass of water that was driven off as vapour during oven drying. This is a direct application of the principle of mass conservation.

**Step 3:** Apply the moisture content formula on a wet basis.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC &= \dfrac{M_w}{W_w} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{1.35}{2.50} \times 100 \\
   &= 54.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Moisture content is reported as a percentage of the original wet mass to allow consistent comparison between different waste samples, regardless of their absolute size.

**Result:** The solid waste has a moisture content of **54.0%** on a wet basis. This indicates the waste is highly wet (typical kitchen waste is 60–80% MC) and is **not suitable for direct incineration** without pre-drying.

---

### Derivation 2: Volatile Solids (VS) and Ash Content

**Given:** The oven-dry weight of a waste sample is 500 g. After combustion in a muffle furnace at 550°C for 2 hours, the residual ash weighs 95 g.

**Step 1:** Identify the variables.
- $W_{dry}$ = dry weight before combustion = **500 g**
- $W_{ash}$ = residual weight after combustion = **95 g**

**Step 2:** Compute the volatile solids content.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VS &= \dfrac{(W_{dry} - W_{ash})}{W_{dry}} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{(500 - 95)}{500} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{405}{500} \times 100 \\
   &= 81.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Combustion drives off all combustible (volatile) matter as gases (CO₂, H₂O, NOₓ, SO₂). The residue is non-combustible ash. The mass difference thus represents the organic (volatile) fraction.

**Step 3:** Compute the ash content.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{W_{ash}}{W_{dry}} \times 100 \\
  &= \dfrac{95}{500} \times 100 \\
  &= 19.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* Ash is the inert mineral residue that remains after complete combustion. It contributes to landfill volume and is a key parameter in incinerator ash disposal planning within an EIA.

**Result:** The waste contains **81.0% volatile solids** and **19.0% ash** on a dry basis. This is typical of a mixed municipal solid waste with high organic content.

---

### Derivation 3: Calorific Value Using Dulong's Formula

**Given:** Ultimate analysis of a solid waste sample (dry basis):
- Carbon (C) = 35.0%
- Hydrogen (H) = 4.5%
- Oxygen (O) = 22.0%
- Sulphur (S) = 0.5%
- Nitrogen (N) = 1.0%
- Ash (A) = 37.0%

**Step 1:** Verify that the ultimate analysis sums to 100%.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= C + H + O + S + N + A \\
            &= 35.0 + 4.5 + 22.0 + 0.5 + 1.0 + 37.0 \\
            &= 100.0\% \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* A valid ultimate analysis must sum to 100% (or close to it, with the remainder being unanalysed elements). This check is mandatory before applying Dulong's formula.

**Step 2:** Apply Dulong's formula (in kcal/kg).

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 8080C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the percentage values directly (since Dulong's formula is calibrated for percentage inputs).

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 8080(35.0) + 34500\left(4.5 - \dfrac{22.0}{8}\right) + 2240(0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify the hydrogen–oxygen correction term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) &= 4.5 - 2.75 = 1.75
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* The "O/8" correction accounts for the fact that 8 grams of oxygen are already bound to 1 gram of hydrogen as water (H₂O) in the original waste, and that portion of hydrogen cannot contribute to combustion heat.

**Step 5:** Compute each term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
8080 \times 35.0 &= 282{,}800 \\
34500 \times 1.75 &= 60{,}375 \\
2240 \times 0.5 &= 1{,}120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Sum all terms to get the calorific value.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 282{,}800 + 60{,}375 + 1{,}120 \\
   &= 344{,}295 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
   &= 344.3 \text{ kcal/kg (in practical engineering scale)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Scaling Clarification:** In practice, Dulong's formula is applied using the **mass fractions (not percentages)** as a fraction of 1, OR the percentages are used directly with the constants scaled. The above constant values (8080, 34500, 2240) already have the percentage scaling incorporated, so a direct percentage substitution is correct and gives the result in **kcal/kg**.

**Step 7:** Convert to SI units (MJ/kg) for international EIA reports.

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV_{MJ/kg} &= \dfrac{344.3}{238.85} \\
           &= 1.44 \text{ MJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Conversion logic:* 1 kcal = 0.0041868 MJ. However, for a more direct conversion, 1 MJ/kg = 238.85 kcal/kg, so dividing kcal/kg by 238.85 gives MJ/kg.

**Result:** The waste has a calorific value of approximately **344 kcal/kg (1.44 MJ/kg)**. This is **low** for an incineration-based disposal system (typical threshold is 1500 kcal/kg). This means **co-combustion with auxiliary fuel** or pre-drying would be required.

---

### Derivation 4: Bulk Density and Compaction Ratio

**Given:** A waste container of volume 0.20 m³ holds 60 kg of loose waste. After compaction, the same mass of waste occupies 0.10 m³.

**Step 1:** Compute the loose bulk density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{loose} &= \dfrac{m}{V_{loose}} \\
             &= \dfrac{60}{0.20} \\
             &= 300 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute the compacted bulk density.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{compacted} &= \dfrac{m}{V_{compacted}} \\
                  &= \dfrac{60}{0.10} \\
                  &= 600 \text{ kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the Compaction Ratio (CR).

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \dfrac{\rho_{compacted}}{\rho_{loose}} \\
   &= \dfrac{600}{300} \\
   &= 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The waste can be compacted to **half its original volume** (CR = 2.0). This is used to size collection vehicles, transfer stations, and landfill cells in the EIA design.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Kitchen Waste | Paper Waste | Plastic Waste | Yard Waste |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Moisture (%) | 60–80 | 4–8 | 1–4 | 30–60 |
| Calorific Value (kcal/kg) | 400–800 | 3000–4000 | 8000–11000 | 1500–3000 |
| Bulk Density (kg/m³) | 300–500 | 80–150 | 30–60 | 100–200 |
| Volatile Solids (%) | 80–90 | 90–95 | 95–99 | 75–85 |
| Biodegradability | High | Medium-High | Very Low | High |
| C:N Ratio | 15–20:1 | 170–200:1 | – | 25–50:1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->


### Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Waste Characterization in EIA

This diagram shows how waste characterization **inputs** feed into the **environmental impact assessment** and **management plan** outputs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    eia1 --> emp3["Leachate and Emission Modelling"]
    emp1 --> finA["EIA Report"]
    emp2 --> finA
    emp3 --> finA
```

### Diagram 3: Decision Tree for Disposal Method Based on Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Solid Waste Sample"] --> testA{"Moisture Content?"}
    testA -- "Less than 40 percent" --> pathB["Compute Calorific Value"]
    testA -- "Greater than 40 percent" --> pathC["Pre-drying Required"]
    pathB --> testB{"Calorific Value?"}
    testB -- "Greater than 1500 kcal/kg" --> outA["Incineration Suitable"]
    testB -- "Less than 1500 kcal/kg" --> outB["Co-combustion with Fuel"]
    pathC --> testC{"Biodegradable Fraction?"}
    testC -- "Greater than 50 percent" --> outC["Composting or Anaerobic Digestion"]
    testC -- "Less than 50 percent" --> outD["Sanitary Landfill with Liners"]
    outA --> finalA["Include Air Emission Modelling in EIA"]
    outB --> finalA
    outC --> finalB["Include Leachate Modelling in EIA"]
    outD --> finalB
```

This aligns with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 fallback protocol for non-physical-schematic topics.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Context:** As per KTU 2024 scheme, the End Semester Examination (ESE) carries a total of 100 marks split across 5 modules. Each module typically has 1 Part A question (3 marks) and 1 Part B question (14 marks, with internal choice). Module 3 of OECET614 follows this pattern. The model questions below are calibrated accordingly.

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 marks]

> **Define the term "Calorific Value" of solid waste and differentiate between Gross Calorific Value (GCV) and Net Calorific Value (NCV).**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* Calorific value of a solid waste is the **amount of heat energy released** when a unit mass of the waste is completely burnt in the presence of oxygen, under specified standard conditions (typically at 25°C and 1 atm).

*Differentiation:*

| Aspect | GCV (Higher Heating Value) | NCV (Lower Heating Value) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Heat released assuming all water vapour formed during combustion **condenses** to liquid | Heat released assuming water vapour formed during combustion **exits as vapour** |
| Magnitude | **Higher** | **Lower** by latent heat of vaporisation of water |
| Use in Design | Used for thermodynamic calculations | Preferred for **incinerator design** in EIA because flue gases exit hot |

**Exam Tip:** Always mention that NCV is used in practice because flue gases in real incinerators leave at high temperature with water as vapour.

---

#### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 3 marks]

> **What is meant by "Proximate Analysis" of solid waste? List the four components determined in proximate analysis and state the significance of each.**

**Model Answer:**

*Definition:* Proximate analysis is the **standardised laboratory procedure** used to determine the four major combustible and non-combustible components of a solid waste sample, namely **Moisture, Volatile Matter, Fixed Carbon, and Ash Content**.

*Components and Significance:*

1. **Moisture (M):** Mass loss on heating at 105°C; reduces calorific value; increases leachate volume in landfills.
2. **Volatile Matter (VM):** Mass loss on heating at 950°C (excluding moisture); high VM indicates high organic content and easy combustibility; important for **incinerator feed design**.
3. **Fixed Carbon (FC):** Solid carbon that burns slowly; contributes to char formation; calculated by difference.
4. **Ash (A):** Incombustible mineral residue; determines landfill volume; important for **bottom ash and fly ash disposal planning** in EIA.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU ESE Internal Choice Format

#### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 marks]

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the **physical characteristics** of municipal solid waste. Explain the significance of each parameter in the context of an Environmental Impact Assessment. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A solid waste sample of mass 5.0 kg in wet condition was oven-dried and the dry mass was found to be 2.0 kg. After combustion in a muffle furnace, the residual ash weighed 0.4 kg. Calculate **(i)** Moisture content (wet basis), **(ii)** Volatile solids content, and **(iii)** Ash content. Comment on the suitability of this waste for incineration. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Physical Characteristics in EIA Context:**

The physical characteristics of municipal solid waste are those attributes that can be measured **without altering the chemical composition** of the waste. They are the **first set of parameters** measured in any waste characterization study and form the **baseline data** for EIA.

1. **Bulk Density (kg/m³):**
   - Definition: Mass of waste per unit volume as collected.
   - Significance: Determines the **storage capacity** required for collection vehicles, transfer stations, and landfill cells. Low bulk density wastes (e.g., plastics) require **more volume per kg**, increasing the land footprint and visual impact in EIA.

2. **Moisture Content (%):**
   - Definition: Mass of water present in the waste, expressed as a percentage of wet or dry weight.
   - Significance: High moisture content (typical in food waste) **reduces the calorific value** and increases **leachate generation** in landfills. In an EIA, this directly affects the **leachate impact assessment on groundwater** and the **energy balance of an incinerator**.

3. **Particle Size and Distribution:**
   - Significance: Affects the **handling, processing, and decomposition rate** of waste. Smaller particles have higher surface area and decompose faster, increasing **methane generation rate** in landfills — a critical parameter for **greenhouse gas (GHG) impact assessment** in EIA.

4. **Porosity and Permeability:**
   - Porosity (n) is the fraction of void space; permeability (k) is the ability of waste to transmit fluids.
   - Significance: In landfill EIA, permeability directly controls the **leachate migration velocity** to groundwater. High permeability wastes require **engineered liners and leachate collection systems**.

5. **Colour and Odour:**
   - Significance: Subjective but critical for **public health and social impact assessment**. Foul odour indicates anaerobic decomposition and impacts the **air quality and nuisance assessment** in the EIA.

**Part (b) – Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Wet weight, $W_w = 5.0$ kg
- Oven-dry weight, $W_d = 2.0$ kg
- Ash weight after combustion, $W_{ash} = 0.4$ kg

**(i) Moisture content (wet basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MC &= \dfrac{(W_w - W_d)}{W_w} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{(5.0 - 2.0)}{5.0} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{3.0}{5.0} \times 100 \\
   &= 60.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Volatile solids content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VS &= \dfrac{(W_d - W_{ash})}{W_d} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{(2.0 - 0.4)}{2.0} \times 100 \\
   &= \dfrac{1.6}{2.0} \times 100 \\
   &= 80.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Ash content:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100 \\
  &= \dfrac{0.4}{2.0} \times 100 \\
  &= 20.0\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Suitability for Incineration – Comment:**

The waste has a **very high moisture content (60%)** and a **moderate organic content (80% VS)**. The expected calorific value will be **very low** (likely below 1000 kcal/kg). Such waste is **not suitable for direct incineration** because:

- High moisture means most of the combustion energy will be spent on **vaporising water**.
- The **flame will not be self-sustaining** and auxiliary fuel (diesel, natural gas, or RDF) will be required.
- **Recommendation:** Pre-drying, co-combustion with high-CV waste, or **composting/anaerobic digestion** would be more environmentally sound options, as suggested in the EIA.

---

#### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 3, 14 marks] – *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **(a)** What is **ultimate analysis** of solid waste? Explain with examples how the elemental composition of waste affects the environmental impact assessment of an **incineration** project. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** The ultimate analysis of a solid waste sample (in % by mass, dry basis) is as follows: C = 40%, H = 5%, O = 25%, N = 1.5%, S = 0.5%, Ash = 28%. Calculate the **calorific value** of the waste using **Dulong's formula**. State whether this waste is suitable for incineration. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) – Ultimate Analysis and EIA of Incineration:**

**Definition:**
Ultimate analysis is the **complete elemental composition** of a solid waste, expressed as the percentage of Carbon (C), Hydrogen (H), Oxygen (O), Nitrogen (N), Sulphur (S), Chlorine (Cl), and Ash (A). Together, these should sum to 100% (with minor analytical tolerance).

**Environmental Impacts in an Incineration EIA:**

The elemental composition of waste directly predicts the **flue gas emissions** from an incinerator, which are the **primary environmental impact parameters** assessed in EIA.

1. **Carbon (C):** Combusts to **CO₂** → major **greenhouse gas (GHG)** contributor; hence the **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** of the incinerator is estimated from the carbon content.

2. **Hydrogen (H):** Combusts to **H₂O**; influences the **flue gas humidity** and **latent heat losses**; affects the **acid dew point corrosion** in the boiler.

3. **Oxygen (O):** Already combined oxygen reduces the **oxygen demand** in the combustion chamber, slightly improving the flame stability.

4. **Nitrogen (N):** Combusts to **NOₓ (NO and NO₂)** → major **air pollutant** causing **acid rain and smog**. High nitrogen waste (e.g., food waste, animal waste) requires **NO�_x control technologies** (SNCR, SCR) — an additional capital cost in the EIA.

5. **Sulphur (S):** Combusts to **SO₂** → contributes to **acid rain**; an **Ambient Air Quality (AAQ) impact assessment** is triggered.

6. **Chlorine (Cl):** Combusts to **HCl** and can form **dioxins/furans** in the post-combustion zone → these are **highly toxic and carcinogenic**; the EIA must include a **dioxin emission modelling** if Cl > 0.5%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** state that chlorine produces Cl₂. In incinerators, the formation of **HCl and dioxins/furans** (PCCD/PCDF) is the correct and expected answer.
> - **Do not** forget to mention **NOₓ** for nitrogen — a common error is to only mention SO₂.
> - For each element, **tie the answer to a specific EIA study area** (air quality, GHG, public health) to score full marks.

**Part (b) – Dulong's Formula Calculation:**

**Given (dry basis, % by mass):**
- $C = 40.0\%$, $H = 5.0\%$, $O = 25.0\%$, $N = 1.5\%$, $S = 0.5\%$, $A = 28.0\%$

**Step 1: Check closure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sum} &= 40.0 + 5.0 + 25.0 + 1.5 + 0.5 + 28.0 = 100.0\% \;\;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Dulong's formula (kcal/kg).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 8080C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240S
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 8080(40.0) + 34500\left(5.0 - \dfrac{25.0}{8}\right) + 2240(0.5)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Simplify the H–O correction.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) &= 5.0 - 3.125 = 1.875
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute each term.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
8080 \times 40.0 &= 323{,}200 \\
34500 \times 1.875 &= 64{,}687.5 \\
2240 \times 0.5 &= 1{,}120
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CV &= 323{,}200 + 64{,}687.5 + 1{,}120 \\
   &= 389{,}007.5 \text{ kcal/kg} \\
   &\approx 389 \text{ kcal/kg} \text{ (in percentage-input scale)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result and Suitability:**

The calorific value is approximately **389 kcal/kg**, which is **far below the typical threshold of 1500 kcal/kg** required for self-sustaining incineration. Hence, this waste is **not suitable for direct incineration** without pre-processing (e.g., Refuse Derived Fuel — RDF) or co-combustion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse the **O/8** term with **O/2** or any other divisor. The factor **1/8** comes from the atomic mass ratio: 1 kg of H₂O contains 8 kg of O per 1 kg of H. This is a **favourite trap question** in KTU exams.
> - **Always verify** that the ultimate analysis sums to 100% before applying Dulong's formula.
> - Remember to **comment on the suitability for incineration** — a numerical answer without a concluding comment loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Solid waste characteristics** are the **physical, chemical, and biological** properties of waste that govern its behaviour during management.
- [x] **Physical characteristics** include: bulk density, moisture content, particle size, porosity, permeability, colour, odour.
- [x] **Chemical characteristics** are categorised into **proximate analysis** (M, VM, FC, A) and **ultimate analysis** (C, H, O, N, S, Cl).
- [x] **Biological characteristics** include: biodegradability, C:N ratio, BOD/COD, microbial activity.
- [x] **Moisture content (wet basis):** $MC = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_w} \times 100$.
- [x] **Moisture content (dry basis):** $MC_{dry} = \dfrac{W_w - W_d}{W_d} \times 100$.
- [x] **Volatile solids:** $VS = \dfrac{W_d - W_{ash}}{W_d} \times 100$.
- [x] **Dulong's formula:** $CV = 8080C + 34500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240S$ in **kcal/kg**.
- [x] **Dulong's formula (SI):** $CV = 33.8C + 144.2\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 9.3S$ in **MJ/kg**.
- [x] **C:N ratio optimum for composting:** **25:1 to 35:1**.
- [x] **Bulk density range** for MSW: **100 – 500 kg/m³** (loose to compacted).
- [x] **Porosity of MSW:** **0.4 – 0.67**.
- [x] **Incineration threshold CV:** **> 1500 kcal/kg** for self-sustaining combustion.
- [x] **Leachate from MSW** has a BOD₅ range of **2000 – 30000 mg/L** and a pH of **5 – 7.5**.
- [x] **GCV > NCV** always; NCV is used for incinerator design.
- [x] **Chlorine** in waste → **HCl and dioxin/furan** emissions in incinerators.
- [x] **Nitrogen** in waste → **NOₓ emissions**; **Sulphur** → **SO₂ emissions**.
- [x] **Solid waste characterization is the first and mandatory step** of any EIA for waste management facilities.
- [x] **Standards:** Indian SWM Rules 2016, CPCB guidelines, IS 14543:1998 for waste characterization methods.
- [x] **Remember to use the correct basis** (wet vs dry) in MC calculations — examiners explicitly test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: E-waste
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** E-waste

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**E-waste (Electronic Waste)** refers to any electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) that has been discarded by its owner as waste, without the intent of re-use, including all components, sub-assemblies, and consumables that are part of the original product at the time of discarding.

Under the **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** of India (which superseded the 2016 Rules), E-waste is formally classified under **Schedule I** of the rules and broadly categorized into **106 categories** spread across **15 broad categories** of electrical and electronic equipment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OECET614 - Module 3):** E-waste encompasses end-of-life electronic goods such as computers, mobile phones, televisions, refrigerators, washing machines, and their associated components (PCBs, batteries, cables, capacitors, displays). It is one of the fastest-growing municipal solid waste streams globally and contains both valuable recoverable materials and hazardous substances.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Toxic Treasure Chest"

Imagine a mobile phone as a **small locked treasure chest**. Inside this chest, you have:
- **Gold (Au), Silver (Ag), Palladium (Pd), Platinum (Pt), Copper (Cu)** — precious metals worth more per gram than the ore from which they are mined.
- **Plastics, glass, and rare earth elements** — recoverable industrial inputs.
- **Lead (Pb), Mercury (Hg), Cadmium (Cd), Beryllium (Be), Brominated Flame Retardants (BFRs)** — silent poisons.

When the phone is discarded carelessly into a regular dustbin, two parallel crises emerge:
1. **A treasure is lost** — India loses over ₹50,000 crore worth of precious metals annually by landfilling e-waste.
2. **A poison is unleashed** — heavy metals leach into groundwater and enter the food chain, causing neurological damage, kidney failure, and cancer.

> [!NOTE]
> **One Metric Ton (1 MT) of circuit boards** contains **40–800 times more gold and 30–40 times more copper** than **1 MT of mined ore**. This is the economic foundation of formal e-waste recycling.

## 1.3 Classification of E-Waste (KTU High-Yield)

| **Category** | **Examples** | **Key Hazardous Components** |
|---|---|---|
| **IT & Telecom Equipment** | PCs, laptops, mobiles, printers | Lead, Hg, Cd, BFRs, PCBs |
| **Consumer Electronics** | TVs, audio systems, cameras | Pb glass, capacitors, CRTs |
| **Household Appliances** | Refrigerators, ACs, washing machines | CFC/HCFC refrigerants, oils, Hg switches |
| **Lighting Equipment** | CFLs, LEDs, fluorescent tubes | **Hg (5 mg per CFL)** |
| **Toys, Leisure & Sports** | Video games, electric toys | Phthalates, Pb in solder |
| **Medical Devices** | Monitors, analyzers (non-infected) | Hg, radioactive isotopes |
| **Monitoring & Control Instruments** | Smoke detectors, thermostats | **Am-241 (radioactive)** |
| **Batteries** | Li-ion, Ni-Cd, lead-acid | **Li, Cd, Pb, electrolyte** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazardous Substance Thresholds (RoHS Directive Reference):**
> - Lead (Pb): **< 1000 ppm**
> - Mercury (Hg): **< 100 ppm**
> - Cadmium (Cd): **< 100 ppm**
> - Hexavalent Chromium (Cr⁶⁺): **< 1000 ppm**
> - PBB / PBDE flame retardants: **< 1000 ppm**

## 1.4 Global & Indian E-Waste Statistics (Critical KTU Data)

| **Parameter** | **Global Figure** | **India Figure (2023-24)** |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Generation | **~62 million tonnes (Mt)** | **~1.6 million tonnes (Mt)** |
| Per Capita Generation | ~7.8 kg/person/year | ~2.9 kg/person/year |
| Recycling Rate | **17.4% (formal)** | **~3% formal, 97% informal** |
| Projected 2030 | **82 Mt** | **~5 Mt** |
| Rank in Global Generation | — | **3rd largest (after China, USA)** |

> [!NOTE]
> India generates **5X more e-waste than it did in 2010**, yet formal recycling infrastructure handles only a tiny fraction. The rest flows into the **informal sector**, where workers (often migrant children) are exposed to toxic fumes from open burning of PCBs.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of E-Waste Generation

### Pillar 1: **Planned Obsolescence**
Manufacturers deliberately design products with a **finite useful life (3–5 years for electronics)** through:
- Non-replaceable batteries (glued into phones).
- Software updates that slow older hardware.
- Unavailability of spare parts after 2–3 years.

### Pillar 2: **Rapid Technological Innovation**
Every 18 months, computing power **doubles (Moore's Law)**. This renders existing devices "obsolete" in user perception, accelerating disposal cycles.

### Pillar 3: **Market Saturation & Falling Prices**
A basic smartphone in India now costs **₹6,000–₹10,000**, making replacement an easy decision rather than repair.

### Pillar 4: **Lifestyle Upgrades (Aspirational Consumption)**
The "newest model" culture — replacing functional devices for marginal upgrades.

### Pillar 5: **Corporate IT Asset Turnover**
Banks, IT firms, and governments replace IT assets every **3–5 years** due to security/data compliance needs.

### Pillar 6: **Lack of Reverse Logistics**
There is **no convenient collection mechanism** in most Indian cities, so devices end up in household storage ("ewaste graveyards in drawers") or mixed municipal waste.

## 2.2 Material Composition of E-Waste (Weight-Wise Average)

$$\text{Composition}_{\text{avg}} = 40\% \text{ metals} + 30\% \text{ plastics} + 20\% \text{ glass/ceramics} + 10\% \text{ other}$$

Where the metal fraction breaks down as:

$$\text{Metal fraction} = 20\% \text{ Fe} + 7\% \text{ Al} + 7\% \text{ Cu} + \text{Au, Ag, Pd, Pt, Pb, Sn, Ni, Zn, Sb}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical **1 kg of mobile phone PCB** contains approximately:
> - **300 mg Gold** | **138 mg Silver** | **35 mg Palladium** | **130 g Copper**
> - At 2024 market prices, this 1 kg of PCB holds approximately **₹18,000–₹22,000 in recoverable precious metals** alone.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Application in E-Waste EIA** |
|---|---|---|
| **Annual E-Waste Growth Rate** | $E_{t+1} = E_t \times (1 + r)^n$ | Forecasting future generation for landfill capacity EIA |
| **Per Capita Generation** | $E_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{population}}$ | Baseline data for EIA scoping |
| **Material Recovery Yield** | $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$ | Efficiency of recycling facility |
| **Toxicity Characteristic Leaching Procedure (TCLP)** | $C_{leachate} = \dfrac{M_{contaminant}}{V_{leachate}}$ | Determines if e-waste is "hazardous" by US EPA standards |
| **Energy Recovery Ratio (Formal Recycling)** | $E_{saved} = E_{virgin} - E_{recycling}$ | Quantifies CO₂ savings vs. virgin ore mining |
| **EPR Collection Target (India 2024-25)** | $C_{target} = X\% \text{ of previous year's sales (weight basis)}$ | Used in regulatory EIA compliance audits |
| **Heavy Metal Leaching Rate** | $\dfrac{dM}{dt} = -k \cdot C^n$ | Predicts groundwater contamination from landfill |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE FORMATTING NOTE:** All mathematical separators inside table cells use `\vert` (e.g., $\dfrac{dM}{\vert dt\vert}$) or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Never use the raw pipe character `|` inside table rows.

## 2.4 Environmental & Health Impact Pathways

### 2.4.1 Environmental Compartments Affected

$$\text{Soil} \xrightarrow{\text{leaching}} \text{Groundwater} \xrightarrow{\text{irrigation}} \text{Crops} \xrightarrow{\text{bioaccumulation}} \text{Humans}$$

$$\text{Open burning} \rightarrow \text{Atmospheric emission of dioxins, furans, PAHs} \rightarrow \text{Respiratory impacts}$$

### 2.4.2 Health Impact Severity Matrix (KTU High-Yield Table)

| **Contaminant** | **Source EEE** | **Health Impact** | **Safe Threshold (WHO/BIS)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lead (Pb)** | Solder, CRT glass | Neurotoxicity, IQ reduction in children | **< 10 µg/dL blood (children)** |
| **Mercury (Hg)** | CFLs, switches, LCD backlights | Kidney damage, fetal developmental issues | **< 5 µg/L blood** |
| **Cadmium (Cd)** | Ni-Cd batteries, resistors | Itai-Itai disease, lung cancer, renal failure | **< 5 µg/g creatinine in urine** |
| **BFRs (Brominated Flame Retardants)** | Plastic casings, PCB | Endocrine disruption, thyroid dysfunction | **No safe threshold (Precautionary)** |
| **Beryllium (Be)** | Motherboards, connectors | Chronic Beryllium Disease (CBD), lung cancer | **< 2 µg/m³ (8-hr TWA)** |

## 2.5 Real-World EIA Utility

In an **EIA for a proposed e-waste recycling facility**, the above formulas feed into:
- **Impact Prediction**: Heavy metal leaching using $C_{leachate} = \dfrac{M_{contaminant}}{V_{leachate}}$ against soil ingestion standards.
- **Mitigation Design**: Setting TCLP limits, designing impervious liners, scrubber efficiency for dioxin emissions.
- **EPR Compliance Auditing**: Verifying whether the producer met collection targets via $\eta_{recovery}$ calculations.
- **Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA)**: Aggregating e-waste burden across multiple informal recycling clusters in a city (e.g., Seelampur, Moradabad, Mandoli in Delhi-NCR).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Forecasting Future E-Waste Generation

### 3.1.1 Problem Context
India generated **1.6 Mt of e-waste in 2023-24**. The Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) for e-waste in India is approximately **r = 21% per annum**. The state of Kerala has **3.4% of India's urban population** and generates e-waste at a similar per-capita rate. **Calculate the projected e-waste from India in 2030.**

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Identify the variables**

$$E_t = 1.6 \text{ Mt} \quad \text{(generation in base year 2024)}$$

$$r = 0.21 \quad \text{(CAGR, dimensionless)}$$

$$n = 2030 - 2024 = 6 \text{ years}$$

**Step 2: Apply the compound growth formula**

$$E_{t+n} = E_t \cdot (1 + r)^n$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$E_{2030} = 1.6 \cdot (1 + 0.21)^6$$

**Step 4: Compute the growth factor**

$$(1.21)^6 = 3.138 \text{ (computed as } 1.21 \times 1.21 \times 1.21 \times 1.21 \times 1.21 \times 1.21\text{)}$$

**Step 5: Final computation**

$$E_{2030} = 1.6 \times 3.138 = 5.02 \text{ Mt}$$

### 3.1.3 Interpretation

By 2030, India will generate approximately **5.02 Mt of e-waste** — more than **3× the 2024 volume**. For an EIA study of a proposed regional recycling facility, the design capacity must accommodate this exponential escalation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This exponential projection is why the **2022 E-Waste Rules** mandate **increasing EPR collection targets** every year (10% YoY escalation), as the regulatory burden on producers scales with the waste stream itself.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Recovery Yield Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Context
A formal e-waste recycling facility in Bengaluru processes **5,000 kg of mobile phone PCBs per day**. During the metallurgical recovery process, the following outputs are recorded in a 24-hour shift:

- Copper recovered: **1,350 kg**
- Gold recovered: **0.75 kg**
- Silver recovered: **0.95 kg**
- Palladium recovered: **0.15 kg**

**Calculate the total material recovery yield (η_recovery).** Also determine if the facility meets the CPCB minimum efficiency benchmark of **η ≥ 28%** for precious metals recovery from PCBs.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total mass recovered (numerator)**

$$M_{recovered} = 1350 + 0.75 + 0.95 + 0.15 = 1351.85 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 2: Input mass (denominator)**

$$M_{input} = 5000 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3: Apply recovery yield formula**

$$\eta_{recovery} = \frac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4: Substitute and solve**

$$\eta_{recovery} = \frac{1351.85}{5000} \times 100\% = 27.04\%$$

### 3.2.3 Interpretation & EIA Compliance

$$\eta_{observed} = 27.04\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad \eta_{benchmark} = 28\%$$

The facility is **just below** the benchmark. In an EIA context, the **mitigation plan** would need to specify:
- **Re-engineering the hydrometallurgical circuit** to improve precious metal extraction.
- **Installing a secondary smelting furnace** to recover residual gold from slag.
- **Operator training on optimal acid leaching pH and residence time.**

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam valuations, **always show the formula, then substitute values with units, then state the final numerical answer with units.** Skipping the formula loses 1 mark out of the 7 allocated for the sub-question.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: TCLP Leaching Concentration for a Proposed Landfill

### 3.3.1 Problem Context
An EIA is being conducted for a proposed secured landfill to dispose of **non-recyclable e-waste residue (500 tonnes)**. The residue contains **15 mg of leachable lead per kg of waste**. When subjected to the **TCLP extraction (using acetic acid buffer at pH 2.88)**, the lead leaches into a total leachate volume of **2,500 liters**.

**Determine if the leachate concentration exceeds the US EPA hazardous waste threshold of 5 mg/L for lead.**

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Calculate total leachable lead mass**

$$M_{Pb} = 500{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 15 \text{ mg/kg} = 7{,}500{,}000 \text{ mg} = 7500 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Apply leachate concentration formula**

$$C_{leachate} = \frac{M_{contaminant}}{V_{leachate}}$$

**Step 3: Convert units consistently**

$$C_{leachate} = \frac{7{,}500{,}000 \text{ mg}}{2{,}500 \text{ L}} = 3000 \text{ mg/L}$$

**Step 4: Compare with regulatory threshold**

$$C_{leachate} = 3000 \text{ mg/L} \gg 5 \text{ mg/L (EPA limit)}$$

### 3.3.3 EIA Interpretation

The leachate is **600× more concentrated** than the hazardous threshold. The EIA report **must mandate**:
- A **double composite liner system** (HDPE + GCL).
- A **leachate collection and treatment system (LCTS)**.
- **Groundwater monitoring wells** at 50 m, 100 m, and 200 m from the landfill boundary.
- **Post-closure monitoring for 30 years** as per CPCB guidelines.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Exam Mistake:** Students often forget to **convert mg to g or kg to mg consistently** before dividing, leading to numerical errors. Always **list unit conversions explicitly in the solution.**

---

| **Management Option** | **Environmental Risk** | **Economic Viability** | **Social Acceptance** | **EIA Preference** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Open Dumping** | **Very High** | Low | Low | **REJECT** |
| **Open Burning** | **Very High** (dioxins) | Low (illegal) | Low | **REJECT** |
| **Informal Recycling** | High (worker exposure) | Low for workers, high for middlemen | Mixed | **REJECT (must be formalized)** |
| **Formal Mechanical Processing** | Low | Medium | High | **CONDITIONALLY ACCEPT** |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define E-waste with five examples. Mention two major hazardous substances found in e-waste.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

E-waste refers to all **discarded electrical and electronic equipment (EEE)** and its components, sub-assemblies, and consumables that have reached end-of-life and are discarded by the owner without intent of reuse.

**Five Examples:**
1. Mobile phones and smartphones
2. Personal computers and laptops
3. Television sets (CRT, LCD, LED)
4. Refrigerators and air conditioners
5. Printed Circuit Boards (PCBs) from any electronic device

**Two Major Hazardous Substances:**
1. **Lead (Pb)** — present in solder, CRT glass, and lead-acid batteries; causes neurotoxicity, especially in children.
2. **Mercury (Hg)** — present in CFLs, LCD backlights, and certain switches; causes kidney damage and bioaccumulates in the food chain.

*(Note: Acceptable alternatives include Cadmium, BFRs, Beryllium, Hexavalent Chromium.)*

---

### Question 2: What is Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) under the Indian E-Waste Management Rules, 2022?

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)** is a policy principle under which **producers (OEMs), importers, brand owners, and manufacturers of electrical and electronic equipment** are held financially and operationally responsible for the **end-of-life management** of the products they introduce into the market.

**Under the 2022 Rules, EPR mandates producers to:**
1. Collect a **specified percentage (weight-based)** of the e-waste generated from their products.
2. Channelize the collected e-waste only to **registered dismantlers and recyclers** with CPCB authorization.
3. File **annual returns** with the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) on collection targets achieved.
4. Maintain **financial guarantees or environmental compensation bonds** to ensure compliance.
5. Promote **eco-design and take-back programs** to reduce future e-waste generation.

EPR shifts the waste management burden from the **public/municipal sector** to the **producer**, internalizing environmental costs into the product price.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / May 2024 - Module 3 Choice]`

**Explain in detail the various sources, composition, and environmental impacts of e-waste. Discuss the major heavy metals and their toxic effects on human health and the ecosystem.**

#### Part (a) — Sources and Composition of E-Waste (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Sources of E-Waste:**

E-waste originates from **six major sources**:

1. **IT & Office Equipment Sector**: PCs, laptops, monitors, printers, scanners, servers. The IT sector replaces assets every 3–5 years, generating bulk e-waste.
2. **Telecommunication Equipment**: Mobile phones, tablets, modems, routers, base stations. With smartphone penetration exceeding 80% in urban India, this is the **fastest-growing** e-waste stream.
3. **Consumer Electronics**: Televisions, audio systems, cameras, gaming consoles. Transition from CRT to LCD/LED has accelerated obsolescence.
4. **Household Appliances**: Refrigerators, washing machines, microwave ovens, air conditioners, geysers. Contain refrigerants (CFCs/HCFCs) and insulating oils.
5. **Medical & Laboratory Equipment**: Monitors, analyzers, imaging devices. Contain radioactive sources and toxic reagents.
6. **Lighting Equipment**: CFLs, tube lights, LEDs. CFLs alone contribute **~100 tonnes of mercury** annually to Indian e-waste.

**Composition of E-Waste (Average by Weight):**

$$\text{E-Waste} = 40\% \text{ metals} + 30\% \text{ plastics} + 20\% \text{ glass/ceramics} + 10\% \text{ other}$$

- **Ferrous metals**: Iron, Steel (~20%)
- **Non-ferrous metals**: Aluminum, Copper (~14%)
- **Precious metals**: Gold, Silver, Palladium, Platinum (trace but high value)
- **Hazardous materials**: Lead, Mercury, Cadmium, BFRs, Beryllium
- **Polymers**: ABS, PVC, polycarbonates with brominated flame retardants

#### Part (b) — Environmental & Health Impacts (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Heavy Metals and Their Toxic Effects:**

1. **Lead (Pb)**: Used in solder, CRT glass, lead-acid batteries. Causes **neurotoxicity**, reduced IQ in children, anemia, kidney damage, and hypertension. Enters the body via inhalation of dust or ingestion of contaminated food/water. WHO safe blood lead level for children is **< 10 µg/dL**.

2. **Mercury (Hg)**: Present in CFLs (5 mg per lamp), LCD backlights, certain switches, and thermometers. Causes **neurological disorders**, fetal developmental issues, and kidney damage. Methylmercury bioaccumulates in fish, creating a major food-chain hazard.

3. **Cadmium (Cd)**: Found in Ni-Cd batteries, resistors, semiconductors. Causes **Itai-Itai disease** (painful bone degeneration), renal failure, and lung cancer. Reaches humans via rice paddies irrigated with contaminated water.

4. **BFRs (Brominated Flame Retardants)**: Used in plastic casings, PCBs, and cables. Act as **endocrine disruptors**, interfere with thyroid function, and are suspected carcinogens. Dioxins and furans released during open burning are among the **most toxic synthetic substances** known.

5. **Beryllium (Be)**: Used in motherboards, X-ray windows, and connectors. Causes **Chronic Beryllium Disease (CBD)** and lung cancer in exposed workers during informal dismantling.

**Environmental Impact Pathways:**

$$\text{Landfill leachate} \rightarrow \text{Soil contamination} \rightarrow \text{Groundwater} \rightarrow \text{Bioaccumulation in crops} \rightarrow \text{Humans}$$

$$\text{Open burning} \rightarrow \text{Atmospheric dioxins, furans, PAHs} \rightarrow \text{Respiratory impacts, cancer clusters}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently list the heavy metals but **fail to connect them to specific EEE sources and health endpoints**. For full marks, every heavy metal must be linked to (i) its **specific source component**, (ii) its **route of human exposure**, and (iii) its **specific disease/health impact**. A bare list of "Pb, Hg, Cd are toxic" scores only 3/7 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 3 Choice]`

**Discuss the salient features of the E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022 of India. Explain the Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) framework, collection targets, and the role of Producer Responsibility Organizations (PROs) in ensuring compliance.**

#### Part (a) — Salient Features of E-Waste Rules 2022 (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **E-Waste (Management) Rules, 2022** came into effect on **April 1, 2023**, replacing the 2016 Rules. Their key salient features are:

1. **Expanded Scope**: The rules cover **106 categories** of EEE across 15 broad categories, with **solar panels** added in a 2024 amendment.

2. **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) Mandate**: Every producer is responsible for collecting and channelizing a **specified percentage of the e-waste generated from their products** to registered recyclers. Targets increase progressively each year.

3. **CPCB Registration Required**: All producers, refurbishers, dismantlers, and recyclers must obtain **registration/authorization from CPCB** through the centralized EPR portal.

4. **Environmental Compensation**: Producers failing to meet EPR targets must pay **environmental compensation** calculated based on the shortfall and the environmental damage caused.

5. **Restriction on Hazardous Substances**: The rules incorporate the **Restriction of Hazardous Substances (RoHS)** framework, limiting the use of lead, mercury, cadmium, hexavalent chromium, and certain flame retardants in new equipment.

6. **Deposit Refund Scheme (DRS)**: For certain high-volume items (planned for future rollout), a deposit is collected at sale and refunded on return, incentivizing take-back.

7. **EPR Credit Trading**: Producers exceeding their targets can **sell excess EPR credits** to producers falling short, creating a market-based compliance mechanism.

8. **No Import of Used EEE**: Import of used/refurbished EEE is **banned** unless the producer commits to a buy-back arrangement.

#### Part (b) — EPR Framework, Collection Targets, and Role of PROs (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**EPR Collection Target Mechanism:**

The CPCB sets **annual collection targets** for each producer based on a percentage of the **weight of products placed in the market in the preceding years**. For example:

$$\text{EPR Target}_{2024\text{-}25} = 60\% \text{ of products placed in market 2022\text{-}23 (weight basis)}$$

$$\text{EPR Target}_{2025\text{-}26} = 70\% \text{ of products placed in market 2023\text{-}24 (weight basis)}$$

Targets escalate by **10% per year** to push toward circularity.

**Compliance Verification:**

Producers must file **annual EPR returns** on the CPCB portal documenting:
- Quantity of products sold.
- Quantity of e-waste collected.
- Quantity channelized to registered recyclers (with weight receipts).
- EPR credits purchased or sold.

Shortfall attracts **environmental compensation = Shortfall weight × Compensation rate per tonne**.

**Role of Producer Responsibility Organizations (PROs):**

PROs are **collective entities** registered with CPCB that aggregate EPR obligations across multiple producers (especially small and medium producers). Their functions are:

1. **Aggregation of Compliance**: Small producers can collectively meet targets through a single PRO, reducing administrative burden.

2. **Operational Collection Network**: PROs set up **collection centers, take-back booths, and reverse logistics** to gather e-waste from consumers.

3. **Channelization Contracts**: PROs enter into agreements with **registered recyclers** and track the movement of e-waste from collection to final recycling.

4. **Consumer Awareness**: PROs run awareness campaigns on **take-back programs** and **eco-friendly disposal**.

5. **Audit and Documentation**: PROs maintain **chain-of-custody documentation** for all e-waste handled, ensuring traceability for CPCB audits.

6. **Fraud Prevention**: PROs use **digital tracking, QR codes on products, and tamper-proof weighbridge receipts** to prevent false reporting.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse **PRO with a recycler.** PRO is a **collection, coordination, and compliance entity**; it does **not** itself dismantle or recycle e-waste. The actual material recovery is done by **CPCB-registered recyclers**. Confusing these two roles loses 2 marks. Also, remember that **EPR credits** are a **trading mechanism**, not a free pass — they must be backed by **actual verified e-waste collection**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

### **Core Conceptual Anchors**
- [x] E-waste = **End-of-life EEE**, formally covered under **Schedule I of E-Waste Rules 2022** in **106 categories** across **15 broad categories**.
- [x] India ranks **3rd globally** in e-waste generation (~**1.6 Mt/year**), with **< 5% formal recycling**.
- [x] 1 tonne of mobile PCBs yields **40–800× more gold** than 1 tonne of mined ore.

### **Key Numbers to Memorize**
- [x] Global e-waste 2023: **~62 Mt**; projected 2030: **~82 Mt**.
- [x] RoHS thresholds: **Pb < 1000 ppm, Hg < 100 ppm, Cd < 100 ppm**.
- [x] EPR annual escalation: **+10% per year** in collection targets.
- [x] Hg per CFL: **5 mg**; total Hg from Indian CFLs: **~100 tonnes/year**.

### **Regulatory Landmarks**
- [x] **Basel Convention 1989** controls transboundary movement of hazardous waste.
- [x] **E-Waste Rules 2011 → 2016 → 2022 → 2024 Amendment (solar panels)**.
- [x] **CPCB** = central regulator; **SPCB** = state-level enforcement.
- [x] **NGT (National Green Tribunal)** = appellate authority for environmental compensation disputes.

### **Formulas (Memorize with Units)**
- [x] $E_{t+n} = E_t(1+r)^n$ (forecasting)
- [x] $\eta_{recovery} = \dfrac{M_{recovered}}{M_{input}} \times 100\%$
- [x] $C_{leachate} = \dfrac{M_{contaminant}}{V_{leachate}}$
- [x] $E_{pc} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{population}}$

### **Process Hierarchy (5 R's)**
- [x] **Reduce → Reuse → Repair → Recycle → Recover** (in descending order of preference).

### **Common Exam Traps to Avoid**
- [x] **PRO ≠ Recycler** (PRO is a coordination entity).
- [x] **Open burning** releases **dioxins + furans**, not just "smoke."
- [x] **EPR credit** is **weight-based**, not value-based.
- [x] Always carry **units** in every formula substitution.
- [x] In India, **CFL mercury** is a major e-waste hazard, often overlooked in answers.
- [x] The **2022 Rules** use **CPCB EPR portal** for all compliance — manual paper filing is invalid.

### **One-Line Definitions for Quick Recall**
- [x] **E-waste**: Discarded EEE under Schedule I, E-Waste Rules 2022.
- [x] **EPR**: Producer's end-of-life responsibility for products they introduce.
- [x] **PRO**: CPCB-registered collective entity facilitating producer's EPR compliance.
- [x] **TCLP**: EPA test determining hazardous waste classification via leachate analysis.
- [x] **RoHS**: Restriction of Hazardous Substances in new electronic equipment.
- [x] **Urban Mining**: Recovery of valuable materials from e-waste streams.
