# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

### Question 1. Define soil salinization. List four modern agricultural practices that contribute to it. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Soil Salinization** is the accumulation of water-soluble salts (Na⁺, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, Cl⁻, SO₄²⁻) in the root zone to a level that impairs crop growth. Saline soils are defined as those with **Electrical Conductivity (EC) > 4 dS/m**.

**Four modern agricultural practices contributing to salinization:**

1. **Over-irrigation without proper subsurface drainage** — raises water table; capillary action brings salts to surface.
2. **Use of poor-quality irrigation water** (high SAR/salinity) — directly deposits salts.
3. **Excessive fertilizer application** — adds anion/cation load beyond crop uptake.
4. **Indiscriminate canal irrigation in arid regions** (e.g., Indira Gandhi Canal, Rajasthan) — without drainage outlet.

---

### Question 2. What is the role of mycorrhiza in soil health? How does modern agriculture affect it? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mycorrhiza** are symbiotic fungi (mainly *Glomus*, *Rhizophagus* species) that colonize plant roots, extending their effective surface area by **100–1000×**. They:
- Enhance **Phosphorus uptake** (which is otherwise immobile in soil).
- Improve **water absorption** and **drought tolerance**.
- Secrete **glomalin**, a glycoprotein that binds soil aggregates and improves structure.

**Impact of modern agriculture on mycorrhiza:**
1. **Tillage** physically disrupts the hyphal network.
2. **Phosphorus over-fertilization** makes plants "lazy" — they suppress mycorrhizal colonization.
3. **Fungicides** like Captan and Carbendazim kill non-target beneficial fungi.
4. **Monoculture** reduces host diversity, decreasing fungal species richness.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part A):** Students often write "mycorrhiza are bacteria" — this is **incorrect**. They are **fungi**. Marks are deducted for taxonomic errors. Also, do not exceed 80–100 words; Part A is concise definition-style.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Model Paper]**

### Question A (Choice 1): Comprehensive Analysis of Modern Agriculture Impacts on Soil

**(a) Discuss the major physical, chemical, and biological impacts of modern agriculture on soil. Cite at least two specific examples for each category. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Physical Impacts (2.5 Marks):**

(a) **Soil Compaction:**
- Caused by repeated use of heavy machinery (e.g., 20-tonne combine harvesters).
- Forms plough pan at 15–30 cm depth.
- Increases **Bulk Density** from 1.3 to 1.7 g/cm³, reducing porosity from 50% to 40%.
- Example: Continuous rice–wheat rotation in Punjab with disc ploughs.

(b) **Accelerated Soil Erosion:**
- Conventional tillage leaves soil bare for 60–90 days between crops.
- **Example:** USLE calculation shows erosion rate of **16.4 t/ha/year** in India vs. tolerable 6.7 t/ha/year (NBSS&LUP, 2016).
- Sheet and rill erosion in maize fields of Karnataka.

**2. Chemical Impacts (2.5 Marks):**

(a) **Acidification:**
- Nitrification of ammonium fertilizers releases H⁺ ions.
- Soil pH drops by 0.5–1.0 unit per decade in tea plantations of Kerala and Assam.
- Mobilizes toxic Al³⁺ at pH < 5.5.

(b) **Heavy Metal Contamination:**
- Phosphate fertilizers contain **Cadmium** (Cd) at 0.5–15 mg/kg.
- Long-term P application → Cd accumulation in surface horizon.
- **Example:** Cd levels in paddy soils of Ludhiana exceed **0.3 mg/kg** threshold.

**3. Biological Impacts (2 Marks):**

(a) **Loss of Soil Microbial Diversity:**
- Earthworm population drops from 200/m² to < 20/m² in heavily pesticide-treated fields.
- **Example:** Endosulfan use in Kasaragod (Kerala) caused 50–90% earthworm mortality.

(b) **Mycorrhizal Disruption:**
- High P-fertilizer rates reduce mycorrhizal colonization by 60–80%.
- Loss of glomalin → soil aggregate stability declines, exacerbating erosion.

---

**(b) Using the Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE), estimate the annual soil loss for a 2-hectare maize field in Kerala. Given: R = 650 MJ·mm/ha·hr·yr; K = 0.28; LS = 2.1; C = 0.35; P = 1.0. Compare with the T-value (6.7 t/ha/year) and recommend three engineering mitigation measures. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: State the USLE equation.**

$$A = R \times K \times LS \times C \times P$$

**Step 2: Substitute the given values.**

$$A = 650 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 0.35 \times 1.0$$

**Step 3: Compute step-by-step.**

$$A = 650 \times 0.28 = 182.0$$

$$A = 182.0 \times 2.1 = 382.2$$

$$A = 382.2 \times 0.35 = 133.77 \approx 134 \text{ t/ha/year}$$

**Step 4: Compare with T-value.**

$$\frac{A}{T} = \frac{134}{6.7} = 20.0$$

> The soil is eroding **20 times faster** than the natural regeneration rate — this is a **critical, irreversible degradation scenario**.

**Step 5: Total soil loss for 2 ha over 1 year.**

$$Total = 134 \times 2 = 268 \text{ t/year}$$

**Step 6: Recommend three engineering mitigation measures.**

1. **Contour Bunding & Terracing:** Reduces LS factor; new P value can drop to 0.3 (60% reduction in erosion).
2. **Mulching / Cover Crops:** Establishes continuous cover; C value drops from 0.35 to 0.10 (70% reduction).
3. **Conservation Tillage (Zero/Reduced Tillage):** Preserves crop residue on surface; C value drops to 0.05.

**Step 7: Recalculate post-mitigation (combining Measures 2 & 3).**

$$A_{new} = 650 \times 0.28 \times 2.1 \times 0.10 \times 0.5 = 19.1 \text{ t/ha/year}$$

$$Reduction = \frac{134 - 19.1}{134} \times 100 = 85.7\%$$

This brings erosion close to the tolerable T-value, demonstrating effective engineering intervention.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Soil Contamination Pathways and Bioremediation

**(a) Explain with chemical equations how modern fertilizer use leads to soil acidification and heavy metal contamination. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Acidification Mechanism (3.5 Marks):**

Modern nitrogen fertilizers are predominantly **ammonium-based** (Urea, NH₄SO₄, NH₄NO₃). When applied to soil, **nitrification** occurs by *Nitrosomonas* and *Nitrobacter* bacteria:

$$NH_4^+ + \frac{3}{2} O_2 \rightarrow NO_2^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$

This is the **first step (nitritation)**.

$$NO_2^- + \frac{1}{2} O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^-$$

This is the **second step (nitratation)**.

Net reaction:

$$NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$$

**Implication:** For every mole of NH₄⁺ nitrified, **2 moles of H⁺** are released. With 200 kg N/ha applied annually as urea, approximately:

$$H^+ = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{14} \times 2 = 28{,}571\ \text{equivalents/ha}$$

This corresponds to roughly **1.4 tonnes of pure H₂SO₄ equivalent** per hectare per year — a substantial acidifying load.

Additionally, **Urea hydrolysis** also contributes:

$$CO(NH_2)_2 + 2H_2O \rightarrow 2NH_4^+ + CO_3^{2-}$$

The carbonate is consumed, and subsequent nitrification releases H⁺.

**2. Heavy Metal Contamination Mechanism (3.5 Marks):**

Phosphate rock (the source of P-fertilizers) contains trace metals:

| Metal | Typical Concentration in Rock Phosphate | Threshold in Soil (mg/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Cadmium (Cd) | 0.5–15 mg/kg | 3 mg/kg (Indian Standard) |
| Lead (Pb) | 5–50 mg/kg | 250 mg/kg |
| Uranium (U-238) | 30–400 mg/kg | — |

When phosphoric acid is manufactured:

$$Ca_3(PO_4)_2 + 3H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2H_3PO_4 + 3CaSO_4$$

The impurities pass into the final fertilizer product. **Pesticides** also contribute **Pb** (lead arsenate) and **Hg** (older fungicides).

**Cadmium bioavailability** increases under acidic conditions:

$$CdCO_3 + 2H^+ \rightarrow Cd^{2+} + H_2O + CO_2$$

Cd²⁺ is then taken up by crops → enters the food chain → causes **Itai-Itai disease** (renal dysfunction) — documented in Japan from contaminated rice.

**Mitigation:** Apply **lime (CaCO₃)** to neutralize acidity and reduce Cd uptake.

$$CaCO_3 + 2H^+ \rightarrow Ca^{2+} + H_2O + CO_2$$

---

**(b) Describe the principles and engineering design of Phytoremediation as a soil restoration strategy for pesticide-contaminated agricultural land. Provide two plant species examples with their target contaminants. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition (1 Mark):**

**Phytoremediation** is the use of living plants and their associated microbiota to remove, contain, degrade, or immobilize soil contaminants. It is a **green engineering technology** that is solar-powered, aesthetically acceptable, and cost-effective (typically **$10–50/m³** vs. **$100–1000/m³** for conventional excavation).

**2. Mechanisms (2 Marks):**

| Mechanism | Description | Best For |
|---|---|---|
| **Phytoextraction** | Plants uptake metals into harvestable biomass | Cd, Pb, Ni, Zn |
| **Phytodegradation** | Plant enzymes (dehalogenase, nitroreductase) break down organics | Pesticides, hydrocarbons |
| **Phytostabilization** | Roots immobilize contaminants in rhizosphere | As, Cr, Pb |
| **Phytovolatilization** | Plants release volatile forms (e.g., methylated Se) | Hg, Se, As |
| **Rhizofiltration** | Roots absorb from aqueous solution | For water bodies |

**3. Engineering Design Parameters (2 Marks):**

For a phytoremediation project:

- **Plant selection:** Hyperaccumulator species with > 100× normal metal concentration in shoots.
- **Agronomic management:** Fertilization to maximize biomass; chelate addition (EDTA) to enhance metal solubility.
- **Harvest frequency:** 2–3 crops per year; post-harvest biomass is **ashed and disposed** as hazardous waste or recycled (e.g., Ni recovery from *Alyssum murale*).
- **Time horizon:** 5–20 years for moderate contamination.
- **Monitoring:** Periodic soil sampling, plant tissue analysis (XRF, ICP-MS).

**4. Two Plant Species Examples (2 Marks):**

| Plant | Contaminant Targeted | Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| ***Helianthus annuus*** (Sunflower) | **Cadmium (Cd), Arsenic (As), Cs-137, Sr-90** | Phytoextraction | Kerala paddy fields post-floods |
| ***Brassica juncea*** (Indian Mustard) | **Lead (Pb), Zn, Cr, Se** | Phytoextraction + rhizodegradation | Industrial contaminated sites |
| *Pteris vittata* (Chinese Brake Fern) | Arsenic (As) | Hyperaccumulator | As-contaminated paddy soils of Bengal |
| *Populus* spp. (Poplar) | TCE, PAH, pesticides | Phytodegradation | Agro-industrial zones |

**5. Limitations:**

- Slow (years to decades).
- Restricted to root zone (typically < 3 m).
- Contaminants must be bioavailable.
- Risk of food-chain transfer if crop species used.

**Conclusion:** Phytoremediation is a **sustainable, low-cost, eco-friendly** method for restoring pesticide-contaminated agricultural soils and should be integrated into EIA mitigation plans for all agricultural projects exceeding 50 ha.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls for 14-Mark Questions:**
> 1. **Skipping units** in USLE calculation → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Writing "pH decreases"** without chemical equation → only 50% credit.
> 3. **Confusing "pesticide" with "fertilizer"** in mitigation answers → -2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting T-value comparison** in erosion problems → lose 1.5 marks.
> 5. **Writing "mycorrhiza are bacteria"** — they are **fungi**; taxonomic error.
> 6. **Ignoring numerical data** — KTU values show-down only when numbers are presented.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Frequency Recap Points (must memorize before the exam):**

- ✅ **USLE Formula:** A = R × K × LS × C × P — master each factor.
- ✅ **T-value (tolerable erosion):** 5–11 t/ha/year.
- ✅ **Salinity threshold:** EC > 4 dS/m.
- ✅ **Sodicity threshold:** ESP > 15%.
- ✅ **SOC healthy range:** 0.5–3.0% in agricultural soils.
- ✅ **CEC range:** 10–30 cmol(+)/kg.
- ✅ **Acidification chemical equation:** $NH_4^+ + 2O_2 \rightarrow NO_3^- + 2H^+ + H_2O$.
- ✅ **Nitrogen use efficiency (NUE) in modern systems:** only 30–50%.
- ✅ **Mycorrhiza are fungi** (not bacteria); they increase P uptake and form glomalin.
- ✅ **Cadmium is the most concerning heavy metal** in phosphate fertilizers.
- ✅ **Endosulfan** — Kerala's landmark POP contamination case (Kasaragod).
- ✅ **Three pillars of sustainable agriculture:** Conservation Tillage + Crop Rotation + Integrated Nutrient Management (INM).
- ✅ **Phytoremediation cost:** 10–50 USD/m³ vs. 100–1000 USD/m³ for excavation.
- ✅ **WHO nitrate limit in drinking water:** 10 mg/L (as NO₃⁻-N).
- ✅ **Soil compaction depth categories:** Surface (0–5 cm), Plough pan (15–30 cm), Subsoil (30–60 cm).
- ✅ **EIA threshold in India:** Agricultural projects > 50 ha require Environmental Clearance (2006 Notification).

> **Final Tip:** Always structure long answers using **headings + equations + examples**. KTU examiners reward **structured, multi-modal answers** (text + math + case study) over paragraph-only responses.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pesticide Pollution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Pesticide Pollution # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pesticide Pollution** is defined as the contamination of the natural environment (soil, water, air, and biota) with synthetic or naturally derived chemical substances used to control, repel, or destroy pests (insects, weeds, fungi, rodents, etc.), where the presence, concentration, or bioaccumulative behaviour of these substances causes adverse ecological, human-health, or ecosystem-service impacts. Pesticides are broadly classified under the **FIFRA (Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act)** framework and include the following functional categories: | Category | Target Organism | Example Compounds | |---|---|---| | Insecticides | Insects | DDT, Malathion, Endosulfan | | Herbicides | Weeds | Atrazine, 2,4-D, Glyphosate | | Fungicides | Fungi | Mancozeb, Captan, Copper Sulphate | | Rodenticides | Rodents | Warfarin, Zinc Phosphide | | Nematicides | Nematodes | Aldicarb, Methyl Bromide | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the EIA context, pesticide pollution is examined as a *project-induced* impact arising from agricultural expansion, industrial effluent, vector-control programmes, and disposal of obsolete pesticide stockpiles. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of pesticides like **strong medicines for crops**. Just as a doctor prescribes antibiotics in precise doses, a farmer sprays pesticides to "cure" a pest attack. However, if the medicine is overused, spilled, or dumped into rivers, it does not disappear — it travels through water, soil, and even up the food chain, much like how a patient who overuses antibiotics develops resistance and the residue pollutes the environment. A more specific analogy: imagine pouring a small amount of ink into a flowing river. The ink disperses, settles in mud, is absorbed by fish, and is then consumed by birds and humans. The same happens with **lipophilic (fat-loving) pesticides** — they dissolve in animal fat and concentrate as they move up the food chain. This is exactly what happened with **DDT** in *Silent Spring* (1962), Rachel Carson's landmark documentation of pesticide bioaccumulation. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following standard metrics are used in pesticide pollution assessment: - **LD50 (Lethal Dose 50%)**: **50 mg/kg** (general regulatory threshold for "highly toxic") - **BCF (Bioconcentration Factor)**: dimensionless ratio, log BCF > **3.0** indicates significant bioaccumulation - **Half-life (t₁/₂)**: persistence indicator; > **100 days** = persistent organic pollutant - **Kow (Octanol-Water Partition Coefficient)**: log Kow > **4.0** = highly lipophilic - **ADI (Acceptable Daily Intake)**: typically expressed in **mg/kg body weight/day** > [!IMPORTANT] > These metrics form the basis of the **Persistent Organic Pollutants (POPs) criteria** under the **Stockholm Convention (2001)**, which initially listed 12 compounds — informally called the **"Dirty Dozen"** — and has been amended several times. ## 1.4 Categories of EIA Significance Pesticide pollution is treated as a **Type I Adverse Environmental Impact** in KTU 2024 EIA frameworks because: - It is **widespread** (spatial extent is large) - It is **persistent** (temporal duration is long) - It causes **irreversible** damage to non-target species - It has **cumulative** and **synergistic** effects # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Source-Pathway-Receptor Framework Pesticide pollution follows the classic **Source → Pathway → Receptor (S-P-R)** model used in EIA. Each link in this chain must be analysed in a formal EIA study. ### 2.1.1 Sources (Point & Non-Point) - **Point sources**: manufacturing plants, formulation units, disposal sites - **Non-point (diffuse) sources**: agricultural runoff, spray drift, atmospheric deposition - **Stockpiles**: obsolete pesticide dumps (a major EIA concern in Kerala — e.g., the Kasaragod Endosulfan crisis) ### 2.1.2 Pathways 1. **Atmospheric transport** (volatilization → long-range transboundary drift) 2. **Surface runoff** (overland flow into rivers, lakes, estuaries) 3. **Leaching** (percolation into groundwater) 4. **Soil adsorption** (binding to organic matter) 5. **Bioaccumulation** (uptake by flora/fauna) ### 2.1.3 Receptors - Humans (via food, water, inhalation) - Wildlife (especially birds, fish, pollinators) - Ecosystems (soil microbiome, aquatic communities) - Sensitive groups (children, pregnant women) ## 2.2 Persistence, Bioaccumulation, Toxicity (PBT) Criteria Pesticides are screened against three scientifically rigorous criteria to determine regulatory action: | Criterion | Threshold (Stockholm / EU REACH) | Significance | |---|---|---| | Persistence (P) | Half-life in water > 40 days, or soil > 120 days, or sediment > 160 days | Long-lasting environmental presence | | Bioaccumulation (B) | BCF > 2000 L/kg, or log Kow > 4.5 | Concentrates in fatty tissue | | Toxicity (T) | NOEC < 0.01 mg/L (long-term aquatic), or CMR category 1A/1B | Adverse biological effects at low dose | ## 2.3 Bioaccumulation vs. Biomagnification — A Critical Distinction > [!NOTE] > **Bioaccumulation** = uptake from ALL pathways (water, food, sediment) over the lifetime of an organism > **Biomagnification** = increase in concentration as one moves UP the food chain (trophic transfer) > **Biotransformation** = metabolic conversion of the parent compound into metabolites (often more toxic — e.g., DDT → DDE) ## 2.4 Routes of Human Exposure 1. **Dietary ingestion** (~90% of total exposure) — food, water, milk 2. **Inhalation** — indoor air, spray drift 3. **Dermal contact** — occupational (farmers, formulators) 4. **Transplacental / Lactational** — mother-to-foetus transfer (critical for DDT, dioxins) ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Equation Number | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | |---|---|---|---| | **E2.1** | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay (concentration over time) | $C_t$ = concentration at time $t$ (mg/L), $C_0$ = initial concentration, $k$ = decay constant (day⁻¹), $t$ = time (days) | | **E2.2** | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life from decay constant | $t_{1/2}$ in days | | **E2.3** | $BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}}$ | Bioconcentration Factor | $C_{organism}$ in mg/kg wet weight, $C_{water}$ in mg/L | | **E2.4** | $\log BCF = 0.85 \cdot \log K_{ow} - 0.70$ | Linear free-energy relationship (LFER) | Empirical regression | | **E2.5** | $BMF = \dfrac{C_{predator}}{C_{prey}}$ | Biomagnification Factor | Dimensionless | | **E2.6** | $TMF = \left(\dfrac{C_{top}}{C_{bottom}}\right)^{1/n}$ | Trophic Magnification Factor | $n$ = number of trophic levels | | **E2.7** | $ADI = \dfrac{NOEL}{SF}$ | Acceptable Daily Intake | $NOEL$ in mg/kg/day, $SF$ = safety factor (typically 100) | | **E2.8** | $HQ = \dfrac{ADD}{RfD}$ | Hazard Quotient | $ADD$ = Average Daily Dose, $RfD$ = Reference Dose | | **E2.9** | $PEC = \dfrac{A \cdot f}{V \cdot d}$ | Predicted Environmental Concentration | $A$ = annual application (kg), $f$ = fraction reaching water, $V$ = volume, $d$ = depth | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not just academic — they drive: - **Pesticide registration** under the Insecticides Act, 1968 (India) - **Environmental Quality Standards (EQS)** for surface and groundwater - **MCL (Maximum Contaminant Level)** in drinking water (e.g., Atrazine MCL = **3 µg/L** as per US EPA) - **Ecological Risk Assessment (ERA)** for new agrochemical introductions - **EIA scoping** for industrial pesticide plants and agricultural expansion projects > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU 2024 EIA methodology, **PEC/PNEC ratio** is used as the core trigger: if **PEC/PNEC > 1**, the site is classified as a *Pollution Hot-Spot* and warrants full Environmental Impact Statement (EIS) preparation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: First-Order Decay Model (Equation E2.1 → E2.2) The pesticide degradation in soil and water typically follows **first-order kinetics** because microbial breakdown is proportional to the substrate concentration. **Step 1 — Setup the differential equation** The rate of disappearance of pesticide $C$ with respect to time $t$ is directly proportional to its concentration: $$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C$$ **Step 2 — Separate variables** $$\dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 3 — Integrate both sides from initial concentration $C_0$ to $C_t$ and from $0$ to $t$** $$\int_{C_0}^{C_t} \dfrac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals** $$\ln(C_t) - \ln(C_0) = -k \cdot t$$ **Step 5 — Apply logarithm rules** $$\ln\left(\dfrac{C_t}{C_0}\right) = -k \cdot t$$ **Step 6 — Exponentiate to isolate $C_t$** (this is the final Equation E2.1) $$C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-k t}$$ **Step 7 — Derive the half-life expression** By definition, at $t = t_{1/2}$, the concentration becomes half of its initial value, so $C_t = C_0 / 2$: $$\dfrac{C_0}{2} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k \, t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 8 — Simplify** $$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-k \, t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 9 — Take natural logarithm of both sides** $$\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$ **Step 10 — Recognise that $\ln(1/2) = -\ln 2 = -0.693$** $$-\ln 2 = -k \cdot t_{1/2}$$ **Step 11 — Rearrange to get the half-life equation (E2.2)** $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$$ --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Decay of Endosulfan **Problem:** Endosulfan is applied at $C_0 = 5.0$ mg/L into a paddy field. The decay constant is $k = 0.021$ day⁻¹. Determine (a) the concentration after **30 days**, and (b) the **half-life** of endosulfan in this environment. **Step 1 — Identify given values** $$C_0 = 5.0 \; \text{mg/L}, \quad k = 0.021 \; \text{day}^{-1}, \quad t = 30 \; \text{days}$$ **Step 2 — Apply Equation E2.1** $$C_{30} = 5.0 \cdot e^{-(0.021)(30)}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the exponent** $$-(0.021)(30) = -0.63$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the exponential** $$e^{-0.63} = 0.5335$$ **Step 5 — Multiply by $C_0$** $$C_{30} = 5.0 \times 0.5335 = 2.67 \; \text{mg/L}$$ **Step 6 — Compute half-life using E2.2** $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.021} = 33.0 \; \text{days}$$ **Interpretation:** After 30 days, only about **47%** of the endosulfan has degraded. The compound is **persistent** (half-life > 30 days) and would be classified as a **POP candidate**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 2: BCF from $K_{ow}$ (Equation E2.4) The relationship between bioaccumulation potential and lipophilicity is empirically derived from linear free-energy relationships. **Step 1 — Start with the empirical regression model** Veith and Kosian (1983) and subsequently refined by Meylan et al. established that: $$\log BCF = a \cdot \log K_{ow} + b$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the empirical coefficients from the standard regression dataset** $$\log BCF = 0.85 \cdot \log K_{ow} - 0.70$$ **Step 3 — Solve for BCF by exponentiating (base 10)** $$BCF = 10^{(0.85 \cdot \log K_{ow} - 0.70)}$$ **Step 4 — Use logarithm identity $x \cdot \log y = \log y^x$** $$BCF = 10^{0.85 \cdot \log K_{ow}} \cdot 10^{-0.70}$$ **Step 5 — Use the identity $10^{\log K_{ow}^{0.85}} = K_{ow}^{0.85}$** $$BCF = \dfrac{K_{ow}^{0.85}}{10^{0.70}}$$ **Step 6 — Compute $10^{0.70} = 5.012$** $$BCF \approx \dfrac{K_{ow}^{0.85}}{5.01}$$ --- ## 3.4 Worked Example — BCF of DDT **Problem:** DDT has an octanol-water partition coefficient $K_{ow} = 1.55 \times 10^{6}$. Calculate the BCF. **Step 1 — Compute $\log K_{ow}$** $$\log K_{ow} = \log(1.55 \times 10^{6}) = 6.19$$ **Step 2 — Apply Equation E2.4** $$\log BCF = 0.85 \cdot (6.19) - 0.70 = 5.26 - 0.70 = 4.56$$ **Step 3 — Convert log to BCF** $$BCF = 10^{4.56} = 36{,}308 \; \text{L/kg}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This **BCF > 2000 L/kg** automatically classifies DDT as a **bioaccumulative substance (B)** under REACH and confirms its inclusion in the **Stockholm Convention POP list**. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 3: Hazard Quotient (HQ) for Pesticide Risk The Hazard Quotient is used in **baseline EIA risk characterization**. **Step 1 — Define Average Daily Dose (ADD)** $$ADD = \dfrac{C \cdot IR \cdot EF \cdot ED}{BW \cdot AT}$$ where: - $C$ = pesticide concentration in exposure medium (mg/L or mg/kg) - $IR$ = ingestion rate (L/day or kg/day) - $EF$ = exposure frequency (days/year) - $ED$ = exposure duration (years) - $BW$ = body weight (kg) - $AT$ = averaging time (days) **Step 2 — Define Reference Dose (RfD)** — empirically determined by US EPA as the daily exposure unlikely to cause adverse effects over a lifetime. **Step 3 — Form the Hazard Quotient (E2.8)** $$HQ = \dfrac{ADD}{RfD}$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation rule** $$HQ \leq 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Acceptable risk (no action required)}$$ $$HQ > 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Potential concern (mitigation / monitoring required)}$$ $$HQ > 10 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Significant risk (remediation mandatory)}$$ --- ## 3.6 Worked Example — HQ for Atrazine in Drinking Water **Problem:** Atrazine concentration in groundwater = **3.5 µg/L** = $3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ mg/L. Adult ingestion rate = **2 L/day**. Body weight = **70 kg**. RfD for atrazine = **0.0035 mg/kg/day**. Calculate HQ. **Step 1 — Calculate ADD (assuming continuous exposure so $EF = 365$ days, $AT = 365 \times 70$ days for non-cancer; for simplicity use per-day basis)** $$ADD = \dfrac{(3.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mg/L}) \cdot (2 \; \text{L/day})}{70 \; \text{kg}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mg/kg/day}$$ **Step 2 — Apply HQ formula** $$HQ = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0286$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation** $$HQ = 0.0286 < 1$$ > The exposure is **well within the safe limit** by a factor of ~35. No regulatory action is triggered, though cumulative exposure from multiple pesticides may shift the aggregate HQ above 1. --- ## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Pesticide Classification Snapshot | Pesticide | Class | Half-life (days) | log Kow | BCF (L/kg) | Stockholm POP? | KTU EIA Concern | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | DDT | Organochlorine | 6,000 (soil) | 6.91 | ~50,000 | Yes | **Critical** | | Aldrin | Organochlorine | 365 | 5.30 | ~12,000 | Yes | **Critical** | | Endosulfan | Organochlorine | 50–150 | 3.83 | ~2,300 | Yes (added 2011) | **High** (Kerala context) | | Atrazine | Triazine | 60–150 | 2.61 | ~12 | No | **Moderate** | | Malathion | Organophosphate | 1–7 | 2.75 | ~100 | No | **Low** | | Glyphosate | Phosphonate | 12–60 | -3.40 | <1 | No | **Moderate** (IARC 2A) | | 2,4-D | Chlorophenoxy | 10–50 | 2.81 | ~10 | No | **Low–Moderate** | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Pesticide Fate and Transport in Environment (Mermaid Flowchart) The following diagram maps the **fate and transport pathways** of a pesticide from its point of application through the various environmental compartments, ending at human and ecological receptors. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application Site
Agricultural Field] --> B[Atmospheric Volatilization] A --> C[Surface Runoff] A --> D[Soil Infiltration] A --> E[Plant Uptake] B --> F[Long Range Atmospheric Transport] B --> G[Wet and Dry Deposition] C --> H[Surface Water Bodies] C --> I[Sediment Adsorption] D --> J[Groundwater Aquifer] D --> K[Soil Microbial Degradation] E --> L[Food Chain Entry] F --> M[Remote Ecosystems] G --> N[Soil and Water Surfaces] H --> O[Aquatic Biota Uptake] I --> P[Benthic Organisms] J --> Q[Drinking Water Wells] K --> R[Breakdown Products Metabolites] L --> S[Livestock and Humans] O --> T[Bioaccumulation] P --> T S --> U[Human Exposure Dietary Dermal] T --> V[Biomagnification Trophic Transfer] V --> W[Top Predators Birds Mammals Humans] R --> X[Secondary Pollution
Often More Toxic] M --> Y[Polar Regions
Global Distillation] Q --> Z[Direct Human Ingestion] N --> A ``` ## 4.2 Biomagnification Pyramid (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following diagram represents a **biomagnification pyramid** showing concentration increase as one moves up the trophic levels, with DDT as a representative case. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Level1[Trophic Level 1 Primary Producers] P1[Phytoplankton
DDT 0.003 ppm] end subgraph Level2[Trophic Level 2 Primary Consumers] P2[Zooplankton and Small Fish
DDT 0.04 ppm] end subgraph Level3[Trophic Level 3 Secondary Consumers] P3[Small Predatory Fish
DDT 0.5 ppm] end subgraph Level4[Trophic Level 4 Tertiary Consumers] P4[Large Fish and Birds
DDT 2.0 ppm] end subgraph Level5[Trophic Level 5 Apex Predators] P5[Eagles Ospreys Humans
DDT 25 ppm] end Level1 --> Level2 Level2 --> Level3 Level3 --> Level4 Level4 --> Level5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Concentration magnification factor (water to humans) = 25 / 0.003 ≈ 8,333×** ## 4.3 EIA Decision-Support Block Diagram for Pesticide Projects This **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates how the EIA study is structured for a proposed pesticide manufacturing or large-scale application project. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Project Proposal
Pesticide Plant or Application] --> B[Screening Phase] B --> C{Is EIA Required?} C -->|No| D[Issue Consent with Conditions] C -->|Yes| E[Scoping Phase] E --> F[Baseline Environmental Studies
Soil Water Air Biota] F --> G[Impact Prediction
PEC PNEC BCF HQ] G --> H[Impact Evaluation
Matrix Leopold Network] H --> I[Significance Rating
Low Medium High Critical] I --> J{Is Impact Significant?} J -->|No| K[Proceed with EMP] J -->|Yes| L[Mitigation and Alternatives] L --> M[Environmental Management Plan] M --> N[Monitoring Protocol
Adaptive Management] N --> O[Audit and Review] O --> P[Long Term Surveillance] B --> Q[Public Consultation] E --> Q G --> Q Q --> R[Stakeholder Inputs] R --> I D --> S[Regulatory File Closure] P --> S ``` ## 4.4 Endosulfan Case Study — Kasaragod (Kerala) — Block Schematic The following Mermaid schematic maps the **Kasaragod Endosulfan Disaster** (1995–2011) into a structured cause-effect-mitigation flow used in teaching as a KTU case reference. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Kasaragod Endosulfan Stockpile
Cashew Plantation Aerial Spray] --> B[Aerial Drift to Nearby Villages] A --> C[Soil and Water Contamination] A --> D[Occupational Exposure of Sprayers] B --> E[Inhalation and Dermal Contact Residents] C --> F[Groundwater Contamination Wells] C --> G[Food Chain Entry Vegetables Milk] D --> H[Acute Poisoning Symptoms] E --> I[Chronic Health Effects
Cancer Birth Defects] F --> J[Drinking Water Exposure] G --> K[Biomagnification in Livestock and Humans] H --> L[Health Impact Assessment HIA] I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M[National Human Rights Commission Inquiry] L --> N[Supreme Court of India Ban 2011] M --> O[Relief and Rehabilitation Package] N --> P[Stockholm Convention Listing] O --> Q[Lessons Learnt for EIA] P --> Q Q --> R[Strengthened EIA for Pesticide Projects] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Kasaragod case** is treated as a **mandatory case study** in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3, illustrating the real-world failure of pre-2011 pesticide regulation in India. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define pesticide pollution. List any four categories of pesticides with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Pesticide pollution is the contamination of environmental compartments (soil, water, air, and living organisms) with pest-control chemicals at concentrations that cause ecological imbalance, human-health hazards, or ecosystem-service degradation. Four categories with examples: - **Insecticides** — DDT, Malathion - **Herbicides** — Atrazine, Glyphosate - **Fungicides** — Mancozeb, Captan - **Rodenticides** — Warfarin, Zinc Phosphide > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write "DDT" for every category. Marks are awarded for **variety and correct pairing**. Memorize at least **one specific example per category**. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between bioaccumulation and biomagnification.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Bioaccumulation | Biomagnification | |---|---|---| | Definition | Net accumulation of a substance in an organism from **all** environmental sources (water, food, sediment) over time | Progressive increase in concentration of a substance as it moves **up the food chain** through trophic transfer | | Pathway | Single organism, multiple routes | Multiple organisms, predator–prey relationship | | Metric | BCF (Bioconcentration Factor) | BMF (Biomagnification Factor) | | Example | DDT uptake in fish from contaminated water alone | DDT concentration: water (0.003 ppm) → zooplankton (0.04 ppm) → fish (0.5 ppm) → birds (2 ppm) | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner Pitfall:** Do NOT use the terms interchangeably. Awarded marks depend on showing that biomagnification is a **trophic-level concept**, not a single-organism concept. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Persistence, Bioaccumulation, and Toxicity (PBT) criteria** used for screening persistent organic pollutants (POPs) under the Stockholm Convention. List the original 12 POPs ("Dirty Dozen") and identify the criteria thresholds for each property. **(b) [7 Marks]** A pesticide has an initial concentration of $C_0 = 8.0$ mg/L in a contaminated lake. The first-order decay constant is $k = 0.035$ day⁻¹. Determine: (i) the concentration after **45 days**, (ii) the **half-life**, and (iii) whether the pesticide qualifies as "persistent" by Stockholm Convention criteria. --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** The **Stockholm Convention (2001)** uses three interlinked criteria to identify POPs: **1. Persistence (P)** — the substance must resist degradation: - Half-life in water > **40 days** - Half-life in soil > **120 days** - Half-life in sediment > **160 days** **2. Bioaccumulation (B)** — the substance must concentrate in organisms: - BCF or BAF > **5,000 L/kg** (revised from earlier 2,000 threshold) - log Kow > **5.0** (very lipophilic) **3. Toxicity (T)** — adverse effects at low concentrations: - NOEC < **0.01 mg/L** for aquatic organisms - Carcinogenic, Mutagenic, or Toxic for Reproduction (CMR category 1A/1B) **Long-Range Transport (LRT)** is an additional criterion, with measured levels in remote regions far from sources. **Original "Dirty Dozen" (12 POPs, 2001):** | Group | Compounds | |---|---| | Pesticides (9) | Aldrin, Chlordane, DDT, Dieldrin, Endrin, Heptachlor, Hexachlorobenzene (HCB), Mirex, Toxaphene | | Industrial chemicals (3) | Polychlorinated Biphenyls (PCBs), Hexachlorobenzene (also pesticide), Dioxins (PCDD), Furans (PCDF) | > Toxaphene was added later. **Endosulfan** was added in **2011** following the Kasaragod case. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Given:** $C_0 = 8.0$ mg/L, $k = 0.035$ day⁻¹, $t = 45$ days **Part (i) — Concentration after 45 days** Apply Equation E2.1: $$C_t = 8.0 \cdot e^{-(0.035)(45)} = 8.0 \cdot e^{-1.575}$$ Compute exponential: $e^{-1.575} = 0.2072$ $$C_{45} = 8.0 \times 0.2072 = 1.658 \; \text{mg/L}$$ **Part (ii) — Half-life** Apply Equation E2.2: $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{0.035} = 19.8 \; \text{days}$$ **Part (iii) — Persistence classification** Soil half-life = **19.8 days**. Comparing with the Stockholm threshold of **120 days** for soil: $$19.8 \; \text{days} < 120 \; \text{days}$$ > The pesticide is **NOT persistent** under Stockholm Convention soil criterion. However, the water half-life is 19.8 days < 40 days threshold — also non-persistent. **Conclusion: The compound fails the PBT persistence screen and would not be classified as a POP based on degradation alone.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Source-Pathway-Receptor (SPR) model** for pesticide pollution with reference to the **Kasaragod Endosulfan case study**. Identify the major sources, dominant pathways, and affected receptors. **(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **Bioconcentration Factor (BCF)** and **Hazard Quotient (HQ)** for a pesticide with the following properties: - Octanol-water partition coefficient $K_{ow} = 10^{4.2}$ - Water concentration $C_w = 1.5$ µg/L - Organism concentration $C_{org} = 0.85$ mg/kg - RfD = 0.005 mg/kg/day - Adult daily water ingestion = 2 L; body weight = 70 kg - Comment on whether the pesticide qualifies as "bioaccumulative" and whether the human exposure is acceptable. --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:** The **SPR model** is a fundamental EIA tool for tracing pollutant movement. **SOURCES in Kasaragod case:** - **Point source**: Endosulfan stockpiling facility (Plantation Corporation of Kerala) holding ~**1,500 tonnes** of obsolete and fresh pesticide - **Non-point source**: Aerial spraying on cashew plantations in surrounding hills - **Secondary source**: Contaminated soil leaching back to water bodies **PATHWAYS:** - **Atmospheric drift** of spray droplets to nearby villages (within 1–2 km) - **Surface runoff** into wells, streams, and paddy fields - **Soil adsorption** and persistence in laterite soils - **Food-chain transfer** via contaminated vegetables, milk, fish **RECEPTORS:** - **Human populations** in 11 affected panchayats (estimated 4,000+ residents with health impacts) - **Spraying workers** (acute occupational exposure) - **Domestic animals** (livestock biomagnification) - **Aquatic ecosystems** (river Meenachil, Kattampally) - **Sensitive groups**: pregnant women, children, foetuses (developmental defects) **Documented health impacts:** Cancer, congenital deformities, neurological disorders, reproductive failures, and mass mortality of farm animals. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:** **Part 1 — BCF from water concentration directly** $$BCF = \dfrac{C_{organism}}{C_{water}} = \dfrac{0.85 \; \text{mg/kg}}{1.5 \; \mu\text{g/L}} = \dfrac{0.85}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 566.7 \; \text{L/kg}$$ **Part 2 — BCF from $K_{ow}$ using Equation E2.4** $\log K_{ow} = 4.2$ $$\log BCF = 0.85 \cdot (4.2) - 0.70 = 3.57 - 0.70 = 2.87$$ $$BCF = 10^{2.87} = 741 \; \text{L/kg}$$ **Part 3 — Bioaccumulation assessment** The measured BCF (566.7) and predicted BCF (741) are both well below the REACH bioaccumulation threshold of **2,000 L/kg** and the Stockholm Convention revised threshold of **5,000 L/kg**. > **Conclusion:** The pesticide does **NOT** qualify as bioaccumulative under either framework. **Part 4 — HQ Calculation** $$ADD = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{mg/L}) \cdot (2 \; \text{L/day})}{70 \; \text{kg}} = 4.286 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{mg/kg/day}$$ $$HQ = \dfrac{4.286 \times 10^{-5}}{0.005} = 0.00857$$ **Part 5 — Risk interpretation** $$HQ = 0.00857 \ll 1$$ > Human exposure via drinking water is **highly acceptable** (more than 100× below the safety threshold). However, **aggregate exposure** from multiple sources (food, air, soil) and **mixture toxicity** with other pesticides should be evaluated in a full EIA. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Pesticide Pollution Questions:** > 1. **Units mixing**: µg/L vs mg/L vs mg/kg — losing 1–2 marks for incorrect unit conversion > 2. **Log vs antilog confusion**: Forgetting to exponentiate log BCF back to BCF > 3. **Misapplication of decay formula**: Using second-order kinetics for a first-order problem > 4. **Omitting environmental compartments**: Incomplete SPR model loses marks; ensure you cover all three > 5. **Confusing BCF with BMF**: Marks specifically allocated for trophic-level distinction --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Pesticide Pollution (Module 3):** - **PBT criteria** for POPs: Persistence (half-life thresholds), Bioaccumulation (BCF > 5000), Toxicity (NOEC < 0.01 mg/L) - **Dirty Dozen** (12 original Stockholm POPs, 2001) — 9 pesticides + 3 industrial chemicals; Endosulfan added 2011 - **First-order decay equation**: $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ and half-life $t_{1/2} = 0.693/k$ - **BCF formula**: $C_{organism} / C_{water}$; LFER approximation $\log BCF = 0.85 \log K_{ow} - 0.70$ - **Hazard Quotient**: $HQ = ADD / RfD$; if $> 1$, mitigation required - **SPR model** is mandatory in every KTU EIA pesticide question - **Kasaragod Endosulfan** is a **mandatory case study** — know sources, pathways, and receptors - **DDT facts**: $\log K_{ow} = 6.91$, BCF ≈ 50,000, half-life ≈ 6,000 days, biomagnification factor > 8,000× from water to apex predators - **Routes of human exposure**: dietary (~90%), inhalation, dermal, transplacental - **Classification memory hook**: Organochlorines = persistent + bioaccumulative; Organophosphates = less persistent + more toxic acutely; Carbamates = reversible toxicity - **Stockholm Convention** entry into force: **17 May 2004**; India ratified **13 January 2006** - **India's Insecticides Act, 1968** governs manufacture, sale, and use; **Banning of Endosulfan** by Supreme Court: **13 May 2011** - **PEC/PNEC ratio** is the **trigger metric** for full EIA in pesticide projects (ratio > 1 = mandatory EIS) - **Cumulative and synergistic effects** must be considered — multiple pesticides in combination can be toxic even when each is below individual HQ = 1 - **Memorize**: Atrazine MCL = 3 µg/L; DDT MCL = 0.1 mg/L (historical); Endosulfan = banned in India (2011)

topics.title: Effect on Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effect on Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term **"Effect on Environment"** refers to the systematic identification, prediction, evaluation, and documentation of the probable consequences — both **adverse (negative)** and **beneficial (positive)** — that a proposed developmental project, programme, or activity is likely to impose on the various components of the natural and socio-economic environment. These effects are quantified or qualified in terms of magnitude, extent, duration, frequency, reversibility, and cumulative impact on environmental receptors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Effect on Environment encompasses the changes induced in physical, chemical, biological, geological, climatic, and socio-economic parameters of the surroundings due to project activities during the **pre-construction, construction, operation, and decommissioning phases** of the project life cycle.

The principal environmental components upon which effects are evaluated are categorized as:

- **Physical Environment:** Air, Water, Soil/ Land, Noise, Climate
- **Biological Environment:** Flora, Fauna, Ecosystems, Biodiversity
- **Socio-Economic Environment:** Human health, Livelihoods, Cultural heritage, Aesthetics

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Stones in a Pond" Model**
>
> Imagine you are throwing a large stone (the **project**, e.g., a new highway, dam, or factory) into a calm pond (the **environment**). The instant the stone hits the water:
> - The water near the splash **shoots upward** (acute local impact)
> - **Ripples spread** outward in every direction (regional impact)
> - Mud at the bottom gets stirred (cumulative and long-term impact)
> - Fish nearby either **flee or get hurt** (biological impact)
> - Birds flying overhead **change path** (fauna displacement)
> - People standing on the bank get **splashed** (socio-economic impact)
>
> **EIA is essentially the "Pond Ripple Study"** — predicting how big the splash will be, how far the ripples travel, how long they last, and whether the pond will recover once the stone sinks. If the splash is too big or the ripples disturb sensitive species, the engineer must either **choose a smaller stone** (alternative site/design) or **build a retaining wall** (mitigation measures).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** Just remember the **5 S's of Environmental Effect** — **S**ource (Project Activity), **S**pread (Pathway), **S**ensitive Receptor (Target), **S**everity (Magnitude), and **S**olution (Mitigation). Every EIA effect follows this chain.

## 1.3 Standard Environmental Metrics Used

The following key **indicators and standard metrics** are evaluated when assessing effects on the environment:

- **PM₂.₅ and PM₁₀** (Particulate Matter in µg/m³)
- **SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, O₃** (Gaseous pollutants in µg/m³ or ppm)
- **Noise Levels** in **dB(A)** with reference to **CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board)** norms
- **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** in mg/L
- **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** in mg/L
- **pH, TDS, DO (Dissolved Oxygen)** for water quality
- **Noise Climate Index (NCI)** = $L_{10} - L_{90}$
- **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq)** in dB(A)

## 1.4 Why "Effect on Environment" is the Heart of EIA

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The entire EIA process revolves around predicting and managing **effects**. Without a clear understanding of the **cause–effect–consequence chain**, the EIA report is reduced to a mere descriptive document and loses its decision-making utility before the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC)** and **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Environmental Effects

The effects of a project on the environment can be systematically classified along **seven key dimensions**. KTU examiners frequently test these classifications.

### 2.1.1 By Nature of Impact

- **Adverse / Negative Effects:** Damage to ecosystems, pollution, displacement.
- **Beneficial / Positive Effects:** Employment generation, improved infrastructure, afforestation, flood control.

### 2.1.2 By Spatial Extent

- **Local / On-site:** Limited to project boundary (e.g., construction dust).
- **Regional:** Spreads to 10–100 km radius (e.g., acid rain from thermal power plant).
- **Global / Transboundary:** Crosses national boundaries (e.g., GHG emissions, ozone depletion).

### 2.1.3 By Temporal Duration

- **Short-term / Temporary:** Lasting days to months (e.g., construction noise).
- **Long-term / Permanent:** Lasting years to centuries (e.g., land-use change due to dam).

### 2.1.4 By Reversibility

- **Reversible:** Environment recovers after the stressor is removed (e.g., air pollution stops when plant shuts down).
- **Irreversible:** Environment cannot return to original state (e.g., extinction of species, deforestation of primary forest).

### 2.1.5 By Magnitude / Intensity

- **High / Severe:** Exceeds regulatory standards, irreversible damage.
- **Moderate:** Detectable, manageable with mitigation.
- **Low / Negligible:** Within natural variability.

### 2.1.6 By Probability / Likelihood

- **Certain:** Will definitely occur.
- **Probable:** Likely to occur.
- **Unlikely / Remote:** Low chance.

### 2.1.7 By Cumulative Nature

- **Single / Direct Impact:** From one project activity.
- **Cumulative Impact:** Combined effect of multiple projects or stressors over time and space (e.g., multiple dams on a river basin).

## 2.2 The Cause–Effect Chain (Source–Pathway–Receptor Model)

Every environmental effect can be decomposed into three essential links:

1. **Source (Stressor):** The project activity that generates the impact (e.g., DG set, effluent discharge pipeline, blasting operation).
2. **Pathway (Medium of Transfer):** The route through which the impact travels (air, water, soil, groundwater, food chain).
3. **Receptor (Target):** The environmental or human component that experiences the impact (humans, flora, fauna, water body, soil, structure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mitigation Logic:** If **any one** of the three links (Source, Pathway, Receptor) is broken or eliminated, the impact is prevented. This is the foundational logic behind the **3R Mitigation Hierarchy: Remove → Reduce → Repair**.

## 2.3 Component-Wise Detailed Effects

### 2.3.1 Effects on Air Environment

| Pollutant | Primary Source | Major Effect | Standard (CPCB NAAQS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | Construction dust, vehicle emissions, combustion | Respiratory diseases, smog, reduced visibility | PM₂.₅ ≤ 60 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| SO₂ | Coal/ oil burning | Acid rain, bronchitis | ≤ 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| NOₓ | High-temperature combustion | Smog, respiratory illness, eutrophication | ≤ 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| CO | Incomplete combustion | Headaches, asphyxiation (binds haemoglobin) | ≤ 4 mg/m³ (1-hr) |
| CO₂ | Fossil fuel combustion | Global warming, climate change | No ambient standard |
| VOC / HC | Solvents, paints, refineries | Photochemical smog, cancer risk | Variable |
| Heavy Metals (Pb, Hg, Cd) | Industrial emissions, incinerators | Neurological damage, kidney disease | Pb ≤ 1.0 µg/m³ (24-hr) |

### 2.3.2 Effects on Water Environment

- **Surface Water Contamination:** Discharge of untreated industrial effluent (containing heavy metals, acids, alkalis, dyes) into rivers, lakes.
- **Groundwater Depletion:** Excessive extraction lowering water table; contamination via leaching.
- **Thermal Pollution:** Hot water discharge from thermal/nuclear power plants reduces DO and disrupts aquatic life.
- **Eutrophication:** Nutrient loading (N, P) from agricultural runoff and sewage causes algal blooms, fish kills.
- **Saline Intrusion:** Coastal projects cause salt water to enter freshwater aquifers.

### 2.3.3 Effects on Soil / Land Environment

- **Soil Erosion:** Loss of topsoil due to vegetation clearance for construction.
- **Soil Contamination:** Spillage of chemicals, pesticides, oil, heavy metals.
- **Land Use Change:** Conversion of agricultural/forest land to industrial use.
- **Soil Compaction:** Heavy machinery reduces porosity and root penetration.
- **Desertification:** In arid zones, improper project siting accelerates desert spread.

### 2.3.4 Effects on Noise Environment

| Source | Typical Level (dB A) | Effect on Human Health |
|---|---|---|
| Whisper | 20 – 30 | None |
| Normal conversation | 50 – 60 | Comfortable |
| Heavy traffic | 70 – 85 | Annoyance |
| Construction / blasting | 90 – 110 | Hearing loss, hypertension |
| Jet aircraft / DG sets | 110 – 140 | Severe damage, irritability |

> [!NOTE]
> **CPCB Ambient Noise Standards (India):**
> - Industrial Area: **75 dB(A)** day / **70 dB(A)** night
> - Commercial Area: **65 dB(A)** day / **55 dB(A)** night
> - Residential Area: **55 dB(A)** day / **45 dB(A)** night
> - Silence Zone (hospitals, schools): **50 dB(A)** day / **40 dB(A)** night

### 2.3.5 Effects on Biological Environment (Flora & Fauna)

- **Habitat Loss & Fragmentation:** Linear projects (roads, canals, pipelines) divide ecosystems.
- **Deforestation & Loss of Biodiversity:** Clearing of vegetation reduces species richness.
- **Wildlife Displacement & Mortality:** Animal-vehicle collisions, electrocution, poaching.
- **Disruption of Migration Patterns:** Birds, fish (e.g., salmon, eels) affected by dams and tall structures.
- **Endangered Species Risk:** Threat to Schedule I species under Wildlife Protection Act, 1972.

### 2.3.6 Effects on Socio-Economic & Human Environment

- **Displacement & Rehabilitation:** Loss of homes, especially in large dam/industrial projects.
- **Loss of Livelihood:** Farmers, fishermen, forest-dwellers (tribal communities) most affected.
- **Health Impacts:** Increased morbidity from pollution (asthma, cancer, waterborne diseases).
- **Cultural & Archaeological Loss:** Destruction of heritage sites, sacred groves.
- **Positive Effects:** Employment, infrastructure, improved services, tax revenue.

### 2.3.7 Effects on Climate

- **Global Warming:** CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs emissions.
- **Microclimate Changes:** Urban Heat Island (UHI) effect, altered rainfall patterns.
- **Albedo Change:** Large reservoirs and cleared land reflect more solar radiation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Equivalent Sound Level (Leq) | $$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right]$$ | Used in noise impact assessment |
| 2 | Noise Climate Index | $NCI = L_{10} - L_{90}$ (dB) | Measures noise variability |
| 3 | Air Quality Index (AQI) | Sub-index formula: $I_{p} = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}}(C_{p} - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | CPCB AQI Calculation |
| 4 | Wind Rose Power Law | $$V_{z} = V_{r}\left(\frac{z}{z_{r}}\right)^{n}$$ | Predicting pollutant dispersion height |
| 5 | Plume Rise (Briggs Equation) | $$\Delta h = \frac{1.6 \cdot F^{1/3} \cdot x^{2/3}}{u}$$ | Stack height design for emissions |
| 6 | Groundwater Drawdown | $$s = \frac{Q}{2\pi T}\left[\ln\left(\frac{R}{r}\right) - 0.5\right]$$ | Cone of depression around well |
| 7 | Soil Erosion (USLE) | $$A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$$ | Predicting average annual soil loss (tonnes/ha/yr) |
| 8 | BOD Reaction Rate | $$L_{t} = L_{0}\,(1 - e^{-k t})$$ | Oxygen demand decay in water body |
| 9 | Streeter–Phelps DO Sag | $$D_{t} = \frac{k_{1}L_{0}}{k_{2} - k_{1}}\left[e^{-k_{1} t} - e^{-k_{2} t}\right] + D_{0}\,e^{-k_{2} t}$$ | Critical dissolved oxygen deficit |
| 10 | Greenhouse Gas CO₂eq | $$CO_{2eq} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} G_{i} \times GWP_{i}$$ | Cumulative climate impact |

Where:
- $L(t)$ = instantaneous sound level at time $t$
- $V_z, V_r$ = wind speed at height $z$ and reference height $z_r$
- $n$ = stability-dependent exponent (typically 0.143 – 0.40)
- $F$ = buoyancy flux, $u$ = wind speed, $x$ = downwind distance
- $R, K, LS, C, P$ = rainfall, erodibility, slope-length, cover, practice factors
- $L_0$ = initial BOD, $k$ = deoxygenation constant
- $GWP$ = Global Warming Potential (CO₂=1, CH₄=25, N₂O=298 per IPCC)

> [!TIP]
> **For 3-mark KTU questions**, memorizing only **Leq formula**, **Wind Rose Power Law**, and **USLE** is usually sufficient to score full marks. Examiner's commonly test these.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Air dispersion modeling** using **AERMOD / ISCST3** for industrial siting decisions.
- **Noise mapping** for highway and airport projects to design **sound barriers**.
- **Hydrological modeling** (HEC-RAS, MODFLOW) for predicting **downstream impacts of dams**.
- **Carbon footprint estimation** for **Climate Impact Assessment** in line with **NDC (Nationally Determined Contributions)** commitments under the **Paris Agreement**.
- **Environmental Sensitivity Index (ESI)** mapping for coastal and offshore projects.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$)

The $L_{eq}$ is the most important and most-tested formula in the noise component of EIA. It expresses the constant sound level that, over a given time period, would deliver the same total acoustic energy as the actual time-varying sound.

### Step 1: Define Sound Pressure Squared
The intensity of sound is proportional to the square of the sound pressure $p(t)$. Therefore, the mean-square sound pressure over time $T$ is:

$$
\overline{p^{2}} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} p^{2}(t)\, dt
$$

### Step 2: Relate Mean-Square Pressure to Sound Level
By definition of sound pressure level in decibels:

$$
L(t) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p(t)}{p_{ref}}\right)
$$

where $p_{ref}$ = 20 µPa is the reference sound pressure. Solving for $p(t)$:

$$
p(t) = p_{ref} \cdot 10^{L(t)/20}
$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Mean-Square Integral
Squaring $p(t)$:

$$
p^{2}(t) = p_{ref}^{2} \cdot 10^{L(t)/10}
$$

Plugging into the mean-square expression:

$$
\overline{p^{2}} = \frac{p_{ref}^{2}}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt
$$

### Step 4: Define the Equivalent Continuous Level
The $L_{eq}$ is defined as the level corresponding to this mean-square pressure:

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\overline{p^{2}}}{p_{ref}^{2}}\right)
$$

### Step 5: Combine the Equations
Substituting the integral form:

$$
L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right]
$$

This is the final working equation. It is the **backbone of all noise impact assessments** in EIA studies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A doubling of acoustic energy corresponds to a **3 dB increase** in $L_{eq}$, not 6 dB. This is a classic KTU exam trap — students often erroneously write 6 dB.

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Universal Soil Loss Equation (USLE)

The USLE predicts the long-term **average annual soil loss** $A$ in tonnes per hectare per year. It is widely used in EIA for hill-road, mining, and reservoir projects.

### Step 1: Write the Base Equation
$$
A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P
$$

### Step 2: Explain Each Factor
- **$R$ — Rainfall Erosivity Factor:** It quantifies the erosive power of rainfall. It is computed as:

$$
R = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{m}(EI_{30})_{jk}
$$

where $EI_{30}$ is the storm's kinetic energy × maximum 30-minute intensity.

- **$K$ — Soil Erodibility Factor:** It is the rate of soil loss per unit of $R$ from a standard plot (slope 9%, length 22.13 m, continuously fallow). Units: $t \cdot h \cdot ha^{-1} \cdot MJ^{-1} \cdot mm^{-1}$.

- **$LS$ — Topographic Factor:** It combines slope length $L$ and slope steepness $S$:

$$
LS = \left(\frac{\lambda}{22.13}\right)^{m} \left(65.41 \sin^{2}\theta + 4.56 \sin\theta + 0.065\right)
$$

where $\lambda$ = slope length (m), $\theta$ = slope angle, $m$ = 0.4 for slopes < 1%, 0.5 for ~1–3%, 0.6 for ~3–5%, 0.7 for >5%.

- **$C$ — Cover Management Factor:** It is the ratio of soil loss from land with specified cover to that from fallow. Ranges from **0 (very good cover)** to **1.5 (bare soil)**.

- **$P$ — Conservation Practice Factor:** It accounts for conservation practices (contour ploughing, terracing). Ranges from **0.1 (excellent)** to **1.0 (no conservation)**.

### Step 3: Numerical Illustration
For a hill road project in Kerala with the following data:
- $R = 350$ MJ·mm/(ha·h·yr)
- $K = 0.32$ t·h/(MJ·mm)
- $\lambda = 100$ m, $\theta = 12°$, so $m = 0.5$
- $C = 0.25$ (partly vegetated)
- $P = 0.6$ (moderate terracing)

**Step 3a: Calculate $LS$**
$$
\sin 12° = 0.2079
$$
$$
LS = \left(\frac{100}{22.13}\right)^{0.5}\left[65.41(0.2079)^2 + 4.56(0.2079) + 0.065\right]
$$
$$
LS = (4.518) \cdot \left[2.827 + 0.948 + 0.065\right]
$$
$$
LS = 4.518 \cdot 3.840 = 17.35
$$

**Step 3b: Compute $A$**
$$
A = 350 \times 0.32 \times 17.35 \times 0.25 \times 0.6
$$
$$
A = 350 \times 0.32 = 112
$$
$$
A = 112 \times 17.35 = 1943.2
$$
$$
A = 1943.2 \times 0.25 = 485.8
$$
$$
A = 485.8 \times 0.6 = 291.48 \text{ tonnes/ha/yr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An erosion rate of **~291 t/ha/yr** is **far above** the tolerable soil loss limit (typically 5–11 t/ha/yr). This indicates the project will cause **severe land degradation** if not mitigated through terracing, vegetative cover, and silt traps.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Significant Impact Identification (Leopold Matrix Concept)

The **Leopold Matrix** (1971) is a qualitative–quantitative tool for impact identification. It cross-tabulates **project actions** (rows) with **environmental components** (columns). Each cell contains two values:
- **Magnitude (1 to 10):** Severity of impact
- **Importance (1 to 10):** Significance / weight of the affected component

$$
S_{ij} = M_{ij} \times I_{ij}
$$

where $S_{ij}$ is the **significance score** for the interaction between action $i$ and component $j$.

### Worked Example Matrix

| Project Action | Air | Water | Soil | Flora | Fauna | Human |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Land Clearing | 5/7 | 3/5 | 8/9 | 9/10 | 8/9 | 6/8 |
| Excavation | 6/6 | 5/6 | 9/10 | 5/7 | 4/6 | 7/7 |
| Construction | 8/8 | 4/5 | 6/6 | 4/5 | 4/5 | 9/9 |
| Operation | 9/10 | 7/8 | 3/5 | 5/6 | 6/7 | 8/9 |
| Decommissioning | 5/6 | 4/5 | 5/6 | 6/7 | 5/6 | 4/5 |

> [!NOTE]
> The highest significance scores ($S > 70$) should be prioritized for detailed quantitative impact assessment and mitigation design.
