# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definitions)

### Question 1: Classify the effects of a project on the environment along any three relevant axes. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

The effects of a project on the environment can be classified along multiple axes. Three important classifications are:

**1. By Nature of Impact**
- **Adverse (Negative):** Pollution, deforestation, displacement, health hazards, biodiversity loss.
- **Beneficial (Positive):** Employment generation, improved infrastructure, flood control, afforestation, tax revenue, technology transfer.

**2. By Spatial Extent**
- **Local / On-site:** Confined to project boundary — e.g., construction dust, noise, soil compaction within 500 m.
- **Regional:** Spreads to 10–100 km radius — e.g., acid rain from a thermal power plant affecting an entire district.
- **Global / Transboundary:** Crosses national boundaries — e.g., greenhouse gas emissions contributing to global warming, persistent organic pollutants (POPs) transported across continents.

**3. By Reversibility**
- **Reversible:** Environment recovers once the stressor is removed — e.g., air pollution subsides when a factory shuts down; noise levels return to baseline after blasting is complete.
- **Irreversible:** Environment cannot return to the original state — e.g., extinction of an endemic species, destruction of a primary forest, draining of a wetland, topographical alteration of a hill.

> [!NOTE]
> Other valid classifications include: **by temporal duration (short-term / long-term)**, **by magnitude (high / moderate / low)**, and **by probability (certain / probable / unlikely)**. Examiners may accept any three well-explained axes.

---

### Question 2: Define "Cumulative Impact Assessment" and list two examples from infrastructure projects. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style] [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA) is the systematic process of evaluating the combined effects of a proposed project with other existing, planned, or reasonably foreseeable future projects and activities, over space and time. It recognizes that the total impact of multiple stressors acting together can be **greater than the sum of individual impacts** — known as **synergistic effects**.

**Mathematical Representation:**
$$
CI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} I_i + \sum_{i \neq j} S_{ij}
$$

where $I_i$ = individual impact, $S_{ij}$ = synergistic interaction between impacts.

**Two Examples:**
1. **River Basin Development:** Multiple dams constructed on the same river (e.g., cascades on the Ganga, Brahmaputra, or Narmada) cumulatively reduce downstream flow, alter sediment transport, fragment fish migration routes, and degrade the delta ecosystem — even if each dam individually has acceptable impacts.
2. **Coastal Zone Development:** A combination of a port, power plant, desalination unit, and tourism resort along the same coastline cumulatively alters coastal hydrodynamics, increases cumulative thermal pollution, destroys mangrove habitats, and causes shoreline erosion that no single project would cause alone.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always mention **time + space + synergistic interaction** when defining CIA — these three keywords are the examiner's scoring anchors.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (Choice 1) [14 Marks]**

#### (a) Discuss in detail the major effects of a thermal power plant on the physical and biological environment. Propose suitable mitigation measures for each. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Effects on Air Environment [2 Marks]**
- **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅):** Emitted from coal handling, ash disposal, and combustion. Causes respiratory ailments, bronchitis, asthma, and reduced visibility.
- **SO₂ and NOₓ:** Formed during combustion of sulphur-containing coal and at high temperatures respectively. Lead to **acid rain**, photochemical smog, corrosion of structures, and eutrophication of water bodies.
- **CO, CO₂, and Mercury (Hg):** CO₂ contributes to **global warming**; mercury and other heavy metals are toxic and bioaccumulative.
- **Fly Ash:** A major solid waste; contains heavy metals and can become airborne.

**2. Effects on Water Environment [1.5 Marks]**
- **Thermal Pollution:** Cooling water discharge raises receiving water temperature by 7–10°C, reducing **dissolved oxygen (DO)**, stressing aquatic life, and promoting algal growth.
- **Ash Pond Runoff:** Leachate containing heavy metals (As, Cd, Se) contaminates surface and groundwater.
- **High Water Consumption:** Thermal power plants consume **~2000–3000 m³ of water per MW per hour**, leading to depletion of local water resources.

**3. Effects on Soil and Land Environment [1 Mark]**
- **Fly Ash Disposal:** Requires vast land (1 acre per MW for ash ponds); contaminates soil with heavy metals; reduces soil fertility.
- **Mining of Coal:** Causes land subsidence, deforestation, and acid mine drainage.

**4. Effects on Biological Environment [1.5 Marks]**
- **Deforestation** at coal mine sites destroys flora and fauna habitat.
- **Thermal shock** to fish and aquatic invertebrates.
- **Acid rain** damages forests, soils, and freshwater ecosystems.
- **Bird mortality** from fly ash ponds and tall chimneys (collisions).

**5. Effects on Socio-Economic Environment [1 Mark]**
- Positive: Employment, infrastructure, electricity supply.
- Negative: Displacement of nearby villages, respiratory health issues, reduced agricultural yield due to ash fall.

**Mitigation Measures [Bonus Marks]**
- **Air:** Electrostatic precipitators (>99.9% efficiency), Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD), selective catalytic reduction (SCR) for NOₓ, tall stacks with rainwater protection.
- **Water:** Cooling towers, closed-cycle cooling, ash water recycling, zero liquid discharge (ZLD) systems.
- **Soil:** Dry fly ash collection, ash utilization in cement/brick making, ash pond lining with HDPE.
- **Biological:** Green belt development (3-tier, 250 m wide), bird deflectors on chimneys, compensatory afforestation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students list **only the negative effects** and forget the **positive effects (employment, power supply)**. Since the question says "effects" (not "adverse effects"), you **lose 0.5–1 mark** if you ignore positives.

---

#### (b) Explain the Leopold Matrix method for impact identification. How is the significance score computed? What are its limitations? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Origin and Concept [1 Mark]**
The Leopold Matrix was developed by **Luna B. Leopold et al. in 1971** as one of the earliest systematic checklists for EIA. It is a **qualitative–quantitative** impact identification tool that cross-tabulates **project actions** (rows) with **environmental components** (columns).

**2. Structure of the Matrix [1.5 Marks]**
- **Rows:** Project actions (e.g., site clearance, excavation, blasting, construction, operation, waste disposal, decommissioning) — typically **88–100 actions**.
- **Columns:** Environmental components (e.g., air, water, soil, flora, fauna, land use, aesthetics, socio-economic) — typically **70–80 components**.
- **Each cell** contains two numerical values:
  - **Magnitude (M):** Severity of impact on a scale of **1 to 10** (with sign: + for beneficial, − for adverse, often split into two columns).
  - **Importance (I):** Weight or significance of the affected component on a scale of **1 to 10**.

**3. Computation of Significance Score [1.5 Marks]**
The significance score $S_{ij}$ for the interaction between action $i$ and component $j$ is:

$$
S_{ij} = M_{ij} \times I_{ij}
$$

The **total significance score** for the entire project is:

$$
S_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{m}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij} \cdot I_{ij}
$$

**4. Interpretation [1 Mark]**
- High $S$ values (>70) → High priority for detailed impact assessment and mitigation.
- Medium $S$ values (30–70) → Standard mitigation.
- Low $S$ values (<30) → Routine monitoring.

**5. Limitations [2 Marks]**
- **Subjectivity:** Both magnitude and importance scores are highly subjective and depend on expert judgment — inter-rater variability is large.
- **No Temporal Component:** Does not distinguish between short-term and long-term impacts.
- **No Spatial Aggregation:** Does not account for spatial overlap or cumulative impacts.
- **Equal Weighting Assumption:** Treats all components with the same scale, but a 7/10 in human health may outweigh 9/10 in aesthetics.
- **No Cause-Effect Linkage:** Does not show the pathway or mechanism.
- **Limited to Identification:** It only identifies and scores; cannot predict, mitigate, or evaluate alternatives.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** Always conclude the limitations answer with a sentence on **"Despite these limitations, the Leopold Matrix remains a useful first-cut screening tool"** — this earns the examiner's goodwill.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) [14 Marks]**

#### (a) Describe the major effects of a hydroelectric dam on the aquatic ecosystem, hydrology, and downstream environment. What is the concept of environmental flow? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Effects on Aquatic Ecosystem [2 Marks]**
- **Blocking of Fish Migration:** Dams obstruct the natural upstream and downstream movement of migratory fish (e.g., Indian major carps, mahseer, hilsa, eel), affecting their breeding and survival.
- **Changes in Water Chemistry:** Reservoir stratification, low DO in hypolimnion, and accumulation of methane (CH₄) in deep water.
- **Sediment Trapping:** Reservoirs trap silt, reducing nutrient supply to downstream floodplains, affecting soil fertility and aquatic productivity.
- **Submergence of Terrestrial Habitat:** Large reservoir submerges forests, agricultural land, and villages, displacing wildlife and humans.

**2. Effects on Hydrology [2 Marks]**
- **Altered Flow Regime:** Seasonal flood pulses are suppressed; downstream flow becomes unnaturally regulated.
- **Reduced Sediment Load:** Silt accumulates in the reservoir; downstream rivers become "hungry" and erode their banks and beds aggressively.
- **Temperature and DO Changes:** Cold water released from deep reservoir affects downstream aquatic life.
- **Groundwater Changes:** Reduced groundwater recharge in floodplains; water table fluctuations in reservoir periphery.

**3. Effects on Downstream Environment [2 Marks]**
- **Loss of Riparian Vegetation:** Reduced flooding starves floodplain flora.
- **Coastal Erosion (Delta):** Reduced sediment delivery causes delta erosion (e.g., Nile, Indus, Godavari deltas).
- **Salinity Intrusion:** Reduced freshwater flow allows saline seawater to penetrate inland aquifers and estuaries.
- **Fishery Decline:** River-dependent fisheries collapse downstream.

**4. Concept of Environmental Flow (E-Flow) [1 Mark]**
**Environmental flow** is the quantity, timing, frequency, and quality of water flow required to sustain freshwater and estuarine ecosystems and the human livelihoods that depend on them. It is determined through methodologies such as:

- **Hydrological Methods:** Tennant Method (Montana Method), Variable Flow Methods.
- **Hydraulic Methods:** Wetted perimeter approach.
- **Habitat Simulation Methods:** PHABSIM (Physical Habitat Simulation).
- **Holistic Methods:** Building Block Methodology, DRIFT (Downstream Response to Imposed Flow Transformations).

$$
Q_{e-flow} = f(\text{ecological requirement, geomorphology, water quality, livelihoods})
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only about **displacement** (socio-economic) and forget the **physical hydrological and ecological** effects. A hydroelectric dam question is **80% about hydrology and ecology**, not just displacement.

---

#### (b) Discuss the methodology for assessing impact on air environment in an EIA study. List the major air pollutants and their source-specific standards as per CPCB. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. Methodology for Air Impact Assessment [3 Marks]**
The assessment follows a standard four-step framework:

**Step 1 — Baseline Data Collection**
- Identify **meteorological parameters**: wind speed, wind direction, temperature, humidity, mixing height, stability class (Pasquill–Gifford A–F), precipitation, atmospheric pressure.
- Measure **existing ambient air quality (AAQ)** at strategic receptor locations using **Respirable Dust Samplers (RDS)** and **Fine Particulate Samplers (FPS)** for PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅.
- Use **gas analyzers** for SO₂ (West-Gaeke method), NOₓ (Jacob & Hochheiser method), CO (NDIR analyzer), and VOC (PID/FID).
- **Frequency:** 24-hourly samples, 8 days per season for one year (per CPCB guidelines).

**Step 2 — Identification of Sources**
- **Point sources:** Stacks, vents, DG sets.
- **Line sources:** Roads, railways.
- **Area sources:** Mining benches, stockyards, townships.
- **Volume sources:** Quarries, open construction sites.

**Step 3 — Prediction of Concentrations**
- Use **dispersion models** such as:
  - **Gaussian Plume Model (ISCST3, AERMOD, CALPUFF)** for point/area sources.
  - **CALINE, CAL3QHCR** for line/roadway sources.
  - **AERSCREEN** for screening-level assessment.
- Key inputs: emission rate (g/s), stack height, exit velocity, exit temperature, downwind distance, stability class.

**Step 4 — Comparison with Standards & Impact Evaluation**
- Compare predicted Ground Level Concentrations (GLC) with **CPCB National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**.
- Compute **incremental GLC** = predicted − baseline.
- Determine **significance** using magnitude, frequency, reversibility, and exceedance factor.

**2. Major Air Pollutants and CPCB NAAQS (2009, revised 2024) [3 Marks]**

| Pollutant | Time-weighted Average | Industrial Area | Residential/Rural | Ecological Sensitive |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| SO₂ | 24-hr | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ |
| NO₂ | 24-hr | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ |
| PM₁₀ | 24-hr / Annual | 100 / 60 µg/m³ | 100 / 60 µg/m³ | 100 / 60 µg/m³ |
| PM₂.₅ | 24-hr / Annual | 60 / 40 µg/m³ | 60 / 40 µg/m³ | 60 / 40 µg/m³ |
| CO | 8-hr | 2 mg/m³ | 2 mg/m³ | 2 mg/m³ |
| O₃ | 8-hr | 100 µg/m³ | 100 µg/m³ | 100 µg/m³ |
| Pb | 24-hr / Annual | 1.0 / 0.5 µg/m³ | 1.0 / 0.5 µg/m³ | 1.0 / 0.5 µg/m³ |
| NH₃ | 24-hr | 400 µg/m³ | 400 µg/m³ | 400 µg/m³ |
| Benzene | Annual | 5 µg/m³ | 5 µg/m³ | 5 µg/m³ |

**3. Mitigation Measures [1 Mark]**
- Electrostatic precipitators / bag filters for particulates.
- FGD for SO₂.
- SCR / SNCR for NOₓ.
- Green belt development (3-tier, 250 m).
- Use of cleaner fuels (CNG, LNG, biomass) and renewable energy.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Errors:**
> 1. Confusing **NAAQS (CPCB)** with **WHO Guidelines** (WHO values are 2–4× stricter).
> 2. Using **mg/m³ instead of µg/m³** for PM and SO₂.
> 3. Forgetting to mention **meteorological data collection** — without it, dispersion modeling is invalid.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 7 Marks-Loss Zones in "Effect on Environment" Questions:**
> 1. **Defining effect as "only adverse"** — Examiners expect both adverse and beneficial. Lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Cause–Effect Chain** with Cause–Response Model. The EIA standard is **Source–Pathway–Receptor**.
> 3. **Writing units incorrectly** — dB(A) for noise, µg/m³ (not mg/m³) for SO₂/NO₂/PM, mg/L (not ppm) for BOD/COD.
> 4. **Skipping environmental flow** in dam/reservoir questions — direct 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Mixing up CPCB NAAQS** values for industrial vs residential vs eco-sensitive zones.
> 6. **Writing Wind Rose Law as V = Kz^a** without defining $z_r$ and $n$ — examiner will deduct 0.5 mark for "incomplete notation."
> 7. **Not mentioning baseline monitoring frequency** ("24-hr × 8 days × 4 seasons = 1 year") — common 1-mark loss in air/water questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — "Effect on Environment"**

- **Definition:** Changes in environmental components (physical, biological, socio-economic) due to project activities across project life cycle.
- **Five Receptors:** Air, Water, Soil, Flora/Fauna, Humans.
- **Three-Link Chain:** Source → Pathway → Receptor. Break any one link to mitigate.
- **Seven Classifications:** Nature, Extent, Duration, Reversibility, Magnitude, Probability, Cumulative.
- **Cumulative Impact = Σ individual + Σ synergistic interactions** — the **whole > sum of parts**.
- **Leq Formula:** $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right]$ — **3 dB doubling rule** (not 6 dB).
- **Noise doubling rule:** Doubling the noise source → +3 dB; 10× sources → +10 dB.
- **CPCB Noise Standards (Residential):** 55 dB(A) day / 45 dB(A) night.
- **NAAQS PM₂.₅:** 60 µg/m³ (24-hr), 40 µg/m³ (annual).
- **NAAQS SO₂:** 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) for all zones.
- **USLE Equation:** $A = R \cdot K \cdot LS \cdot C \cdot P$ — unit: tonnes/ha/yr.
- **Tolerable Soil Loss:** 5–11 t/ha/yr.
- **Wind Profile Power Law:** $V_z = V_r (z/z_r)^n$; $n$ = 0.143 (unstable) to 0.40 (stable).
- **Leopold Matrix (1971):** Rows = actions (~100), Columns = components (~75), Magnitude × Importance = Significance Score.
- **Environmental Flow (E-Flow):** Quantity, timing, frequency, quality of water to sustain ecosystems and livelihoods.
- **Methods for E-Flow:** Tennant, Wetted Perimeter, PHABSIM, DRIFT, Building Block.
- **Dam impacts:** Habitat loss, fish migration block, sediment trap, downstream erosion, salinity intrusion, methane emission.
- **Thermal Power Plant impacts:** PM, SO₂, NOₓ, fly ash, thermal pollution, GHG.
- **Mining impacts:** Land degradation, acid mine drainage, dust, blasting vibration, resettlement.
- **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Avoid → Reduce → Restore → Offset → Monitor.
- **AERMOD:** Modern Gaussian plume model for air dispersion; replaces ISCST3.
- **CPCB NAAQS Revision Year:** 2009, with PM₂.₅ added.
- **GWP Values (IPCC AR6):** CO₂ = 1, CH₄ = 28, N₂O = 265.
- **Act to remember:** EIA Notification 2006 (and 2020 amendment) — mandates EIA for 39 categories.
- **Bird deflectors, green belts (3-tier, 250 m), E-flow release, ash utilization, ZLD** — all high-frequency mitigation keywords.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Mantra:** *"Effects come from sources, travel through pathways, hit receptors — and our job in EIA is to **identify, predict, evaluate, mitigate, and monitor** each one."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Pollution; Classification of Pollution and Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution; Classification of Pollution and Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Air Pollution

**Air Pollution** is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants in the atmosphere in quantities, characteristics, or durations that are injurious to human health, animal or plant life, or property, or that unreasonably interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or the conduct of business.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (per Clean Air Act framework):**
> Air pollution refers to the introduction into the atmosphere of chemicals, particulate matter, or biological materials that cause harm or discomfort to humans or other living organisms, or damage the natural environment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Soup"

Imagine the atmosphere as a giant, transparent bowl of clear soup that covers the entire Earth. Now imagine someone sprinkling dust, blowing exhaust fumes, releasing invisible chemicals, and stirring up smoke into this soup. After a while, the "soup" becomes hazy, smelly, and toxic. Breathing this contaminated soup is what we call **air pollution**.

- The **bowl** = Earth's troposphere (the breathable atmospheric layer up to ~10–12 km).
- The **dust, fumes, and chemicals** = Pollutants.
- The **stirring** = Human and natural activities.
- The **haziness** = Smog, reduced visibility.
- The **toxicity** = Health impacts like asthma, bronchitis, and cancer.

> [!NOTE]
> The atmosphere is essentially a **shared global commons** — pollution released in one location (say, Delhi or Beijing) can travel thousands of kilometers via wind currents, affecting communities continents away (acid rain in Scandinavia from UK emissions, for example).

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

Key physical and regulatory constants used throughout air pollution studies:

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| **Standard Temperature** | **25 °C (298 K)** |
| **Standard Pressure** | **1 atm (101.325 kPa)** |
| **Primary Standard (NAAQS)** | **Public health protection** |
| **Secondary Standard (NAAQS)** | **Public welfare protection (visibility, crops)** |
| **PM₂.₅ Limit (24-hr, India NAAQS)** | **60 µg/m³** |
| **PM₁₀ Limit (24-hr, India NAAQS)** | **100 µg/m³** |
| **SO₂ Limit (24-hr, India CPCB)** | **80 µg/m³** |
| **NO₂ Limit (24-hr, India CPCB)** | **80 µg/m³** |
| **CO Limit (8-hr, India CPCB)** | **4 mg/m³** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** set by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB), India, define the legal threshold concentrations of pollutants. Exceeding these triggers the declaration of **"Non-Attainment Cities"** under India's National Clean Air Programme (NCAP).

---

## 1.4 What is a "Pollutant"?

A **pollutant** (or air contaminant) is any substance in the air that, in sufficient concentration, can cause harm. The mere presence of a chemical is not pollution — it is the **concentration**, **duration of exposure**, and **toxicity** that determine whether it becomes a pollutant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Idea (KTU syllabus focus):** "The dose makes the poison" — even water or oxygen can be toxic at extreme concentrations. Therefore, **classification** of pollutants is essential for regulation, monitoring, and control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Air Pollution

Air pollution can be classified based on **origin**, **source characteristics**, **location of generation**, and **dispersion pattern**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be fluent in all major classification schemes.

---

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on **Origin / Source**

#### (A) Natural Pollution (Biogenic)
Pollution arising from natural processes without human intervention.

- **Volcanic eruptions** → emit SO₂, CO₂, HF, HCl, ash.
- **Forest fires (wildfires)** → CO, CO₂, NOₓ, PM.
- **Dust storms** → Particulate matter (silica, soil dust).
- **Biological decay** → CH₄, H₂S, NH₃.
- **Pollen and spores** → Biogenic particulates (allergens).
- **Oceans (sea spray)** → Salt aerosols, sulfates.
- **Vegetation** → Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) like isoprene, terpenes.

#### (B) Anthropogenic Pollution (Man-Made)
Pollution arising from human activities — the primary concern of EIA studies.

- **Stationary sources** → Power plants, factories, refineries, waste incinerators.
- **Mobile sources** → Automobiles, aircraft, ships, trains.
- **Area sources** → Agricultural burning, domestic cooking, construction dust.
- **Line sources** → Highways and railway corridors.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** EIA studies predominantly focus on **anthropogenic pollution** because it is (a) localized, (b) controllable through regulation, and (c) the dominant contributor in urban-industrial corridors.

---

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on **Physical State of Pollutant**

| Physical State | Examples | Key Concern |
|---|---|---|
| **Gaseous** | SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, VOCs, H₂S | Respiratory illness, acid rain, smog |
| **Particulate (solid)** | Dust, soot, fly ash, metal oxides | Respiratory disease, visibility |
| **Particulate (liquid)** | Mist, fog droplets, sulfuric acid mist | Acid deposition, corrosion |
| **Aerosol (mixed)** | Smog, smoke, fumes | Combined health impact |

---

### 2.1.3 Classification Based on **Dispersibility**

- **Dispersed (Primary) Pollutants** — Emitted directly from a source (CO, SO₂, PM).
- **Dispersible / Secondary Pollutants** — Formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions (O₃, PAN, smog, acid rain).

---

### 2.1.4 Classification Based on **Spatial Scale of Impact**

| Scale | Range | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Local** | < 1 km | Smoking chimney, vehicle exhaust at intersection |
| **Urban** | 1–100 km | City-wide smog (Delhi, Los Angeles) |
| **Regional** | 100–1000 km | Acid rain across Europe, Asian brown cloud |
| **Global** | > 1000 km | CO₂-driven climate change, ozone depletion |

---

### 2.1.5 Classification Based on **Indoor vs Outdoor**

- **Outdoor (Ambient) Pollution** — Open atmosphere pollution.
- **Indoor Air Pollution (IAP)** — Inside enclosed spaces (homes, offices, schools). WHO estimates IAP causes **~3.2 million deaths/year**, mainly from solid-fuel cooking in developing nations.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Air Pollutants (KTU High-Yield)

This is the most heavily tested area in KTU examinations. Pollutants are classified by **chemistry**, **origin**, and **regulatory status**.

---

### 2.2.1 Primary vs Secondary Pollutants

#### Primary Pollutants
**Definition:** Substances emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a form that is harmful.

Examples:
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — Coal/oil combustion, smelting.
- **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ = NO + NO₂)** — Vehicle exhaust, power plants.
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — Incomplete combustion.
- **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅)** — Dust, soot, smoke.
- **Lead (Pb)** — Leaded petrol (now phased out in India), battery recycling.
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** — Solvents, paints, petroleum.
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO₂)** — Combustion (also acts as secondary greenhouse gas).
- **Hydrogen Sulphide (H₂S)** — Refineries, sewage.
- **Ammonia (NH₃)** — Agriculture, fertilizers.

#### Secondary Pollutants
**Definition:** Substances formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents.

Key formation examples:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

$$
2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalyst}} 2\text{SO}_3
$$

$$
\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad \text{(Sulphuric Acid — acid rain)}
$$

$$
2\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{HNO}_2 \quad \text{(Nitric Acid)}
$$

$$
\text{VOCs} + \text{NO}_x + \text{Sunlight} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 \quad \text{(Photochemical smog)}
$$

Important secondary pollutants:
- **Ozone (O₃)** — Tropospheric (bad) vs Stratospheric (good).
- **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** — Eye irritant in smog.
- **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — Acid rain.
- **Nitric Acid (HNO₃)** — Acid rain.
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** — Photochemical smog.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Distinction:** Primary pollutants are emitted **as-is**; secondary pollutants are **created in the atmosphere** via photochemical, oxidation, or hydrolysis reactions.

---

### 2.2.2 Criteria vs Non-Criteria Pollutants

#### Criteria Pollutants (Regulated)
The U.S. EPA designated **six criteria pollutants** under the Clean Air Act (1970), all of which are now part of India's NAAQS:

| # | Pollutant | Primary Health Concern |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CO** (Carbon Monoxide) | Hemoglobin binding, hypoxia |
| 2 | **Pb** (Lead) | Neurotoxin, especially in children |
| 3 | **NO₂** (Nitrogen Dioxide) | Respiratory, smog precursor |
| 4 | **O₃** (Ozone) | Lung damage, smog |
| 5 | **PM** (Particulate Matter) | Respiratory, cardiovascular |
| 6 | **SO₂** (Sulphur Dioxide) | Acid rain, bronchitis |

> [!NOTE]
> These are called "criteria" because EPA developed **human health-based criteria (scientific benchmarks)** for setting permissible levels.

#### Non-Criteria Pollutants
Includes hundreds of hazardous air pollutants (HAPs) such as **benzene, mercury, dioxins, asbestos, PAHs, vinyl chloride**. Regulated under different statutes (e.g., India's 2009 Hazardous Waste Rules).

---

### 2.2.3 Classification by Chemical Nature

| Class | Examples |
|---|---|
| **Sulphur compounds** | SO₂, SO₃, H₂S |
| **Nitrogen compounds** | NO, NO₂, N₂O, NH₃, HNO₃ |
| **Carbon compounds** | CO, CO₂, CH₄, VOCs, formaldehyde |
| **Halogen compounds** | HF, HCl, Cl₂, CFCs |
| **Particulate matter** | Dust, soot, smoke, fumes, aerosols |
| **Photochemical oxidants** | O₃, PAN, H₂O₂ |
| **Radioactive pollutants** | Radon-222, I-131, Sr-90 |
| **Heavy metals** | Pb, Hg, Cd, Cr, As |

---

### 2.2.4 Classification by Effect

- **Toxic pollutants** → Cause direct poisoning (CO, H₂S, Pb, Hg).
- **Carcinogenic pollutants** → Cause cancer (benzene, PAHs, asbestos, formaldehyde).
- **Smog-forming pollutants** → NOₓ + VOCs → ozone, PAN.
- **Acid rain precursors** → SO₂, NOₓ.
- **Greenhouse pollutants** → CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs, O₃, H₂O (vapor).
- **Ozone-depleting substances (ODS)** → CFCs, halons, HCFCs.
- **Eutrophying pollutants** → NH₃, NOₓ (when deposited).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for solving KTU numerical problems on air quality.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | Concentration of pollutant | µg/m³ or mg/m³ |
| 2 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{air}}} \times 10^6$ | Parts per million | ppm |
| 3 | $C_{ppb} = \dfrac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_{\text{air}}} \times 10^9$ | Parts per billion | ppb |
| 4 | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times P}{R \times T}$ | ppm to µg/m³ conversion | µg/m³ |
| 5 | $C_{ppm} = C_{\mu g/m^3} \times \dfrac{R \times T}{MW \times P}$ | µg/m³ to ppm conversion | ppm |
| 6 | $AQI = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}}(C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Air Quality Index (linear interpolation) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = C \times Q \times t$ | Emission = Concentration × Flow × Time | mass |
| 8 | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(C_i - \bar{C})^2}{n-1}}$ | Standard deviation of pollutant concentration | same as C |
| 9 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\,dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous sound level | dB |
| 10 | $PM_{2.5}/PM_{10}$ ratio | Indicator of combustion vs dust source | dimensionless |

Where:
- **MW** = Molecular weight (g/mol)
- **P** = Pressure (atm)
- **T** = Temperature (K)
- **R** = Universal gas constant = **0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)** or **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake in KTU Papers:** Students forget to convert T to Kelvin (T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15) when using the ideal gas conversion formula. Always verify units.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Classification in EIA

- **Regulatory compliance** → Industries must monitor only the regulated (criteria) pollutants.
- **Source apportionment** → PM₂.₅/PM₁₀ ratio helps determine whether pollution is from combustion (low ratio) or dust (high ratio).
- **Control technology selection** → Classification guides whether to use scrubbers (for SO₂), catalytic converters (for NOₓ/CO/VOCs), electrostatic precipitators (for PM), or activated carbon (for VOCs).
- **Health impact assessment** → Toxicity classification drives permissible exposure limits.
- **Modeling and forecasting** → AERMOD, CALPUFF, and WRF-Chem models require pollutant class as input.
- **Climate change policy** → Greenhouse gas classification drives carbon trading and ESG reporting.

---

## 2.5 Step-by-Step Reasoning for Classification

**Why classify pollutants?**
1. To prioritize regulatory action.
2. To select appropriate monitoring instruments.
3. To design suitable control technologies.
4. To assess health/environmental impacts systematically.
5. To enable international harmonization (e.g., WHO vs NAAQS vs EU standards).

**How is classification done?**
1. Identify source (anthropogenic vs natural).
2. Determine physical/chemical state (gas, particle, aerosol).
3. Check if it is emitted directly (primary) or formed in the atmosphere (secondary).
4. Compare against regulatory lists (criteria vs non-criteria).
5. Categorize by impact (toxic, carcinogenic, greenhouse, acid-rain precursor).

> [!NOTE]
> This stepwise approach is the same logic followed during the **screening** and **scoping** stages of an EIA study.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ppm ↔ µg/m³ Conversion Formula

This is the **single most important numerical conversion** in KTU air pollution problems.

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Ideal Gas Law

The ideal gas law states:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

Where:
- $P$ = absolute pressure (atm)
- $V$ = volume (L)
- $n$ = moles of gas
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Step 1:** Solve for molar volume $V_m$ (volume occupied by 1 mole of ideal gas at P, T):

$$
V_m = \dfrac{V}{n} = \dfrac{RT}{P}
$$

At standard conditions (1 atm, 25 °C = 298 K):

$$
V_m = \dfrac{0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 24.47 \text{ L/mol} = 0.02447 \text{ m}^3\text{/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Consider 1 ppm of any gas = 1 part in $10^6$ parts of air by volume.
For a 1 m³ air sample, the volume of pollutant = $10^{-6}$ m³.

**Step 3:** Convert pollutant volume to moles:

$$
n = \dfrac{V_{\text{pollutant}}}{V_m} = \dfrac{10^{-6}}{0.02447} \text{ mol per m}^3
$$

**Step 4:** Convert moles to mass using molecular weight $MW$ (g/mol):

$$
\text{Mass per m}^3 = n \times MW = \dfrac{10^{-6} \times MW}{0.02447} \text{ g/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Convert g to µg (1 g = $10^6$ µg):

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{10^{-6} \times MW \times 10^6}{0.02447} = \dfrac{MW}{0.02447} \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Generalizing for any concentration in ppm:**

$$
\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times P}{R \times T}}
$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** The concentration of SO₂ in ambient air is 0.1 ppm. Calculate the equivalent concentration in µg/m³ at 25 °C and 1 atm. (MW of SO₂ = 64 g/mol)

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- $C_{ppm} = 0.1$ ppm
- $MW = 64$ g/mol
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $P = 1$ atm
- $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K)

**Step 2:** Calculate molar volume:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = \dfrac{0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 24.47 \text{ L/mol}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the formula:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m} \times 1000
$$

The factor 1000 converts L to m³ (since V_m is in L/mol and we want concentration per m³).

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = 0.1 \times \dfrac{64}{24.47} \times 1000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = 0.1 \times 2.615 \times 1000 = 261.5 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Result:** $C_{SO_2} = 261.5$ µg/m³ — this exceeds the 24-hour Indian NAAQS limit of **80 µg/m³**, indicating a violation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Air Quality Index (AQI) Formula

AQI follows a **piecewise linear interpolation** model prescribed by EPA/CPCB.

$$
\boxed{I = I_{Lo} + \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C - C_{Lo})}
$$

Where:
- $I$ = Calculated AQI for the pollutant
- $C$ = Measured concentration of the pollutant
- $C_{Lo}, C_{Hi}$ = Concentration breakpoints (lower, upper)
- $I_{Lo}, I_{Hi}$ = AQI breakpoints (lower, upper)

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. The AQI scale runs from **0 to 500** (and beyond for hazardous).
2. Each pollutant has a defined concentration–AQI table.
3. The AQI for a mixture is the **maximum** of all sub-indices.
4. Linear interpolation within each concentration band is the standard method.

**Example Breakpoint Table (PM₂.₅, 24-hr average):**

| $C_{Lo}$ (µg/m³) | $C_{Hi}$ (µg/m³) | $I_{Lo}$ | $I_{Hi}$ | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 12.0 | 0 | 50 | Good |
| 12.1 | 35.4 | 51 | 100 | Moderate |
| 35.5 | 55.4 | 101 | 150 | Unhealthy for Sensitive |
| 55.5 | 150.4 | 151 | 200 | Unhealthy |
| 150.5 | 250.4 | 201 | 300 | Very Unhealthy |
| 250.5 | 350.4 | 301 | 400 | Hazardous |
| 350.5 | 500.4 | 401 | 500 | Hazardous |

**Worked AQI Calculation:**

Suppose PM₂.₅ = 80 µg/m³. Find AQI.

This falls in the band: $C_{Lo} = 55.5$, $C_{Hi} = 150.4$, $I_{Lo} = 151$, $I_{Hi} = 200$.

$$
I = 151 + \dfrac{200 - 151}{150.4 - 55.5} \times (80 - 55.5)
$$

$$
I = 151 + \dfrac{49}{94.9} \times 24.5
$$

$$
I = 151 + 0.5163 \times 24.5 = 151 + 12.65 = 163.65
$$

**Result:** AQI ≈ **164 → "Unhealthy"** category.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Effective Stack Height (Davidson-Bryant Formula)

Used in EIA for predicting ground-level concentration from a stack.

$$
\boxed{H_e = h + \Delta h}
$$

Where the plume rise $\Delta h$ is given by:

$$
\Delta h = \dfrac{v_s \cdot d}{u} \times \left( 1.5 + 2.68 \times 10^{-3} \cdot P \cdot \dfrac{T_s - T_a}{T_s} \cdot d \right)
$$

Where:
- $h$ = physical stack height (m)
- $v_s$ = stack gas exit velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = stack diameter (m)
- $u$ = wind speed (m/s)
- $P$ = atmospheric pressure (mbar)
- $T_s$ = stack gas temperature (K)
- $T_a$ = ambient air temperature (K)

**Engineering Use:** Industries use this in **EIA reports** to demonstrate that the proposed stack height keeps ground-level pollutant concentrations within NAAQS limits.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant Sources, Health Effects & Controls

| Pollutant | Primary Sources | Health Effect | Control Technology |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CO** | Vehicle exhaust, incomplete combustion | Hemoglobin binding, hypoxia, death | Catalytic converter, proper combustion |
| **SO₂** | Coal/oil combustion, smelters | Bronchitis, acid rain | Wet/dry scrubbers, low-S fuel |
| **NO₂** | Vehicles, power plants | Smog, respiratory disease | SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction) |
| **PM₂.₅/PM₁₀** | Vehicles, industry, dust | Lung disease, cancer | ESP, baghouse filters, cyclones |
| **O₃ (tropospheric)** | Secondary from NOₓ + VOCs | Lung damage, crop loss | VOC/NOₓ emission limits |
| **Pb** | Leaded fuel, battery plants | Neurotoxin, IQ reduction | Unleaded fuel, phase-out |
| **VOCs (BTEX)** | Solvents, petrochemical | Cancer (benzene), smog | Vapor recovery, adsorption |
| **CO₂** | Combustion, deforestation | Climate change (GHG) | Carbon capture, renewables |
| **CH₄** | Landfills, livestock, gas | Greenhouse gas (28× CO₂) | Capture, anaerobic digestion |
| **CFCs** | Old refrigerants, aerosols | Ozone depletion | Montreal Protocol phase-out |
| **Hg (Mercury)** | Coal plants, chlor-alkali | Neurotoxin, bioaccumulation | Activated carbon injection |
| **Radon (Rn-222)** | Soil, building materials | Lung cancer | Ventilation, sealing |

---

## 3.5 Time-Weighted Average (TWA) Calculations

Occupational exposure limits often use 8-hour TWA:

$$
\boxed{C_{TWA} = \dfrac{\sum (C_i \times t_i)}{8 \text{ hours}}}
$$

Where $C_i$ is concentration during time period $t_i$ (in hours).

**Example:** Worker exposed to CO at:
- 2 hours at 25 ppm
- 4 hours at 50 ppm
- 2 hours at 10 ppm

$$
C_{TWA} = \dfrac{(25 \times 2) + (50 \times 4) + (10 \times 2)}{8} = \dfrac{50 + 200 + 20}{8} = 33.75 \text{ ppm}
$$

Compare to OSHA PEL (50 ppm 8-hr TWA) — **compliant**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, Set A]**

> **"Distinguish between primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples of each."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Primary Pollutants:** These are substances emitted directly into the atmosphere from a source in a form that is harmful. They retain their original chemical identity after emission.

*Examples:*
1. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** — emitted from coal-fired power plants.
2. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** — emitted from vehicle exhaust due to incomplete combustion of petrol/diesel.

**Secondary Pollutants:** These are substances formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents (e.g., water vapor, oxygen, sunlight). They do not have a direct source.

*Examples:*
1. **Ozone (O₃)** — formed in the troposphere by photochemical reactions between NOₓ and VOCs in presence of sunlight.
2. **Sulphuric Acid (H₂SO₄)** — formed when SO₂ reacts with atmospheric oxygen and water vapor, leading to acid rain.

| Aspect | Primary | Secondary |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Direct emission | Atmospheric reaction |
| Control | At the source | Difficult (reactions) |
| Example | SO₂, CO, PM, NOₓ | O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄ |

*(Examiner tip: Always give at least one example for each type — marks awarded for examples alone. Drawing a comparison table fetches full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Set B]**

> **"What are criteria pollutants? Name them as per the U.S. EPA Clean Air Act, 1970."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**Criteria Pollutants** are those air pollutants for which national ambient air quality standards have been established by regulatory authorities based on documented human health and environmental effects (i.e., "criteria documents"). The U.S. EPA originally identified **six criteria pollutants** under the Clean Air Act, 1970:

1. **CO** — Carbon Monoxide
2. **Pb** — Lead
3. **NO₂** — Nitrogen Dioxide
4. **O₃** — Ozone
5. **PM** — Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)
6. **SO₂** — Sulphur Dioxide

These six pollutants are regulated by setting **Primary Standards** (to protect public health, including sensitive populations) and **Secondary Standards** (to protect public welfare — crops, animals, structures, visibility).

In India, all six criteria pollutants are also regulated under the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** issued by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "EPA identified criteria" but **forget to name all six**. Naming fewer than six results in 0.5 mark deduction per missing pollutant. **Use the mnemonic "Clever Lions Never Observe Proper Sleep"** → CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper, Set A]**

> **(a)** Classify air pollutants based on their origin, physical state, and chemical nature, with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** A power plant emits SO₂ at a concentration of 0.5 ppm. Convert this concentration to µg/m³ at 25 °C and 1 atm. Comment on its compliance with the 24-hour Indian NAAQS limit. (MW of SO₂ = 64 g/mol; NAAQS 24-hr SO₂ limit = 80 µg/m³) *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Classification Based on Origin (2 Marks)**

| Type | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural / Biogenic** | Released by natural processes (no human cause) | Volcanic SO₂, sea-salt aerosols, forest-fire CO, pollen |
| **Anthropogenic / Man-made** | Released due to human activity | Vehicle exhaust CO, factory SO₂, power plant NOₓ, biomass burning |

**2. Classification Based on Physical State (2 Marks)**

| Physical State | Examples | Major Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Gaseous | SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, VOCs | Respiratory, acid rain, smog |
| Particulate (solid) | Dust, soot, fly ash, smoke | Lung disease, visibility |
| Particulate (liquid) | Mist, fog, sulfuric acid droplets | Acid deposition |
| Aerosol | Smog, fume, smoke | Combined health impact |

**3. Classification Based on Chemical Nature (3 Marks)**

- **Sulphur Compounds** — SO₂, SO₃, H₂S
- **Nitrogen Compounds** — NO, NO₂, N₂O, NH₃
- **Carbon Compounds** — CO, CO₂, CH₄, formaldehyde, VOCs
- **Halogen Compounds** — HF, HCl, CFCs
- **Particulate Matter** — organic and inorganic aerosols
- **Photochemical Oxidants** — O₃, PAN, H₂O₂
- **Radioactive Pollutants** — Radon-222, Iodine-131
- **Heavy Metals** — Pb, Hg, Cd, As, Cr

*(Examiner tip: A clean table fetches full marks. Use heading-level clarity.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $C_{ppm} = 0.5$ ppm
- $MW = 64$ g/mol
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298$ K
- $P = 1$ atm
- $R = 0.0821$ L·atm/(mol·K)
- NAAQS 24-hr SO₂ limit = 80 µg/m³

**Step 1:** Calculate molar volume of air at given conditions:

$$
V_m = \dfrac{RT}{P} = \dfrac{0.0821 \times 298}{1} = 24.47 \text{ L/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply ppm → µg/m³ conversion formula:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW}{V_m} \times 1000
$$

The factor 1000 converts L to m³.

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = 0.5 \times \dfrac{64}{24.47} \times 1000
$$

**Step 4:** Compute step-by-step:

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = 0.5 \times 2.6154 \times 1000
$$

$$
C_{\mu g/m^3} = 0.5 \times 2615.4 = 1307.7 \text{ µg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Compare with NAAQS:

$$
\dfrac{1307.7}{80} \approx 16.35
$$

**Result:** The concentration is **1307.7 µg/m³**, which is **~16.35 times higher** than the NAAQS 24-hour limit of 80 µg/m³. Therefore, the power plant is **non-compliant**, and the EIA must mandate **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)**, use of low-sulphur coal, or alternative fuel switching.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Common errors include:
> 1. Forgetting to convert °C to K (T = 25 + 273 = **298 K**).
> 2. Forgetting the ×1000 conversion factor for L→m³.
> 3. Not comparing the result with the NAAQS limit (loses 1 mark for "comment on compliance").
> 4. Writing MW in kg/mol instead of g/mol.

---

### OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper, Set B]**

> **(a)** Discuss the formation, characteristics, and health effects of **photochemical smog**. *(7 Marks)*
>
> **(b)** The 8-hour average PM₂.₅ concentration in a city was measured as 90 µg/m³. Compute the AQI for PM₂.₅ using the CPCB breakpoint table. Is this AQI in the "Unhealthy" category? *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**1. Definition and Formation (3 Marks)**

**Photochemical Smog** is a secondary air pollution phenomenon formed when **NOₓ** and **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** react in the presence of **sunlight (UV radiation)** to produce a mixture of harmful secondary pollutants — primarily **ozone (O₃)**, **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)**, **formaldehyde (HCHO)**, and **acrolein**.

**Key Reaction Sequence:**

$$
\text{NO}_2 + h\nu (\text{UV}) \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}
$$

$$
\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M
$$

$$
\text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2
$$

$$
\text{VOC} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^{\bullet}
$$

$$
\text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2
$$

The NO₂ regenerated promotes further ozone formation — creating a **chain reaction**. PAN forms when aldehydes react with NO₂:

$$
\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COOONO}_2 \quad \text{(PAN)}
$$

**2. Characteristics of Photochemical Smog (2 Marks)**

- **Brownish haze** due to NO₂ absorption of visible light.
- **Reduced visibility** from suspended aerosols.
- **High oxidant concentration** — O₃, PAN, H₂O₂.
- **Worst in summer afternoons** (peak photolysis, peak temperature).
- **Typical in cities** with high traffic and sunlight (Los Angeles, Delhi, Mexico City, Beijing).

**3. Health Effects (2 Marks)**

- **Eye irritation** (mainly from PAN, formaldehyde, acrolein).
- **Respiratory distress** — coughing, chest pain, asthma aggravation (from O₃).
- **Headache and fatigue** from CO accumulation.
- **Crop damage** — ozone damages plant leaf tissues.
- **Material damage** — rubber cracking, paint erosion.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Given:** $C_{PM_{2.5}} = 90$ µg/m³ (24-hr average)

**Step 1:** Identify the relevant AQI breakpoint band.

| $C_{Lo}$ | $C_{Hi}$ | $I_{Lo}$ | $I_{Hi}$ | Category |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 12.0 | 0 | 50 | Good |
| 12.1 | 35.4 | 51 | 100 | Moderate |
| 35.5 | 55.4 | 101 | 150 | Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups |
| **55.5** | **150.4** | **151** | **200** | **Unhealthy** |
| 150.5 | 250.4 | 201 | 300 | Very Unhealthy |

Since 90 µg/m³ lies in the band [55.5, 150.4], the AQI will lie in [151, 200] → **"Unhealthy"** category.

**Step 2:** Apply the AQI formula:

$$
I = I_{Lo} + \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C - C_{Lo})
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
I = 151 + \dfrac{200 - 151}{150.4 - 55.5} \times (90 - 55.5)
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify:

$$
I = 151 + \dfrac{49}{94.9} \times 34.5
$$

$$
I = 151 + 0.5163 \times 34.5
$$

$$
I = 151 + 17.81 = 168.81
$$

**Step 5:** Final Result and Interpretation:

$$
\boxed{I \approx 169 \rightarrow \text{Unhealthy Category}}
$$

**Interpretation:** AQI of 169 is in the **"Unhealthy"** range (151–200), meaning **everyone may begin to experience health effects**, and members of sensitive groups (children, elderly, asthmatics) may experience **more serious health impacts**. The city should be classified as a **Non-Attainment City** under NCAP, and emergency mitigation measures (vehicle restrictions, industrial shutdowns) are warranted.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:**
> 1. Students often pick the **wrong breakpoint band** (e.g., using the 12.1–35.4 band when value is 90) — this is the **#1 most common mistake**.
> 2. Forgetting to compute the AQI difference in the numerator (49, not 51 or 200–151 written backwards).
> 3. Not stating the **category name** explicitly (just writing a number loses 1 mark).
> 4. **Unit confusion:** Always use µg/m³ for PM, not ppm.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Air Pollution Classification**

- **Air Pollution** = harmful substances in the atmosphere beyond permissible limits.
- **Primary pollutants** are emitted directly; **secondary pollutants** form via atmospheric reactions.
- **Six criteria pollutants** (EPA, 1970): **CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂** — memorize via mnemonic.
- **Particulate Matter** is divided into **PM₁₀** (thoracic, <10 µm) and **PM₂.₅** (respirable, <2.5 µm).
- **Photochemical smog** forms from **NOₓ + VOCs + sunlight**; key products are **O₃, PAN, formaldehyde**.
- **Acid rain** is caused by **SO₂ → H₂SO₄** and **NOₓ → HNO₃**; pH of normal rain ~ 5.6, acid rain < 5.6.
- **Greenhouse gases**: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs, O₃, H₂O vapor — drive global warming.
- **Ozone depletion** is caused by **CFCs, halons, HCFCs** — addressed by the **Montreal Protocol (1987)**.
- **Indoor air pollution** kills ~3.2 million/year (WHO) — solid-fuel cooking is the major cause.
- **Conversion formula (HIGH-YIELD):**
  $$C_{\mu g/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times P}{R \times T} \times 1000 \quad \text{(at 25°C, 1 atm)}$$
- **Standard molar volume at 25 °C, 1 atm = 24.47 L/mol** — used in all ppm↔µg/m³ problems.
- **AQI formula:** Linear interpolation using EPA/CPCB breakpoint tables.
- **Effective stack height** is the sum of physical height + plume rise — critical in EIA for new industries.
- **Indian NAAQS** is enforced by **CPCB** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**.
- **NCAP (National Clean Air Programme)** launched in **2019** to address 131 non-attainment cities.
- **Source contribution** (in urban India): Vehicles > Industries > Domestic cooking > Construction dust.
- **Health impact hierarchy (worst to mildest):** PM₂.₅ > O₃ > NO₂ > SO₂ > CO.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Weightage:** This topic is **Module 4 (Air Pollution)** — typically contributes **15–20%** of the End Semester Exam (ESE). Expect at least one **Part A (3-mark)** question and one **Part B (14-mark)** question from this module in every KTU cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Pollutants ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In environmental engineering and atmospheric science, air pollutants are broadly classified into two fundamental categories based on their **mode of generation and emission pathway**: **Primary Pollutants** and **Secondary Pollutants**. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Exam Standard)** > > **Primary Pollutants** are those substances which are emitted directly into the atmosphere from identifiable sources in a form that is already harmful to human health, ecosystems, or materials. They retain their original chemical identity at the point of discharge. > > **Secondary Pollutants** are substances that are **not emitted directly** but are formed in the atmosphere through **chemical reactions, photochemical transformations, or physical interactions** between two or more primary pollutants, or between primary pollutants and naturally occurring atmospheric constituents (such as water vapor, oxygen, or hydroxyl radicals). > [!NOTE] **Classification Basis (KTU 2024 Module 4)** > The classification hinges on the **origin of formation** — whether the pollutant enters the atmosphere already in its harmful form (primary) or whether it is synthesized in-situ within the troposphere/stratosphere (secondary). This distinction is critical in EIA studies because the **mitigation strategy differs entirely** for each class. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Think of the atmosphere as a giant **kitchen where cooking is happening continuously**: - **Primary Pollutants** are like the **raw ingredients** that come out of a factory chimney — smoke, soot, sulfur dioxide, carbon monoxide, and ash. They are released **"as-is"** from a combustion or industrial process. The factory cannot claim innocence saying "it formed later" — it was discharged directly. - **Secondary Pollutants** are like the **new chemical compound created on the stove** when those raw ingredients interact with heat (sunlight) and other elements (moisture, oxygen) already present in the kitchen (atmosphere). For example, when nitrogen oxides (raw ingredient) and volatile organic compounds (raw ingredient) are "cooked" by sunlight (UV radiation), they form **ozone** (O₃) and **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)** — these were never emitted by the factory; the atmosphere cooked them up. > [!TIP] **Student Mnemonic** > **"Primary = Pre-formed; Secondary = Sky-formed."** > > - **P**rimary pollutants are **P**roduced at the source and **P**oured directly into the air. > - **S**econdary pollutants are **S**ynthesized in the **S**ky (atmosphere). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | Application | |---|---|---|---| | Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | **101.325 kPa** | Pascal | Standard reference | | Tropospheric temperature (ISA) | **288.15 K** | Kelvin | Reaction kinetics | | Solar constant (AM1.5) | **1361 W/m²** | W/m² | Photolysis rate | | OH radical concentration (background) | **~10⁶ molecules/cm³** | cm⁻³ | Atmospheric oxidation | | Molar mass of air | **28.97 g/mol** | g/mol | Gas-law calculations | | Universal gas constant (R) | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | J/(mol·K) | Reaction thermodynamics | ### 1.4 Categorical List of Major Pollutants (KTU Board Focus) **Primary Pollutants (Criteria Pollutants under EPA / CPCB):** - Carbon Monoxide (CO) - Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) - Nitrogen Oxides (NO, NO₂ — collectively NOₓ) - Particulate Matter (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) - Lead (Pb) and Heavy Metals - Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs) - Ammonia (NH₃) - Methane (CH₄) and Non-methane hydrocarbons (NMHC) **Secondary Pollutants:** - Ground-level Ozone (O₃) - Photochemical Smog components: PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate), PBN - Sulfuric Acid (H₂SO₄) → acid rain precursor - Nitric Acid (HNO₃) → acid rain precursor - Secondary Organic Aerosols (SOA) - Secondary Inorganic Aerosols (SIA): Sulfates, Nitrates ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic of Primary Pollutant Generation The emission of primary pollutants follows a structured **Source-Pathway-Receptor** model: 1. **Source Identification (Anthropogenic or Natural):** - Stationary sources: Power plants, refineries, incinerators - Mobile sources: Automobiles, aircraft, ships - Area sources: Agricultural burning, construction dust - Natural sources: Volcanic SO₂, wildfires, dust storms 2. **Emission Mechanism:** - Combustion of fossil fuels (coal, petroleum, natural gas) - Incomplete combustion → CO, unburnt hydrocarbons, PM - High-temperature combustion → thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich mechanism) - Sulfur in fuel → SO₂ (oxidation of pyrite, organic S) 3. **Direct Discharge Pathway:** - Released in toxic form into the troposphere - Disperses via advection, diffusion, convection - Subject to dry/wet deposition ### 2.2 Operational Logic of Secondary Pollutant Formation Secondary pollutants require **three preconditions** simultaneously: 1. **Precursor availability** — Two or more primary pollutants must coexist 2. **Energy source** — Sunlight (UV radiation) for photolysis, or thermal energy 3. **Reaction time** — Residence time sufficient for atmospheric chemistry > [!IMPORTANT] **The "Three P's" of Secondary Pollution** > > **P**recursors (NOₓ + VOC) + **P**hotons (hν, λ < 400 nm) + **P**atience (residence time) = **Photochemical Smog** ### 2.3 Key Atmospheric Transformation Reactions (KTU High-Yield) #### (A) Photochemical Smog Formation (Los Angeles-type smog) **Step 1: Photolysis of NO₂** $$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}$$ **Step 2: Ozone formation** $$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$$ **Step 3: NO + O₃ recycle** $$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$ **Step 4: OH radical generation** $$\text{O}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}(^1\text{D})$$ $$\text{O}(^1\text{D}) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^{\bullet}$$ **Step 5: VOC oxidation (RH = hydrocarbon)** $$\text{RH} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Step 6: PAN formation** $$\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)O}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 \text{ (PAN)}$$ #### (B) Acid Rain Formation **Sulfuric Acid pathway:** $$2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_3$$ $$\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$ **Nitric Acid pathway:** $$2\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{HNO}_2$$ $$4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3$$ #### (C) Secondary Particulate Matter (Sulfate Aerosol) $$\text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet}$$ $$\text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{SO}_3$$ $$\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (aerosol)}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) | Formula | Description | Variables & Units | Application | |---|---|---|---| | $[C]_t = [C]_0 \, e^{-kt}$ | First-order decay (pollutant removal) | $k$ = rate constant (s⁻¹), $t$ = time (s) | Atmospheric residence time | | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ | Half-life of pollutant | $k$ = rate constant (s⁻¹) | \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | Gaussian plume concentration | $Q$ = emission rate, $u$ = wind speed, $\sigma_{y,z}$ = dispersion coefficients | Primary numerical on residence time or half-life appears, **always show the units explicitly** and use $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. State assumptions like "assuming first-order kinetics" and "well-mixed atmosphere." ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Air Quality Monitoring (CPCB-NAQI):** Designing monitoring networks requires distinguishing primary from secondary pollutants, since secondary pollutants (O₃) often peak **downwind** of precursor sources. - **EIA Reports:** Predicting secondary pollutant formation is essential in Environmental Impact Assessment for thermal power plants, highways, and petrochemical complexes. - **Pollution Control Devices:** - Primary: Electrostatic precipitators (ESP), bag filters, scrubbers - Secondary: Cannot be controlled at source — require **regional precursor control strategies** (e.g., catalytic converters in vehicles reducing NOₓ + VOC) - **Health Impact Assessment:** Ozone and PM₂.₅ (largely secondary) cause the bulk of **premature mortality** in urban India. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Atmospheric Residence Time and Half-Life of a Primary Pollutant **Problem Statement:** A primary pollutant $X$ is released into the atmosphere and decays via first-order kinetics. Derive an expression for its half-life and the time required for its concentration to fall to 1% of its initial value. **Step 1: Rate Law Statement** For a first-order decay process, the rate of disappearance of pollutant $X$ is proportional to its concentration: $$-\dfrac{d[C]}{dt} = k[C]$$ where $k$ is the first-order rate constant (s⁻¹) and $[C]$ is the instantaneous concentration. **Step 2: Separation of Variables** Rearranging and separating variables: $$\dfrac{d[C]}{[C]} = -k \, dt$$ **Step 3: Integration from $t=0$ to $t=t$** $$\int_{[C]_0}^{[C]_t} \dfrac{d[C]}{[C]} = -k \int_0^t dt$$ **Step 4: Solving the Integral** $$\ln[C]_t - \ln[C]_0 = -kt$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[C]_t}{[C]_0}\right) = -kt$$ **Step 5: Exponential Form** $$\dfrac{[C]_t}{[C]_0} = e^{-kt}$$ $$[C]_t = [C]_0 \, e^{-kt} \quad \text{...(Equation A)}$$ **Step 6: Defining Half-Life** At half-life $t = t_{1/2}$, we have $[C]_t = \dfrac{[C]_0}{2}$. **Step 7: Substituting into Equation A** $$\dfrac{[C]_0}{2} = [C]_0 \, e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$ $$\dfrac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$ **Step 8: Taking Natural Logarithm** $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$-\ln 2 = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}} \quad \text{...(Equation B)}$$ **Step 9: Time to Reach 1% of Initial Concentration** Set $[C]_t = 0.01 \, [C]_0$: $$0.01 = e^{-k t_{99\%}}$$ $$\ln(0.01) = -k t_{99\%}$$ $$-4.605 = -k t_{99\%}$$ $$\boxed{t_{99\%} = \dfrac{4.605}{k}}$$ **Step 10: Numerical Verification** For SO₂, $k \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}$ s⁻¹ (gas-phase oxidation by OH): $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 46{,}200 \text{ s} \approx 12.83 \text{ hours}$$ $$t_{99\%} = \dfrac{4.605}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 307{,}000 \text{ s} \approx 3.55 \text{ days}$$ > [!NOTE] **Interpretation:** SO₂ persists in the atmosphere for several days, allowing long-range transport — which is why acid rain impacts regions thousands of kilometers from the source. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Stoichiometry of Photochemical Ozone Formation **Problem Statement:** Given a mixture of NOₓ and VOC, derive the net ozone production stoichiometry per NOₓ molecule oxidized in a VOC-limited regime. **Step 1: Start with NO₂ Photolysis** $$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad \text{(1 photon required)}$$ **Step 2: Atomic Oxygen + O₂ → O₃** $$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$$ **Step 3: NO Recycles with O₃ (titration)** $$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$ **Step 4: Net Cycle (Adding 1 + 2 + 3)** In the **NOₓ-only** system, the net reaction is null (steady-state O₃). To produce **net O₃**, NO must be converted back to NO₂ **without consuming O₃**. This requires peroxy radicals (RO₂•). **Step 5: VOC → RO₂• Production** $$\text{RH} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ $$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^{\bullet}$$ **Step 6: RO₂• Converts NO to NO₂** $$\text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2$$ **Step 7: Net Reaction (Adding all steps and cancelling intermediates)** The net per NO molecule oxidized: $$\text{NO} + \text{VOC} + 2\text{O}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_3 + \text{products}$$ **Step 8: Generalized Net Equation** For every mole of NO converted: $$\boxed{\text{Production of O}_3 \text{ per mole of NO}_x \text{ oxidized} = 1 \text{ mole O}_3}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] **Implication for KTU Board** > The Leighton Ratio $\Phi = \dfrac{[\text{O}_3]}{[\text{NO}_x]} \approx 1$ to $10$ in urban plumes. The actual ratio depends on VOC/NOₓ ratio and sunlight intensity. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant Behaviour Comparison | Property | Primary Pollutant | Secondary Pollutant | |---|---|---| | Formation | Direct emission at source | In-situ atmospheric reaction | | Control | At source (end-of-pipe) | Precursor control (regional) | | Peak location | Near source (downwind plume) | Far from source (regional) | | Diurnal pattern | Tied to emission schedule | Tied to sunlight (afternoon peak) | | Examples | CO, SO₂, NO, PM, Pb | O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, SOA | | Spatial scale | Local to urban | Urban to continental | | Health impact | Respiratory (PM, SO₂) | Oxidative stress (O₃), acid rain | | Control technology | ESP, scrubber, cyclone, catalytic converter | Fuel switching, vehicle emission norms, regional policies | | Persistence | Variable (CO: months; SO₂: days) | Variable (O₃: hours to weeks; sulfate aerosol: days) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Primary to Secondary Pollutant Transformation Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Stationary Sources
Power Plants, Refineries] S2[Mobile Sources
Vehicles, Aircraft] S3[Natural Sources
Volcanoes, Wildfires] S4[Area Sources
Agricultural Burning] P1[Primary Pollutants
NOx, SO2, CO, VOC, PM] ATM[Atmosphere
Troposphere] SUN[Sunlight
h nu, UV Radiation] H2O[Atmospheric Moisture
H2O, OH Radical] SPN[Secondary Pollutants
O3, PAN, H2SO4, HNO3] REC[Receptors
Humans, Ecosystems, Materials] S1 --> P1 S2 --> P1 S3 --> P1 S4 --> P1 P1 --> ATM ATM --> SPN SUN --> SPN H2O --> SPN P1 --> REC SPN --> REC ATM --> H2O ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - The **left column** represents the four major source categories. - All sources emit **Primary Pollutants** directly. - The **central atmospheric block** is the "reaction chamber" where sunlight and moisture act as catalysts. - The **right column** shows the synthesized **Secondary Pollutants** and the receptors that suffer the consequences. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Photochemical Smog Formation Sequence ```mermaid flowchart LR A[NO2 Emission
Primary Pollutant] B[Sunlight
UV less than 400 nm] C[NO plus O Atom] D[O plus O2 plus M] E[O3 Formation
Secondary Pollutant] F[VOC plus OH Radical] G[RO2 Peroxy Radical] H[NO to NO2 Conversion] I[PAN Formation
CH3COOONO2] J[O3 Accumulation
Photochemical Smog] A --> C B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> J F --> G G --> H H --> J A --> H G --> I I --> J ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - **NO₂ + sunlight** is the photochemical trigger. - The **upper loop** (NO₂ → O → O₃) is the ozone production cycle. - The **lower loop** (VOC → RO₂• → NO → NO₂) is the radical chain that prevents NO from titrating O₃ back, allowing net ozone buildup. - **PAN** is a parallel product of the peroxy radical pathway — an eye-irritant in smog. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Acid Rain Formation Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD AS[Anthropogenic SO2 Emission
Coal Combustion] AN[Anthropogenic NOx Emission
Vehicles, Power Plants] VS[Volcanic SO2
Natural Source] DS[Dissolution in Cloud Droplets] OS[Oxidation by OH and O3] SA[Sulfuric Acid H2SO4] NA[Nitric Acid HNO3] AR[Acid Rain
pH less than 5.6] LD[Lake Acidification] FD[Forest Damage] BD[Building Corrosion] HD[Human Respiratory Impact] AS --> DS AS --> OS VS --> OS AN --> DS AN --> OS DS --> SA OS --> SA DS --> NA OS --> NA SA --> AR NA --> AR AR --> LD AR --> FD AR --> BD AR --> HD ``` **Interpretation of the Diagram:** - The **left vertical stack** shows emission sources (anthropogenic + natural). - The **middle block** shows the two chemical pathways: dissolution vs. oxidation. - The **right block** shows the four major environmental and health impacts of acid rain. > [!TIP] **KTU Memory Aid for Diagram Drawing in Exam** > If asked to draw in the exam, label arrows with **reaction conditions** (hν, OH, O₃, H₂O). Always distinguish between **anthropogenic** and **natural** sources with separate boxes — this earns extra credit. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]` **Differentiate between primary and secondary air pollutants. Give two examples of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Primary Pollutant | Secondary Pollutant | |---|---|---| | Definition | Emitted directly from source in harmful form | Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reaction of primary pollutants | | Source control | Yes, can be controlled at point source | Difficult; requires precursor control | | Examples | (i) Carbon monoxide (CO) from vehicles, (ii) Sulfur dioxide (SO₂) from coal combustion | (i) Ground-level ozone (O₃) from NOₓ + VOC + sunlight, (ii) Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) from SO₂ oxidation | > [!TIP] **Allocation:** 1 mark for definition of each + 0.5 mark × 2 examples × 2 categories = 1 mark; final 1 mark for the comparison table or distinction statement. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]` **What is photochemical smog? List the two main precursors and the secondary pollutants formed.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Photochemical smog is a **visible, noxious mixture of secondary pollutants** formed by the **photochemical reaction of nitrogen oxides (NOₓ) and volatile organic compounds (VOCs)** in the presence of sunlight. - **Precursors (Primary):** NOₓ and VOCs - **Secondary Pollutants Formed:** Ozone (O₃), Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN), aldehydes, secondary organic aerosols > [!WARNING] **Common Mistake:** Writing "smog = smoke + fog" is the *London-type* (classical/reducing) smog, primarily SO₂ + particulate. KTU expects *photochemical (oxidizing/Los Angeles-type) smog* when not specified — clarify both. --- ### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]` **(a)** Explain the formation mechanism of photochemical smog with all the major reaction steps. Discuss the role of sunlight and NOₓ in the process. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the half-life and the time required for 90% decay of SO₂ in the atmosphere, given that the rate constant for gas-phase oxidation by OH radical is $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$. Assume first-order kinetics. **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks** Photochemical smog formation is a complex chain of free-radical reactions occurring in the lower troposphere. The mechanism is described as follows: **Step 1 — Initiation (Photolysis of NO₂):** The reaction is initiated by absorption of UV radiation (wavelength < 424 nm) by NO₂, which is the only common atmospheric species that absorbs strongly in the visible-UV range. $$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}(^3\text{P})$$ **Step 2 — Ozone Formation:** The atomic oxygen produced rapidly combines with molecular oxygen in the presence of a third body M (N₂ or O₂) to form ozone. $$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + M \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + M$$ **Step 3 — Titration Equilibrium:** In the absence of VOCs, NO and O₃ react in a null cycle that maintains a photostationary state with low O₃. $$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2$$ **Step 4 — OH Radical Generation:** O₃ undergoes photolysis in the UV (Hartley band, λ < 320 nm) to produce excited singlet O atom, which reacts with water vapor to form hydroxyl radicals (the "detergent of the atmosphere"). $$\text{O}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}(^1\text{D})$$ $$\text{O}(^1\text{D}) + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{OH}^{\bullet}$$ **Step 5 — VOC Oxidation:** OH• abstracts a hydrogen atom from VOCs (RH), forming alkyl radicals, which add O₂ to produce peroxy radicals. $$\text{RH} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ $$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^{\bullet}$$ **Step 6 — Net NO to NO₂ Conversion:** The peroxy radical oxidizes NO to NO₂ **without consuming O₃**, breaking the null cycle and allowing net O₃ accumulation. $$\text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2$$ **Step 7 — PAN Formation:** The acyl peroxy radicals (especially CH₃C(O)OO•) react with NO₂ to form PAN, a powerful lachrymator and phytotoxin. $$\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2 \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 \text{ (PAN)}$$ **Role of Sunlight:** Sunlight (specifically UV) is the **trigger** that initiates the chain by photolyzing NO₂ and O₃. Without sunlight, no smog forms — hence the late afternoon peak and seasonal maximum in summer. **Role of NOₓ:** NOₓ acts as the **catalytic intermediate** in O₃ production. Each NOₓ molecule cycles many times, producing multiple O₃ molecules — this is why NOₓ control is central to ozone abatement strategies. > [!TIP] **Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 marks):** > - Initiation step (NO₂ photolysis) — 1 mark > - O₃ formation — 1 mark > - OH radical generation — 1 mark > - VOC → RO₂• → NO → NO₂ cycle — 2 marks > - PAN formation — 1 mark > - Roles of sunlight and NOₓ — 1 mark --- ##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** $k = 1.5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}^{-1}$, first-order kinetics. **Step 1: First-order decay equation** $$[C]_t = [C]_0 \, e^{-kt}$$ **Step 2: Half-life formula** $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$ $$t_{1/2} = 46{,}200 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3: Convert to hours** $$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{46{,}200}{3600} = 12.83 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 4: Time for 90% decay** (i.e., $[C]_t = 0.10 [C]_0$) $$\dfrac{[C]_t}{[C]_0} = 0.10 = e^{-kt_{90\%}}$$ **Step 5: Take natural logarithm** $$\ln(0.10) = -k t_{90\%}$$ $$-2.303 = -1.5 \times 10^{-5} \times t_{90\%}$$ **Step 6: Solve for $t_{90\%}$** $$t_{90\%} = \dfrac{2.303}{1.5 \times 10^{-5}} = 153{,}533 \text{ s}$$ **Step 7: Convert to hours and days** $$t_{90\%} = \dfrac{153{,}533}{3600} = 42.65 \text{ hours} \approx 1.78 \text{ days}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{t_{1/2} \approx 12.83 \text{ hours}; \quad t_{90\%} \approx 42.65 \text{ hours} \approx 1.78 \text{ days}}$$ > [!WARNING] **Common Marks Lost Here:** > 1. Forgetting to convert seconds to hours — examiner deducts 0.5 mark. > 2. Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ — gives wrong numerical answer. > 3. Not mentioning the assumption of first-order kinetics — deduct 0.5 mark. > 4. Writing "0.693" without the symbol $k$ in denominator — formula must be symbolic. --- #### **Question B (Choice 2)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model]` **(a)** What are secondary particulate matters? How are they formed in the atmosphere? Discuss their environmental significance. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coal-fired power plant emits SO₂ at a rate of 100 g/s. The conversion efficiency of SO₂ to H₂SO₄ aerosol is 20%. Calculate the daily mass of sulfuric acid aerosol formed and the volume of 0.1 M NaOH solution required to neutralize this acid (assume complete neutralization). **(Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol).** **(7 marks)** --- ##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Definition:** Secondary particulate matter (SPM) refers to airborne particles that are **not emitted directly** as solids or liquids but are **formed in the atmosphere** through gas-to-particle conversion processes involving primary gaseous precursors such as SO₂, NOₓ, NH₃, and VOCs. **Formation Mechanisms:** **1. Gas-Phase Oxidation followed by Nucleation:** $$\text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{SO}_3 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (vapor)}$$ $$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (vapor)} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (aerosol, sub-micron size)}$$ **2. Aqueous-Phase Oxidation (in cloud droplets):** $$\text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{SO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HSO}_3^-$$ $$\text{HSO}_3^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **3. Nitrate Aerosol Formation:** $$\text{NO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3$$ $$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{NH}_3 \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{NO}_3 \text{ (aerosol)}$$ **4. Secondary Organic Aerosol (SOA):** Oxidation of VOCs produces semi-volatile organic compounds that condense onto pre-existing particles. **Environmental Significance:** - **Health:** PM₂.₅ penetrates deep into alveoli, causing asthma, bronchitis, cardiovascular disease. Secondary sulfate and nitrate constitute up to 60–80% of urban PM₂.₅ mass. - **Visibility:** Sulfate aerosols scatter light, causing **haze** and reducing atmospheric visibility (regional haze in Indo-Gangetic plain). - **Climate:** Sulfate aerosols scatter solar radiation (negative radiative forcing, **global dimming**); organic aerosols absorb/reflect. - **Acid Deposition:** Precursors of acid rain. - **Ecosystem Damage:** Eutrophication from NH₄⁺ deposition. > [!TIP] **Mark Allocation (7 marks):** Definition (1) + Formation mechanisms (3) + Environmental significance (3). --- ##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** - SO₂ emission rate: $Q = 100 \text{ g/s}$ - Conversion efficiency: $\eta = 20\% = 0.20$ - Molar mass of H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol - NaOH concentration: 0.1 M **Step 1: Mass of SO₂ emitted per day** $$\text{Time} = 24 \times 3600 = 86{,}400 \text{ s/day}$$ $$m_{\text{SO}_2} = 100 \text{ g/s} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s} = 8.64 \times 10^6 \text{ g/day} = 8.64 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ **Step 2: Moles of SO₂ emitted per day** $$n_{\text{SO}_2} = \dfrac{8.64 \times 10^6}{64} = 1.35 \times 10^5 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 3: Stoichiometry of conversion** The conversion reaction: $\text{SO}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is 1:1 in moles. $$n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = \eta \times n_{\text{SO}_2} = 0.20 \times 1.35 \times 10^5 = 2.7 \times 10^4 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 4: Mass of H₂SO₄ formed** $$m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 2.7 \times 10^4 \times 98 = 2.646 \times 10^6 \text{ g/day} = 2.646 \text{ tonnes/day}$$ **Step 5: Neutralization reaction** $$\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ Mole ratio: 1 mol H₂SO₄ : 2 mol NaOH $$n_{\text{NaOH}} = 2 \times n_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 2 \times 2.7 \times 10^4 = 5.4 \times 10^4 \text{ mol/day}$$ **Step 6: Volume of 0.1 M NaOH** $$V = \dfrac{n}{M} = \dfrac{5.4 \times 10^4}{0.1} = 5.4 \times 10^5 \text{ L/day}$$ **Step 7: Express in cubic meters** $$V = 5.4 \times 10^5 \text{ L} = 540 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{m_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} = 2.646 \text{ tonnes/day}; \quad V_{\text{NaOH}} = 540 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}}$$ > [!WARNING] **Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the 1:2 stoichiometry** between H₂SO₄ and NaOH (most common error — costs 2 marks). > 2. **Using 20% to multiply mass instead of moles** — wrong answer, but the value 529.2 g/day is similar; deduct 1 mark. > 3. **Not converting g/s to g/day** — 86,400 factor is critical. > 4. **Not stating the 1:1 mole ratio for SO₂ → H₂SO₄** explicitly in the solution. --- ### 🎯 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Frequent Mark-Deduction Patterns in This Topic:** > > 1. **Confusing primary with secondary pollutants** — students often wrongly classify CO₂ as secondary (it is **NOT a pollutant** in the criteria sense; it is a primary emission from combustion, but only listed when its atmospheric concentration is anomalous). > > 2. **Writing "O₃ is a primary pollutant"** — WRONG.
