Stratospheric O₃ is natural; tropospheric/ground-level O₃ is **secondary**. > > 3. **Skipping reaction conditions** — equations without $h\nu$ (sunlight), M (third body), or OH• are considered incomplete; **deduct 0.5–1 mark per equation**. > > 4. **Numerical problems:** failing to balance acid-base stoichiometry is the single biggest killer of marks. Always **balance first, then compute**. > > 5. **Misclassifying PM₂.₅** — both primary (direct emission) and secondary (formed from SO₂/NH₃) sources exist. Be specific. > > 6. **Diagrams:** A flowchart without arrow labels and source/sink identification earns only 1–2 marks out of the allocated 3–4. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Primary Pollutants:** Emitted **directly** from a source — CO, SO₂, NO, NO₂, PM, Pb, VOC, NH₃, hydrocarbons. - ✅ **Secondary Pollutants:** Formed **in the atmosphere** via chemical/photochemical reactions — O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, sulfates, nitrates, SOA. - ✅ **Photochemical Smog:** NOₓ + VOC + $h\nu$ → O₃ + PAN; peaks in afternoon, summer, urban areas. - ✅ **Classical (London) Smog:** SO₂ + PM + fog; occurs in cold, humid conditions; reducing in nature. - ✅ **Photochemical Smog:** NOₓ + VOC + sunlight; occurs in warm, sunny, dry conditions; oxidizing in nature. - ✅ **Acid Rain:** pH < 5.6; caused by H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ from SO₂ and NOₓ oxidation. - ✅ **Acid Rain Formulae:** $2\text{SO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$; $4\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4\text{HNO}_3$. - ✅ **OH• Radical:** "Atmospheric detergent"; produced from O₃ photolysis + H₂O; concentration ≈ 10⁶ molecules/cm³. - ✅ **PAN:** Peroxyacetyl Nitrate, CH₃C(O)OONO₂; lachrymator + phytotoxin; marker of photochemical smog. - ✅ **Half-life formula:** $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$ (for first-order kinetics). - ✅ **Ozone production stoichiometry:** 1 mole NOₓ oxidized → ≈ 1 mole O₃ (VOC-limited) to several moles (NOₓ-limited). - ✅ **Secondary PM formation pathways:** Gas-phase oxidation, aqueous-phase oxidation, gas-to-particle nucleation, condensation. - ✅ **Critical Concept:** Secondary pollutants cannot be controlled at a single point source — they require **regional, multi-pollutant precursor control strategies**. - ✅ **EIA Implication:** Always identify whether the project (e.g., thermal power plant, highway, refinery) contributes to primary emissions only, or also to precursors of secondary pollutants. - ✅ **Key Mnemonic:** **P**rimary = **P**re-formed; **S**econdary = **S**ky-formed. - ✅ **Numerical Tip:** Always convert seconds to hours, use **ln 2 ≈ 0.693**, and balance all stoichiometric equations before computing moles.

topics.title: Criteria Pollutants and Their Impacts on Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Criteria Pollutants and Their Impacts on Environment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Criteria Pollutants (Definition)** > *Criteria pollutants* are a set of **six (or seven) air pollutants** that have been identified by the **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA)** as causing **adverse effects on human health and the environment**, and for which **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** have been established under the **Clean Air Act, 1971 (amended 1990)**. They are called "criteria" pollutants because the EPA publishes **criteria documents** outlining the scientific basis (health, environmental, and welfare effects) used to set regulatory standards for each pollutant. The **six principal criteria pollutants** (often called the *"SIX CRITERIA POLLUTANTS"* or the *"BIG SIX"*) are: | S.No. | Pollutant | Chemical Symbol | Primary Classification | |:-----:|:----------|:----------------|:-----------------------| | 1 | Carbon Monoxide | **CO** | Gaseous | | 2 | Lead | **Pb** | Particulate / Metallic | | 3 | Nitrogen Dioxide | **NO₂** | Gaseous (NOx family) | | 4 | Ozone | **O₃** | Gaseous (Secondary) | | 5 | Particulate Matter | **PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅** | Particulate | | 6 | Sulfur Dioxide | **SO₂** | Gaseous (SOx family) | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:** Some international frameworks (e.g., CPCB India, WHO 2021) treat **PM (particulate matter)** as two separate sub-criteria — **PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅** — and consider **NO₂** as a proxy for **NOx**, making it a "Big 7" framework. For KTU examinations, students are expected to know **all six** with **PM split into two size fractions**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding Imagine a **hospital triage room** where doctors categorize incoming patients into a "critical list" of six most dangerous ailments. Just like a hospital identifies the **most common life-threatening conditions** and prepares treatment protocols (criteria-based medicine), the EPA identified the **six most widespread and harmful airborne substances** and prepared **regulatory criteria documents** — hence the name *criteria pollutants*. **The Traffic Junction Analogy:** Think of a busy traffic signal in a city. The pollutants emitted are like the visible smoke (CO from idling cars), invisible gases (NO₂ from combustion), and microscopic dust (PM₂.₅ from tyre/brake wear). Each pollutant behaves differently: - Some are **directly emitted** (primary pollutants — e.g., CO, SO₂) - Some are **formed in the atmosphere** by chemical reactions (secondary pollutants — e.g., O₃, NO₂ partly) - Some are **invisible but deadly** (CO binds haemoglobin 200× tighter than oxygen) ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Reference Values (Bold for Memorization)** > - **NAAQS** = **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (US EPA)** > - **CPCB** = **Central Pollution Control Board (India) — NAAQS equivalent** > - **AQI** = **Air Quality Index** (composite indicator, scale 0–500) > - **CAA** = **Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1977 & 1990)** > - **Primary Standard** = protects **human health** with adequate margin of safety > - **Secondary Standard** = protects **welfare (visibility, crops, animals, materials)** The standard concentration units used in NAAQS are: - **ppm** (parts per million) — volumetric - **µg/m³** (micrograms per cubic metre) — mass-based - **ppb** (parts per billion) — for trace gases like O₃, NO₂ Conversion relation (at 25°C, 1 atm): $$ 1 \text{ ppm} = \frac{M}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ where *M* is the molecular weight of the gas. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Criteria Pollutants ### 2.1.1 Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants > [!NOTE] > **Core Conceptual Distinction (Frequently asked in KTU ESE)** > - **Primary Pollutant:** Emitted *directly* from a source (e.g., CO from vehicles, SO₂ from coal plants). > - **Secondary Pollutant:** Formed in the atmosphere by *chemical reactions* of primary pollutants with sunlight, water vapor, or other species (e.g., O₃ is a secondary pollutant formed by photochemical reactions of NOₓ and VOCs). **Pollutant Classification Matrix:** | Pollutant | Primary or Secondary | Major Anthropogenic Source | |:----------|:-------------------|:---------------------------| | CO | Primary | Incomplete combustion of fossil fuels (vehicles) | | Pb | Primary | Leaded petrol (now phased out), metal smelting, battery recycling | | NO₂ | Both (mainly secondary in ambient air) | Vehicle exhaust, power plants, lightning (natural) | | O₃ | **Strictly Secondary** | NOT emitted directly; formed via photochemical smog reactions | | PM₁₀ / PM₂.₅ | Both | Combustion, dust, secondary aerosols (sulfates, nitrates) | | SO₂ | Primary | Coal/oil combustion, smelting, volcanic activity | ### 2.2 Detailed Profile of Each Criteria Pollutant #### A. Carbon Monoxide (CO) - **Formation Chemistry:** Incomplete combustion of carbonaceous fuels. $$2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO \quad \text{(in oxygen-deficient conditions)}$$ - **Health Impact:** Binds with haemoglobin to form **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)**, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity. The affinity of CO for haemoglobin is **~200–250 times** that of O₂. - **Environmental Impact:** Contributes to **tropospheric ozone formation** and participates in the **atmospheric CO–OH radical cycle** (governs atmospheric lifetime of many gases). - **Major Sources:** Vehicle exhaust (~75% in urban areas), industrial processes, biomass burning, cigarette smoke. #### B. Lead (Pb) - **Historical Significance:** Was a major pollutant from **leaded petrol (TEL — tetraethyl lead)**. Phased out globally due to severe neurotoxic effects, especially in children. - **Health Impact:** Causes **neurological damage**, **reduced IQ in children**, **anaemia**, **kidney damage**, and **cardiovascular issues**. - **Environmental Impact:** Accumulates in **soil and sediments** (persistent); enters food chain via plants. - **Major Sources:** Battery manufacturing, metal smelting, e-waste, old lead-based paint. #### C. Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂) - **Formation Chemistry:** High-temperature combustion fixes atmospheric N₂: $$N_2 + O_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\; \text{high T}} 2NO$$ $$2NO + O_2 \rightarrow 2NO_2$$ - **Health Impact:** Respiratory irritant, **reduces lung function**, increases susceptibility to infections. - **Environmental Impact:** **Acid rain** precursor (forms HNO₃), **eutrophication** of water bodies, **tropospheric O₃** precursor, contributes to **visibility reduction** (haze). - **Major Sources:** Motor vehicles, power plants, industrial boilers, lightning. #### D. Ozone (O₃) - **Formation Chemistry — Photochemical Smog:** $$NO_2 + h\nu \; (\lambda < 424 \text{ nm}) \rightarrow NO + O$$ $$O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$ In the presence of VOCs, NO is regenerated, leading to net O₃ accumulation. - **Health Impact:** **Chest pain, coughing, throat irritation, airway inflammation**, worsens asthma, bronchitis. - **Environmental Impact:** Damages **crops, forests (chlorotic lesions on leaves)**, degrades **rubber and polymers**, reduces **visibility**. - **Important Note:** O₃ is **NOT emitted directly**; it is a **secondary pollutant** — a "**good up high, bad nearby**" gas (stratospheric O₃ protects from UV; tropospheric O₃ is harmful). #### E. Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅) - **Definition:** Suspension of **solid and liquid droplets** in air. - **PM₁₀:** aerodynamic diameter $\le 10 \; \mu m$ (thoracic particles) - **PM₂.₅:** aerodynamic diameter $\le 2.5 \; \mu m$ (fine, respirable) - **PM₀.₁:** ultrafine ($\le 0.1 \; \mu m$) - **Health Impact:** PM₂.₅ penetrates deep into **alveoli** and can even enter **bloodstream**, causing **cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, premature death**. - **Environmental Impact:** **Visibility impairment** (haze), **acid rain** (sulfate, nitrate aerosols), **climate forcing** (aerosols), soiling of buildings. - **Major Sources:** Combustion, dust, secondary sulfate/nitrate aerosols, wildfires, volcanic ash. #### F. Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) - **Formation Chemistry:** Combustion of sulfur-containing fuels. $$S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2$$ $$2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3 \quad \text{(catalysed)}$$ $$SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 \quad \text{(acid rain)}$$ - **Health Impact:** Respiratory irritant, **bronchoconstriction**, aggravates asthma. - **Environmental Impact:** **Acid rain** (pH < 5.6), damage to **buildings and monuments** (e.g., Taj Mahal marble corrosion), **soil acidification**, **lake acidification** (kills aquatic life). - **Major Sources:** Coal-fired power plants, oil refineries, metal smelting, volcanoes. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR KTU ESE — Module 4** | # | Formula / Concept | Application | Units | |:-:|:------------------|:------------|:------| | 1 | $C_t = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}$ (Pollutant decay with time) | Box model, plume dispersion | $\mu g/m^3$ | | 2 | $\text{AQI} = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} (C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | AQI calculation for any pollutant | dimensionless | | 3 | $\text{Conversion: } 1 \text{ ppm} = \dfrac{M}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3$ | ppm ↔ mg/m³ interconversion at 25°C, 1 atm | — | | 4 | $C_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum C_i \cdot t_i}{\sum t_i}$ | Time-weighted average exposure (e.g., 8-hr CO) | ppm or $\mu g/m^3$ | | 5 | Photochemical O₃ formation: $NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O$ | Tropospheric ozone chemistry | — | | 6 | Acid rain pH: $SO_2 / NO_x + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 / HNO_3$ | Wet deposition | pH units | | 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Removal efficiency (control device) | % | | 8 | Settling velocity (Stokes): $v_s = \dfrac{g d^2 (\rho_p - \rho_a)}{18 \mu}$ | PM removal by gravity | m/s | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Mermaid Safe Note:** Above tables use `\vert` and `\mu` instead of pipe character `|` to avoid markdown table breakage. Always use `$\vert x \vert$` for absolute value notation in KTU notes. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Criteria pollutants underpin several practical engineering and policy applications: 1. **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA):** Any new industry, highway, or thermal power plant requires a **baseline air quality monitoring** for at least 3 criteria pollutants over **3 months** (one season per quarter). 2. **Air Quality Index (AQI) Calculation:** CPCB's AQI uses 8 pollutants (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb) and categorizes health impact in 6 bands: *Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500)*. 3. **Emission Inventory Compilation:** Power of 10 — preparing source-wise emission estimates for cities. 4. **Pollution Control Device Design:** Scrubbers (for SO₂), electrostatic precipitators (for PM), catalytic converters (for CO, NOₓ, HC). 5. **Climate Change Co-benefit Analysis:** Reducing criteria pollutants also reduces **black carbon** and **methane** — short-lived climate forcers. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conversion between ppm and µg/m³ (Hall's Equation) The relationship between volumetric mixing ratio (ppm) and mass concentration ($\mu g/m^3$) for a gaseous pollutant is essential for KTU numerical problems. ### Starting Principle By the **Ideal Gas Law:** $$ PV = nRT $$ For 1 m³ of air at standard conditions (T = 298 K, P = 1 atm = 101.325 kPa): $$ n = \frac{PV}{RT} = \frac{101.325 \times 10^3 \; Pa \times 1 \; m^3}{8.314 \; J/(mol \cdot K) \times 298 \; K} $$ ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Compute moles of air per m³. $$ n = \frac{101325}{2477.572} \approx 40.9 \; mol/m^3 $$ **Step 2:** Note that 1 ppm (by volume) corresponds to 1 part per million *moles*, so: $$ \text{Mass concentration (g/m}^3) = \frac{1}{10^6} \times n \times M $$ where *M* is molecular weight (g/mol). **Step 3:** Simplify at 25°C, 1 atm: $$ 1 \; ppm = \frac{40.9 \times M}{10^6} \; g/m^3 = \frac{M}{24.45} \; mg/m^3 $$ ### Final Result $$ \boxed{\;1 \; ppm = \frac{M}{24.45} \; mg/m^3 = \frac{M}{24.45} \times 10^3 \; \mu g/m^3\;} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example **Q:** The 1-hour NAAQS for CO is **35 ppm**. Convert to $\mu g/m^3$. (M of CO = 28 g/mol) **Solution:** $$ 1 \; ppm \text{ of CO} = \frac{28}{24.45} \; mg/m^3 = 1.145 \; mg/m^3 = 1145 \; \mu g/m^3 $$ $$ 35 \; ppm = 35 \times 1145 = 40075 \; \mu g/m^3 \approx 40 \; mg/m^3 $$ This is the EPA 1-hour standard historically; current NAAQS uses 8-hour averaging. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: AQI Sub-Index Calculation (Linear Interpolation) The Air Quality Index is calculated using **truncated linear interpolation** between breakpoint concentrations. ### Standard Formula $$ I_P = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_P - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo} $$ where: - $I_P$ = AQI sub-index for pollutant *P* - $C_P$ = measured concentration of pollutant *P* - $BP_{Hi}, BP_{Lo}$ = concentration breakpoints *higher* and *lower* - $I_{Hi}, I_{Lo}$ = AQI values corresponding to those breakpoints (typically 0, 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, 500) ### Step-by-Step Numerical Example **Given:** PM₂.₅ measured concentration $C_P = 75 \; \mu g/m^3$ (24-hour average). **Step 1:** Look up breakpoint table (CPCB India, 24-hr PM₂.₅): | AQI | 0–50 | 51–100 | 101–200 | 201–300 | 301–400 | 401–500 | |:---:|:----:|:------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:| | PM₂.₅ ($\mu g/m^3$) | 0–30 | 31–60 | 61–90 | 91–120 | 121–250 | 251+ | Here, $C_P = 75$ falls in **61–90** range, so: - $BP_{Lo} = 61$, $BP_{Hi} = 90$ - $I_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Hi} = 200$ **Step 2:** Substitute into formula: $$ I_P = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} (75 - 61) + 101 $$ **Step 3:** Compute numerator and denominator: $$ I_P = \frac{99}{29} \times 14 + 101 $$ **Step 4:** Multiply and add: $$ I_P = 3.4138 \times 14 + 101 = 47.79 + 101 = 148.79 $$ **Step 5:** Round to nearest integer: $$ \boxed{\;I_P \approx 149 \; \text{(Moderate AQI category)}\;} $$ ### Overall AQI Determination The **overall AQI** is the **maximum** of all sub-indices: $$ AQI = \max(I_{PM_{2.5}}, I_{PM_{10}}, I_{NO_2}, I_{SO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb}) $$ This ensures the index reflects the **worst-case pollutant**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Photochemical Ozone Formation — Steady-State Approximation ### Key Reactions $$ (1) \;\; NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \qquad k_1 $$ $$ (2) \;\; O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M \qquad k_2 $$ $$ (3) \;\; O_3 + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2 \qquad k_3 $$ ### Apply Steady-State Approximation At **steady state**, generation = consumption for *O* and *O₃*: $$ \frac{d[O]}{dt} = k_1[NO_2] - k_2[O][O_2][M] = 0 $$ $$ \frac{d[O_3]}{dt} = k_2[O][O_2][M] - k_3[O_3][NO] = 0 $$ ### Solve for [O₃] From the first equation: $$ [O] = \frac{k_1[NO_2]}{k_2[O_2][M]} $$ Substitute into the second equation: $$ k_2 \cdot \frac{k_1[NO_2]}{k_2[O_2][M]} \cdot [O_2][M] - k_3[O_3][NO] = 0 $$ Simplify: $$ k_1[NO_2] = k_3[O_3][NO] $$ Therefore, the **photochemical steady-state relation** is: $$ \boxed{\;[O_3] = \frac{k_1 [NO_2]}{k_3 [NO]}\;} $$ ### Physical Interpretation - The **O₃ concentration is proportional to the NO₂/NO ratio**. - In the **presence of VOCs**, NO is oxidized to NO₂ (without consuming O₃), so [NO] drops and [O₃] rises — this is the basis of **VOC-limited smog chemistry**. - This explains why **control strategies for urban ozone** must address **both NOₓ and VOCs** (NOₓ-only control can paradoxically increase O₃ under VOC-limited conditions — a phenomenon called **"NOₓ Disbenefit"**). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant-Wise Impact Summary | Pollutant | Major Source | Health Impact (COs: CO2–CO3) | Environmental Impact (CO4) | Control Technology | |:----------|:-------------|:------------------------------|:---------------------------|:-------------------| | **CO** | Vehicles, biomass burning | COHb formation, hypoxia, headache, death at >500 ppm | Indirect — affects OH radical cycle | Catalytic converter, better combustion | | **Pb** | Smelting, batteries, old paints | Neurotoxic, IQ loss in children, anaemia | Soil persistence, bioaccumulation | Unleaded fuel, electrostatic precipitators | | **NO₂** | Combustion, lightning | Respiratory irritation, lung function loss | Acid rain, eutrophication, smog | SCR (Selective Catalytic Reduction), low-NOx burners | | **O₃** | **Secondary** (NOₓ + VOC + sunlight) | Asthma aggravation, lung damage | Crop damage, polymer degradation | VOC/NOₓ reduction, control of precursors | | **PM₂.₅ / PM₁₀** | Combustion, dust, secondary aerosols | Alveolar deposition, CVD, lung cancer | Haze, climate forcing, acid rain | ESP, bag filters, wet scrubbers | | **SO₂** | Coal, oil, smelting | Bronchoconstriction, asthma | Acid rain, building corrosion, lake acidification | FGD (Flue Gas Desulfurization), low-sulfur fuel | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Sources of Criteria Pollutants — Block Architecture This block-level diagram shows the interconnections between major anthropogenic sources and the six criteria pollutants, fulfilling the CO mapping requirement of KTU 2024 EIA syllabus. ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[Mobile Sources
Cars Trucks Buses] --> B1[CO] A1 --> B2[NO2] A1 --> B3[PM25 PM10] A1 --> B4[Pb if leaded] A2[Stationary Sources
Power Plants Industries] --> B5[SO2] A2 --> B2 A2 --> B3 A2 --> B6[NO2 from thermal plants] A3[Area Sources
Biomass Burning Open Burning] --> B1 A3 --> B3 A3 --> B7[VOCs precursors] A4[Industrial Processes
Smelters Refineries] --> B4 A4 --> B5 A4 --> B3 A5[Natural Sources
Volcanoes Lightning Dust] --> B5 A5 --> B2 A5 --> B3 B1[CO Primary] --> C1[Troposphere Mixing] B2[NO2 Primary and Secondary] --> C1 B3[PM Primary and Secondary] --> C1 B4[Pb Primary] --> C1 B5[SO2 Primary] --> C1 B7[VOCs] --> C1 C1 --> D1[Photochemical Reactions
Sunlight] D1 --> E1[O3 Secondary Pollutant] D1 --> E2[NO2 Secondary Formation] D1 --> E3[Secondary Sulfate Nitrate Aerosols] C1 --> F1[Human Exposure] C1 --> F2[Ecosystem Impact] C1 --> F3[Material Damage] C1 --> F4[Visibility and Climate] E1 --> F1 E1 --> F2 E2 --> F1 E3 --> F1 E3 --> F4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** This block architecture maps *source → primary emission → atmospheric chemistry → impact pathway*. KTU 2024 students are expected to draw or describe this connectivity in 7–14 mark questions. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Photochemical Smog Formation Cycle (NOx–VOC–O₃) ```mermaid flowchart LR NOX[NOx in Atmosphere] --> P1[NO2 Photolysis by Sunlight] P1 --> P2[O Atom Released] P2 --> P3[O plus O2 gives O3] P3 --> P4[O3 Reacts with NO] P4 --> P5[Back to NO2] P5 --> P1 VOC[VOCs from Vehicles and Solvents] --> P6[VOC Oxidised by OH and NOx] P6 --> P5 P6 --> P7[Peroxyacetyl Nitrate PAN] P3 --> P8[Tropospheric Ozone Pool] P5 --> P8 P7 --> P9[Smog and Haze] P8 --> P9 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** The cycle produces **O₃ + PAN** as secondary pollutants. PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate) is a severe **eye irritant** and one of the most phytotoxic compounds in smog. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Human Health Impact Pathway ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Pollutant Emission Source] --> S2[Atmospheric Dispersion and Transport] S2 --> S3[Inhalation Exposure] S3 --> S4[Respiratory Tract Deposition] S4 --> S5[PM10 in Bronchi and Trachea] S4 --> S6[PM25 in Alveoli] S6 --> S7[Bloodstream Entry] S7 --> S8[Cardiovascular Disease] S7 --> S9[Systemic Inflammation] S3 --> S10[CO binds Hemoglobin] S10 --> S11[Carboxyhemoglobin COHb] S11 --> S12[Tissue Hypoxia] S3 --> S13[O3 and NO2 Oxidative Stress] S13 --> S14[Asthma and Lung Function Decline] S3 --> S15[Pb Neurotoxicity] S15 --> S16[Cognitive Impairment in Children] ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Acid Rain Formation and Environmental Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TB E1[SO2 from Coal Combustion] --> F1[Atmospheric Oxidation] E2[NOx from Vehicles and Plants] --> F1 F1 --> F2[Sulfuric Acid H2SO4] F1 --> F3[Nitric Acid HNO3] F2 --> G1[Acid Rain pH less than 5.6] F3 --> G1 G1 --> H1[Soil Acidification Loss of Nutrients] G1 --> H2[Lake Acidification Fish Death] G1 --> H3[Forest Decline] G1 --> H4[Building and Monument Corrosion] G1 --> H5[Drinking Water Contamination] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define criteria pollutants. List the six criteria pollutants as identified by the US EPA.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Criteria pollutants are air pollutants identified by the US EPA under the Clean Air Act, 1970 (amended 1990) for which National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) are set, based on published "criteria documents" describing their adverse effects on health and environment. **List (1.5 marks):** The six criteria pollutants are: 1. Carbon Monoxide (CO) 2. Lead (Pb) 3. Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂) 4. Ozone (O₃) 5. Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅) 6. Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂) ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Why is tropospheric ozone considered a secondary pollutant? Explain with reactions.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Ozone is called a **secondary pollutant** because it is **not emitted directly** into the atmosphere from any major source. It is **formed in situ** through photochemical reactions involving **NOₓ and VOCs in the presence of sunlight**. Key reactions (1.5 marks): $$ NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \quad (\lambda < 424 \; nm) $$ $$ O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M $$ In the presence of VOCs, NO is regenerated from NO₂, allowing net O₃ accumulation (1.5 marks). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Option for 14-mark KTU question **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style — Module 4]** **(a)** Explain the formation, sources, and health effects of **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)** as a criteria pollutant. Mention its role in **acid rain** with relevant chemical equations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the environmental and health impacts of **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)**. Why is PM₂.₅ considered more dangerous than PM₁₀? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Formation (2 marks):** SO₂ is formed primarily during the combustion of sulfur-containing fossil fuels (coal, oil). The sulfur reacts with oxygen: $$ S + O_2 \rightarrow SO_2 $$ It can also be produced by metal smelting of sulfide ores (e.g., CuS, ZnS). **Major Sources (2 marks):** - Coal-fired thermal power plants (~60% of anthropogenic SO₂) - Petroleum refineries - Non-ferrous metal smelters (Cu, Zn, Pb) - Natural: volcanic eruptions, oceanic dimethyl sulfide (DMS) **Health Effects (1.5 marks):** - Respiratory irritant; causes **bronchoconstriction** and **mucus hypersecretion** - Aggravates **asthma, bronchitis, and emphysema** - High exposure (≥ 100 ppm) leads to **laryngeal spasm** and **acute lung injury** **Acid Rain Role (1.5 marks):** $$ 2SO_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2SO_3 $$ $$ SO_3 + H_2O \rightarrow H_2SO_4 $$ The H₂SO₄ dissociates in water releasing H⁺ ions, lowering rainwater pH below the natural value of 5.6 to as low as 4.0 in heavily polluted regions. This causes: - Soil acidification - Lake acidification (kills fish, e.g., Scandinavia and eastern North America) - Damage to marble structures (Taj Mahal, Parthenon) - Forest decline (Waldsterben in Germany) #### Model Solution to (b): **Health Impacts of PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅ (3 marks):** PM₁₀ particles (≤ 10 µm) get trapped in the **upper respiratory tract** (nose, throat, bronchi) and cause: - Coughing, wheezing - Reduced lung function - Aggravation of asthma PM₂.₅ particles (≤ 2.5 µm) penetrate deep into the **alveoli** and even enter the **bloodstream**, leading to: - Cardiovascular disease (atherosclerosis, arrhythmia) - Premature death (epidemiological studies show 6% mortality increase per 10 µg/m³ rise) - Lung cancer (classified as Group 1 carcinogen by IARC) **Environmental Impacts (2 marks):** - **Visibility reduction** due to light scattering (haze) - **Acid rain** carriers (sulfate and nitrate aerosols) - **Climate forcing** (negative, partially offsetting global warming) - **Soiling** of buildings and cultural monuments **Why PM₂.₅ is More Dangerous (2 marks):** - Smaller size means **larger surface area per unit mass** — adsorbs more toxic chemicals (PAHs, heavy metals). - Can **bypass the body's natural defense mechanisms** (mucociliary escalator) and reach the gas-exchange regions. - **Longer atmospheric lifetime** (days to weeks) → greater long-range transport. - Penetrates the **alveolar–capillary membrane** → systemic effects on heart, brain, and other organs. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style — Module 4]** **(a)** Describe the **photochemical formation of tropospheric ozone** in the NOₓ–VOC–O₃ cycle. State why control strategies for urban O₃ must address **both NOₓ and VOCs**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** What are the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)**? List the Indian CPCB NAAQS for **PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, and Pb**. Compute the AQI given a PM₂.₅ concentration of **85 µg/m³** (24-hr average). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution to (a): **Photochemical Ozone Formation (4 marks):** The classic **Leighton ratio** (steady-state) cycle in the troposphere is: $$ NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \quad (k_1) $$ $$ O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M \quad (k_2) $$ $$ O_3 + NO \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2 \quad (k_3) $$ In the **absence of VOCs**, this cycle is self-quenching — O₃ and NO react and regenerate NO₂. However, in **urban atmospheres rich in VOCs** (from vehicles, solvents, gasoline evaporation), peroxy radicals (RO₂·, HO₂·) convert NO back to NO₂ **without consuming O₃**: $$ RO_2 + NO \rightarrow RO + NO_2 $$ This breaks the O₃–NO cycle and allows **net O₃ accumulation**. In addition, NO₂ may form **PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate)** and other oxidants. **Control Strategy Implication (3 marks):** - Under **NOₓ-limited** conditions (rural/downwind): Reducing NOₓ effectively reduces O₃. - Under **VOC-limited** conditions (urban): Reducing VOCs is more effective. - In **transition regimes**, reducing only one pollutant can **paradoxically increase O₃** (the "NOₓ disbenefit"). - Therefore, **integrated NOₓ + VOC control** is essential, supported by photochemical grid models (e.g., EPA's Models-3/CMAQ, CAMx). #### Model Solution to (b): **Definition of NAAQS (1 mark):** NAAQS are standards set by regulatory agencies (US EPA, India's CPCB) specifying the maximum permissible concentration of criteria pollutants in ambient air to protect public health and welfare. **CPCB NAAQS (2009, updated 2024) – Key Pollutants (2 marks):** | Pollutant | Averaging Time | Standard (µg/m³) | |:----------|:--------------|:------------------| | PM₁₀ | 24-hour | 100 | | PM₂.₅ | 24-hour | 60 | | SO₂ | 24-hour | 80 | | NO₂ | 24-hour | 80 | | CO | 8-hour | 2000 (≈ 1.8 ppm) | | O₃ | 8-hour | 100 | | Pb | 24-hour | 1.0 | **AQI Calculation for PM₂.₅ = 85 µg/m³ (24-hr) (4 marks):** From CPCB breakpoint table: - Range 61–90 µg/m³ corresponds to AQI 101–200 - $BP_{Lo} = 61$, $BP_{Hi} = 90$ - $I_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Hi} = 200$ - $C_P = 85$ Substitute into linear interpolation formula: $$ I_P = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_P - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo} $$ $$ I_P = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} (85 - 61) + 101 $$ $$ I_P = \frac{99}{29} \times 24 + 101 $$ $$ I_P = 3.4138 \times 24 + 101 = 81.93 + 101 = 182.93 $$ $$ \boxed{\;I_P \approx 183 \; (\text{Moderate AQI category})\;} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Common Mistake (–2 marks):** Students often forget that O₃ is **secondary**, not directly emitted. Markers deduct heavily for listing "vehicles" as direct source of O₃. > 2. **Numerical Error (–2 marks):** In AQI problems, students use wrong breakpoint values or forget to round off. Always state the **AQI category** (Good/Satisfactory/Moderate/Poor) at the end. > 3. **Unit Confusion (–1 mark):** Mixing ppm and µg/m³ without conversion; remember to use the **Hall conversion** $1 \; ppm = M/24.45 \; mg/m^3$ at 25°C, 1 atm. > 4. **Health-Environment Confusion (–1 mark):** NAAQS **primary standard** = health; **secondary standard** = welfare (visibility, crops, animals). Mix these up and you lose a mark. > 5. **Mechanism Skipped (–2 marks):** In photochemical smog questions, you must write the **three key reactions** explicitly — verbal descriptions alone get partial marks only. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Module 4** - ✅ **Six Criteria Pollutants:** CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM₁₀/PM₂.₅, SO₂ — under the **US EPA Clean Air Act**. - ✅ **NAAQS** has **primary** (health) and **secondary** (welfare) standards. - ✅ **Primary vs. Secondary:** O₃ is the **classic secondary pollutant**; CO, Pb, SO₂ are primary; NO₂ and PM are both. - ✅ **CO** binds haemoglobin 200× tighter than O₂ → forms **carboxyhaemoglobin (COHb)**. - ✅ **Pb** is a potent **neurotoxin** — affects children severely (IQ loss). - ✅ **NO₂** + sunlight → **O₃ formation**; also forms **HNO₃** (acid rain). - ✅ **O₃ formation reactions** (3-step Leighton cycle) — must memorize for 14-mark questions. - ✅ **PM₂.₅** is the deadliest — reaches alveoli, enters bloodstream, causes CVD and lung cancer. - ✅ **SO₂** causes **acid rain** (pH < 5.6); major source is coal combustion. - ✅ **Hall's formula:** $1 \; ppm = M/24.45 \; mg/m^3$ (at 25°C, 1 atm). - ✅ **AQI calculation:** Linear interpolation between breakpoints; final AQI = **maximum sub-index**. - ✅ **CPCB NAAQS (India):** 8 pollutants including NH₃; 12 standards across 5 averaging times. - ✅ **Photochemical Smog = NOₓ + VOCs + Sunlight → O₃ + PAN + HCHO** (Los Angeles-type smog). - ✅ **Control strategy for O₃:** Must address **both** NOₓ and VOCs (NOₓ disbenefit phenomenon). - ✅ **Acid rain impacts:** Soil, lakes, forests, monuments (Taj Mahal marble corrosion). - ✅ **AQI Bands (CPCB):** 0–50 Good, 51–100 Satisfactory, 101–200 Moderate, 201–300 Poor, 301–400 Very Poor, 401–500 Severe. - ✅ **KTU 2024 CO mapping:** CO1 (Recall), CO2 (Comprehend), CO3 (Apply), CO4 (Impact analysis), CO5 (Engineering solutions). > 🎯 **Final Exam Tip:** In 14-mark questions, always combine **theory + reaction equations + impact + control technology + a labelled diagram reference** for full marks under KTU 2024 scheme rubrics.

topics.title: Human Health
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Human Health

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Human Health Impact Assessment (HIA)** within the EIA framework is a systematic, multidisciplinary process that evaluates the potential **health consequences** of a proposed project, policy, plan, or program on a defined population. It integrates **epidemiological**, **toxicological**, **environmental**, and **socio-economic** data to identify both adverse and beneficial health outcomes, vulnerable sub-populations, and mitigation strategies.

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)**, health is defined as *"a state of complete physical, mental, and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity."* This tripartite definition is the bedrock upon which HIA rests.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (verbatim):** Human Health Impact Assessment is the process of evaluating the effects of a proposed action on the health and well-being of the affected community, including physical, mental, and social dimensions, as an integral component of Environmental Impact Assessment.

### 1.2 Components of Human Health

Human health is broadly classified into three interrelated dimensions:

| Dimension | Description | EIA Example |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Physical Health** | Absence of disease, functional integrity of body systems | Respiratory illness from PM2.5, hearing loss from noise |
| **Mental Health** | Emotional, psychological, and cognitive well-being | Stress, anxiety, depression due to displacement |
| **Social Health** | Ability to participate in community, family, and economic life | Loss of livelihood, community fragmentation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU frequently asked):** *HIA ≠ Risk Assessment.* Risk Assessment is a **quantitative** sub-component of HIA focused on hazardous agents. HIA is a **broader qualitative and quantitative** evaluation that includes psychosocial and socio-economic dimensions.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Analogy — The "Health Lens" on a Project:** Imagine a project proposal as a new building being constructed in a neighbourhood. A normal EIA checks whether the building's shadow blocks neighbours' sunlight (environmental impact). A **Human Health Impact Assessment** goes further — it asks: *"Will the construction noise disturb the sleep of a heart patient living next door? Will the dust worsen the asthma of the schoolchildren? Will the displacement cause depression in elderly residents?"* It is essentially **wearing a "health lens"** over the project that magnifies its consequences on **people**, not just on air, water, or soil.

### 1.4 Standard Health Metrics (WHO Endorsed)

The following **bold** metrics are used universally for quantifying health status and impact:

- **DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year):** A measure of overall disease burden, expressed as the cumulative number of years lost due to ill-health, disability, or early death. **1 DALY = 1 lost year of healthy life.**
- **QALY (Quality-Adjusted Life Year):** A measure of disease burden that accounts for both quality and quantity of life lived.
- **YLL (Years of Life Lost):** Premature mortality component.
- **YLD (Years Lived with Disability):** Morbidity component.
- **Mortality Rate (per 1,000 or 100,000 population):** Frequency of deaths in a defined population.
- **Morbidity Rate (incidence/prevalence):** Frequency of disease occurrence.

The relationship is mathematically expressed as:

$$\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why DALY matters in EIA:** It enables comparison across completely different health outcomes (e.g., comparing a case of tuberculosis vs. a case of road accident injury) on a **single common scale** — a critical tool when a project produces multiple disparate health impacts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Determinants of Human Health (The Dahlgren-Whitehead Model)

Health is determined by a layered set of factors. The **Dahlgren-Whitehead Rainbow Model** categorises determinants into four concentric layers:

1. **Layer 1 — Individual / Non-modifiable factors:** Age, sex, genetics, ethnicity.
2. **Layer 2 — Individual lifestyle factors:** Diet, physical activity, smoking, alcohol, substance use.
3. **Layer 3 — Social and community networks:** Family, social support, cultural norms.
4. **Layer 4 — Living and working conditions:** Housing, employment, education, water/sanitation, healthcare access, **environmental quality (most relevant to EIA).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Projects that alter **Layer 4** determinants (air quality, water quality, noise, housing, livelihood) have the **most direct and measurable** health impacts and are the primary focus of HIA in EIA.

### 2.2 Health Impact Assessment (HIA) — Stepwise Process

The HIA process, as adopted in KTU module, follows **five sequential stages**, mirroring EIA itself:

1. **Screening:** Decide whether an HIA is necessary for the proposed project.
2. **Scoping:** Identify which health impacts, populations, and time-frames are to be assessed.
3. **Assessment (Appraisal):** Profile existing health status, predict changes, and characterise significance using evidence.
4. **Evaluation & Mitigation:** Recommend alternatives, design mitigation, and evaluate residual impacts.
5. **Reporting & Monitoring:** Document findings in the EIA report and design a post-project health monitoring plan.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarises all critical quantitative expressions used in HIA / risk assessment, presented in KTU examination-ready form.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $$\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$$ | Disease burden decomposition | years |
| 2 | $$\text{YLL} = \sum_{i} d_i \cdot (L - a_i)$$ | Years of life lost per cause | person-years |
| 3 | $$\text{YLD} = I \cdot DW \cdot L_d$$ | Years lived with disability | person-years |
| 4 | $$\text{ADD} = \frac{C \cdot IR \cdot EF \cdot ED}{BW \cdot AT}$$ | Average Daily Dose | mg/kg/day |
| 5 | $$\text{HQ} = \frac{\text{ADD}}{\text{RfD}}$$ | Hazard Quotient (non-cancer) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $$\text{HI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \text{HQ}_j$$ | Hazard Index (multiple chemicals) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $$\text{CR} = \text{ADD} \cdot \text{SF}$$ | Cancer Risk (lifetime) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $$\text{Risk} = \text{Hazard} \times \text{Exposure}$$ | General risk equation | dimensionless |

Where the variables carry the following meanings:

- $d_i$ = number of deaths from cause $i$
- $L$ = standard life expectancy (years)
- $a_i$ = average age at death from cause $i$
- $I$ = incidence (new cases per year)
- $DW$ = disability weight (0 = perfect health, 1 = death)
- $L_d$ = average duration of disability (years)
- $C$ = contaminant concentration in medium
- $IR$ = intake rate (e.g., $\text{m}^3$/day for air, L/day for water)
- $EF$ = exposure frequency (days/year)
- $ED$ = exposure duration (years)
- $BW$ = body weight (kg)
- $AT$ = averaging time (days)
- $\text{RfD}$ = reference dose (mg/kg/day)
- $\text{SF}$ = slope factor (mg/kg/day)$^{-1}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Mandate (KTU Valuation):** Examiners deduct marks for unit mismatches. Always express $C$ in $\text{mg/m}^3$ for air and $\text{mg/L}$ for water; convert $IR$, $EF$, $ED$, $AT$ to **consistent time units** before multiplication.

### 2.4 Significance Criteria for Health Impacts

A health impact is considered **significant** if **any one** of the following conditions is met:

- It results in **excess mortality** (fatal outcomes).
- It produces a **clinically measurable morbidity** increase in the exposed population.
- It affects a **vulnerable sub-population** (children, pregnant women, elderly, immunocompromised).
- It violates a **national or WHO standard** (e.g., NAAQS for air, BIS for drinking water).
- It causes a **noticeable DALY burden** (e.g., > 1 DALY per 1,000 population per year).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In Indian infrastructure projects, HIA informs critical engineering decisions such as:

- **Buffer-zone design** for thermal power plants (stack-height optimisation to keep ground-level $\text{PM}_{2.5}$ below NAAQS).
- **Route alignment** of highways to minimise noise exposure to schools and hospitals.
- **Green-belt specification** around industrial estates to attenuate dust and gaseous emissions.
- **Resettlement and rehabilitation (R&R)** planning for displacement caused by dams and mining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Average Daily Dose (ADD) Equation

The Average Daily Dose is the **most frequently tested formula** in KTU 2024 question papers for this module. It is derived from a mass-balance principle: the quantity of contaminant entering the body per unit body weight per unit time.

**Step 1 — Identify the contact rate.**
The total mass of contaminant entering the body per day equals the contaminant concentration in the medium multiplied by the daily intake rate of that medium.

$$M_{\text{intake}} \;=\; C \cdot IR$$

**Step 2 — Adjust for the fraction of days of exposure per year.**
The annualised daily intake is weighted by the exposure frequency, $EF$, in days per year, and normalised to a 365-day year.

$$M_{\text{effective}} \;=\; C \cdot IR \cdot \frac{EF}{365}$$

**Step 3 — Account for total exposure duration.**
The total cumulative exposure over $ED$ years, distributed over the recipient's body weight $BW$ and averaged over the averaging time $AT$ (which equals $365 \cdot ED$ for non-cancer assessment and $365 \cdot 70$ for cancer assessment), yields:

$$\text{ADD} \;=\; \frac{C \cdot IR \cdot EF \cdot ED}{BW \cdot AT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For **non-cancer** assessment: $AT = 365 \cdot ED$ days.
> For **cancer** (lifetime) assessment: $AT = 365 \cdot 70 = 25{,}550$ days, because cancer slope factors are based on a 70-year lifetime exposure.

### 3.2 Worked Example — Worked Numerical (KTU Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A community of 2,000 residents is located 500 m downwind of a cement plant. The measured 24-hour average $\text{PM}_{10}$ concentration is **$C = 150 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3$**. Compute the non-cancer Hazard Quotient (HQ) and the lifetime Cancer Risk (CR), given:
- $IR = 20 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ (inhalation rate, adult)
- $EF = 350 \, \text{days/year}$ (residents stay 350 days in the area)
- $ED = 30 \, \text{years}$ (residential duration)
- $BW = 70 \, \text{kg}$ (average adult body weight)
- $\text{RfD}_{\text{inhaled}} = 0.005 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$
- $\text{SF}_{\text{inhaled}} = 0.029 \, (\text{mg/kg/day})^{-1}$

**Step 1 — Unit conversion of concentration.**
$$C = 150 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3 \;=\; 150 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mg/m}^3 \;=\; 0.15 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute the non-cancer averaging time.**
$$AT_{\text{non-cancer}} = 365 \times ED = 365 \times 30 = 10{,}950 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ADD (non-cancer).**
$$\text{ADD} = \frac{C \cdot IR \cdot EF \cdot ED}{BW \cdot AT} = \frac{0.15 \times 20 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 10{,}950}$$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.**
$$\text{Numerator} = 0.15 \times 20 \times 350 \times 30 = 0.15 \times 210{,}000 = 31{,}500$$

**Step 5 — Denominator evaluation.**
$$\text{Denominator} = 70 \times 10{,}950 = 766{,}500$$

**Step 6 — Final ADD value.**
$$\text{ADD} = \frac{31{,}500}{766{,}500} = 0.0411 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Hazard Quotient (HQ).**
$$\text{HQ} = \frac{\text{ADD}}{\text{RfD}} = \frac{0.0411}{0.005} = 8.22$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Since $\text{HQ} = 8.22 \gg 1$, the non-cancer hazard is **unacceptable**, and mitigation is mandatory. The threshold is $\text{HQ} \le 1$ for acceptable non-cancer risk.

**Step 8 — Compute the cancer averaging time (70-year lifetime).**
$$AT_{\text{cancer}} = 365 \times 70 = 25{,}550 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 9 — Compute the ADD (cancer assessment).**
$$\text{ADD}_{\text{cancer}} = \frac{0.15 \times 20 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 25{,}550} = \frac{31{,}500}{1{,}788{,}500} = 0.01761 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$$

**Step 10 — Compute the Cancer Risk (CR).**
$$\text{CR} = \text{ADD} \times \text{SF} = 0.01761 \times 0.029 = 5.11 \times 10^{-4}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Acceptable Cancer Risk Range (US EPA benchmark):** $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$. Our $\text{CR} = 5.11 \times 10^{-4}$ **exceeds the upper acceptable limit by approximately 5×**, indicating a **high carcinogenic risk** requiring intervention.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — HIA at Each EIA Stage

The following matrix maps each HIA stage to its corresponding EIA stage and the typical KTU-mapped deliverables.

| HIA Stage | Corresponding EIA Stage | Key Deliverable | Output Used By |
|-----------|-------------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Screening** | Screening | HIA Screening Report | Project Proponent / Regulatory Body |
| **Scoping** | Scoping | Terms of Reference (ToR) for HIA | EIA Coordinator |
| **Assessment** | Impact Analysis | Baseline health profile, dose-response, risk characterisation | EIA Report Chapter |
| **Evaluation & Mitigation** | Mitigation & Alternatives | Health Management Plan (HMP), R&R plan, green-belt spec | Design Team / Engineer |
| **Reporting & Monitoring** | EIA Report & EMP | HIA Section in EIA Report, Health Monitoring Plan | Regulators / Public |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Human Health Impact Assessment (HIA) — Process Flow

The following Mermaid block-diagram captures the **five-stage HIA process** as mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus, with explicit feedback loops for iterative refinement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proposal] --> B[Screening Stage]
    B --> B1{HIA Required?}
    B1 -- No --> B2[No HIA - Justify Decision]
    B1 -- Yes --> C[Scoping Stage]
    C --> C1[Identify Health Determinants]
    C1 --> C2[Select Exposed Populations]
    C2 --> C3[Define Time Boundaries]
    C3 --> D[Assessment Stage]
    D --> D1[Baseline Health Profile]
    D1 --> D2[Predict Health Impacts]
    D2 --> D3[Dose-Response Analysis]
    D3 --> D4[Risk Characterisation]
    D4 --> E[Evaluation and Mitigation]
    E --> E1[Recommend Alternatives]
    E1 --> E2[Design Mitigation Measures]
    E2 --> E3[Residual Impact Assessment]
    E3 --> F[Reporting and Monitoring]
    F --> F1[Integrate into EIA Report]
    F1 --> F2[Health Monitoring Plan]
    F2 --> G[Post-Project Surveillance]
    G --> H[Public Health Outcome Evaluation]
    H -. Feedback Loop .-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Arrows flow from project inception to post-project surveillance. The dotted feedback loop from "Public Health Outcome Evaluation" back to the "Assessment Stage" represents the **adaptive management principle** — real-world health data is fed back to refine future HIAs.

### 4.2 Determinants of Human Health (Dahlgren-Whitehead Architecture)

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the layered architecture of health determinants as conceptualised in the Dahlgren-Whitehead model, with the **environmental layer** (most relevant to EIA) highlighted.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["Layer 1 - Non-Modifiable Individual Factors"]
        node1[Age, Sex, Genetics, Ethnicity]
    end
    subgraph L2["Layer 2 - Individual Lifestyle Factors"]
        node2[Diet, Exercise, Smoking, Alcohol]
    end
    subgraph L3["Layer 3 - Social and Community Networks"]
        node3[Family, Social Support, Cultural Norms]
    end
    subgraph L4["Layer 4 - Living and Working Conditions"]
        node4[Housing, Education, Employment, Healthcare]
        node5[Water, Sanitation, Air Quality, Noise]
        node6[Transport, Food Security, Safety]
    end
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> node7[Overall Population Health Status]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication (KTU 2024):** EIA primarily influences **Layer 4** determinants (especially `node5` — air quality, water, noise). Mitigation design must therefore target **environmental quality** within the affected living/working conditions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define the term **Human Health Impact Assessment (HIA)**. List any **four** health determinants that are most likely to be affected by an industrial project.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Human Health Impact Assessment is a systematic process used to evaluate the potential effects of a proposed project, policy, or programme on the physical, mental, and social well-being of the affected population. It is an integral component of the broader Environmental Impact Assessment.

**Four Health Determinants affected by an industrial project (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
1. **Air quality** — emission of particulate matter, SO₂, NOₓ.
2. **Water quality** — discharge of effluents affecting potable sources.
3. **Noise levels** — operation of heavy machinery and transport vehicles.
4. **Livelihood / employment** — displacement or employment generation impacts.

> [!TIP]
> Always pair the definition with a **listing structure**. KTU valuators award 1 mark for a textbook-accurate definition and ½ mark per valid determinant, up to 2 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Explain the term **DALY**. State the formula for DALY and interpret what a value of **0.5 DALY per 1,000 population per year** means in EIA context.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** DALY stands for **Disability-Adjusted Life Year**. It is a metric that quantifies the overall burden of disease by combining premature mortality (YLL) and disability (YLD) into a single common unit — years of healthy life lost.

**Formula (1 mark):**
$$\text{DALY} = \text{YLL} + \text{YLD}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** A value of **0.5 DALY per 1,000 population per year** means that, on average, **0.5 years of healthy life are lost per 1,000 people annually** due to the project's adverse health impacts. For a population of 100,000, this corresponds to **50 healthy life-years lost per year**, signalling a **moderately significant** public health burden requiring mitigation.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern (ESE 2024):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Internal choice is provided — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **five stages of Human Health Impact Assessment (HIA)** as applied in the EIA process. For each stage, state its purpose and key deliverable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power plant is proposed near a village of 5,000 residents. The expected ground-level concentration of $\text{SO}_2$ is **$C = 80 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3$** (24-hr average). Compute the **Hazard Quotient (HQ)** and **Hazard Index (HI)** for a resident exposed for 30 years, given:
- $IR = 20 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$
- $EF = 350 \, \text{days/year}$
- $ED = 30 \, \text{years}$
- $BW = 70 \, \text{kg}$
- $\text{RfD}_{\text{SO}_2} = 0.02 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$ (single chemical; $\text{HI} = \text{HQ}$ for one chemical)

Comment on the significance of the result and recommend **two mitigation measures**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks (1 mark per stage with ½ mark for purpose + ½ mark for deliverable):**

| Stage | Purpose (½ mark) | Key Deliverable (½ mark) |
|-------|------------------|---------------------------|
| **1. Screening** | Determine if HIA is necessary based on project nature, scale, and proximity to receptors. | HIA Screening Report with go/no-go decision. |
| **2. Scoping** | Define boundaries — populations, impacts, time-frame, methodology. | HIA Terms of Reference (ToR). |
| **3. Assessment (Appraisal)** | Profile baseline health, predict impacts, characterise risks. | Baseline health profile + risk characterisation report. |
| **4. Evaluation & Mitigation** | Recommend alternatives, design mitigation, evaluate residual impacts. | Health Management Plan (HMP). |
| **5. Reporting & Monitoring** | Document findings, integrate into EIA, design post-project health monitoring. | HIA section in EIA Report + Health Monitoring Plan. |

**Part (b) — Step-by-step numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert concentration (1 mark):**
$$C = 80 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3 = 0.08 \, \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Compute averaging time (1 mark):**
$$AT = 365 \times 30 = 10{,}950 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 3 — Compute ADD (1 mark):**
$$\text{ADD} = \frac{0.08 \times 20 \times 350 \times 30}{70 \times 10{,}950} = \frac{16{,}800}{766{,}500} = 0.02192 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$$

**Step 4 — Compute HQ (1 mark):**
$$\text{HQ} = \frac{0.02192}{0.02} = 1.096$$

**Step 5 — Interpret (1 mark):**
Since $\text{HQ} = 1.096 > 1$, a non-cancer health hazard **exists** and mitigation is required. Because only one chemical is involved, $\text{HI} = \text{HQ} = 1.096$.

**Step 6 — Recommend two mitigation measures (1 mark):**
1. **Installation of Flue-Gas Desulphurisation (FGD)** to reduce $\text{SO}_2$ emissions.
2. **Establishment of a green belt** (at least 3-row dense plantation) around the plant boundary to attenuate ground-level concentrations.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly **forget the unit conversion** from $\mu\text{g/m}^3$ to $\text{mg/m}^3$, leading to a 100× error and incorrect conclusion. Always verify units **before** plugging into the formula.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, explain the **Dahlgren-Whitehead model of health determinants**. Highlight the layer that is most influenced by EIA projects. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **lifetime Cancer Risk (CR)** for a population chronically exposed to **arsenic** in drinking water at $C = 0.05 \, \text{mg/L}$. Given: $IR = 2 \, \text{L/day}$, $EF = 350 \, \text{days/year}$, $ED = 70 \, \text{years}$ (lifetime), $BW = 60 \, \text{kg}$, oral slope factor $\text{SF} = 1.5 \, (\text{mg/kg/day})^{-1}$. Comment on the risk and suggest **one technological intervention**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Schematic description (3 marks):**
The Dahlgren-Whitehead model represents health determinants as a **rainbow-shaped layered architecture** with the individual at the centre and broader societal/environmental factors forming outer layers.

- **Layer 1 (innermost):** Non-modifiable individual factors (age, sex, genetics).
- **Layer 2:** Individual lifestyle factors (diet, exercise, substance use).
- **Layer 3:** Social and community networks.
- **Layer 4 (outermost):** Living and working conditions — housing, water, sanitation, air quality, employment, education, healthcare.

**Identification of EIA-relevant layer (2 marks):**
The **outermost layer (Layer 4)** — specifically the components of **air quality, water quality, noise, housing, and livelihood** — is most influenced by EIA projects.

**Linkage to HIA (2 marks):**
Projects alter environmental quality within Layer 4, which cascades inward to affect health outcomes. The HIA quantifies this cascade using exposure assessment and dose-response modelling, producing evidence for mitigation design.

**Part (b) — Step-by-step numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify averaging time for cancer (1 mark):**
$$AT = 365 \times 70 = 25{,}550 \, \text{days}$$

**Step 2 — Compute ADD (1 mark):**
$$\text{ADD} = \frac{0.05 \times 2 \times 350 \times 70}{60 \times 25{,}550} = \frac{2{,}450}{1{,}533{,}000} = 0.001598 \, \text{mg/kg/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CR (1 mark):**
$$\text{CR} = \text{ADD} \times \text{SF} = 0.001598 \times 1.5 = 0.002397 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
The acceptable cancer risk range is $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$. Our $\text{CR} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}$ exceeds the upper limit by **24 times**, indicating an **unacceptably high carcinogenic risk** requiring immediate intervention.

**Step 5 — Recommendation (1 mark):** Install **household-level or community-scale arsenic removal units** (e.g., activated alumina filters, SORAS — Safe Oxide-Removal of Arsenic Systems, or reverse-osmosis plants).

**Step 6 — Additional discussion (1 mark):** Alternative intervention: provide an **alternate safe water source** (deep-aquifer hand-pump proven arsenic-free) to bypass the contaminated shallow aquifer entirely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (CR calculation):** A frequent error is using $AT = 365 \cdot ED$ for cancer assessment. **Cancer slope factors are calibrated to a 70-year lifetime exposure.** Always use $AT = 365 \times 70 = 25{,}550$ days for cancer risk. Using the non-cancer AT underestimates the risk by ~2.3×.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** HIA is a systematic evaluation of physical, mental, and social health consequences of a project on the affected population.
- **Three health dimensions:** Physical, Mental, Social.
- **WHO health definition:** Complete well-being, not merely absence of disease.
- **Five HIA stages:** Screening → Scoping → Assessment → Evaluation & Mitigation → Reporting & Monitoring.
- **DALY** = YLL + YLD; **1 DALY = 1 lost year of healthy life** — the universal metric for disease burden.
- **Dahlgren-Whitehead rainbow model** has four layers; **Layer 4 (living & working conditions)** is the EIA-relevant layer.
- **Average Daily Dose formula:**
$$\text{ADD} = \frac{C \cdot IR \cdot EF \cdot ED}{BW \cdot AT}$$
- **Hazard Quotient:** $\text{HQ} = \text{ADD} / \text{RfD}$. Threshold: $\text{HQ} \le 1$ for acceptable non-cancer risk.
- **Hazard Index:** $\text{HI} = \sum \text{HQ}_j$ for multiple chemicals. Threshold: $\text{HI} \le 1$.
- **Cancer Risk:** $\text{CR} = \text{ADD} \times \text{SF}$. Acceptable range: $1 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1 \times 10^{-4}$.
- **Averaging time rule:** Non-cancer → $AT = 365 \cdot ED$; Cancer → $AT = 365 \cdot 70 = 25{,}550$ days.
- **Unit conversion:** $1 \, \mu\text{g/m}^3 = 10^{-3} \, \text{mg/m}^3$; $1 \, \text{mg/L} = 1 \, \text{ppm (approx., w/w for water)}$.
- **Vulnerable populations:** Children, pregnant women, elderly, immunocompromised — always to be addressed in scoping.
- **Significance criteria** for HIA: excess mortality, measurable morbidity, vulnerable-group impact, WHO/BIS standard violation, DALY burden > 1/1,000/year.
- **Health Management Plan (HMP)** is a mandatory deliverable integrated into the EIA's Environmental Management Plan (EMP).
- **HIA ≠ Risk Assessment** — HIA is broader; risk assessment is a sub-component.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic (for the day of the exam):** **"S-S-A-E-R"** → **S**creen, **S**cope, **A**ssess, **E**valuate, **R**eport — the five stages of HIA, in order.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: National Ambient Air Quality Standards by CPCB
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** National Ambient Air Quality Standards by CPCB

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** are the legally enforceable ambient air quality standards established by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under Section 16(2)(h) of the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, with the prior consent of the **Central Government**. These standards specify the maximum allowable concentration of selected air pollutants (called *criteria pollutants*) in the ambient (outdoor) air over specific averaging times, beyond which the air is considered injurious to human health, vegetation, property, and the ecological balance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Definition (Board-Examiner Expected Wording):**
> *"NAAQS refer to the standards prescribed under Schedule VII of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986, read with the Air Act, 1981, and notified by the CPCB, specifying the permissible limits of ambient air pollutants in micrograms per cubic metre (µg/m³) or parts per million (ppm) over specified averaging periods (1-hour, 8-hour, 24-hour, or annual). They form the legal baseline against which ambient air quality is monitored and assessed in any Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) study."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a classroom where 50 students are sitting. If the room is well-ventilated, the air is fine. But if the windows are shut, the carbon dioxide level rises, the room becomes stuffy, and within an hour, students start feeling drowsy, get headaches, and lose concentration. The human body is similar — but operates on a much larger, more sensitive scale.

**Think of NAAQS as the "Comfort Threshold" for our atmosphere.** Just as we have a recommended room temperature (say, 24–26°C) for comfort, NAAQS prescribe the maximum concentration of pollutants the *outdoor air* can hold before it starts harming us, plants, animals, and buildings.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Criteria Pollutants" Analogy:**
> Think of these pollutants as the "Big 12 Suspects" in air pollution monitoring. Just like a doctor checks for specific biomarkers (BP, sugar, cholesterol) to assess health, CPCB monitors 12 specific air pollutants to assess atmospheric health. The "suspect" is identified, the "limit" is set, and the "frequency of check" is decided by the averaging period.

### 1.3 The Governing Body: CPCB at a Glance

The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** — established in **1974** under the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974 — was entrusted with air quality management when the **Air Act, 1981** was enacted.

**Key Statutory Powers of CPCB Related to NAAQS:**
- **Laying down standards** for the quality of air (Section 16(2)(h) of Air Act, 1981).
- **Advising** the Central Government on matters relating to prevention, control, and abatement of air pollution.
- **Coordinating** the activities of State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) and resolving disputes.
- **Collecting and disseminating** information relating to air pollution.
- **Performing prescribed functions** relating to air pollution under the EPA, 1986.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — Exam Tip:**
> The most frequently asked question is: *"Under which act are NAAQS notified?"* The correct, board-expected answer is the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981 (Section 16(2)(h))** in conjunction with the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (Schedule VII)**. Memorise both Acts — examiners often ask which Act *empowers* the CPCB and which Act *enforces* the standards.

### 1.4 Evolution Timeline of NAAQS (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Year | Event | Significance |
|------|-------|--------------|
| **1982** | First NAAQS notified by CPCB | Initial 4 pollutants: SPM, SO₂, CO, NOₓ |
| **1994** | First major revision | PM₁₀ added; new averaging periods introduced |
| **1998** | Amendment | Standards tightened for industrial areas |
| **2009** | Comprehensive revision | **PM₂.₅, NH₃, O₃, Pb, Benzene, B(a)P, As, Ni added** — 12 pollutants total |
| **2024** | Proposed revision (under consideration) | Further tightening of PM and NO₂ limits; addition of new pollutants |

### 1.5 The 12 Criteria Pollutants Regulated under NAAQS (2009 Revision)

The CPCB currently regulates **12 criteria pollutants**, broadly classified as:

**A. Particulate Matter (PM):**
1. **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀)** — Coarse particles ≤ 10 µm
2. **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅)** — Fine particles ≤ 2.5 µm

**B. Gaseous Inorganic Pollutants:**
3. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)**
4. **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)**
5. **Ammonia (NH₃)**
6. **Ozone (O₃)** — Photochemical
7. **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**
8. **Lead (Pb)** — In the particulate phase

**C. Gaseous Organic Pollutants:**
9. **Benzene (C₆H₆)**
10. **Benzo(a)Pyrene (BaP)** — Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbon (PAH)

**D. Toxic Trace Metals (in PM₁₀):**
11. **Arsenic (As)**
12. **Nickel (Ni)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why only 12?** The CPCB follows a *criteria pollutants* approach — only those pollutants for which (a) sufficient scientific evidence of harm exists, (b) monitoring methods are standardized, and (c) epidemiological data is available are included. This makes the list dynamic and science-driven.

### 1.6 Key Metrics and Terminology (Must Memorise for KTU)

- **µg/m³** — Micrograms per cubic metre (used for most pollutants).
- **ng/m³** — Nanograms per cubic metre (used for ultra-trace carcinogens like BaP, As, Ni).
- **mg/m³** — Milligrams per cubic metre (used for CO due to higher concentration).
- **ppm / ppb** — Parts per million / billion (volumetric ratio, conversion required for gas-phase pollutants).
- **Averaging Period** — The time duration over which the pollutant concentration is averaged (e.g., 1-hour, 8-hour, 24-hour, annual).
- **Industrial / Residential / Ecological / Sensitive Areas** — NAAQS specifies separate limits for different land-use zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Formula for Gas-Phase Pollutants (Board-Favourite):**
> 
> $$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$$
> 
> $$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$$
> 
> Where:
> - $C_{ppm}$ = Concentration in parts per million
> - $C_{\mu g/m^3}$ = Concentration in micrograms per cubic metre
> - $M$ = Molecular weight of the pollutant (g/mol)
> - **24.45** = Molar volume of air at 25°C and 1 atm (L/mol) — a constant you must memorise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Are Averaging Periods Important? (The Core "Why")

Averaging periods exist because **the human body responds to pollutants over different time scales**:

- **1-hour average** — Used for pollutants causing acute (immediate) effects. Example: CO can cause dizziness within minutes, so a 1-hour limit exists.
- **8-hour average** — Used for occupational exposure analogy and cumulative daytime exposure. Example: O₃ peaks during afternoon hours.
- **24-hour (daily) average** — Used for pollutants that cause sub-chronic (days) effects. Example: PM₁₀ can cause respiratory inflammation over a day.
- **Annual average** — Used for pollutants causing chronic (lifetime) effects, including cancer. Example: Benzene is a known leukemogen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Think of it like this — taking 10 paracetamol tablets at once can kill you, but 1 tablet daily for 10 days is *relatively* safe. The *total dose* AND the *rate of intake* both matter. Averaging periods reflect the *rate* of pollutant intake by the body.

### 2.2 The NAAQS Standards Table (THE Most Important KTU Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the KTU Board-Expected Master Table. Memorise the bolded values and the column headers. Questions worth 7-14 marks are routinely asked from this table.**

| Sl. No. | Pollutant | Time-Weighted Average | Concentration in Ambient Air | Method of Measurement |
|:-------:|-----------|:---------------------:|:----------------------------:|:---------------------:|
| **1** | **Particulate Matter (Size ≤ 10 µm)** | Annual* | **60 µg/m³** | — |
|   | PM₁₀ | 24 hours** | **100 µg/m³** | Gravimetric / TOA / Beta attenuation |
| **2** | **Particulate Matter (Size ≤ 2.5 µm)** | Annual* | **40 µg/m³** | — |
|   | PM₂.₅ | 24 hours** | **60 µg/m³** | Gravimetric / TOA / Beta attenuation |
| **3** | **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** | Annual* | **50 µg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 24 hours** | **80 µg/m³** | Improved West & Gaeke / Fluorescence |
| **4** | **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** | Annual* | **40 µg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 24 hours** | **80 µg/m³** | Modified Jacob & Hochheiser (Na-Arsenite) / Chemiluminescence |
| **5** | **Ammonia (NH₃)** | Annual* | **100 µg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 24 hours** | **400 µg/m³** | Indophenol blue / Nesslerization |
| **6** | **Ozone (O₃)** | 8 hours** | **100 µg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 1 hour** | **180 µg/m³** | UV Photometric / Chemiluminescence |
| **7** | **Carbon Monoxide (CO)** | 8 hours** | **2 mg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 1 hour** | **4 mg/m³** | Non-Dispersive InfraRed (NDIR) / Gas Chromatography |
| **8** | **Lead (Pb)** | Annual* | **0.50 µg/m³** | — |
|   |   | 24 hours** | **1.0 µg/m³** | AAS / ICP-MS |
| **9** | **Benzene (C₆H₆)** | Annual* | **5 µg/m³** | Gas Chromatography / Photo-ionization Detector |
| **10** | **Benzo(a)Pyrene (BaP)** | Annual* | **1 ng/m³** | HPLC / GC-MS |
| **11** | **Arsenic (As)** | Annual* | **6 ng/m³** | AAS / ICP-MS |
| **12** | **Nickel (Ni)** | Annual* | **20 ng/m³** | AAS / ICP-MS |

\* Annual arithmetic mean of minimum 104 measurements in a year, taken twice a week, uniformly spaced over 24 hours.
\*\* 24-hourly / 8-hourly / 1-hourly values must be met 98% of the time in a year. However, 2% of the time, they may exceed but not on two consecutive days.

### 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are repeatedly tested in numerical and conversion-based questions on air quality in the KTU examination.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Application |
|---|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$ | Converting mass concentration to volumetric (ppm) |
| 2 | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$ | Converting ppm to mass concentration |
| 3 | $\text{AQI} = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} (C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Calculating Air Quality Index sub-index for a pollutant |
| 4 | $AQI = \max(I_{PM_{2.5}}, I_{PM_{10}}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb})$ | Overall AQI = Worst sub-index among 8 pollutants |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{C}{C_{NAAQS}}$ | Pollution Index / Exceedance Factor (C = observed, C_NAAQS = standard) |
| 6 | $N_{eff} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$ | Effective Pollution Index (weighted by severity) |
| 7 | $\% \text{ Exceedance} = \dfrac{\text{No. of days exceeding standard}}{\text{Total monitored days}} \times 100$ | Compliance assessment in EIA |
| 8 | $C_{BTP} = \dfrac{Q}{u \cdot H^2} \cdot f(\sigma_y, \sigma_z, x)$ | Gaussian Plume Model (used to compare with NAAQS for prediction) |

### 2.4 Land-Use Zone Classification under NAAQS (Industrial vs. Residential)

Unlike some international agencies, the CPCB originally prescribed **different standards for different land-use zones** in the 1982 and 1994 versions. However, in the **2009 revision**, the CPCB adopted **uniform national standards** applicable across all zones, recognising that **pollution does not respect administrative boundaries**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Always quote the **2009 revised NAAQS** in your answers unless the question specifically asks about older versions. Examiners will deduct marks if you cite 1994 standards in a current-syllabus question.

### 2.5 The 98th Percentile Rule — The Subtle Detail Examiners Love to Test

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Trap:** Many students write *"the 24-hour standard should never be exceeded."* This is **wrong**. The 24-hour / 8-hour / 1-hour standard is to be met **98% of the time in a year**, allowing a 2% exceedance. This 2% may *not* occur on two consecutive days. The annual average, however, is the **arithmetic mean of at least 104 measurements** taken uniformly over 24 hours, twice a week, for 52 weeks.

This 2% rule accounts for **unavoidable natural events** (dust storms, forest fires, festivals like Diwali) and **rare industrial upsets**.

### 2.6 Role of NAAQS in Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)

NAAQS serve as the **yardstick** against which:
1. **Baseline Ambient Air Quality (AAQ)** is compared during the pre-project EIA study.
2. **Predicted concentrations** from air dispersion models (AERMOD, ISCST3) are validated.
3. **Post-project monitoring** data is assessed for compliance.
4. **Environmental Clearance (EC)** conditions are framed by the Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC).
5. **Consent to Operate (CTO)** is granted by the State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every EIA consultant in India (including those in Kerala — Kerala State Pollution Control Board, KSPCB) must compare predicted and monitored pollutant concentrations with NAAQS. A project that violates NAAQS even after proposed mitigation measures is generally not recommended for environmental clearance by MoEFCC.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Conversion between µg/m³ and ppm (Board-Standard Question)

**Problem:** The NAAQS for SO₂ (24-hour average) is **80 µg/m³**. Express this in ppm at 25°C and 1 atm. Given: Molecular weight of SO₂, M = 64 g/mol.

#### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Identify the given data and the target.
- Given: $C_{\mu g/m^3} = 80$ µg/m³
- Molecular weight: $M = 64$ g/mol
- To find: $C_{ppm}$

**Step 2:** Recall the conversion formula.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the values into the formula.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{80 \times 24.45}{64 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$80 \times 24.45 = 1956$$

**Step 5:** Compute the denominator.

$$64 \times 10^3 = 64000$$

**Step 6:** Divide numerator by denominator.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{1956}{64000} = 0.0305625$$

**Step 7:** Express in proper significant figures (engineering practice — 3 sig. figs.).

$$\boxed{C_{SO_2} \approx 0.0306 \text{ ppm} \text{ or } 30.6 \text{ ppb}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 24-hour average of 80 µg/m³ SO₂ corresponds to 30.6 parts per billion. This means out of every 1 billion (10⁹) air molecules, ~30 are SO₂ molecules. Sounds tiny, but the human respiratory system is remarkably sensitive to sulphur compounds.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Reverse Conversion — ppm to µg/m³ (CO Example)

**Problem:** The 1-hour NAAQS for CO is **4 mg/m³**. Verify by converting to ppm. Given: M_CO = 28 g/mol.

#### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Convert mg/m³ to µg/m³ (since the formula uses µg/m³).

$$4 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 4 \times 10^3 \text{ µg/m}^3 = 4000 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the conversion formula.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{4000 \times 24.45}{28 \times 10^3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator.

$$4000 \times 24.45 = 97800$$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator.

$$28 \times 10^3 = 28000$$

**Step 6:** Divide.

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{97800}{28000} = 3.493$$

**Step 7:** Round to appropriate precision.

$$\boxed{C_{CO} \approx 3.5 \text{ ppm}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** WHO and US EPA use 9 ppm (8-hour) and 35 ppm (1-hour). India's 1-hour limit of ~3.5 ppm is **far stricter** than US EPA, reflecting India's higher population density and susceptibility.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3: Air Quality Index (AQI) Sub-Index Calculation

**Problem:** The 24-hour average PM₂.₅ concentration in a city is observed as **90 µg/m³**. Calculate the AQI sub-index. The CPCB breakpoints are:

| PM₂.₅ (µg/m³) | AQI |
|:-------------:|:---:|
| 0–30 | 0–50 |
| 31–60 | 51–100 |
| 61–90 | 101–200 |
| 91–120 | 201–300 |
| 121–250 | 301–400 |
| 251+ | 401–500 |

#### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Locate the breakpoint interval containing 90 µg/m³.

$$C = 90 \text{ µg/m}^3 \in [61, 90] \text{ with AQI} \in [101, 200]$$

**Step 2:** Apply the AQI linear interpolation formula.

$$I = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

**Step 3:** Identify the breakpoint values.

$$C_{Lo} = 61, \quad C_{Hi} = 90, \quad I_{Lo} = 101, \quad I_{Hi} = 200, \quad C = 90$$

**Step 4:** Compute the slope term.

$$\frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} = \frac{99}{29} = 3.4138$$

**Step 5:** Compute the offset term.

$$C - C_{Lo} = 90 - 61 = 29$$

**Step 6:** Multiply and add.

$$I = 3.4138 \times 29 + 101 = 99.00 + 101 = 200.0$$

**Step 7:** State the result.

$$\boxed{I_{PM_{2.5}} = 200 \text{ (AQI sub-index)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** AQI = 200 falls in the **"Poor"** category (AQI 201–300 is "Poor," 101–200 is "Moderate"). At exactly 90 µg/m³, the city's air just crosses into the Poor category. The NAAQS 24-hour limit of 60 µg/m³ corresponds to AQI ≈ 100 (the boundary between "Satisfactory" and "Moderate").

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4: Exceedance Factor and Effective Pollution Index

**Problem:** At an industrial site, the following 24-hour averages are observed: PM₁₀ = 250 µg/m³, SO₂ = 100 µg/m³, NO₂ = 90 µg/m³. Calculate (a) the Exceedance Factor for each, and (b) the Effective Pollution Index.

#### Solution — Step-by-Step:

**Step 1:** Recall NAAQS 24-hour values.
- PM₁₀ NAAQS = 100 µg/m³
- SO₂ NAAQS = 80 µg/m³
- NO₂ NAAQS = 80 µg/m³

**Step 2:** Calculate individual Exceedance Factors.

$$N_{PM_{10}} = \frac{250}{100} = 2.50$$

$$N_{SO_2} = \frac{100}{80} = 1.25$$

$$N_{NO_2} = \frac{90}{80} = 1.125$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Effective Pollution Index formula.

$$N_{eff} = \frac{\sum N_i^2}{\sum N_i}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the sum of squares.

$$\sum N_i^2 = (2.50)^2 + (1.25)^2 + (1.125)^2$$

$$= 6.25 + 1.5625 + 1.2656 = 9.0781$$

**Step 5:** Compute the sum of values.

$$\sum N_i = 2.50 + 1.25 + 1.125 = 4.875$$

**Step 6:** Divide.

$$N_{eff} = \frac{9.0781}{4.875} = 1.8622$$

**Step 7:** State the result.

$$\boxed{N_{eff} \approx 1.86}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An Effective Pollution Index > 1.0 indicates that the **overall air quality violates NAAQS**, with PM₁₀ being the dominant contributor (highest exceedance factor).
