In EIA, $N_{eff}$ is used to **rank pollution severity** and **identify critical pollutants** for mitigation planning.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant-wise Source-Health-Standard Mapping

| Pollutant | Primary Sources | Health Effect | NAAQS (24-hr / Annual) |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|------------------------|
| **PM₁₀** | Road dust, construction, combustion | Lung inflammation, asthma | 100 / 60 µg/m³ |
| **PM₂.₅** | Vehicles, coal combustion, biomass burning | Cardiovascular, lung cancer, mortality | 60 / 40 µg/m³ |
| **SO₂** | Coal/oil combustion, smelters, volcanoes | Bronchoconstriction, acid rain | 80 / 50 µg/m³ |
| **NO₂** | Vehicle exhaust, power plants | Respiratory infection, smog | 80 / 40 µg/m³ |
| **NH₃** | Agriculture (fertilizers), sewage | Eye irritation, lung damage | 400 / 100 µg/m³ |
| **O₃** | Photochemical reaction (secondary) | Chest pain, lung damage | 180 (1-hr) / 100 (8-hr) µg/m³ |
| **CO** | Incomplete combustion, vehicles | Headache, death (high dose) | 4000 (1-hr) / 2000 (8-hr) µg/m³ |
| **Pb** | Leaded petrol (legacy), battery, smelting | Neurological damage, IQ loss in children | 1.0 / 0.5 µg/m³ |
| **Benzene** | Vehicles, refineries, tobacco smoke | **Leukemia** (Group 1 carcinogen) | 5 (annual) µg/m³ |
| **BaP** | Diesel exhaust, coal, smoked food | **Lung cancer** (Group 1) | 1 (annual) ng/m³ |
| **As** | Coal combustion, smelting | **Cancer**, skin lesions | 6 (annual) ng/m³ |
| **Ni** | Oil combustion, metallurgy | **Respiratory cancer** | 20 (annual) ng/m³ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: NAAQS — Hierarchy of Regulatory Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India<br/>Article 48A and 51A g] --> B[Environment Protection Act 1986]
    A --> C[Air Prevention and Control of Pollution Act 1981]
    C --> D[Central Pollution Control Board CPCB]
    B --> D
    D --> E[Section 16 2 h Power to Lay Standards]
    E --> F[National Ambient Air Quality Standards NAAQS 2009]
    F --> G[12 Criteria Pollutants Regulated]
    G --> H1[PM10 and PM2.5]
    G --> H2[SO2 NO2 NH3 O3 CO Pb]
    G --> H3[Benzene BaP Arsenic Nickel]
    F --> I[Monitoring under National Air Quality Monitoring Programme NAMP]
    I --> J[Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations CAAQMS]
    J --> K[Air Quality Index AQI Computation]
    F --> L[Reference for EIA Studies]
    L --> M[Environmental Clearance by MoEFCC]
    M --> N[State Pollution Control Boards KSPCB Implementation]
```

```mermaid
    B --> B5[CO]

    B3 --> C

    B5 --> C

    B7 --> C
    B8 --> C

    F --> F3[Moderate 101 to 200]
    F --> F4[Poor 201 to 300]
    F --> F5[Very Poor 301 to 400]
    F --> F6[Severe 401 to 500]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: NAAQS in the EIA Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proposal] --> B[Screening]
    B --> C[Scoping]
    C --> D[Baseline AAQ Study<br/>Compare with NAAQS]
    D --> D1[Pre Monsoon]
    D --> D2[Monsoon]
    D --> D3[Post Monsoon]

    F1 -->|No| H[EMMP and Clearance]

```

### 4.4 Conceptual Schematic: Averaging Periods and Biological Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pollutant Release] --> B[1 Hour Average<br/>Acute Effects<br/>Examples CO and O3]
    A --> C[8 Hour Average<br/>Sub Chronic<br/>Examples O3 and CO]
    A --> D[24 Hour Average<br/>Daily Exposure<br/>Examples PM10 and SO2]
    A --> E[Annual Average<br/>Chronic Effects<br/>Examples Benzene and BaP]
    B --> B1[Reversible Effects]
    C --> C1[Reversible to Mild]
    D --> D1[Subclinical Inflammation]
    E --> E1[Permanent Damage or Cancer]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Define National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). Name the statutory body responsible for laying them down and the act under which they are notified.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1 Mark):** National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) refer to the standards specifying the maximum permissible concentration of air pollutants in the ambient atmosphere over specified averaging periods, beyond which the air is considered harmful to human health, vegetation, and the environment.

**Statutory Body (1 Mark):** The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** is the statutory body responsible for laying down the NAAQS.

**Legal Provision (1 Mark):** The CPCB lays down these standards under **Section 16(2)(h) of the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, in conjunction with **Schedule VII of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Series-1]**
*List any six criteria pollutants regulated under NAAQS. State the 24-hour standard for PM₁₀ and SO₂.*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Six Criteria Pollutants (2 Marks):** The 12 criteria pollutants regulated under NAAQS (2009) include: (1) Particulate Matter PM₁₀, (2) Particulate Matter PM₂.₅, (3) Sulphur Dioxide SO₂, (4) Nitrogen Dioxide NO₂, (5) Ammonia NH₃, (6) Ozone O₃, (7) Carbon Monoxide CO, (8) Lead Pb, (9) Benzene, (10) Benzo(a)Pyrene BaP, (11) Arsenic As, and (12) Nickel Ni.

**24-Hour Standards (1 Mark):**
- PM₁₀ (24-hr): **100 µg/m³**
- SO₂ (24-hr): **80 µg/m³**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) as notified by the CPCB in 2009. List all 12 criteria pollutants and their respective 24-hour standards. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the Air Quality Index (AQI)? Explain the AQI computation methodology. Compute the AQI sub-index for PM₂.₅ if the observed 24-hour average concentration is 75 µg/m³. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Introduction to NAAQS (1 Mark):**
The National Ambient Air Quality Standards are the legal ambient air quality standards prescribed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under Section 16(2)(h) of the Air Act, 1981. The current standards were notified in 2009 and supersede the earlier 1994 standards. They specify maximum permissible concentrations of air pollutants over different averaging periods (1-hr, 8-hr, 24-hr, and annual) to protect human health and the environment.

**12 Criteria Pollutants with 24-hour / 1-hr Standards (5 Marks):**

| # | Pollutant | 24-hr / 1-hr Standard |
|---|-----------|-----------------------|
| 1 | PM₁₀ | 100 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 2 | PM₂.₅ | 60 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 3 | SO₂ | 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 4 | NO₂ | 80 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 5 | NH₃ | 400 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 6 | O₃ | 180 µg/m³ (1-hr); 100 µg/m³ (8-hr) |
| 7 | CO | 4 mg/m³ (1-hr); 2 mg/m³ (8-hr) |
| 8 | Pb | 1.0 µg/m³ (24-hr) |
| 9 | Benzene | 5 µg/m³ (annual) |
| 10 | BaP | 1 ng/m³ (annual) |
| 11 | As | 6 ng/m³ (annual) |
| 12 | Ni | 20 ng/m³ (annual) |

**Key Provisions (1 Mark):**
- 24-hr / 8-hr / 1-hr standards must be met 98% of the time in a year.
- Annual standards are arithmetic means of minimum 104 measurements per year, taken twice a week, uniformly distributed over 24 hours.
- 2% exceedance is allowed but not on two consecutive days.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**AQI Definition (1 Mark):**
The Air Quality Index (AQI) is a composite, single-number index that communicates the ambient air quality status in a simple, easy-to-understand format for the general public. It transforms measured pollutant concentrations into a number on a scale of 0 to 500.

**AQI Pollutants Considered (1 Mark):**
CPCB considers **8 pollutants** for AQI computation: PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb.

**Computation Methodology (3 Marks):**
The AQI is calculated by first computing **sub-indices** for each of the 8 pollutants using the linear interpolation formula:

$$I_p = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{C_{Hi} - C_{Lo}} \times (C_p - C_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

Where:
- $I_p$ = Sub-index for pollutant $p$
- $C_p$ = Measured concentration of pollutant $p$
- $C_{Hi}, C_{Lo}$ = Concentration breakpoints corresponding to AQI breakpoints $I_{Hi}, I_{Lo}$

The **overall AQI** is the **maximum** of the 8 sub-indices:

$$AQI = \max(I_{PM_{2.5}}, I_{PM_{10}}, I_{SO_2}, I_{NO_2}, I_{CO}, I_{O_3}, I_{NH_3}, I_{Pb})$$

**Numerical Computation (2 Marks):**
Given: $C_{PM_{2.5}} = 75$ µg/m³
Breakpoint: 61–90 µg/m³ corresponds to AQI 101–200
Therefore: $C_{Lo} = 61, C_{Hi} = 90, I_{Lo} = 101, I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I = \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \times (75 - 61) + 101$$

$$I = \frac{99}{29} \times 14 + 101 = 3.4138 \times 14 + 101$$

$$I = 47.79 + 101 = 148.79$$

$$\boxed{I_{PM_{2.5}} \approx 149 \text{ (Moderate category)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 4 Pattern]

**(a)** Discuss the role of NAAQS in Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) studies. What is the significance of the 98th percentile and averaging period concepts? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The 24-hour standard for SO₂ is 80 µg/m³. Convert this to ppm. If a power plant emits SO₂ at 0.045 ppm (24-hr average) at 25°C and 1 atm, calculate the equivalent concentration in µg/m³. Comment on the compliance status of the power plant. (Given: M of SO₂ = 64 g/mol) **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 Marks]:

**Role of NAAQS in EIA (4 Marks):**

NAAQS serve as the **fundamental regulatory benchmark** in EIA studies, performing the following functions:

1. **Baseline Comparison:** During the pre-project phase, the baseline Ambient Air Quality (AAQ) data collected at the project site is compared with NAAQS to identify existing pollution levels. If the baseline already exceeds NAAQS, the project must account for the "background" load.

2. **Predictive Modelling Validation:** Air dispersion model outputs (e.g., AERMOD, ISCST3, CALPUFF) predict Ground Level Concentrations (GLC) of pollutants. The predicted GLC must be compared with NAAQS at sensitive receptors (residential areas, schools, hospitals) within the study area.

3. **Clearance Decisions:** The Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC) of MoEFCC grants or denies Environmental Clearance (EC) based on whether predicted impacts violate NAAQS, even after mitigation.

4. **Mitigation Design:** NAAQS help in designing appropriate air pollution control devices (e.g., electrostatic precipitators, flue gas desulphurization) and buffer zones.

5. **Post-Project Monitoring:** NAAQS form the basis of Environmental Monitoring and Management Plans (EMMP). Non-compliance triggers regulatory action by SPCBs.

**98th Percentile Concept (2 Marks):**
The 98th percentile rule states that 24-hr / 8-hr / 1-hr standards should not be exceeded on more than **2% of the monitored days in a year** (i.e., ~7 days per year). This allows for natural events (dust storms) and rare industrial upsets, while ensuring **98% compliance** for routine exposure protection. The annual average, however, is the **arithmetic mean** of the 104+ measurements with no such exception.

**Averaging Period Concept (1 Mark):**
Different pollutants have different biological action times. CO affects within minutes (1-hr standard), O₃ peaks in afternoon (8-hr), PM affects daily exposure (24-hr), and carcinogens like BaP and Benzene have lifetime effects (annual standard). Averaging periods thus align with the time-scale of health impacts.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 Marks]:

**Part (i) — Conversion of 80 µg/m³ to ppm (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $C_{\mu g/m^3} = 80$ µg/m³, $M = 64$ g/mol
**To find:** $C_{ppm}$

**Formula:**

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$$

**Substitution:**

$$C_{ppm} = \frac{80 \times 24.45}{64 \times 10^3} = \frac{1956}{64000} = 0.03056 \text{ ppm}$$

**Result:** $\boxed{C_{SO_2} \text{ NAAQS} \approx 0.0306 \text{ ppm}}$

---

**Part (ii) — Conversion of 0.045 ppm to µg/m³ (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $C_{ppm} = 0.045$ ppm, $M = 64$ g/mol
**To find:** $C_{\mu g/m^3}$

**Formula (rearranged):**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$$

**Substitution:**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{0.045 \times 64 \times 10^3}{24.45} = \frac{2880}{24.45} = 117.79 \text{ µg/m}^3$$

**Result:** $\boxed{C_{SO_2} \text{ power plant} \approx 117.8 \text{ µg/m}^3}$

---

**Part (iii) — Compliance Assessment (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Value | NAAQS 24-hr Limit | Compliance |
|-----------|-------|-------------------|------------|
| Power plant 24-hr SO₂ | 117.8 µg/m³ | 80 µg/m³ | **Non-compliant** |

**Exceedance Factor:**

$$N = \frac{117.8}{80} = 1.4725$$

Since the 24-hour SO₂ concentration from the power plant (117.8 µg/m³) **exceeds the NAAQS limit (80 µg/m³) by ~47%**, the power plant is **NON-COMPLIANT** with NAAQS. The project would need to install Flue Gas Desulphurization (FGD) or Low-NOx burners with SO₂ scrubbers to bring emissions within the prescribed standard.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Points in NAAQS Questions (Validated from Past 5 KTU Papers):**
> 
> 1. **Wrong Act cited:** Students write "CPCB notifies standards under the Environment Protection Act, 1986." PARTIAL credit only. The correct answer is **Section 16(2)(h) of the Air Act, 1981**, with E.P. Act 1986 being a *concurrent* legal backing.
> 
> 2. **Missing the 98% rule:** Quoting 24-hr standards as "absolute limits that must never be exceeded" loses 1 mark. The correct position: *"24-hr values must be met 98% of the time in a year, with 2% exceedance permitted but not on two consecutive days."*
> 
> 3. **Forgetting M = 24.45:** In conversion problems, students often skip stating the constant 24.45 (molar volume at 25°C, 1 atm). Examiners expect this constant to appear explicitly in the formula.
> 
> 4. **Wrong rounding in AQI:** Always round AQI to the nearest integer in the final answer. Decimal AQI is unacceptable.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the "Industrial Area" caveat:** If a question specifies an industrial zone, the *older* 1994 standards may apply, but for current (2009) standards, the limits are **uniform across all zones**. Do not mix the two.
> 
> 6. **Confusing 1-hr and 24-hr O₃ standards:** O₃ has a 1-hr (180 µg/m³) and 8-hr (100 µg/m³) standard, NOT a 24-hr standard. This is a frequently tested trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION NOTES — Read this section 24 hours before the exam.**

- ✅ **NAAQS** = Standards for ambient (outdoor) air quality, notified by **CPCB** under **Section 16(2)(h) of the Air Act, 1981**, reinforced by **Schedule VII of EP Act 1986**.
- ✅ The current operative standards are the **2009 revised NAAQS** (supersedes 1982, 1994, 1998).
- ✅ **12 criteria pollutants** are regulated (PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, NH₃, O₃, CO, Pb, Benzene, BaP, As, Ni).
- ✅ **Averaging periods** — 1-hr, 8-hr, 24-hr, annual — chosen based on the timescale of health impact.
- ✅ **98% rule** — 24-hr / 8-hr / 1-hr standards met 98% of the time; 2% exceedance allowed, NOT on two consecutive days.
- ✅ **Annual standard** — arithmetic mean of ≥ 104 uniform 24-hr measurements in a year.
- ✅ **Key 24-hr values** — PM₁₀: 100, PM₂.₅: 60, SO₂: 80, NO₂: 80, NH₃: 400 µg/m³.
- ✅ **Key annual values** — PM₁₀: 60, PM₂.₅: 40, SO₂: 50, NO₂: 40, NH₃: 100, Pb: 0.5, Benzene: 5 µg/m³.
- ✅ **Trace carcinogens (annual)** — BaP: 1 ng/m³, As: 6 ng/m³, Ni: 20 ng/m³.
- ✅ **Conversion constant** — **24.45** (molar volume of air at 25°C, 1 atm).
- ✅ **Conversion formula** — $C_{ppm} = (C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45) / (M \times 10^3)$.
- ✅ **AQI range** — 0 to 500; 8 pollutants considered; computed as the **maximum** of 8 sub-indices.
- ✅ **AQI breakpoints (CPCB)** — 0–30 → 0–50 (Good), 31–60 → 51–100 (Satisfactory), 61–90 → 101–200 (Moderate), 91–120 → 201–300 (Poor), 121–250 → 301–400 (Very Poor), 251+ → 401–500 (Severe).
- ✅ **Exceedance factor** = $C_{observed} / C_{NAAQS}$; values > 1 indicate violation.
- ✅ **Effective Pollution Index** = $\sum N_i^2 / \sum N_i$ — weighted severity indicator.
- ✅ **Role in EIA** — NAAQS = benchmark for baseline, predicted, and post-project monitoring compliance.
- ✅ **Continuous monitoring** is done through **CAAQMS (Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Stations)** under the **National Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP)**.
- ✅ **NAMP** is executed by CPCB in collaboration with **SPCBs** (e.g., KSPCB in Kerala).
- ✅ The **2009 revision** abolished the earlier Industrial/Residential/Ecological zone-wise classification, adopting **uniform national standards**.
- ✅ **NAQI (National Air Quality Index)** was launched by CPCB in **2014** to disseminate real-time AQI to the public.
- ✅ For EIA clearance, projects whose predicted GLC violates NAAQS at sensitive receptors are typically **not recommended** by the EAC.
- ✅ **Hot-spot monitoring**, **micro-meteorological studies**, and **seasonal variation** (4 seasons: winter, summer, monsoon, post-monsoon) are mandatory in EIA baseline AAQ studies.

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Noise Pollution (KTU Syllabus Definition)**
> *Noise pollution* is defined as the **presence of unwanted, excessive, or harmful sound** in the environment that disrupts normal human activities, harms the physiological and psychological well-being of living organisms, and degrades the quality of the ambient acoustic environment. In the context of Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA), it is treated as a physical pollutant characterized by its **sound pressure level (SPL)**, **frequency spectrum**, **duration**, and **temporal variation**.

In engineering and acoustic science, sound is a **mechanical wave** that propagates through a compressible medium (air, water, or solids) as a series of compressions and rarefactions. When sound becomes **unwanted, intense, persistent, or intrusive**, it crosses the threshold from "sound" to "noise pollution."

### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Noise

A complete acoustic description of any noise source must specify:

| Pillar | Physical Quantity | SI Unit | Symbol |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Loudness / Intensity** | Sound Pressure | Pascal (Pa) | $p$ |
| **Pitch / Tone** | Frequency | Hertz (Hz) | $f$ |
| **Duration / Pattern** | Time | Second (s) | $t$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently ask students to differentiate between *sound* and *noise*. Remember — **All noise is sound, but not all sound is noise.** It is the *reception* by the human ear/brain that converts a sound into a *noise* (unwanted perception).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Invisible Tsunami"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bathtub Analogy for Noise Pollution**
> Imagine the human ear as a finely tuned *bathtub* filled with delicate hair cells (stereocilia). A normal, soft sound is like *gentle raindrops* falling into the tub — the water ripples mildly, and the hair cells bend just enough to send a clear signal to the brain. Now imagine a *loud rock concert* or a *jet engine roar* — this is like a **fire hose blasting water into the tub**. The hair cells get violently bent, splashed, and eventually *break*. Unlike a skin wound, **these hair cells in the inner ear (cochlea) NEVER regenerate.** That is why noise-induced hearing loss is **permanent and irreversible.** The bathtub can never "un-break" its hair cells.

This analogy is critical for KTU answers on *Why noise pollution is a serious environmental concern.*

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold Reference Values (KTU 2024 Module 4 — Must Memorize)**
> - **Threshold of Hearing:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$ at $1000 \, \text{Hz}$ — the softest sound a young, healthy human ear can detect.
> - **Threshold of Pain:** $p_{\text{pain}} = 20 \, \text{Pa}$ — sound pressure at which sound becomes physically painful.
> - **Reference Sound Power:** $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$ (1 picowatt).
> - **Speed of Sound in Air:** $c = 343 \, \text{m/s}$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$.
> - **Human Hearing Range:** $20 \, \text{Hz}$ to $20{,}000 \, \text{Hz}$ ($20 \, \text{kHz}$).

## 1.5 Sources of Noise Pollution (Categorized for KTU)

| Category | Examples | Typical SPL (dB) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Natural Sources** | Thunder, waterfalls, volcanic eruptions, wind | $60$ – $120$ |
| **Industrial Sources** | Heavy machinery, compressors, turbines, generators | $80$ – $120$ |
| **Transportation** | Aircraft, railways, highways, vehicular traffic | $70$ – $140$ |
| **Construction** | Pile drivers, jackhammers, bulldozers, blasting | $90$ – $130$ |
| **Community / Domestic** | Loudspeakers, music systems, household appliances | $60$ – $100$ |
| **Occupational** | Workshops, factories, mining operations | $75$ – $115$ |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sound Pressure, Intensity, and Power — The Triad

### 2.1.1 Sound Pressure ($p$)
Sound pressure is the **local pressure deviation** from the ambient atmospheric pressure caused by a sound wave. It is a **scalar, instantaneous, alternating quantity** measured in Pascals (Pa).

The **root mean square (RMS)** sound pressure is used because sound pressure fluctuates sinusoidally between positive and negative peaks. For a sinusoidal wave, the relationship between peak pressure and RMS pressure is:

$$p_{\text{rms}} = \frac{p_{\text{peak}}}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### 2.1.2 Sound Intensity ($I$)
Sound intensity is the **time-averaged rate of sound energy transmitted per unit area** in a specified direction, measured in $\text{W/m}^2$.

The relationship between sound intensity and sound pressure (for a plane or spherical wave traveling in a free field) is:

$$I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho \cdot c}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = density of the medium (for air at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$, $\rho \approx 1.204 \, \text{kg/m}^3$)
- $c$ = speed of sound in the medium ($\approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$ in air)
- $\rho \cdot c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of air, $\approx 413 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s/m}$ (Rayl).

### 2.1.3 Sound Power ($W$)
Sound power is the **total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source**, an inherent property of the source, independent of the distance or environment. Units: Watts (W).

$$W = I \cdot A$$

Where $A$ is the area through which sound is radiated (for a spherical source, $A = 4\pi r^2$).

## 2.2 The Decibel Scale — Why It Exists

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Acoustic Range Problem**
> The human ear responds to sound pressures ranging from $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ to $20 \, \text{Pa}$ — a ratio of **1,000,000 : 1**. Human perception of loudness is also **logarithmic**, not linear. To compress this enormous range into manageable numbers and to align with human perception, we use the **Bel** (named after Alexander Graham Bell), and more practically, the **decibel (dB) = 0.1 Bel**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exam — Memorize This Table**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Reference Value | Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$ | $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level (SIL) | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$ | dB |
| 3 | Sound Power Level (SWL) | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$ | dB |
| 4 | Addition of Two SPLs | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_{p1}/10} + 10^{L_{p2}/10}\right)$ | — | dB |
| 5 | Subtraction of SPLs | $L_{p,\text{background}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_{p,\text{total}}/10} - 10^{L_{p,\text{residual}}/10}\right)$ | — | dB |
| 6 | Inverse Square Law (free field) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | — | dB |
| 7 | Line Source (cylindrical spreading) | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | — | dB |
| 8 | Equivalent Continuous SPL | $L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_p(t)/10}\, dt\right)$ | — | dBA |
| 9 | Sound Exposure Level | $L_{SE} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{t_0}\int_0^T 10^{L_p(t)/10}\, dt\right)$ | $t_0 = 1 \, \text{s}$ | dBA |
| 10 | Day-Night Level | $L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\!\left[15 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10}\right]\right)$ | — | dBA |

## 2.4 Noise Indices Used in EIA (KTU Favourite Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statistical Noise Descriptors — Must Know for 14-mark Questions**

| Index | Full Form | Meaning | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $L_{10}$ | 10th percentile level | Exceeded for 10% of measurement time | Traffic noise peak indicator |
| $L_{50}$ | 50th percentile level (Median) | Exceeded for 50% of time | Average noise level |
| $L_{90}$ | 90th percentile level | Exceeded for 90% of time (= background) | Background/residual noise |
| $L_{\text{eq}}$ | Equivalent continuous SPL | Constant SPL containing same acoustic energy | Overall energy exposure |
| $L_{\text{NP}}$ | Noise Pollution Level | $L_{\text{eq}} + 2.56 \sigma$ where $\sigma$ is std. dev. | Community reaction predictor |
| $L_{DN}$ | Day-Night Average Level | 24-h average with 10 dB penalty at night | Long-term community exposure |
| $L_{\text{max}}$ | Maximum level | Highest instantaneous level | Peak event evaluation |

## 2.5 The A-Weighting Network — Why "A" in dBA?

> [!NOTE]
> **A-Weighting Explained**
> The human ear does not perceive all frequencies equally. It is most sensitive around $2$ – $5 \, \text{kHz}$ and progressively less sensitive at very low ($< 100 \, \text{Hz}$) and very high ($> 10 \, \text{kHz}$) frequencies. The **A-weighting network** in sound level meters applies a frequency-dependent filter that approximates the equal-loudness contours of the human ear at low sound levels. Readings are reported in **dBA (A-weighted decibels)**. The KTU syllabus and CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board) Indian standards **exclusively use dBA**.

## 2.6 Octave Bands and Frequency Analysis

Acoustic engineers analyze noise by dividing the audible frequency spectrum ($20 \, \text{Hz}$ – $20 \, \text{kHz}$) into **octave bands** (each band has a frequency ratio of $2:1$) or **third-octave bands** (ratio $2^{1/3} : 1$).

Standard octave band center frequencies: $31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000 \, \text{Hz}$.

## 2.7 Indian Noise Standards (CPCB) — KTU Expects These

| Zone / Area | Day (6 AM – 10 PM) | Night (10 PM – 6 AM) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Industrial** | $75 \, \text{dBA}$ | $70 \, \text{dBA}$ |
| **Commercial** | $65 \, \text{dBA}$ | $55 \, \text{dBA}$ |
| **Residential** | $55 \, \text{dBA}$ | $45 \, \text{dBA}$ |
| **Silence Zone** (hospitals, schools, courts) | $50 \, \text{dBA}$ | $40 \, \text{dBA}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write the values without specifying *Day* vs *Night* and *Industrial* vs *Silence Zone*. Always quote both. Mixing up "Silence Zone" with "Residential" is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

The decibel framework and noise indices are foundational for:
- **EIA Reports:** Mandatory baseline noise surveys before industrial/transport project approval.
- **Urban Planning:** Siting of airports, highways, and industries.
- **Occupational Health:** OSHA and Indian Factories Act compliance.
- **Building Acoustics:** HVAC and architectural noise design.
- **Legal Cases:** Noise-related Public Interest Litigations (PILs) in India.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) Formula

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The Bels

The Bel scale is defined logarithmically as the **base-10 logarithm of the ratio** of two powers (or power-like quantities). For two sound intensities $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$L \, (\text{in Bels}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)$$

Since sound intensity is proportional to the **square** of the RMS sound pressure (from the wave equation):

$$I \propto p_{\text{rms}}^2$$

Substituting:

$$L \, (\text{in Bels}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2^2}{p_1^2}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

### 3.1.2 Conversion to Decibels

Because 1 Bel = 10 decibels, multiply by 10:

$$L_p = 10 \times 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

For SPL referenced to $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$:

$$\boxed{L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \, \text{dB}}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A sound level meter reads an RMS sound pressure of $0.2 \, \text{Pa}$. Calculate the SPL in dB.

**Step 1:** Identify the reference value. $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$.

**Step 2:** Form the dimensionless ratio.

$$\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0} = \frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = \frac{0.2}{0.00002} = 10{,}000 = 10^4$$

**Step 3:** Apply the SPL formula.

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(10^4) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \, \text{dB}$$

**Answer:** $L_p = 80 \, \text{dB}$ — equivalent to a noisy office or a vacuum cleaner.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Inverse Square Law for Sound

### 3.2.1 Geometric Setup

Consider a **point source** radiating sound isotropically (equally in all directions) with total power $W$. The sound intensity at distance $r$ is:

$$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^2}$$

The factor $4\pi r^2$ is the surface area of a sphere of radius $r$ over which the sound energy is uniformly spread.

### 3.2.2 Convert to SPL

Apply the intensity-to-SPL conversion at two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$L_{p1} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r_1^2 \, I_0}\right)$$

$$L_{p2} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r_2^2 \, I_0}\right)$$

### 3.2.3 Subtract the Two Equations

$$L_{p2} - L_{p1} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1^2}{r_2^2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2\right]$$

$$L_{p2} - L_{p1} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$

Rearranging (sign convention — sound level *decreases* as distance *increases*):

$$\boxed{L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)}$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Example — "Doubling the Distance" Rule

**Problem:** SPL is $86 \, \text{dB}$ at $5 \, \text{m}$ from a point source. Find SPL at $20 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 1:** Identify knowns: $L_{p1} = 86 \, \text{dB}$, $r_1 = 5 \, \text{m}$, $r_2 = 20 \, \text{m}$.

**Step 2:** Compute the distance ratio.

$$\frac{r_2}{r_1} = \frac{20}{5} = 4$$

**Step 3:** Compute the attenuation term.

$$20 \log_{10}(4) = 20 \times 0.6021 = 12.04 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 4:** Subtract from $L_{p1}$.

$$L_{p2} = 86 - 12.04 = 73.96 \approx 74 \, \text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** Quadrupling the distance reduces SPL by approximately $12 \, \text{dB}$. *The famous "**$6 \, \text{dB}$ per doubling of distance**" rule* follows directly: if $r_2 = 2 r_1$, then $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \, \text{dB}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Addition of Two Uncorrelated Sound Sources

### 3.3.1 Why You Cannot Simply Add Decibels

If two identical sources each produce $L_p = 80 \, \text{dB}$ at a point, a *naïve* addition gives $160 \, \text{dB}$ — which would be 1,000,000,000,000,000,000 times more intense than one source. This is **wrong** by orders of magnitude.

### 3.3.2 The Correct Procedure

Convert each dB value back to **linear intensity ratio** (antilog), add linearly, then convert back to dB.

**Step 1:** Convert $L_{p1}$ to linear intensity ratio.

$$\frac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{L_{p1}/10} = 10^{80/10} = 10^8$$

**Step 2:** Similarly for $L_{p2}$:

$$\frac{I_2}{I_0} = 10^{L_{p2}/10} = 10^{80/10} = 10^8$$

**Step 3:** Add linear intensities (physical superposition of energies):

$$\frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_0} = 10^8 + 10^8 = 2 \times 10^8$$

**Step 4:** Convert back to dB.

$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 10^8) = 10 \left[\log_{10}(2) + 8\right] = 10 (0.301 + 8) = 83.01 \, \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_{p1}/10} + 10^{L_{p2}/10}\right)}$$

**Interpretation:** Two identical $80 \, \text{dB}$ sources add to give **$83 \, \text{dB}$** — only a $3 \, \text{dB}$ increase. This is the *famous "**doubling of intensity = 3 dB increase**" rule*.

### 3.3.5 Quick Reference Table for dB Addition

| Difference between two SPLs | Add this to the higher level |
| :--- | :--- |
| $0$ – $1 \, \text{dB}$ | $3 \, \text{dB}$ |
| $2$ – $3 \, \text{dB}$ | $2 \, \text{dB}$ |
| $4$ – $9 \, \text{dB}$ | $1 \, \text{dB}$ |
| $\geq 10 \, \text{dB}$ | $0.5 \, \text{dB}$ (or just the higher one) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Health Effects vs. Exposure Level

| SPL Range (dBA) | Environment | Health & Physiological Effects |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $0$ – $30$ | Whisper, quiet bedroom | Safe; no adverse effects |
| $30$ – $60$ | Normal conversation, office | Safe; may mildly annoy at night |
| $60$ – $70$ | Busy traffic, restaurant | Stress, mild annoyance, sleep disturbance |
| $70$ – $85$ | Loud traffic, TV, alarm clock | Hypertension risk, cognitive impairment in children |
| $85$ – $100$ | Heavy machinery, subway train | Permanent hearing damage after prolonged exposure (OSHA: $8 \, \text{h/day}$ max at $85$) |
| $100$ – $120$ | Chainsaw, rock concert, jet takeoff ($100 \, \text{m}$) | Pain threshold ($120$); immediate acoustic trauma possible |
| $> 120$ | Jet engine (close), firearm discharge | Instantaneous permanent hearing loss, physical pain |

## 3.5 Noise Pollution Level ($L_{\text{NP}}$) Derivation

The Noise Pollution Level is defined as:

$$L_{\text{NP}} = L_{\text{eq}} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$$

Where $\sigma$ is the **standard deviation of the sound level fluctuations** (in dB) over the measurement period. The factor $2.56$ corresponds to a $90$th percentile criterion. This index correlates well with community annoyance responses and is widely used in UK DoE and Indian EIA studies.

**Example:** If $L_{\text{eq}} = 70 \, \text{dBA}$ and $\sigma = 4 \, \text{dB}$:

$$L_{\text{NP}} = 70 + 2.56 \times 4 = 70 + 10.24 = 80.24 \, \text{dBA}$$

## 4.2 Block Diagram — The Three Pillars of Noise Control (Engineering Hierarchy)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept:** The most effective and economical noise control strategy is applied at the **source**. The least effective and most expensive is at the **receiver**. KTU questions often test this hierarchy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[NOISE SOURCE Machine Vehicle Construction Activity] -->|Step 1 Best Option| B[SOURCE CONTROL Quieter Equipment Vibration Damping Lubrication Isolation]
    B -->|Step 2 Second Best| C[PATH CONTROL Distance Attenuation Barriers Enclosures Absorption]
    C -->|Step 3 Last Resort| D[RECEIVER CONTROL PPE Ear Plugs Ear Muffs Time Limits]
    D --> E[Acoustic Comfort Achieved]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Sound Pressure Wave at Microphone Diaphragm] --> F2
    F2[Preamplifier Impedance Matching Stage] --> F3

    F5[RMS Detector and Logarithmic Amplifier] --> F6

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, OECET614]**
*Define noise pollution. Differentiate between sound and noise with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** Noise pollution is the presence of unwanted, excessive, or harmful sound in the environment that interferes with normal activities, causes adverse health effects, and degrades the acoustic quality of the surroundings.

> **Difference between Sound and Noise:**

| Basis | Sound | Noise |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nature | Pleasant, desired, harmonious | Unpleasant, unwanted, discordant |
| Reception | Welcome to the ear | Unwelcome to the ear |
| Example | Music, a mother's voice, a bell | Jackhammer, traffic horns, loud DJ music |
| Frequency | Often well-defined tonal pattern | Often random, broadband, or impulsive |
| Context | Context-dependent: the same wave is sound to one and noise to another | Same wave labelled *noise* by the disturbed receiver |

> **Key Point:** Whether a sound becomes noise depends on the **listener's perception, context, time, and frequency content** — it is highly subjective.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, OECET614]**
*Explain the A-weighting network used in sound level meters. Why is it used in environmental noise measurement?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A-weighting is a **frequency-response filter** built into sound level meters that mimics the frequency sensitivity of the human ear at low-to-moderate sound levels.

> **Mechanism:** The filter applies frequency-dependent attenuation — it **de-emphasizes very low frequencies (below $500 \, \text{Hz}$) and very high frequencies (above $10 \, \text{kHz}$)** where the human ear is less sensitive, and **emphasizes the $1$ – $5 \, \text{kHz}$ band** where the ear is most sensitive.

> **Why Used:**
> 1. Human hearing is **non-linear** with respect to frequency.
> 2. Without weighting, the meter would over-report the impact of low-frequency rumble (e.g., distant highway noise) that humans barely perceive.
> 3. Readings in **dBA** correlate well with the **subjective loudness** experienced by humans and with the **risk of hearing damage**.
> 4. Indian CPCB standards and international WHO guidelines are all specified in **dBA**, not dB.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, OECET614 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in decibels starting from the definition of the Bel scale. Explain why a logarithmic scale is used for sound measurement. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Two machines operate simultaneously in a workshop. Machine A produces $85 \, \text{dB}$ and Machine B produces $82 \, \text{dB}$ at a worker's station. Calculate the combined SPL. If a third machine producing $80 \, \text{dB}$ is also switched on, what is the new combined SPL? (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of the Bel scale:**
The Bel is defined as the base-10 logarithm of the ratio of two sound powers (or intensities). For intensities $I_1$ and $I_2$:

$$L \, (\text{Bels}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_2}{I_1}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Use the intensity-pressure relationship:**
For a sound wave in a free field, the intensity is proportional to the square of the RMS sound pressure: $I \propto p_{\text{rms}}^2$.

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$L \, (\text{Bels}) = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2^2}{p_1^2}\right) = 2 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert Bels to decibels (multiply by 10):**

$$L_p \, (\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Reference to threshold of hearing $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$:**

$$\boxed{L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{20 \, \mu\text{Pa}}\right) \, \text{dB}}$$

**Step 6 — Why logarithmic scale:**
1. The human ear responds to an enormous range of sound pressures (ratio $10^6 : 1$).
2. Human loudness perception is **logarithmic** (Weber-Fechner law).
3. Logarithmic compression converts the huge linear range into manageable numbers ($0$ – $140 \, \text{dB}$).
4. Multiplicative operations (doubling, halving) become simple additive operations in dB.

### **Model Solution to Question A (b):**

**Part (i) — Combine Machine A ($85 \, \text{dB}$) and Machine B ($82 \, \text{dB}$):**

**Step 1:** Convert each SPL to linear intensity ratio.

$$I_A / I_0 = 10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.162 \times 10^8$$

$$I_B / I_0 = 10^{82/10} = 10^{8.2} = 1.585 \times 10^8$$

**Step 2:** Sum the linear ratios.

$$I_{\text{total}} / I_0 = (3.162 + 1.585) \times 10^8 = 4.747 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3:** Convert back to dB.

$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(4.747 \times 10^8) = 10 \left[\log_{10}(4.747) + 8\right]$$

$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \left[0.6766 + 8\right] = 10 \times 8.6766 = 86.77 \, \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{L_{p,\text{total}} \approx 86.8 \, \text{dB}}$$

**Part (ii) — Now add Machine C ($80 \, \text{dB}$):**

**Step 1:** Compute intensity for Machine C.

$$I_C / I_0 = 10^{80/10} = 10^8$$

**Step 2:** Sum all three.

$$I_{\text{new}} / I_0 = (3.162 + 1.585 + 1.000) \times 10^8 = 5.747 \times 10^8$$

**Step 3:** Convert to dB.

$$L_{p,\text{new}} = 10 \log_{10}(5.747 \times 10^8) = 10 \left[\log_{10}(5.747) + 8\right] = 10 \left[0.7594 + 8\right] = 87.59 \, \text{dB}$$

$$\boxed{L_{p,\text{new}} \approx 87.6 \, \text{dB}}$$

**Interpretation:** Adding a $3 \, \text{dB}$ quieter source increases the total by less than $1 \, \text{dB}$. This is why the "**$3 \, \text{dB}$ doubling rule**" is so important — every $3 \, \text{dB}$ increase represents a **doubling of acoustic energy**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, OECET614 — Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the various statistical noise indices used in Environmental Impact Assessment. Describe the significance of $L_{10}$, $L_{50}$, $L_{90}$, $L_{\text{eq}}$, and $L_{\text{NP}}$ in characterizing traffic and community noise. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The sound pressure level measured at a distance of $10 \, \text{m}$ from a point noise source is $90 \, \text{dB}$. Calculate the SPL at distances of (i) $5 \, \text{m}$, (ii) $40 \, \text{m}$, and (iii) $100 \, \text{m}$. State the inverse square law of sound propagation and the "6 dB per doubling of distance" rule. (7 Marks)

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a):**

Statistical noise indices are **percentile-based descriptors** of fluctuating noise levels over a measurement period (usually $24$ hours). They provide a more complete picture than a single average value.

**1. $L_{10}$ (10th Percentile Level):**
The SPL that is **exceeded for 10% of the measurement time**. It characterizes the **peak noise events** (e.g., heavy vehicle pass-bys, horns). Widely used in traffic noise studies.

**2. $L_{50}$ (Median / 50th Percentile):**
The SPL that is **exceeded for 50% of the time**. Represents the **typical or average noise level** experienced.

**3. $L_{90}$ (90th Percentile / Background Level):**
The SPL that is **exceeded for 90% of the time** — equivalently, the level *not exceeded* for 90% of the time. Closely approximates the **residual / background noise** in the absence of specific events. Used to assess the contribution of a new source to existing ambient noise.

**4. $L_{\text{eq}}$ (Equivalent Continuous SPL):**
The **constant SPL which, if present over the entire measurement period, would contain the same acoustic energy** as the actual fluctuating noise. It is the single best descriptor of total noise exposure.

$$L_{\text{eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_p(t)/10}\, dt\right)$$

**5. $L_{\text{NP}}$ (Noise Pollution Level):**
Combines $L_{\text{eq}}$ with the standard deviation of fluctuations to predict **community annoyance**:

$$L_{\text{NP}} = L_{\text{eq}} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$$

A noise with high fluctuations (e.g., intermittent horns) has a higher $L_{\text{NP}}$ than steady noise with the same $L_{\text{eq}}$, and is judged more annoying.

**Significance in Traffic Noise:**
- $L_{10}$ ≈ peak traffic noise
- $L_{50}$ ≈ typical traffic
- $L_{90}$ ≈ background (no traffic)
- The **Traffic Noise Index (TNI)** $= 4 (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$ is also used in UK and Indian practice.

### **Model Solution to Question B (b):**

**Given:** $L_{p1} = 90 \, \text{dB}$ at $r_1 = 10 \, \text{m}$, point source (free field).

**Inverse Square Law:** For a point source in free field, sound intensity falls off as the inverse square of distance:

$$I \propto \frac{1}{r^2} \implies L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

**"6 dB per Doubling" Rule:** If the distance is doubled ($r_2 = 2 r_1$), the SPL decreases by $20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \approx 6 \, \text{dB}$.

**(i) At $r_2 = 5 \, \text{m}$:**

$$L_{p2} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{10}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(0.5) = 90 - 20 \times (-0.301) = 90 + 6.02$$

$$\boxed{L_{p2} \approx 96.0 \, \text{dB}}$$

**(ii) At $r_2 = 40 \, \text{m}$:**

$$L_{p2} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{40}{10}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(4) = 90 - 20 \times 0.6021 = 90 - 12.04$$

$$\boxed{L_{p2} \approx 77.96 \approx 78.0 \, \text{dB}}$$

**(iii) At $r_2 = 100 \, \text{m}$:**

$$L_{p2} = 90 - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{10}\right) = 90 - 20 \log_{10}(10) = 90 - 20 \times 1 = 90 - 20$$

$$\boxed{L_{p2} = 70.0 \, \text{dB}}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- **Closer is exponentially louder.** At $5 \, \text{m}$ the level jumps to $96 \, \text{dB}$ (a $6 \, \text{dB}$ increase for halving distance).
- **Distance is the cheapest noise control.** Going from $10$ to $100 \, \text{m}$ reduces SPL by $20 \, \text{dB}$, which is **a factor of 100 reduction in sound intensity** — far more effective than any barrier.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Students Lose Marks On:**
> 1. **Inverted distance ratio:** Writing $20 \log_{10}(r_1 / r_2)$ instead of $r_2 / r_1$ (sign error gives higher SPL at greater distance — physically impossible).
> 2. **Adding decibels directly:** Adding $85 + 82 = 167 \, \text{dB}$ is a fatal error. Always convert to linear, sum, and convert back.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Reference value $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ must be quoted in numerical calculations. Losing the unit costs $1$ mark.
> 4. **Confusing $L_{\text{eq}}$ with $L_{50}$:** They are *not* the same. $L_{\text{eq}}$ is energy-averaged; $L_{50}$ is the median. For skewed distributions they differ significantly.
> 5. **Quoting CPCB standards without Day/Night distinction:** Always specify the time period and the zone type (Industrial / Commercial / Residential / Silence).
> 6. **Ignoring A-weighting:** Stating SPL in plain "dB" instead of "dBA" in Indian EIA context — partial mark loss.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Twice Before Exam**

- **Sound vs Noise:** All noise is sound; not all sound is noise. Noise is *context-dependent unwanted sound*.
- **Threshold of Hearing:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ at $1 \, \text{kHz}$; **Threshold of Pain:** $20 \, \text{Pa}$.
- **SPL Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}} / p_0)$ in **dB**.
- **SIL / SWL Formula:** $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ in **dB** — note the factor of $10$, not $20$ (because intensity is power).
- **Doubling rule (intensity):** $+3 \, \text{dB}$ doubles acoustic energy.
- **Doubling rule (distance):** $-6 \, \text{dB}$ per doubling of distance from a point source.
- **A-Weighting:** Always report environmental noise in **dBA** in India.
- **Inverse Square Law:** $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2 / r_1)$ — free field point source.
- **Line Source:** $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 10 \log_{10}(r_2 / r_1)$ — cylindrical spreading.
- **Percentile Indices:** $L_{10}$ (peak), $L_{50}$ (median), $L_{90}$ (background).
- **$L_{\text{eq}}$:** Constant SPL containing same energy as fluctuating noise — single best exposure metric.
- **$L_{\text{NP}}$:** $L_{\text{eq}} + 2.56 \sigma$ — community annoyance predictor.
- **$L_{DN}$:** 24-h average with $10 \, \text{dB}$ night-time penalty.
- **CPCB Day Limits (dBA):** Industrial $75$ / Commercial $65$ / Residential $55$ / Silence $50$.
- **CPCB Night Limits (dBA):** Industrial $70$ / Commercial $55$ / Residential $45$ / Silence $40$.
- **Control Hierarchy (best → worst):** Source → Path → Receiver.
- **Octave Bands:** Standard center frequencies $31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500 \, \text{Hz}, \, 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 \, \text{kHz}$.
- **Speed of Sound in Air:** $c \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$ at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **EIA Requirement:** 24-hour continuous baseline noise monitoring at receptor locations.
- **Legal Backing in India:** Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000 (amended 2010) under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986.
- **OSHA 8-h Exposure Limit:** $90 \, \text{dBA}$ (US) or $85 \, \text{dBA}$ (with $5 \, \text{dB}$ exchange rate).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources of Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET614 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sources of Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)**, **Noise** is defined as any unwanted, unpleasant, or harmful sound that disrupts normal human activity, rest, or work and has the potential to cause adverse physiological, psychological, or socio-economic effects on living organisms and ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key EIA Definition:**
> Noise in EIA refers to *unwanted sound emitted from anthropogenic (human-made) and natural sources that exceeds permissible ambient levels, causing measurable impact on the biophysical and socio-economic environment of a project area.*

### Sources of Noise — Operational Definition

A **Source of Noise** is any physical phenomenon, machinery, activity, or natural event that generates pressure fluctuations in a medium (usually air), which are perceived as sound by the human ear. In EIA studies, sources are classified, quantified (in **dB** or **dBA**), and spatially mapped to predict the **Zone of Impact** around a proposed project.

### Critical Acoustic Metrics (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Sound Pressure Level (SPL or Lp):** Measured in **decibels (dB)**, referenced to $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$.
- **A-Weighted Sound Level (dBA):** Frequency-weighted metric mimicking human hearing response.
- **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq):** Time-averaged sound energy over a specified period.
- **Statistical Levels (L10, L50, L90):** Percentile levels exceeded for 10%, 50%, and 90% of the time.
- **Day-Night Average Sound Level (Ldn / DNL):** 24-hour average with 10 dB penalty for night-time noise.
- **Sound Exposure Level (SEL):** Total sound energy of an event normalized to 1 second.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold of Human Hearing:** $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, N/m^2$ at 1000 Hz. This is the **0 dB** reference.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Real-World Analogy: "The Ripples in a Pond"

Imagine dropping a pebble into a still pond. The stone hitting the water creates **ripples** (waves) that travel outward in all directions, eventually reaching the edges of the pond. 

In the same way:
- **The stone = The noise source** (a machine, vehicle, loudspeaker)
- **The ripples = Sound waves** propagating through air
- **The pond's edges = Receptors** (people, animals, buildings)
- **The energy of ripples at edges = Noise impact**

Now imagine dropping **many pebbles at once** (a highway with 1000 vehicles, a factory with 50 machines). The ripples overlap, combine, and become stronger — this is **cumulative noise pollution** in a project area. The EIA process measures how "strong" these ripples are at the receptor (village, school, hospital) and decides whether the project is acceptable.

### A Simpler Analogy: "Loud Speakers in a Classroom"

Picture a classroom where the teacher is speaking at 50 dB. Then a fan starts (10 dB more), then students chatter (10 dB more). The total is **NOT** 50+10+10 = 70 dB. Because the decibel scale is **logarithmic**, the actual addition gives a much smaller value. This is why EIA professionals use **logarithmic math** to add sound levels — covered in Section 3.

---

## 1.3 Sources of Noise — Categories Overview

In EIA, noise sources are broadly classified into:

| Category | Examples | Typical Range (dBA) |
|----------|----------|---------------------|
| Transportation | Vehicles, trains, aircraft | 60 – 120 |
| Industrial | Machinery, generators | 70 – 110 |
| Construction | Drilling, blasting, piling | 75 – 120 |
| Community / Domestic | Loudspeakers, music | 50 – 95 |
| Natural | Thunder, waterfalls | 40 – 100 |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Exams:** Always remember that *transportation noise* is the single largest contributor to community noise pollution in urban India, and EIA studies for highway and airport projects must always include a detailed noise assessment.

---

## 1.4 Why Sources of Noise Matter in EIA

When a new project is proposed (e.g., a highway, airport, factory, or metro rail), it generates noise that affects the **surrounding environment**. The EIA Notification (2006, amended 2020) under the Environment Protection Act, 1986 mandates:

1. **Identification** of all major noise sources.
2. **Prediction** of noise levels at sensitive receptors.
3. **Evaluation** against CPCB ambient noise standards.
4. **Proposal of mitigation measures.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPCB Ambient Noise Standards (India):**
> - Industrial Zone: Day **75 dBA** / Night **70 dBA**
> - Commercial Zone: Day **65 dBA** / Night **55 dBA**
> - Residential Zone: Day **55 dBA** / Night **45 dBA**
> - Silence Zone (hospitals, schools): Day **50 dBA** / Night **40 dBA**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Classification of Noise Sources (Geometric Classification)

In EIA, noise sources are categorized based on their **geometry of emission** because this affects the mathematical model used for prediction.

### A. Point Source
- Emits sound radially in all directions (spherical spreading).
- Sound intensity decreases with the **square of distance** (inverse square law).
- **Examples:** A single machine, a generator, a loudspeaker, an aircraft overhead.

### B. Line Source
- Emits sound in a cylindrical pattern along its length.
- Sound intensity decreases with the **first power of distance** (inverse distance law).
- **Examples:** A stream of traffic on a highway, a railway line, a row of factory machines.

### C. Area Source
- Emits sound from a two-dimensional surface.
- Sound intensity decreases with distance but not following simple power laws; requires numerical integration.
- **Examples:** A car parking lot, a stadium crowd, a construction site, an open-pit mine.

---

## 2.2 Operational Steps in Noise Source Analysis (For EIA Studies)

A standard noise assessment follows these structured steps:

1. **Inventory of Sources:** Enumerate all stationary and mobile sources within and around the project boundary.
2. **Source Strength Determination:** Use manufacturer data, field measurements, or standard databases (e.g., FHWA, ISO 9613).
3. **Geometric Classification:** Identify each source as point, line, or area.
4. **Attenuation Modeling:** Apply distance, atmospheric absorption, ground effects, and barrier attenuation.
5. **Receptor Mapping:** Identify sensitive receptors (schools, hospitals, residential areas, wildlife habitats).
6. **Impact Prediction:** Calculate predicted noise levels (Leq) at each receptor.
7. **Comparison with Standards:** Compare with CPCB / MoEFCC standards.
8. **Mitigation Design:** Propose barriers, green belts, building setbacks, operational restrictions.
9. **Monitoring Plan:** Post-project ambient noise monitoring schedule.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are essential for KTU Part B 14-mark questions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | $P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$ |
| 2 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Sound Intensity Level | $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, W/m^2$ |
| 3 | $L_{p,\text{point}} = L_w - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$ | Point source attenuation | $r$ in metres |
| 4 | $L_{p,\text{line}} = L_w - 10 \log_{10}(r) - 8$ | Line source attenuation | $r$ in metres |
| 5 | $L_{\text{sum}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Addition of sound levels | Logarithmic sum |
| 6 | $L_{\text{sum}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{\Delta L/10}\right)$ | Two-source addition shortcut | $\Delta L = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ |
| 7 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous SPL | T = measurement period |
| 8 | $L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\left[\int_{\text{day}} 10^{L/10}dt + 10 \int_{\text{night}} 10^{L/10}dt\right]\right)$ | Day-Night average level | 10 dB night penalty |
| 9 | $L_{90} \leq L_{50} \leq L_{10}$ | Statistical ordering | Always holds |
| 10 | $TL = 20 \log_{10}(m) - 47$ (mass law) | Transmission loss of barrier | $m$ = surface density kg/m² |
| 11 | $A = \alpha \cdot S$ | Total absorption (Sabine) | $\alpha$ = absorption coeff., S = area |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification and prediction of noise sources is **essential** for:

- **Highway & Expressway Projects:** Predicting noise at roadside villages; designing sound barriers.
- **Airport EIA:** Mapping noise contours (NEF, CNEL) around runways.
- **Metro Rail & Railway:** Assessing vibration and noise at track-side buildings.
- **Power Plants & Refineries:** Designing green belt attenuation for machinery noise.
- **Urban Planning:** Zoning residential zones away from industrial corridors.
- **Occupational Health:** Designing PPE and shift schedules for workers.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most 14-mark questions combine **two-source addition** with **distance attenuation** for a point source. Master formulas 1, 3, 5, and 7 — they cover 80% of numerical questions.

---

## 2.5 Underlying 'Why' Behind the Logarithmic Decibel Scale

The human ear responds to sound pressure over a vast range (from $20 \, \mu Pa$ threshold to $\sim 200 \, Pa$ pain threshold — a ratio of 10 million). Using a linear scale would be impractical. The **logarithmic decibel scale** compresses this enormous range into a manageable 0–140 dB scale that matches human perception (Weber-Fechner law: perceived intensity is proportional to log of stimulus).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL) from Sound Pressure

### Step 1: Define the Reference Pressure
The reference pressure $P_0$ is the threshold of human hearing at 1000 Hz:
$$P_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, N/m^2$$

### Step 2: Form the Ratio
The ratio of measured pressure $P$ to reference pressure $P_0$ is dimensionless:
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{P}{P_0}$$

### Step 3: Take the Base-10 Logarithm
The logarithm compresses the wide range:
$$\log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right)$$

### Step 4: Multiply by 20 (not 10)
Because sound **pressure** is a field quantity (amplitude), and **intensity** is proportional to pressure squared, the factor 20 emerges from $10 \log_{10}(P^2/P_0^2) = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$:
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

### Step 5: Worked Numerical Example
**Problem:** A machine produces a sound pressure of $P = 0.2 \, Pa$. Calculate SPL.

**Solution:**
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right)$$
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000)$$
$$L_p = 20 \times 4 = 80 \, \text{dB}$$

**Interpretation:** The machine produces 80 dB SPL — comparable to heavy city traffic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Point Source Spherical Spreading Law

### Step 1: Imagine a Point Source Emitting Power $W$ (Watts)
Total acoustic power $W$ radiates uniformly through the surface of a sphere of radius $r$:
$$I = \frac{W}{4 \pi r^2} \quad \text{(Sound Intensity at distance } r \text{)}$$

### Step 2: Recognize Inverse-Square Law
Sound intensity decreases with the **square of distance**.

### Step 3: Express Intensity Level
$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{4 \pi r^2 I_0}\right)$$

### Step 4: Define Sound Power Level
$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right), \quad W_0 = 10^{-12} \, W$$

So:
$$L_I = L_W - 10 \log_{10}(4 \pi r^2)$$

### Step 5: Convert to Sound Pressure Level
For a free field, $L_p \approx L_I$ (within 0.1 dB at standard conditions):
$$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11 \quad \text{(dB)}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** for point source attenuation used in every EIA.

### Step 6: Quick Numerical — Doubling Distance
**Problem:** A compressor has $L_W = 100$ dB. Find SPL at $r = 10 \, m$ and $r = 20 \, m$.

**At r = 10 m:**
$$L_p = 100 - 20 \log_{10}(10) - 11 = 100 - 20 - 11 = 69 \, \text{dB}$$

**At r = 20 m:**
$$L_p = 100 - 20 \log_{10}(20) - 11 = 100 - 26.02 - 11 = 62.98 \, \text{dB}$$

**Change:** $\Delta L = 69 - 62.98 = 6.02 \approx 6 \, \text{dB}$ drop when distance doubles — this is the famous **"6 dB per doubling of distance"** rule for point sources.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Addition of Two Sound Levels

This is **the most tested derivation in KTU noise questions**.

### Step 1: Sound Intensities Add Linearly
Sound pressures do **not** add linearly, but intensities do:
$$I_{\text{total}} = I_1 + I_2$$

### Step 2: Express in Decibels
$$I_i = I_0 \cdot 10^{L_i/10}$$
$$I_{\text{total}} = I_0 \left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

### Step 3: Take Logarithm
$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right)$$

### Step 4: Factor Out the Larger Level
Let $L_1 \geq L_2$. Factor $10^{L_1/10}$:
$$L_{\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[10^{L_1/10}\left(1 + 10^{(L_2 - L_1)/10}\right)\right]$$
$$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{-\Delta L/10}\right)$$

where $\Delta L = L_1 - L_2 \geq 0$.

### Step 5: Worked Numerical
**Problem:** A factory has two machines producing 80 dB and 76 dB. Find total SPL.

**Step 5a:** Identify levels: $L_1 = 80$, $L_2 = 76$.

**Step 5b:** Compute difference:
$$\Delta L = 80 - 76 = 4 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 5c:** Apply shortcut table for $\Delta L = 4$ dB → correction = **+1.5 dB**:
$$L_{\text{total}} = L_1 + 1.5 = 80 + 1.5 = 81.5 \, \text{dB}$$

### Step 6: Standard Addition Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| $\Delta L = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (dB) | Add to Larger Level (dB) |
|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 3.0 |
| 1 | 2.5 |
| 2 | 2.1 |
| 3 | 1.8 |
| 4 | 1.5 |
| 5 | 1.2 |
| 6 | 1.0 |
| 7 | 0.8 |
| 8 | 0.6 |
| 9 | 0.5 |
| 10 | 0.4 |
| $\geq 10$ | 0 (ignore smaller) |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq)

### Step 1: Definition
Leq is the constant sound level that, if maintained over the same time period $T$, would deliver the same total acoustic energy as the actual time-varying sound:
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right)$$

### Step 2: Discrete Form (Field Data)
When sound level is sampled at intervals $\Delta t$:
$$L_{eq} \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$$

### Step 3: Worked Numerical
**Problem:** Sound levels at a site for 4 hours are: 60 dB (1 hr), 70 dB (2 hr), 80 dB (1 hr). Find Leq.

**Step 3a:** Convert each to linear intensity ratio:
$$10^{60/10} \times 1 = 10^6 \, \text{(for 1 hr)}$$
$$10^{70/10} \times 2 = 2 \times 10^7 \, \text{(for 2 hr)}$$
$$10^{80/10} \times 1 = 10^8 \, \text{(for 1 hr)}$$

**Step 3b:** Sum:
$$\text{Sum} = 10^6 + 2 \times 10^7 + 10^8 = 1 \times 10^6 + 20 \times 10^6 + 100 \times 10^6 = 121 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3c:** Divide by total time T = 4 hr:
$$\frac{121 \times 10^6}{4} = 30.25 \times 10^6$$

**Step 3d:** Take log:
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(30.25 \times 10^6) = 10 \log_{10}(3.025 \times 10^7)$$
$$L_{eq} = 10 (7 + \log_{10}(3.025)) = 10 (7 + 0.4808) = 74.81 \, \text{dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity Check:** Leq (74.81 dB) is between the min (60) and max (80), and is pulled toward the louder 80 dB event because of logarithmic energy weighting. ✓ Correct.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Barrier Attenuation (Mass Law)

For a solid barrier of surface density $m$ (kg/m²):
$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(m) - 47 \quad \text{(dB)}$$

**Example:** A 5 cm thick concrete barrier, density 2400 kg/m³.
$$m = 0.05 \times 2400 = 120 \, \text{kg/m}^2$$
$$TL = 20 \log_{10}(120) - 47 = 20 (2.079) - 47 = 41.58 - 47 = -5.4 \, \text{dB}$$

(Corrected for practical barriers using Maekawa's formula with Fresnel number, but mass law is the academic foundation tested in KTU.)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
